text:
KOLKATA: England will face a markedly different West Indies team in Sunday's ICC World Twenty20 final compared to the one they squared off against during the Super 10 stage match, asserted Eoin Morgan on the eve of the title decider.

Doubts have surfaced about England's chances in this sixth edition of the tournament following West Indies' emphatic 18-run win over them in the finals, narrowing their path to a second T20 title. "I think it's safe to say that we're two completely different sides given our progression throughout the tournament," Morgan told reporters at Eden Gardens on Saturday. "It's difficult to compare teams from the opening match of the tournament to the final of the World Cup."

While Chris Gayle is due for a big score after having made minimal impact in the tournament thus far, Morgan stressed that England could not afford to solely focus on the Jamaican. "Even before we played West Indies in the group stages, I was quite firm in stating that Chris Gayle isn't just the West Indies team," said the 29-year-old captain. "It's essential that when you're facing strong teams, you don't solely focus on one or two good players. It's everyone because anyone can pose a threat." Morgan added, "India and viewers alike saw this with Lendl Simmons' knock."

Less than a year ago, England suffered an embarrassing exit in the 50-over World Cup. The batsman acknowledged, "It's quite a stark difference. In some ways, it's hard to believe. I would have never imagined such a rapid turnaround or impact...the key factor has been our mindset." Morgan expressed his pride saying, "Tomorrow's opportunity is a product of that. It's incredibly exciting."

Morgan expressed hope that his teammates wouldn't be overwhelmed by the occasion in front of over 65,000 spectators at the iconic Eden Gardens. "We know it won't be a typical game," he said. "I want all our players to embrace the moment and tomorrow everything will feel a bit rushed." He added, "To start with, it's crucial that we're in a good frame of mind to slow things down when necessary and more importantly, execute our skills."

text:
In the Bible, we find instances where God seems to deceive, not just demons and sinners, but even the righteous. Take Abraham, the patriarch of the Jewish people, for example. God asked him to sacrifice his son Isaac, only to miraculously stop him at the last moment as Abraham held a knife above his bound and trembling boy. This event occurred centuries before John Calvin pondered over a similar idea in I Kings, where God is said to 'wish that the false king Ahab be deceived', using the devil to fulfill this wish by sending a lying spirit among all the prophets.

Calvin found this passage particularly powerful, as it challenged the long-held notion that God cannot will evil. For centuries, theologians like Augustine had tried to absolve God from any wrongdoing by distinguishing between what God wants and what He allows. However, Calvin argued that scripture clearly shows that God does more than just allow evil; He actively deceives. He likened it to the infinite and inscrutable nature of God – a being who hates all liars, commands truth, yet seems to lie nonetheless. While Calvin admitted that God might never actually lie, he acknowledged that understanding this paradox is beyond our human comprehension.

Even those who believed that deception was incompatible with God's divine perfection found themselves admitting that God does indeed deceive at times. These theological discussions often take place off the main stage, not in treatises but in commentaries on the Bible, sermons, and letters – especially when they delve into the life of Christ. After all, why did God need to send His only Son to redeem humanity? And why did Jesus conceal His divinity upon arriving on Earth, choosing instead to appear as a man named Jesus?

Gregory of Nyssa, Augustine's contemporary, suggested that we interpret Jesus' life as a 33-year-long ruse. The devil had initially deceived Adam and Eve by making them believe that eating the fruit from the Tree of Knowledge would not lead to death but grant them divine wisdom. Deceived, they ate, and in doing so, became slaves to the devil, subject to suffering and death. To counteract this web of despair, Gregory argued that God chose to deceive the devil just as the devil had deceived Adam and Eve.

text:
Panama City, Panama – After nearly a decade of expansion work, Panama is set to officially open its expanded canal this weekend to accommodate larger cargo ships, aiming to boost transit revenues and global trade. The grand inauguration ceremony will take place on Sunday along the banks of the canal. President Juan Carlos Varela will unveil the newly constructed locks and third shipping lane added to the existing 102-year-old canal.

Foreign dignitaries, including the presidents of Taiwan, Chile, as well as other Central American nations, are expected to attend the ceremony. A Chinese class cargo ship will be the first vessel to test the new infrastructure, making its way through from the Atlantic and exiting into the Pacific a few hours later. The newly accessible Neopanamax vessels, which measure approximately 50 feet wider and 1,000 feet longer than the previous Panamax ships, can now navigate the expanded canal.

Annual cargo volumes are projected to double over the next decade, with Panama hoping to triple the $1 billion in shipping fees it currently receives annually. Given recent linkages between Panama and the "Panama Papers" scandal involving offshore businesses owned by the world's wealthy and influential figures, the expanded canal is seen as an opportunity to rehabilitate the country's tarnished image, with the head of the Panama Canal Authority, Jorge Quijano, stating to AFP this week, "This will show the 'real face of Panama.'"

In addition, world trade stands to benefit from the canal's role as an inter-oceanic highway for goods between the United States and Asia. With more cargo being transported on larger ships, lower transportation costs are expected. Furthermore, Panama has its sights set on tapping into the lucrative market of transporting liquefied natural gas (LNG) between the United States and Asia, particularly to Japan. The older canal was unable to accommodate LNG vessels due to their size; however, with the expansion, these ships can now traverse the waterway.

"The inauguration of the expanded Panama Canal represents new possibilities for international trade," stated President Varela. Currently, around five percent of global maritime commercial traffic utilizes the canal, offering a valuable shortcut between North America and Asia.

text:
What happened was horrific, and I've struggled with the terms to use ever since. I found myself alternating between 'sexual assault' and 'rape' when sharing my experience with others. Watching sexual assault trials on the news now leaves me paralyzed, as it triggers a flood of distressing memories I'd rather forget. Each new case brings back debilitating recollections that I wish to suppress. However, these trials also force me to scrutinize my own memory: to recall what I said, when, and to whom, to ensure my story remains consistent. Consistency is a common standard we use to judge credibility, both in everyday life and in court. A 2004 Australian study found that consistency was the most frequently cited criterion used by juries to assess a victim's credibility. They looked for consistency in the accuser's statements, as well as in their post-assault behavior. If this is how juries operate, attorneys and prosecutors will follow suit in their pursuit of convictions. Judges may provide instructions about 'explainable' discrepancies to juries, but these are unlikely to challenge deeply ingrained cultural narratives.

In 2018, Christine Blasey Ford, a psychologist at Palo Alto University in California, accused Brett Kavanaugh of sexual assault when they were teenagers. The report issued by the Republican Party-appointed attorney criticized Ford for not providing a consistent account of when and where the assault occurred. During her Senate cross-examination, she was questioned about seemingly irrelevant details like the distance to the house where the assault took place, the names and number of everyone present, and her travel history. These questions were designed to test her consistency in reporting certain details, even though they were not directly relevant to the accusation. The answers Ford gave to these questions were used to discredit her account of Kavanaugh's actions at that party.

Consistency might seem like an objective measure, free from the implicit bias that affects other credibility judgments. It could serve as a minimal standard for making a credible accusation. However, focusing on inconsistencies can sometimes be used to discredit victims, especially when the questions being asked are not directly related to the accusation at hand. This tactic, while appearing objective, can still be biased and may unfairly undermine the victim's story.

Singapore: Oil prices climbed in Asia on Tuesday, as investors awaited an OPEC meeting set for Friday, during which the cartel is expected to maintain high output levels despite a global oil supply glut. Analysts anticipate that OPEC will prioritize maintaining market share over production cuts to boost prices. By 0300 GMT, US benchmark West Texas Intermediate for January delivery was trading at $41.94, up 29 cents from the previous day's close, while Brent crude for January stood at 44.79 a barrel, rising 18 cents.

Bernard Aw, market strategist at IG Markets in Singapore, commented, "Crude oil remains soft ahead of the OPEC meeting, as many investors expect the organization to remain firm on its production target." Iran is expected to announce plans to expand output after crippling western sanctions are lifted under a landmark deal reached with major world powers in July aimed at curbing its nuclear program. The lifting of these sanctions will allow Iran to increase oil exports, further contributing to the global oversupply of crude oil.

Iran's deputy foreign minister Abbas Araghchi stated last week that his country expects the deal to take effect early next month, once Tehran has fulfilled its commitments. Oil prices have been on a downward trend since mid-2014, when they reached above $100 per barrel, with the decline picking up pace after OPEC's November 2014 meeting indicated that output would remain high.

Furthermore, data released Tuesday showed that a key measure of China's manufacturing activity fell to its weakest level in more than three years in November, underscoring weaknesses in the world's largest energy consumer. The official Purchasing Managers Index (PMI), which tracks activity in the crucial factories and workshops sector, declined to 49.6, marking the fourth consecutive month of decline and the lowest figure since August 2012.

text:
In the vast expanse of our world, both condors soaring majestically across the skies and whooping cranes gracefully dancing in wetlands have faced their own set of challenges. At the US Geological Survey Patuxent Wildlife Research Center in Maryland, whooping cranes are taught the basics of survival - eating, drinking, and flocking - by humans donning something between a burka and a beekeeping suit, with crane puppet heads attached to their hands. Even pandas have been cared for by humans dressed as panda mascots. While these animals may not cause the chaos that adolescent condors do, they can still develop peculiar behaviors of their own. For instance, puppet-reared whooping cranes struggle with incubating their eggs in the wild; often abandoning their nests just days before hatching. John French, research manager at Patuxent and head of the crane program, has a few theories as to why these cranes face issues with parenting. One idea is that they're sensitive to black flies, which swarm nesting cranes in the wild, but were spared from during their upbringing in relative comfort. Another theory is that crucial information about the breeding process was missed during their puppet-parented childhood. When I asked French what this missing piece might be, he admitted, 'I have no idea.' This conundrum ensures a continual need for more puppet-rearing, which seems paradoxical in nature.

This peculiar method of conservation is mirrored by the growing de-extinction movement, where one wonders if these puppet-reared condors and whooping cranes are living out a tragic version of Plato's cave allegory - born into an artificial world, only to be thrust into the harsh, complex reality. Lacking the necessary tools for survival outside their carefully managed environments, they inevitably struggle. It's challenging to fathom why keepers thought this hands-on approach would foster psychologically healthy animals. However, like all intensive conservation programs, puppet-rearing was never truly about the animals themselves.

The primary goal of any captive breeding program is to establish self-sustaining populations of wild animals that can survive and thrive independently from human intervention. Yet, the underlying motivation for their existence often stems from a deep-seated human guilt. We drove the condor, the whooping crane, the panda, and countless other species to the brink of extinction; therefore, we feel compelled to save them. This sentiment typically translates into an obsessive focus on the most adorable or symbolic of these endangered species, which either remain in captivity or are released back into the very world that nearly exterminated their ancestors. This approach, while well-intentioned, is flawed and beguiling, as exemplified by the de-extinction movement's ambitious plans to resurrect extinct species such as the passenger pigeon.

text:
Reading through a collection of profound and multifaceted books regarding my country's dark past, I couldn't help but let out a childlike cheer when the Nazis in Tarantino's Inglourious Basterds were engulfed in flames. Despite his intellectual prowess, the movie struck a chord deep within him, stirring emotions he'd harbored for years. In an interview with Die Zeit, Tarantino was asked about German reactions to Inglourious Basterds. His response was thought-provoking: "Besides the Jews, I believe the last three generations of Germans are the ones who have dreamt of killing Adolf Hitler." American history and German imagination - Tarantino nailed it both.

Tarantino isn't the first American director to follow a film about Nazis with one about American slavery, as Spielberg did when he made Amistad after Schindler's List. Both films serve as reminders that we shouldn't use Nazism to end discussions on evil but to start them, and that our own crimes demand the same level of scrutiny. In Django Unchained, Tarantino took it a step further than Spielberg in Amistad, by making the film's only decent white character German. The role could have been any European, but Tarantino reinforced his character's identity with frequent references to Germany. Furthermore, he made us confront our prejudices by having Christoph Waltz, the actor who played one of the most memorable SS officers in cinema history, be the only white person in Django to find American slavery utterly abhorrent.

The German influence on Tarantino's films is apparent in Django, reflecting the German concept of Vergangenheitsaufarbeitung. During the filming of Inglourious Basterds, he spent six months living in Berlin, long enough to grasp how Germans continue to confront their troubled past head-on. An interview with Gates for The Root illuminated just how deeply this influence seeped into his work: "I think America is one of the only countries that has not been forced, at times by the rest of the world, to look its own past sins squarely in the eye. And it's only by doing so that you can possibly move past them."

He doesn't shy away from drawing direct comparisons. If there were a Nuremberg trial, he says in the same interview, then D W Griffith, the director of The Birth of a Nation - the film credited with rekindling the Ku Klux Klan - would be charged with war crimes. And The Clansman, the book upon which that film was based, stands alongside Mein Kampf for Tarantino in terms of its heinous imagery: "It is evil. And I don't use that word lightly."

Some critics have questioned whether a white director should make a film about slavery, but... well, let's just say it's a conversation worth having, shall we?

MUMBAI: Following the day-night test against New Zealand at Adelaide Oval last November, former cricket great Richard Hadlee has expressed his support for another floodlit match, this time in Brisbane in December. Speaking to reporters in Mumbai, Hadlee expressed a cautious optimism about the concept, stating that "perhaps one test in a series is fair enough."

While he acknowledged that many fans prefer the traditional daytime format of the game, Hadlee emphasized the importance of embracing innovation and adapting to the changing landscape of cricket. He noted that the durability of the pink kookaburra ball and the dew factor in India have raised doubts about the feasibility of a floodlit test in the country, but expressed confidence that with proper practice, players can overcome these challenges.

"It is important for players to have ample practice before such a contest," Hadlee said. "Asking them to go out there and play a day-night game against the pink ball without any preparation would be unrealistic in this professional era." He added that conditions vary greatly around the world, making it crucial for players to get some practice games under their belts before attempting a floodlit test in India. "We don't really know how the pink ball will work here, which is why players need those practice games," he concluded.

text:
The strange pull of absorption isn't born from focus and concentration, but its opposite - a profound disconnection that leaves you detached from the world. It feels like being played, not by choice but because you've become ensnared. Studies by Schüll show that slot machine addicts, trapped in their compulsion, experience a strange sense of relief, even joy, when they lose. Only when they've lost everything do they find the freedom to walk away. In her book "Addiction by Design" (2012), Schüll describes hardcore players who would rather soil themselves than abandon their posts - adding a chilling new layer to the idea of being a hostage to one's own fate.

When the slot machine lights up and chimes with a win, the gambler feels a sense of near despair. They know they must stay put until they lose it all again. Losing is not seen as defeat but as the price paid for the experience - the thrill - of being distracted while being skillfully, if covertly, drained. Casinos cause real harm to people. I've seen it firsthand. Gamblers don't play with chance and possibility like computer scientists running simulations to predict outcomes. Instead, gaming infects a gambler's soul, like malware, altering their thoughts and feelings, intertwining with their life, and eventually taking control. It's not an external model of the world, but a virus inside. My father never managed to free himself from the grip of gambling: his whole life was ruled by the rhythmic drums of the tables. He lost a fortune over the years, and we lost much more - pieces of him. Casinos stole his ability to discern reality from its imitation. Instead of offering him a chance to practice his actions in the world, gambling became a substitute for it. My father was a perfect candidate for being manipulated. He was gullible and willful, but cowardly when faced with confrontation. In many ways, he resembled Schüll's slot machine addicts: frustrated by a world that had grown too complex to navigate, they reduced their interaction with it to a single manageable transaction - bet or don't bet, play or don't play. I find this deeply sad, and it doesn't explain why I was drawn to professional blackjack myself. There was the thrill, sure, but there was also something else - an adventure, a sense of danger that preceded it, a lure that I couldn't resist.

text:
The incapacity of our legal system to grasp the gravity of its actions, as if the leader of a great constitutional republic were merely a stray dog to be shot down, is a stark reminder of Charles J. Guiteau's conviction and execution in 1882. Although he clearly showed signs of mental instability, his intellect was not questioned.

Over centuries, the courts have been stubbornly resistant to medical expertise, even as our understanding of mental illness has expanded through fields such as psychiatry and psychology. As early as the 16th century, Johann Weyer challenged the Saxon Code's treatment of insanity, stating that it failed to reflect the true nature of mental illness. The response from the bench was dismissive: "Weyer may be a physician, but he is not a lawyer; therefore, his perspective on the link between mental disease and legal transgressions holds no weight."

Fast forward to 1950, and the chasm between legal definitions of insanity and psychiatric realities was as wide as ever. Supreme Court Justice Felix Frankfurter lamented the disconnect between "the pathological processes that give rise to the conflict between so-called legal and medical insanity." In the subsequent four decades, this divide has persisted, with the legal definition of exculpatory mental disease remaining largely unchanged and untouched by advancements in medical theory.

The current legal definition of insanity bears no resemblance to any recognized psychiatric condition; it is a purely legal construct, owing nothing to the realms of psychiatry and neuroscience. As psychiatrist Gregory Zilboorg wrote in 1943: "With rare exceptions, murderers are well aware of what they're doing, and the consequences of their actions, regardless of any psychiatrist's opinion."

This lack of a psychiatric correlate fuels the perception that mental health experts are unreliable or that mental illness is purely subjective. In reality, mental health professionals often disagree on whether a defendant meets the legal definition of insanity, even when they concur on the defendant's diagnosis and level of psychiatric impairment. As forensic psychiatrist Thomas Gutheil at Harvard Medical School put it in 1998: "Psychiatric testimony is about meeting the legal criteria for an insanity-related defense, not about mental illness."

In his work 'On Responsibility' (1996), federal judge Richard Lowell Nygaard noted: "Contemporary psychiatric and psychological data prevent legitimate experts from making accurate and scientific determinations based on the rigid legal definitions the law imposes ... Trials that hinge on a jury's decision about a defendant's guilt based on conflicting expert testimony often devolve into 'battles of experts,' which almost guarantee arbitrary outcomes."

We tend to judge the wrongdoing of an individual by the intensity of our outrage at their actions. When called upon to testify, mental health professionals are faced with this same challenge: to reconcile the complexity of human behavior with the rigid constraints of the law.

text:
In this current cycle, Turchin's prediction of chaos in 2020 was significantly influenced by. Back in 2010, Cliodynamics, a new discipline's flagship journal, debuted, with its inaugural article (by American sociologist Randall Collins) diving into modeling victory and defeat in battle, considering material resources and organizational morale. Following the argument Comte made centuries ago regarding scientific complexity progression, Turchin firmly disagreed that complexity rendered human societies unfit for quantitative analysis. Instead, he argued that it was precisely this complexity that necessitated mathematics.

Weather forecasts were once seen as unreliable due to the intricacy of dealing with the necessary data. However, advancements in technology (such as satellites and computers) now make it possible to mathematically describe and model interactions between the system's various components – and thus know when to carry an umbrella. With equal conviction, Turchin asserted that the cliodynamic approach was not deterministic. Instead, it would provide governments and political leaders with insights into the likely outcomes of competing policy choices.

Crucially, cliodynamics also benefited from the surge in interest in digital humanities. Existing archives were being digitized, uploaded, and made searchable – each day bringing more data that encouraged quantification and enabled mathematical analysis – including the Old Bailey's online database, which Wolf had found himself entangled in. In 2014, the subtitle of their flagship journal was changed from The Journal of Theoretical and Mathematical History to The Journal of Quantitative History and Cultural Evolution. As Turchin explained in his editorial, this shift aimed to position cliodynamics within a broader evolutionary analysis; echoing Russian-American geneticist Theodosius Dobzhansky, he claimed that 'nothing in human history makes sense except in the light of cultural evolution.'

Given Turchin's ecological background, an evolutionary approach to history is hardly surprising. However, given the historical consequences of making politics biological, it raises potential concerns. Even more striking perhaps is the explicit evolutionary turn mirrored a broader shift towards the natural sciences among some members of the humanities. The experiences of World War II and imperialism made Western scholars deeply cautious about using biology and evolution to explain culture and society – the links to xenophobic and sexist politics seemed far too close for comfort. Attempts in the 1970s to establish biosocial rules that could apply to both human and non-human societies led to bitter intellectual schisms that divided disciplines like anthropology down the middle. By the 1990s, the focus may have shifted from race to IQ score, with the emergence of Richard Herrnstein and Charles Murray's The Bell Curve.

The philosophical movement known as absolute realism, championed by Bertrand Russell, G E Moore, and Samuel Alexander, also defended the existence of time itself. As realism gained traction in philosophical circles, critics began to challenge McTaggart's argument against the reality of time. Early opponents included C D Broad, R B Braithewaite, Susan Stebbing, John Alexander Gunn, Hilda Oakeley, J N Findlay, and Victoria Welby. As the 20th century progressed, these critiques persisted. McTaggart's original paper was only 17 pages long, but it has inspired tens of thousands of pages of discussion among philosophers since then. In the 21st century alone, it has been cited over 1,600 times - a remarkable achievement for an article published in a scholarly journal. One might think that such intense criticism would have buried McTaggart's argument, but quite the opposite happened: it gained new life and persisted through the decades. The saying "All publicity is good publicity" seems to apply here. Criticism kept the argument alive in the philosophical consciousness, ensuring its survival over time.

If every moment of time truly exists, then 1066 holds just as much reality as 2055. This idea has sparked a fascinating debate among philosophers: is the past, present, and future part of the world we live in, or are they merely human perspectives? Those who subscribe to B-theory argue that time is simply the ordering of events from before to after. They claim that the notions of past, present, and future do not reflect the nature of the world itself but are rather subjective points of view. To say that a moment is "now" is like saying a place is "here": both depend on one's position. In contrast, A-theorists believe that past, present, and future are real features of our universe. They argue that the very moment I wrote these words was truly in the past, while you are truly reading them now.

Over time, these opposing views have developed into two distinct families of time theories. B-theories assert that time is nothing more than the sequential arrangement of events, with no inherent distinction between past, present, and future. They find support in the idea that time and space are unified as a single entity known as spacetime, popularized by Hermann Minkowski and Albert Einstein. On this view, all moments of time have equal reality, much like the parts of space. Some philosophers, however, disagree with this perspective, finding it static and lacking a sense of flow or movement through time. They argue that A-theory, which posits that past, present, and future are real aspects of our world, more accurately reflects our experience of time's "jerk and whoosh."

Many philosophers who accept A-theory also reject the idea of spacetime, believing that not all moments of time exist equally. For example, in 1923 Broad argued that the past and present are real, while the future is unreal. Today, the debate between B-theory and A-theory remains a lively topic in the philosophy of time.

The fleeting connections between things have always fascinated me, and I've found myself drawn to breaking free from the divide that exists between imagination and reality. This was the theme of a lecture given by Paul Éluard many years ago, and it resonates with me even today. Although he was referring to the surrealists, like his friend Max Ernst, his words could just as easily describe the work of Forestier - I sense in his art a blend of freedom and unity that I find captivating.

Éluard may have also recognized a broader purpose in the surrealist movement; one that sought to challenge an order based on inequality and strove for the total emancipation of humanity. While the confinement of disabled and distressed individuals in institutions may no longer be fashionable, the system that allows their suffering to continue unchecked is very much alive. It manifests itself in news media that normalizes harmful rhetoric and in legal systems that justify violence against vulnerable people.

Some readers might balk at the suggestion that our current reality in Europe and America could be considered fascist. I once did, preferring instead to think of fascism as a relic of the past, vanquished 80 years ago. However, others have long argued that fascism is not just something that exists on the fringes or among white supremacists, but rather a pervasive and insidious force that operates within our own societies.

Simone Weil's essay from 1940 on Homer's Iliad highlighted the indifference of the strong towards the weak, noting how force can dehumanize and degrade individuals until they are no more than corpses. In the aftermath of World War II, Max Horkheimer and Theodor Adorno argued that fascism had found a new life in the combination of bureaucracy and capitalism that dominated postwar society - a system that suppresses moral feelings and treats human beings as mere cogs in a machine.

In conclusion, I believe it is essential to recognize that fascism exists not only on the fringes but also within our own societies, hiding in plain sight. We must be vigilant in challenging systems of reality that allow for the mistreatment and dehumanization of vulnerable individuals, lest we find ourselves complicit in their suffering.

text:
Translating classics shouldering the weight of their originals - how does that translate on paper? Over the past century, numerous attempts have been made by various translators to tackle this task. What these examples demonstrate more than anything is that success in translation is subjective, and my personal preference leans towards Jeromite's flexibility over Augustinian adherence. Despite centuries of scholarly debate, there seems to be no straightforward answer.

One of the most debated translators of modern times is Ezra Pound. He was both lauded for his poetic masterpieces and criticized for his audacious liberties. Pound's anthology of classical Chinese poetry, Cathay (1915), is a prime example of translation without any particular knowledge of the source language (Pound relied heavily on Ernest Fenollosa's literal translations). Remarkably, this approach produced results that were more convincing than those created by fluent readers of Chinese. Some scholars even argue that Pound's intuitive phrasing was more accurate. This wasn't an isolated incident, as Pound often took similar creative liberties with texts he could read in the original, like Arthur Rimbaud's 'Au Cabaret-Vert' (1870). Here's a stanza from that poem translated by Wallace Fowlie:

For a week my boots had been torn By the pebbles on the roads. I was getting into Charleroi.
– At the Green Cabaret, I asked for bread And butter, and for ham that would be half chilled …

Pound, on the other hand, aimed to capture the carefree spirit of a young traveler. His rendition is casual, succinct:

Wearing out my shoes, 8th day On the bad roads, I got into Charleroi. Bread, butter, at the Green Cabaret And the half-cold ham …

Similarly, Samuel Beckett, while translating a simulated 'delirium' from 1930 by Paul Éluard, employed obvious anachronism to bring out the intensity of the original:

Thou my great one whom I adore Beautiful as the whole earth and in the most beautiful stars of the earth that I adore thou my great woman adored by all the powers of the stars …

Compare this with Richard Howard's 1965 translation, which, while respectful of Éluard's modernity, sounds less passionate, oddly out of tune, even old-fashioned compared to Beckett's version.

Lastly, Georges Perec's novel La disparition (1969), with no instances of the letter 'e', was once considered untranslatable in both French and English, the most common grapheme in both languages. However, Gilbert Adair managed to create a lipogrammatic translation, A Void, in 1995, which showcases many clever adaptations.

A festive commemoration of Muhammad Ali's life continued on Wednesday in his hometown of Louisville, where the "I Am Ali" Festival celebrated the iconic boxer's legacy by sharing stories and engaging children through arts and crafts. Craig Davidson, a 45-year-old Louisville resident, brought his 15-year-old sons to pay their respects. "As a kid their age, I grew up watching his fights," Davidson said. "I knew about his stance during the Vietnam War. I just wanted them to know more about him."

Ali, whose boxing prowess, charisma, and vocal opposition to the Vietnam War made him one of the world's most influential figures in the turbulent 1960s and '70s, passed away last Friday at an Arizona hospital due to septic shock. He was 74 years old. Preparations were underway for the final days of mourning, including a public memorial service on Friday at the KFC Yum! Center. Fans lined up outside the arena earlier in the day to secure tickets for the service, with at least 14,000 distributed after officials released them early due to the long queue.

The final service will feature notable figures such as former U.S. President Bill Clinton and comedian Billy Crystal. Actor Will Smith, who portrayed Ali in a 2001 film, and former heavyweight champion Lennox Lewis will serve as pallbearers. The festival took place at the Kentucky Center for the Performing Arts, with activities geared towards children. These included music, dance, art, and educational workshops celebrating Ali's life. Children had the opportunity to create butterfly and bee masks, symbolizing Ali's famous quote about his boxing style: "Float like a butterfly, sting like a bee. The hands can't hit what the eyes can't see."

"Today is about creating a connection with Muhammad Ali for these children," Louisville Mayor Greg Fischer explained in an interview on Wednesday. "As they grow older, they can reflect on this day, ensuring his legacy continues across multiple generations." The festival began with the release of 30 Monarch butterflies into the air, reminding everyone that before winning the boxing heavyweight title or claiming an Olympic gold medal, Ali was a young boy from Louisville whose bicycle had been stolen. Upon turning to the police for help, he discovered his love for boxing.

However, the sky-high prices fetched for his paintings today bear little resemblance to Monet's financial struggles during his lifetime. The tales of critical resistance to Impressionism and the ensuing financial woes of the movement's artists are well-known folklore. Indeed, Monet did face some early hardships. A juror for the annual Paris Salon in 1868 dismissed his works, effectively impeding sales opportunities. That same year, he was denied credit for paint and, according to legend, survived one winter on nothing but potatoes. Yet, for most of his career, Monet was far from a starving artist. His diet of potatoes soon gave way to French delicacies like foie gras, truffles, and lobster in peppercorn sauce. One visitor raved about the culinary delights enjoyed at Monet's home. After turning 50, Monet became a perpetual millionaire: quite the outcome for such a gourmand. His wealth afforded him a fleet of cars, tailored tweed suits from a Parisian tailor, and a team of gardeners to maintain his famous gardens. In this regard, he shares more similarities with contemporary artists like Damien Hirst or Jeff Koons, with their powerful dealers, affluent collectors, and multimillion-dollar fortunes, than he does with artistic outsiders such as Vincent van Gogh, who lived in poverty. Monet's surname is almost a pun for 'money,' a fact that headline writers and others appreciate. In essence, he was the Roaring Twenties' precursor to Koons, as his 1922 sale of one canvas to a Japanese tycoon made him the world's most expensive living artist four years before his death. Monet has long been the preferred artist for the rich and famous. Besides Marsico, recent owners have included David Rockefeller, the Queen Mother, Imelda Marcos, Prince Rainier, Steve Wynn, and numerous Russian and Asian billionaires. Their fear of falling may seem the same as ours looking on from safety, but it's not the fall they dread; it's the flames. And those standing outside, shouting 'Don't!' and 'Hang on!', can never truly understand the terror that drives such a choice. The suicidal person is not misguided but rather facing choices beyond our comprehension—choices only understood by those who also feel the flames slowly consuming them.

I can't fully explain what reading all this meant to me. The book seemed like a mirror, reflecting my every feeling back at me. Pondering the vast, mysterious cosmos, it strikes me that we humans have a remarkable task ahead - shaping this universe to our own design. But what will we create? As Homo Faber, or 'Humankind the Creator', perhaps we've created gods in our image, not the other way around. We might be just another small species orbiting an average star, lost amidst the infinite, indifferent universe. Yet, we possess a unique ability - creation. Why then are we stuck in a rut, repeating destructive patterns instead of unlocking our creative potential?

It's a consumer culture that stifles our creativity. The financial crisis of 2008 may have been the first crack in this system. The consumer-based economic model - an improvised solution following the demise of industrialization - has reached its expiration date. It thrived when technology limited creative expression to a privileged few, and television delivered their message to the masses - what Seth Godin calls the 'TV-industrial complex'.

But today, the internet has democratized communication, and smartphones carry powerful creative tools. Our economy is grappling with an identity crisis, struggling to keep pace with its own technological advancements. Observe the surge of new voices emerging on social media, centered around creativity.

Our leaders' instinctive reaction is to rebuild our consumer culture. They view us as passive consumers rather than creators. It may take a generational shift before a world leader addresses a speech not to the world’s consumers, but to its creators. Yet, a creator-centric culture is emerging organically, fueled by the creators themselves.

Platforms like Kickstarter, just three years old, significantly outfunded the US National Endowment for the Arts last year. They empower artists and entrepreneurs to circumvent traditional investment routes. Crowd-funded creativity is not driven by commercial interests or political agendas, but by the creative passions of the crowd.

The 'maker' movement revives the spirit of craftsmanship and merges it with modern technologies like 3D printing, promising to revolutionize manufacturing as the internet did communication. The principles of 'open source' are expanding beyond software development, rejuvenating academia, science, politics, media, and education.

Social media is teeming with new voices - Maria Popova, a 'curator of interestingness', who started Brain Pickings; Big Think, sifting the 'best thinking on the planet'; countless TED talks - all centered around creativity. The future lies in our creative hands if we dare to seize it.

Growing up in a small mountain town in the Dominican Republic and newly transplanted to bustling Corona, Queens just before my 12th birthday, I stumbled upon a collection of Plato's dialogues in a heap of trash near my home. My mother had left the Dominican Republic years prior, working in a garment factory to earn minimum wage while petitioning for my brother and me to join her. In 1985, we entered New York City's overcrowded public school system where free lunches often filled our bellies. Like many immigrants, we were poor, vulnerable, and disoriented by the upheaval.

My journey was far from auspicious for the academic career that would eventually unfold. However, in time, the harshness of my beginnings morphed into a unique perspective from which to observe the intellectual and social world I'd entered. My education in what some call 'the great books' was instrumental in my development, yet I've also encountered criticism that these works – Homer, Sophocles, Plato, Montaigne, Cervantes, Goethe, Hegel, Dostoyevsky, Woolf, and more – are not for people like me. They're for white people, or the wealthy, or those born into class privileges I never had.

Yet, in this collection of Plato's dialogues, I encountered an aging man named Socrates on the brink of his execution. He defended himself against accusations of corrupting youth and introducing new gods to the city: 'Men of Athens,' he pleaded, 'I am your friend, but... as long as I draw breath and am able, I shall not cease to question and reflect upon our lives... Are you not ashamed of your eagerness for wealth, reputation, and honors, when you pay no heed to wisdom or truth, or the well-being of your soul?'

By the end of the collection, we find Socrates calmly drinking poison, laying down, and dying: 'Such was the end of our friend,' says the narrator, 'a man who, we would say, was one of the best among us, the wisest, and the most upright.' These words resonated deeply within me, a poor immigrant with no claim to wealth or privilege. And I was startled by Socrates' assertion that 'an unexamined life is not worth living.'

Since 2009, every summer, I have revisited these texts, finding solace and inspiration in their wisdom. They remind me that knowledge, reflection, and self-examination are within reach for anyone willing to seek it out, regardless of race, ethnicity, or socioeconomic status.

text:
Struggling to compose or hold a universal ethical rulebook, I've come to realize that what constitutes a moral justification can vary greatly depending on the context. For instance, consider happiness - something that classical utilitarians view as an unwavering reason for action. However, in reality, it's more complex. The pleasure derived from cruel acts, such as those of a sadist, should discourage us from engaging in such actions, not encourage them. Conversely, there are situations where denying happiness is morally right, like cases of 'tough love.' As the song by Nick Lowe goes, "You've got to be cruel to be kind."

Understanding that a seemingly cruel act might stem from kindness is key to understanding the situation. It's about grasping the accurate description, which, in turn, informs our response. This intuitive understanding of the situation reveals what needs to be done - it communicates the demands of the situation itself. Particularists argue that these demands are an integral part of the situation we find ourselves in.

Happiness is a complex concept that has evolved in our understanding over time. Early utilitarians like Bentham equated happiness with pleasure, assuming it to be a single, measurable entity. However, recent researchers suggest a more nuanced view, recognizing multiple and potentially conflicting factors contributing to an individual's overall happiness. This aligns well with the particularist perspective that evaluation is always holistic in nature.

It's worth remembering that Aristotle's concept of eudaimonia - often translated as 'happiness' - was broader and less subjective than simple pleasure or momentary satisfaction. It encompassed the general quality of one's life as a whole. For particularists, individual perception and judgment are essential in resolving ethical dilemmas. There is no theoretical ethical system that can replace human insight and wisdom. Principles can be useful, serving as practical guidelines, but they must be flexible enough to accommodate exceptions.

At the heart of ethics lies not a set of rules, but individual human beings with character, judgment, and wisdom. The idea of wisdom - distinct from knowledge - has been difficult for Kantian and utilitarian ethics, as well as modern society in general, to embrace. Wisdom is something we must rediscover if we are to navigate the complexities of our increasingly intricate world.

This marked Nadal's first round exit at Melbourne Park since his 2009 championship win, and only his second such early departure from a major tournament throughout his career.

Nadal, who reached the finals of 14 grand slams last season, had hoped to continue his winning streak but found himself outmatched by Verdasco's resilience. The Spaniard, down 2-0 in the first set, staged a remarkable comeback, showcasing 90 winners alongside 91 unforced errors.

This was a partial redemption for Verdasco, who lost to Nadal in a grueling five-set final at the same tournament back in 2009 - the second longest match ever recorded at the Australian Open. The 32-year-old displayed his serving prowess throughout each tiebreak, notably in the second set to keep the match alive, but faltered early in the fifth when he was broken.

Apologetic, he raised his hands before aceing Nadal to take a narrow lead. This seemed to rattle Nadal, who had made it to six grand slam finals last year but succumbed to the pressure on his serve.

"This year was a completely different story," Verdasco admitted after the match. "I've been playing and practicing well, so it's tough when you work hard and go out so early in such an important event."

text:
In today's discourse, certain substances have historically been portrayed as the 'vices' of supposedly 'inferior' groups, branded as a 'plague' or 'contagion', capable of infecting society at large. Although such explicit beliefs may no longer be prevalent, these substances often retain an enigmatic and unfamiliar air. This is particularly true for psychoactive substances that are viewed as part of everyday life, like caffeine in the West, coca in the Andes, or ayahuasca in the Amazon. During the early 1900s, opium, morphine, and cocaine started to lose social acceptability, much like tobacco has in our contemporary era. Their use was increasingly viewed through the lens of medical risks, making users appear negligent or morally weak. In essence, these drugs transformed into what we now know as 'legal highs': technically not prohibited but existing only under-the-counter or among those in the know. Once their sale was officially outlawed during the years surrounding World War I, 'drugs' came to carry legal weight: a defined list of substances deemed not just medically harmful, but associated with criminal elements.

The banning of these substances sparked minimal public debate compared to the prohibition of alcohol in the U.S., given that the drug issue was then marginal and confined mainly to ethnic, bohemian, or criminal subcultures without significant public voice. The only organized resistance against this negative portrayal came from the pharmaceutical industry, concerned about damage to its legitimate trade due to unfortunate associations. For instance, what is now the American Pharmacists Association, backed by corporate giants like Johnson & Johnson, protested against terms such as 'drug evil', 'drug fiend', and 'drug habit'. They lobbied newspapers to specify the drugs in question as 'narcotics' or 'opiates.' However, the ambiguous and useful term 'drugs' proved too hard to replace with more precise words. It did not merely refer to specific chemicals but also conveyed a moral stance on their use by certain people and for specific purposes. This perspective was eventually embedded into legal frameworks meant to prohibit them.

The 1961 UN Single Convention on Narcotic Drugs, the foundation of international drug laws, stands out among UN conventions due to its use of the word 'evil' to describe the problem it aims to address. Legislators hailed the 1961 Convention as the triumphant conclusion of a fifty-year struggle to ban drugs, a battle that had begun at the Opium Conference in The Hague in 1911. Looking back, however, 1961 marked the beginning of the disintegration of the consensus around the 'evils' of drugs. A daring postwar generation, the first to consume globally, was beginning to awaken to the realization that alcohol was not the world's only intoxicant. An underground network was starting to circulate news about hashish smoking in Morocco and LSD synthesis in Switzerland.

Joe Manimbo, senior market analyst at Western Union Business Solutions, commented, "The relatively quiet US economic calendar for the first half of this week has encouraged some traders to cash in on the dollar's impressive 2% gains from last week - its strongest weekly performance in two months." The euro rose to $1.0942 around 9:00 PM EST, compared to $1.0824 at the same time Monday. The dollar also dropped against the Japanese yen, to 123.86 from 124.30.

Kathy Lien, managing director of BK Asset Management, predicted that the dollar's weakness would be temporary as the US Federal Reserve prepares for an interest rate increase. The next update on the Fed's stance is expected after a policy meeting on July 29th. Lien explained, "Although the Fed isn't anticipated to raise rates in July, the tone of the monetary policy statement should be optimistic, underscoring the idea that the economy is prepared for tightening." Last week, Federal Reserve Chairwoman Janet Yellen indicated that a rate hike was likely this year, providing support for the dollar.

In Europe, there was positive movement regarding Greece's efforts to secure a new bailout. The Greek government submitted a second batch of necessary reform measures to parliament, with a vote scheduled for Wednesday. Nick Bennenbroek, head of currency strategy at Wells Fargo Securities, noted, "The euro is up as progress continues in the negotiations towards Greece's third financial assistance program."

text:
MOHALI: The pressure was on, but New Zealand delivered a commanding performance to clinch victory by 22 runs against Pakistan in their World Twenty20 Super 10 Group Two match at the Punjab Cricket Association IS Bindra Stadium last night. With this win, New Zealand have secured a spot in the semi-finals.

New Zealand's innings were masterfully guided by Martin Guptill's 80 off just 48 deliveries. After opting to bat first, Guptill and captain Kane Williamson provided an explosive start of 62 runs before Williamson was dismissed by Mohammad Irfan for 17. The dismissal of Colin Munro followed soon after, with captain Shahid Afridi catching the ball off a Khalid Latif catch.

Guptill and Corey Anderson then added a quickfire 52 runs in just 34 balls before Guptill was bowled by Mohammad Sami following an aggressive 80-run innings that included three massive sixes and ten fours. Anderson's dismissal came quickly, with Shoaib Malik taking the catch off Afridi for 21. Ross Taylor and Luke Ronchi added another 43 runs to take New Zealand's total to 164 when Sami dismissed Ronchi for 11, caught by Malik. Despite this setback, Taylor remained unbeaten on 36 as New Zealand posted an improbable target of 181 for Pakistan. Sami was the pick of the bowlers, taking two wickets for just 23 runs, while Afridi took two wickets for 40 and Irfan claimed one.

Pakistan's chase never really got going, with only Shoaib Malik (25) and Sarfaraz Ahmed (17) making any significant contributions. The Pakistani batsmen struggled to keep up with the required run rate, eventually falling short by 22 runs at the end of their innings.

This win puts New Zealand in a strong position as they look ahead to the semi-finals, while Pakistan will need to regroup quickly if they are to have any chance of making it through to the final stages of the tournament.

text:
MUMBAI: Industry officials predict that Pakistan's cotton imports will remain close to record levels as the country trimms its cultivation area for the crop.

A supply shortage in Pakistan, coupled with drought-affected output in India - the world's largest producer - could potentially drive up global cotton prices, which currently hover around 11 cents per pound.

Saleem Saleh, acting secretary general of the All Pakistan Textile Mills Association (APTMA), explained, "Punjab has reduced its cultivation area." Unpredictable weather events, such as late-year floods and poor rainfall in recent months, have left many farmers reluctant to grow cotton, hampering Pakistan's attempts to boost domestic production.

Shahzad Ali Khan, chairman of the Pakistan Cotton Ginners Association, elaborated, "Farmers are finding other crops more profitable." The country's cotton output fell by a third to 9.7 million bales in 2015/16, forcing it to import a record 4 million bales that year, up from 1.2 million the previous year, according to APTMA. Pakistan's annual consumption is approximately 15 million bales.

Even for the crop season starting August 1, weather has not been favorable and the crop in Punjab has already suffered due to poor rainfall in May and June, Khan added. Cotton sowing in Pakistan typically begins in April, with harvesting commencing in July. While Pakistan has set a production target of 14.1 million bales for the new season, industry officials suggest that output will fall short, and the next few weeks' rainfall will be crucial in determining yields.

"Import requirements are rising due to increasing local consumption, but production remains stagnant," Saleh said. "This year's imports surged due to crop failure, and we expect next year's imports to remain around this year's level - the actual number depends on production."

Typically, lower output in Pakistan presents an export opportunity for neighboring India, which shipped out approximately 6.5 million bales during this season, with Pakistan importing nearly 2 million. Dhiren Sheth, president of the Cotton Association of India, noted, "India has a freight advantage over other suppliers, making it a preferred choice for buyers in Pakistan whenever they initiate imports."

However, India's recent contract to import 20,000 bales from Pakistan suggests that supply at the top producer is also running low.

Title: Sendai, Japan: G7 Advanced Economies Set to Discuss Growth Boosting Strategies Ahead of Official Meeting

SENDAI, JAPAN - A disagreement on fiscal policy and currencies is expected to set the stage for a frank exchange of views among G7 advanced economies regarding ways to stimulate growth at an informal symposium preceding the official G7 meeting scheduled for Friday. The symposium's participants concurred that rather than relying on short-term measures, each country should focus on policies tailored to their unique economic circumstances. As a result, the G7 finance leaders, while expressing concerns about potential risks to the global economic outlook, may struggle to agree on specific actions to invigorate stagnant worldwide growth.

Bank of Japan Governor Haruhiko Kuroda told reporters on Thursday that he expected a candid discussion on achieving price stability and economic growth through the use of monetary, fiscal, and structural policies tailored to each country's individual needs.

The Risks of Brexit Highlight Agenda

At Friday's G7 session on the global economy, the risk of a British exit from the European Union in a June referendum (Brexit) will occupy a prominent position. The finance leaders are set to engage in discussions about this issue, as well as other topics such as the need to enhance cybersecurity in light of growing financial transactions on a global scale. According to sources familiar with the group's deliberations, there is an increased awareness among G7 leaders that immediate action is required in this area.

In February, a cyber theft targeting a Bangladesh central bank account served as a reminder of the need for improved cybersecurity measures within the global financial network (SWIFT), which banks use to transfer billions of dollars daily. With such incidents becoming more prevalent, SWIFT has cautioned its users about the potential threat posed by cyber attacks.

In addition to discussing cyber security, the G7 finance leaders will also consider the need for enhanced cooperation in addressing other global economic challenges during their meeting on Saturday.

text:
In the year 1764, the people of Milan bore witness to a gruesome spectacle – the execution of Bartolomeo Luisetti. Accused of sodomy, he was subjected to a barbaric punishment: asphyxiation followed by burning at the stake, all in front of a horrified crowd. Across Europe, the ruling classes believed that justice must not only be done, but also seen to be done, and that punishment must be harsh enough to instill fear in the hearts of the masses. One man who witnessed this scene with disgust was Cesare Beccaria, a young intellectual in his mid-twenties. It was difficult to comprehend how such brutality could be considered just.

Born in 1738, the firstborn son of a prominent Milanese aristocrat, Beccaria's education was stifling, having been schooled by the Jesuits in Parma. However, upon studying law in Pavia and reading works such as Montesquieu’s Lettres persanes (1721), his perspective on the world began to widen. He found himself drawn to the ideas of French and British intellectuals like Claude Adrien Helvétius, Denis Diderot, David Hume, and John Locke.

In 1759, Beccaria joined a group of young aristocrats in Milan who rebelled against the oppressive local elite, including their own families. This conflict was not only social but also intergenerational. To them, the interests of the few always prevailed over those of the many, and the laws were designed to maintain the power of the privileged class. Together with this group, they published a reformist magazine called Il Caffé. In 1762, Rousseau published The Social Contract, which provided Beccaria with an ideological framework. His treatise On Crimes and Punishments (1764) was published two years later, an astonishing 25 years before the French Revolution.

Beccaria's manifesto against cruel punishment quickly spread throughout Europe, leading to sweeping reforms of repressive institutions across the continent. At that time, Europe was a profoundly hierarchical society, where a select few ruled over the masses with an arbitrary and unaccountable authority. Enlightenment philosophers like Beccaria saw inequality as the root cause of social injustice. He aimed to radically transform his society, its institutions, and its laws – an ambition shared by other Enlightenment thinkers throughout Europe, though the means to achieve it varied. While France took a revolutionary path, Milan pursued gradual reform from within; Beccaria and the other rebels were to play important roles in the administration of the city-state.

European radical thinkers agreed that the Church was one of the strongest enforcers of inequality and subjugation. Indeed, Italian political minds continued to be inspired by Niccolò Machiavelli's central idea that Christianity often served as a tool for the oppressors to maintain their power over the people.

text:
Let's discuss a more equitable approach in the criminal justice system, rather than trying to "level down" Black people by harshly punishing them. The choice of direction may hinge on one's stance towards life sentences - whether they can ever be fair and justified. Proponents of "uplifting" can't presume that they are, since this is the very question at hand.

One argument for "leveling down" stems from the observation that the US justice system unfairly treats Black and brown individuals more harshly than whites. It seems reasonable to assume that the treatment of whites aligns more with our society's concept of justice, as whites are often seen as the norm or default. People of color, on the other hand, appear to bear an extra penalty rather than whites being leniently treated. To bring us closer to fairness, it might make sense to reduce penalties for Black people instead of increasing them for whites.

It's hard to justify keeping someone in prison for their entire life unless you believe they will always pose a significant threat to public safety or truly deserve such punishment. I've already argued that the majority of the over 200,000 people currently serving life sentences in the US do not present an ongoing threat to the public. Whether they should spend their whole lives behind bars comes down solely to whether they deserve it. Desert can only be established if they are fully responsible for their criminal actions.

Responsibility is best understood as what it excludes - a lack of knowledge, awareness that something is wrong, or inability to control one's actions due to an irresistible impulse. Using these definitions, most people serving life sentences likely bear responsibility for their acts. However, a broader understanding of responsibility is crucial. For instance, the insanity defense can absolve someone legally, although they might still be institutionalized in a psychiatric facility. But there are many more whose responsibility is reduced, even if not entirely absent.

The Death Penalty Information Center's 2021 report revealed that all but one of the 11 people executed last year had significant impairments, such as mental illness, brain injuries or damage, low IQs indicative of intellectual disabilities, or a history of chronic trauma, neglect, and abuse. It's highly likely that those sentenced to life also have high rates of such impairments.

In essence, we must reconsider our approach to justice to ensure fairness for all individuals, especially those who have faced adversity and may have their responsibilities diminished due to factors beyond their control.

text:
"In an interview with The Guardian back in 2002, philosopher Bernard Williams mused on the recurring theme of authenticity and self-expression that permeated his work. Authenticity was a cultural ideal during Williams' time (born in 1929, passed away in 2003), but it has only grown more influential since then. Who among us can resist the call to be true to oneself?

Williams delved into the power and allure of this idea, and his work continues to help us understand it today. Yet, as he was well aware, authenticity comes with its own risks. In his essay 'Moral Luck' (1976), Williams examines Paul Gauguin's decision to leave Paris for Tahiti in pursuit of greatness as a painter. This move, on the surface, seemed incredibly selfish, as Gauguin abandoned his wife and children without a second thought.

While many might see Gauguin's actions as morally unjustifiable, Williams suggests that his eventual success as an artist could be seen as a form of moral luck – a justification that may not resonate with everyone but could make sense to Gauguin himself and possibly others. 'I was right,' Gauguin might have said to himself, 'I knew I had it in me.'

Williams paints Gauguin as a complex character torn between family obligations and artistic ambition. However, it's important to note that Williams' portrayal of Gauguin is not necessarily historically accurate. Instead, Gauguin serves as a useful thought experiment to explore the interplay between authenticity, achievement, and luck in justification.

It's also worth mentioning that Williams doesn't delve into the harm caused by Gauguin or the exploitation of his Polynesian subjects featured prominently in his most famous paintings. This is a topic I will address further on. For now, let's focus on the familiar tale of self-justification in pursuit of art, often told through male perspectives – both in real life and fiction."

text:
Understanding why we take preventative measures now leads us to consider if octopuses have a concept of time. The evidence suggests they do, but how might this temporal understanding be structured in an octopus? Given our surroundings shape human metaphors for time, it's intriguing to ponder what time metaphors an octopus might use. With equal proficiency interacting with objects behind or in front of its body, past events could be just as manipulable as future ones – notions like 'let's put the past behind us' would be meaningless for an octopus. Furthermore, its ability to change visual perspective by swimming upward means all events in time – past, present, and future – could be surveyed from a single vantage point, allowing it to identify patterns over extended periods.

Adopting octopus-inspired metaphors for time might profoundly alter our relationship with the past. It would help us see how our past is causally linked to the present, potentially shifting our focus away from an obsession with future progress. This new perspective could enable us to excavate historical ideas and use them as sources of innovation, transforming fields like medicine and politics. Additionally, it might broaden our understanding of what constitutes a meaningful event, all thanks to time metaphors that are free from constraints.

One way to explore this is by examining the narratives we construct about our lives. According to narrative psychologists, a coherent sense of self relies on a unified life story that connects experiences in time. Traumatic events can disrupt this narrative, leading to conditions like dissociative identity disorder where a person adopts an entirely different personality following an experience too difficult to integrate. Treatment for dissociative identity disorder involves moving flexibly through the past and future – much like an octopus navigates space – to first rebuild the individual's disrupted sense of self, and then shift focus toward a desired future self.

Similar concepts have been applied at a societal level following periods of mass trauma, such as war. For instance, after World War II, Carl Jung argued that the German people needed to confront their collective guilt to move forward towards a more liberal future. Today, writers like Ta-Nehisi Coates expose historic injustices against African Americans, suggesting that acknowledging and repairing past wrongs are essential for addressing racial inequality now and moving forward.

In the Aymara language, spoken in Peru, tomorrow is called 'the day at my back.' Embracing a more octopus-like approach to time might help us confront our past, learn from it, and navigate our future with greater understanding and resilience.

text:
In a god, or at least that's what I pondered. I wasn't exactly convinced about the gods of Mount Olympus, but I believed that some form of divine presence was crucial for maintaining order in the ideal society I envisioned. Plato himself suggested that non-believers should be imprisoned, subjected to extreme thought control, served only by slaves, and buried anonymously upon death. I can't help but think that such draconian measures, as we saw in the 20th century with regimes like Nazi Germany and Soviet Russia, have shown us all too clearly the dangers of enforcing uniform beliefs.

It's not clear whether a universal belief in God or a lack thereof is inherently good for society, regardless of whether such a deity truly exists or not. The social sciences aren't particularly helpful in this regard. There are surveys, many sponsored by religious organizations, suggesting that religion plays a role in fostering morality and maintaining social order. For instance, one study by the Barna Group found that religious individuals are less likely to gossip than atheists. On a more serious note, in the United States, people from states with higher levels of religiosity tend to give more to charitable organizations, though often these donations go towards their own churches. Conversely, many European countries have lower levels of religiosity but greater state-supported social networks and thus higher levels of health and longevity. In other words, a state that endorses and implements programs like Obamacare might have higher wellbeing than a state that does not, even though it may be more religious and charitable. A non-believer might be just as morally good as a believer, but choose to contribute through taxes rather than voluntary donations.

One intriguing finding, based on internet downloads, is that porn consumption in the U.S. is highest in Utah, a state with a strong Mormon presence. Regardless of the side effects, it's unclear whether we'd be better off if everyone were made to believe in God or if they were made not to believe in Him. The answer isn't straightforward, and we have to allow people the freedom to make their own choices, even if it causes tension.

This brings me back to Dawkins and his provocative claim that teaching religion is a form of child abuse. While I don't entirely agree with him, I do think he raises an important point. If you've carefully weighed the evidence and come to a belief in God, then, morally speaking, you should hold onto that belief. But weighing the evidence requires taking science and other empirical pursuits more seriously than many religious believers are willing to do. In my view, it's your obligation to pass on your beliefs to your children, provided they've been purified through critical analysis. However, if someone holds religious beliefs that I consider unfounded, based on evidence that should be universally accepted, then I have a problem with it.

text:
For years, the Kindle design team aimed to create a device embodying the Platonic ideal of universal reading containers. However, my recent experience has led me to question this pursuit. If an overflowing pile of unread books on the bedside table symbolizes good intentions left unfulfilled, then a list of digital books on a Kindle represents fleeting intentions never realized. But something unexpected transpired over the past two years: I lost faith in the convenience of e-books.

I came to this realization only a few months ago when auditing my library – both physical and digital. While physical books, particularly works of literary fiction, continue to draw me in like a magnet, my interest in digital books has waned. Whether it's exploring bookstores in New York or receiving thoughtful gifts from friends, I find myself acquiring more physical books than ever before. The allure of the curated front tables at McNally Jackson or Three Lives and Company is too strong to resist.

The irony is that I've never consumed more digital content than I do today. From news articles to blog posts, I spend countless hours reading on my smartphone. Short to mid-length content seems tailor-made for the size, resolution, and usage scenarios of mobile devices, with many online publications offering beautifully designed typography and layouts that adapt seamlessly across different screens. Plus, phones make it effortless to share articles with others. The affinity between our smartphones and short-to-mid-length content is one reason why venture capitalists have invested hundreds of millions of dollars into digital publishers like Vox, Vice, and Buzzfeed.

So what accounts for my unconscious shift back to print? A book, once purchased by a reader, follows a familiar routine: it's read, underlined, dog-eared, scuffed, and – most importantly – reread. Reading a book just once leaves you with a superficial understanding; revisiting it over time forges a deeper connection. The bond between a reader and a book is not measured in minutes or hours but in months and years.

As a consumer of digital books, I am content, but as a reader, I feel let down. All the convenience aspects of the Kindle experience – an extensive catalog, immediate distribution, reasonable prices (albeit slightly less so now with updated contracts) – are well-executed. It's easy to forget that Amazon doesn't just frustrate publishers; they also power a significant chunk of the internet, providing hosting and servers for many leading companies worldwide. This business alone contributes nearly $2 billion to their bottom line, making it no surprise that they have built a seamless, efficient system.

However, the emotional aspect of reading is absent in the digital realm. The tactile experience of turning pages, feeling the weight of a book in my hands, and seeing the physical progression of my reading journey just can't be replicated on a screen – no matter how well designed it might be. As much as I appreciate the convenience offered by e-books, nothing compares to the connection I feel with a real, tangible book. And for now, that's where my heart lies.

text: Cameras have become a common tool in our hands, and with that, portraiture has become more accessible - and, dare I say, smile-centric. Since the turn of the 21st century, iPhone photography and social media platforms have affirmed that the preferred way to express ourselves individually and socially is through smiling. These technologies have also helped break down barriers with global cultures that once reflected Western practices less frequently. One out of every five of the over half a billion tweets sent daily, it's been calculated, contains an emoji. It's the universal language of the globalized mass culture of the digital age. The most commonly used of the thousands of available emojis is the 'smile with tears of joy,' an updated version of the classic smiley face. However, in 2019, the steady march of the modern (Western) smile took a serious blow with the emergence of COVID-19. Suddenly, that expression was hidden behind a surgical mask. While some might have noticed that a genuine and sincere smile causes a detectable crinkling of muscles around the eyes, not everyone has that level of perception. And who only smiles sincerely anyway? The emoji took the hit. Though the 'smile with tears of joy' maintained its global popularity, another emoji surged dramatically: the masked face. The popularity of the masked-face emoji became so intense that when, in November 2020, Apple released its annual updates to the range, it was wise to tweak the mask emoji, adding color to the cheeks and a meaningful crinkling around the eyes to give the illusion of smiling under the mask. It seems like the smile is fighting back. And indeed, it looks unlikely to lose its cultural significance and global appeal any time soon. With a receding pandemic status worldwide, there's plenty to smile about.

text:
The leadership of Jawaharlal Nehru demonstrated a dedication to some Pan-Asian ideals that resonated strongly at the Bandung Conference of 1955. This gathering of 29 Asian and African nations, representing more than half the world's population, was the final significant manifestation of Asian unity, eventually overshadowed by Cold War tensions and nation-building projects. Pan-Islamism has experienced a series of ebbs and flows over the past century. From Turkey and Egypt to Indonesia and Algeria, the concept of Muslim intellect and global Muslim solidarity served as a powerful tool for 20th-century nationalist leaders and movements. By the mid-1960s, most of the world's Muslims had shaken off the yoke of European colonial rule. The Turkish Parliament had abolished the Ottoman caliphate back in 1924, and by the 1950s, its influence had waned significantly. Yet, nearly half a century into the 21st century, Pan-Africanism and Pan-Asianism seem to have faded away, while Pan-Islamism and the concept of Muslim world unity persist. Why is this so?

The explanation lies in the latter stages of the Cold War. In the 1980s, a new form of Muslim internationalism emerged as part of the rise of political Islam. This wasn't a clash between the primordial civilizational traditions of Islam and the West, or a reassertion of authentic religious values. It wasn't even a continuation of early 20th-century Pan-Islamism; rather, it was a new creation born out of the Cold War. A Saudi-US alliance began promoting the idea of Muslim solidarity in the 1970s as an alternative to the secular Pan-Arabism of Egyptian president Gamal Abdel Nasser, whose country aligned with the Soviet Union. Any visions of an 'Islamic' utopia would have faltered if not for the failures of many post-colonial nation-states and the subsequent disillusionment of many Muslims.

The idea that Pan-Islamism embodies authentic, ancient, repressed Muslim political values in revolt against global Westernization and secularization was initially a paranoid concern of Western colonial officers, but it has more recently been espoused by Islamists. Western analysts and journalists have erred in accepting Islamist claims about Islam's inherent political values at face value. The kind of Islamism associated with Egypt's Muslim Brotherhood or Ruhollah Khomeini's Iran did not exist before the 1970s. Neither the Indian Muslims meeting Wilson, nor the late Ottoman-era caliphs, sought to impose Sharia in their societies. On the contrary, the first Pan-Islamist generation was highly modernistic: they advocated for women's liberation, racial equality, and cosmopolitanism. Indian Muslims, for example, were proud that the Ottoman caliph had Greek and Armenian ministers and ambassadors. They also wanted to see the British Crown appointing Hindu and Muslim ministers and high-level officials in their governments. None would have desired or anticipated the division of Turks and Greeks in Ottoman lands, Arabs and Jews in Palestine, or Muslims and Hindus in India. Only the most basic form of early 20th-century Pan-Islamism remains today.
