text:
Doubting Thomas has been unfairly characterized as a symbol of weak-willed faithlessness. Instead, his steadfast loyalty to Jesus, despite initial disbelief in stories of His resurrection, should be commended. True skepticism lies not in blind disbelief, but in the balanced suspension of judgment until there is enough evidence to support a belief.

The early Church's insistence on a legalistic, faith-based version of Christianity set the stage for the Enlightenment and modern science to challenge the Church's claims. The Church required weekly professions of faith, which inadvertently provided ammunition for skeptics who demanded evidence to support their beliefs.

In contrast to Thomas, contemporary skepticism is more about going along with the status quo out of a sense of obligation, rather than expecting it to deliver on its promises. We all have our own versions of Thomas' stories – the translucent wraith seen at a stately home or the disembodied footsteps heard upstairs when everyone is asleep. These tales are no more believable than a long-range weather forecast, yet they occasionally crossed the line between highly unlikely and just barely credible. However, as the culture of spectacle has become more prevalent, it seems that the laws of physics have no greater claim to truth than poorly produced YouTube videos or other sensational claims.

The distinction between the visible and invisible, material and spiritual, phenomenal and noumenal has blurred over time. They are now mutually permeable, with each affecting the other in ways we can't fully understand or explain. We find ourselves peering into the heart of things, trying to make sense of the mysteries that surround us.

text:
In my classroom, I learned to suppress my physical self. However, it was outside the academy where I truly found myself - through the realm of fanfiction. I stumbled upon this fascinating world in 2001, at the tender age of 16, a time when I felt lost and misunderstood, often mistaken for a boy in women's restrooms. Desperate to connect with others, I joined various web mailing listservs and found solace in the stories these writers shared - one such story was about a character from The Lord of the Rings that left me spellbound. It was unlike anything I had ever read before. For years, I had dreamt of stepping into books and embarking on adventures with my favorite characters. Now, I was penning down these dreams and sharing them with the world. Strangers would leave heartwarming comments on each chapter, validating my newfound ability to express myself creatively.

Later, I came across a term for this kind of story: 'Mary Sue'. The name originated from a short satire called 'A Trekkie's Tale', written by mathematician Paula Smith in 1973 and published in a Star Trek fanzine. The satire was a critique of the influx of fanfiction stories she deemed poor, featuring all-American teenagers who, like my own early stories, would strut into the world of the Starship: Enterprise and capture Captain Kirk's heart. As I delved deeper into fanfiction, I discovered a treasure trove of beautifully written pieces that offered insights into characters I held dear.

As I devoured Harry Potter fanfiction during my high school and university years, I found myself simultaneously studying classical and medieval literature. I learned Latin and read works by Virgil, Cicero, and St Augustine in their original language. I wrote analytical essays that were as cold and sober as the academic world demanded, but when night fell, I would lose myself in fanfiction, my secret indulgence.

Eventually, I moved to Canada for graduate school. By then, I knew that fantasizing about meeting Geoffrey Chaucer or imagining Queen Eleanor of Aquitaine's encounters with Sir Lancelot was not the way serious readers behaved. But I grappled with cognitive dissonance, as it became increasingly clear that fanfiction had honed my reading skills.

Fanfiction enhances the richness of its source texts for its dedicated readers. It deepens allusions and unearths hidden undercurrents, delves into characterization and subtle nuances of plot, and spends time exploring these elements in detail. After immersing myself in fanfiction, I return to my beloved texts with a fresh perspective - often a more discerning one. For instance, reading hundreds of stories that reimagined the relationship between Remus Lupin and Sirius Black in Harry Potter made me realize that their bond might have been the reason for Sirius's family's estrangement from him.

text:
Penises come in all shapes and sizes, from spiky to bone-like, sometimes even two in the same animal. This diverse array of genitalia has left researchers puzzled for years - specifically, they've been wondering how many times the penis has evolved in amniotes (a group that includes mammals, reptiles, and birds).

One thing we know for sure is that the development of a penis is an ongoing process. For instance, only 3% of bird species possess penises, with ducks being one of the most notable examples due to their corkscrew-like, ballistically everted versions. The rest of the bird population relies on a structure that doesn't serve much purpose or lacks a penis entirely.

In vertebrates without a penis, the transfer of gametes (reproductive cells) happens internally through a structure called the cloaca - Latin for 'sewer'. This all-purpose organ passes fluid, including semen, out of the body. Animals can engage in what's known as the cloacal kiss, where the two 'sewers' are brought together to facilitate the transfer of semen. Among reptiles, this behavior is relatively rare; only one species - the tuatara, a reptile that traces its lineage back to the dinosaur era - regularly practices it.

The absence of a penis in the tuatara, which sits at the base of the amniote family tree, suggests that a later lineage evolved this appendage. However, given the variety of penises across amniote species, one question that has lingered is: how many times did the penis evolve anew?

Answers have been as varied as the penises themselves, but rarely does 'once' come up. However, recent discoveries involving tuatara embryos have provided a clearer answer. In the early 20th century, a zoologist named Arthur Dendy, working in New Zealand, sent some eggs from local tuatara nests to a US museum. These specimens were processed and then lost for decades. But a lucky recovery of these samples many years later revealed something remarkable: the embryos were at the right stage of development to determine whether these animals initiate a starter penis that eventually disappears, much like human embryos begin developing a tail that later vanishes.

After researchers digitally reconstructed the tuatara samples, they could see that the embryonic region where penises start to develop showed signs of penis growth in the tuatara embryo. This was significant because living tuatara can live for up to a century, and those born around the time these eggs were collected demonstrated that the starter penis never fully developed.

With this evidence from the tuatara, researchers now have proof that a penis - whether it survives the embryonic stage or not - was present at the base of the amniote family tree. In other words, the program for making a penis evolved only once in our lineage. The same timing and starting materials used to build penises are found in tuataras as in other amniotes.

It's an exciting time to witness the mesmerizing displays of these enchanting creatures. What used to require costly and sophisticated equipment is now accessible with devices as simple as a GoPro or a smartphone. Inspired by this, I embarked on a cross-country journey with my lab to capture the synchronous flashes of fireflies. The goal was to forge a stronger bond between the mathematical theories I've grown up with and the behavior of these insects in the wild.

Our journeys took us deep into national forests every spring, setting up camp beneath canopies illuminated by these luminous orbs that, as poet Richard Newman puts it, 'outshine the stars'. Female fireflies wait a full two seconds before responding with their own half-second flash if they're interested. Although animal communication is often associated with ecologists and zoologists, physicists and mathematicians can offer valuable insights into the energetic cost of communicating, the information transmitted, and the recipient's ability to detect the sender's signal.

For instance, anyone who's dabbled in online dating knows the struggle of deciding how much effort to put into a profile, how many messages to send, or which emojis to use. It turns out that these concerns about cost, signal-to-noise ratio, and compressibility are fundamental criteria for almost any communication system. The firefly system, with its traceable mode of communication and the ability to track signals from all individuals in the swarm, offers unique advantages for studying 'mate-search communication'.

Fireflies' captivating light displays are actually courtship rituals. Males fly overhead, advertising their suitability to grounded females through a species-specific on/off light pattern. A good example is Photinus carolinus, a synchronous firefly found in the southeastern United States. Every 15 seconds or so, a male flies about a meter off the ground and emits several quick flashes, one second apart. If a female is interested, she remains close to the ground in low vegetation. Two seconds pass before she responds with her own half-second flash at the third second. The act of flashing is voluntary, triggered by the release of the neurotransmitter octopamine which initiates a chemical reaction in the insect's light-producing organ.

The prevailing theory suggests that fireflies control their flashing by regulating the oxygen supply to their light-producing organs, although this is still under debate. What's clear, however, is that unlike a traditional light bulb, a firefly produces 'cold light', with minimal energy lost as heat. This is crucial for the firefly's survival, as overheating could prove fatal to these delicate creatures. The female fireflies then use their flashes to signal their interest or disinterest.

In this essay, I'm going to discuss a three-step approach to instigating political change through what Deleuze and Guattari called 'schizoanalysis.'

First, we should identify and focus on the desires that run counter to capitalism. Rather than being constrained by its rules, we should take these desires to their logical extremes so they can break free from capitalist shackles. The final step is to unite these diverse desires in a movement I'll call a 'molecular revolution.' This term might sound abstract, but Deleuze and Guattari argued that it's impossible to predict what such a revolution would look like in advance. Instead of forcing change according to a predetermined plan, they suggested an experimental approach to politics.

Two examples they frequently cited were the theatre of cruelty by the French dramatist Antonin Artaud and the drug-fueled explorations of American writer William S Burroughs. They believed that those oppressed by patriarchy (women), racism (people of color), and heteronormativity (what we now call the LGBT community) were all suffering from the same machinery of despotic and imperial capitalism. To bring about change, they argued for a broader alliance, including all marginalized groups under one umbrella.

The traditional Marxist view that the working classes are the driving force of change was rejected by Deleuze and Guattari. They suggested that through a process of 'becoming-woman' and 'becoming-minoritarian,' we can finally overcome the specter of individuality based on the Western white male subject. Their book, Anti-Oedipus, stirred up quite a controversy. Lacan was infuriated and prohibited any discussion of it in his seminars. Many leftist political activists, while sympathetic to their aims, criticized them for being reckless and unconventional. Despite (or perhaps because of) these criticisms, the book became a bestseller and attracted a two-page review in Le Monde.

Not only was the content groundbreaking, but so were their writing style and collaboration methods. Deleuze and Guattari's books are like a chorus of voices, not easily attributable to either author. In A Thousand Plateaus, they wrote, 'Since each of us was several, there was already quite a crowd.' This sentiment was later echoed by Guattari: 'We are very different... I’m more attracted to adventurous things; let's call it a conceptual commando who likes to visit foreign lands. Gilles, on the other hand, is a philosophical heavyweight, he has a whole bibliographic administration.' They wrote together like two streams merging into one. By working in this way, they aimed to combat the stagnation and death that individuality can bring, both practically and theoretically. A Thousand Plateaus contains 15 chapters that cover a wide range of topics, from geology to linguistics.

In summary, Deleuze and Guattari proposed a unique approach to political change through schizoanalysis. They encouraged us to focus on desires that run counter to capitalism, take them to their logical extremes, and unite diverse groups in a 'molecular revolution.' Their methods defied conventional writing styles and collaboration methods, making their work as revolutionary as their ideas.

The violence perpetrated by the LRA in the context of so-called love marriages is indeed disturbing to our contemporary notions of love and marriage. However, it doesn't mean that their relationship was inherently violent or meaningless, nor was she a passive victim of a sexual crime. Labeling their relationship as a 'crime against humanity' oversimplifies the situation and ignores their unique experiences, simply because it challenged the relatively recent and not universally accepted idea that marriages should be between two consenting individuals who are already in love.

Forced marriages were just one of the many ways in which the LRA violated the principles of human community membership, according to some Acholi civilians. These civilians questioned the humanity of the LRA because they associated them with the 'bush' (lum), a space considered cruel and inhospitable for human life by ordinary people. While people might venture into the lum for firewood or hunting, they didn't view it as an ideal living environment. The LRA was seen as dangerous wild animals - lions and elephants - wreaking havoc and violence upon civilians. However, the rebels experienced the lum differently; to them, it represented life, purity, and development. Their base camps in the bush were not unlike their civilian counterparts' homes in terms of daily activities such as cooking, bathing, etc. They found solace, affection, and training in the bush, even considering it sacred at times.

Rebels also built holy spaces (yards) where they derived supernatural powers through spiritual practices to heal and perform miracles. Compared to Acholi social life, which they saw as rife with drunkenness, adultery, and laziness, the lum was a space of discipline and hard work. They abstained from alcohol in the bush, enforced strict rules against adultery, and worked tirelessly, traveling long distances through dense vegetation.

The LRA practiced what they called 'gorilla warfare', which differed significantly from traditional guerrilla tactics. While many civilians claimed that the rebels had lost their humanity and become mere animals, this was not entirely accurate. They had transformed themselves into something more - cunning, fierce, and respected gorillas. Identifying as animals wasn't a fall from grace but an elevation to a higher status.

Many rebels eventually returned to civilian life, either through capture or desertion. Civilians and rehabilitation organizations who processed returning rebels were wary of them, showing a mix of fear and pity. The returning rebels underwent close scrutiny as a result.

Once more, the seeds of unease were sown by 20th-century technology, this time it was a videotape harboring an unseen terror. Echoes of this can be traced back to the telephone in earlier times. Ringu is a prime example of J-horror - a genre that thrives on fearsome, vengeful spirits drawing heavily from Japan's kabuki tradition. However, are the Japanese yūrei (ghosts) now destined to disappear completely into the abyss of consumerism, irony, and eventual disregard? If so, then maybe Yanagita was right to have his concerns about the people of Tokyo and Osaka at the dawn of the 20th century. Despite Japan's impressive technological advancements and adoption of global ideologies, there was a risk of losing sight of the most profound question that these spirits could provoke: not 'Are they real?', but rather 'How real are we?'

This question lingers at the summit of Osorezan, but it dissipates as you descend, surrounded by paved roads, streetlights, and Japan's omnipresent 24-hour convenience stores. A world constructed by human hands, on a human scale, with human purposes in mind reinforces our illusion of mastery - strengthening the 'me' that Yanagita feared could dominate us. It's no wonder remote mountaintops are so often associated with revelations and purity - everyday life rarely exposes its artificial nature, its 'virtual' essence.

Yet, perhaps Yanagita underestimated our hunger for a balance between mastery and mystery. We seem to crave both. J-horror thrives on the curiosity, doubts, and fears about the world that reside within us - about the darker aspects of human existence, about the pain we inflict on each other, creating grudges that can never truly be left behind. Even about a hidden world more fundamental than the one we navigate daily, where there might be more shadow than light.

In Japan, some speculate whether the traumas of 2011 may have triggered a subtle cultural shift - a recalibration of sorts. This change may not become apparent until looking back retrospectively. They observe a broader context in which people are seeking a more harmonious coexistence between human control and spiritual mystery, a balance that perhaps we've lost sight of amidst our relentless pursuit of progress.

In a small fishing village, a female North Atlantic right whale lies on her side, her body floating limply in the icy water. Her calls echo through the stillness, a desperate plea for help that brings in a flurry of curious males. One after another, they arrive, their powerful tails slicing through the waves as they attempt to reach her. It's mating season, but something doesn't seem right. The whales are behaving oddly, and this has left scientists scratching their heads.

"There's no other animal on Earth where we know so little about its basic reproduction," says marine biologist Dr. John Hunt, his voice tinged with frustration. "We can't even figure out why these whales are mating at the wrong times of year."

The question that looms large is whether these whales might be storing sperm for later use or if they mate just for fun. Dr. Hunt and his team have been trying to answer this question through the study of a unique tissue found in whales: baleen.

In 2014, Dr. Raphaela Stimmelmayr, a biologist and wildlife veterinarian from Barrow, Alaska, suggested applying the keratin approach to baleen. "It suddenly got really exciting when we realized we might be able to get a pregnancy signature out of the baleen," said Dr. Hunt.

The team began their research with bowhead whales, whose subsistence hunts are the only sustainable and reliable source of baleen. The slow-growing baleen plates of these whales, which capture 25 years of growth, proved to be an excellent starting point for their study.

By analyzing the tissues for stress hormones like cortisol and reproductive hormones like progesterone, they were able to distinguish between males and females, and pregnant and non-pregnant females. The team then used existing estimates of baleen growth rates as a chronometer for their samples.

As they looked at older baleen from mature females, they found changes in progesterone that neatly matched the estimated gestation length of bowhead whales. It seemed that the baleen was documenting past pregnancies.

To cross-check their technique, the team needed baleen samples from whales with known pregnancy histories. That's where Stumpy and another North Atlantic right whale nicknamed Staccato came in. Both whales had a nearly 30-year-long file of regular sightings, including two recent pregnancies. And fortunately, their carcasses happened to be found and dissected by scientists who had the foresight to collect plates of their baleen for future study.

"They're really valuable samples," said Dr. Hunt. "With these, we can finally start to unlock some of the secrets of these mysterious creatures."

Title: Embracing Diversity for Revolution: Lessons from Deleuze and Guattari's Schizoanalysis

In pursuit of political transformation, Deleuze and Guattari proposed a three-step practice called schizoanalysis. This process begins by identifying desires that deviate from the capitalist norm, then pushing these to their extremes to free them from capitalistic constraints, and finally, uniting these diverse desires to ignite a 'molecular revolution.' But what does this term really mean? For Deleuze and Guattari, it's impossible to predict the exact form such a revolution would take. Instead, they advocated for a politics of experimentation, encouraging exploration of alternative desire organizations through unconventional means, such as Antonin Artaud's 'theatre of cruelty' or William S Burroughs' drug-induced adventures.

Deleuze and Guattari viewed a wide range of individuals as potential participants in this endeavor. They rejected the traditional Marxist notion that the working class alone holds the key to change. Instead, they aimed to unite all marginalized groups—those oppressed by patriarchy (women), racism (people of color), and heteronormativity (now known as the LGBT community)—under a shared umbrella. They argued that these 'minoritarians' were all victims of the same machinery: despotic, imperial capitalism. Only by joining forces could they bring about an anti-capitalist revolution.

The philosophical image of the individual is rooted in the seemingly autonomous figure of the white male subject. To eliminate this specter, Deleuze and Guattari suggested a process of 'becoming-woman' and 'becoming-minoritarian.' This provocative work, Anti-Oedipus, stirred controversy. Lacan was incensed, banning any discussion of the text in his seminars. Although many on the political Left sympathized with Deleuze and Guattari's goals, they criticized their audacity and unorthodoxy. Despite—or perhaps because of—these criticisms, Anti-Oedipus became an instant bestseller, garnering a two-page review in Le Monde.

Their writing style and collaboration were as revolutionary as their ideas. Deleuze and Guattari's books don't speak with a single voice; instead, they resonate with multiple voices that defy easy categorization into either author. 'Since each of us was several,' they said in A Thousand Plateaus (1980), the sequel to Anti-Oedipus, 'there was already quite a crowd.' This collective spirit was later echoed by Guattari: 'We are very different... I'm more attracted to adventurous things; let's call it a conceptual commando who likes to visit foreign lands. Gilles, on the other hand, is a philosophical heavyweight, he has a whole bibliographic administration.'

Their collaborative approach aimed to combat the stifling individuality that can lead to sterility and death. A Thousand Plateaus spans 15 chapters on a wide range of topics, from geology to linguistics. Their aim was not just theoretical but practical: to spark a revolution by uniting diverse voices in pursuit of a common goal—to free ourselves from the shackles of capitalism and create a world where desire thrives.

text:
The fluidity of relationships between various aspects of life has always been intriguing, especially for those who yearn to transcend the divide between imagination and reality. This desire can be traced back to André Breton's lecture, given several years before he visited Saint-Alban. The artists Breton referred to were indeed the surrealists, including his friend Max Ernst. However, I believe that description could just as well apply to Forestier, whose work showcased a unique blend of freedom and unity that Breton seemed to appreciate.

I suspect that Breton also saw in him a hint of a broader mission, one that he himself championed through the surrealist movement: a rebellion against an absurd order based on inequality, striving for the complete emancipation of humanity. While the concentration of distressed and disabled individuals in secure institutions may no longer be as popular an issue as it once was, the reality that fosters apathy towards their plight remains very much alive.

This reality can be observed in a media landscape that provides a platform for comments like, "Two-thirds of COVID-19 fatalities would have probably died within a year anyway." It becomes evident when acts of violence against disabled people are euphemistically called 'mercy killings.' And it surfaces when lawyers justify the death of an individual by claiming they died due to 'underlying health conditions.'

Many readers may find it unsettling to label this prevailing reality in Europe and the US as 'fascism.' I used to be one of them, preferring to view fascism as something vanquished 80 years ago in Europe, or at least dormant until the actions of a few fringe elements. Others may understand fascism as an enduring threat, but perceive it as a distinct political ideology, primarily advocated by white supremacists and ethno-nationalists who have gained prominence over the past decade.

However, the concept that fascism should be viewed as something more extensive and insidious – something that's not just out there but also present within our own societies – has a rich history. In her 1940 essay on Homer's Iliad, Simone Weil observed the casual disregard of the strong towards the weak and reflected on the use of force throughout human history: "Exercised to its extreme, it turns a man into an object in the most literal sense – it makes a corpse out of him while he is still alive."

In the aftermath of the war, Max Horkheimer and Theodor Adorno were clear that fascism had persisted in the combination of bureaucracy and capitalism that defined the post-war world, a system that "relieves its people of the burden of moral feelings" and is "deeply in accord with pure reason... [as it] treats human beings as things."

This essay attempts to shed light on these insidious aspects of our reality, not to label everything we dislike as 'fascism,' but to encourage a more thoughtful, empathetic, and critical approach towards the world around us.

SINGAPORE — Crude oil prices dipped on Thursday as concerns over a global economic slowdown, particularly in China, and record-high U.S. crude inventories at the Cushing delivery point weighed heavily on markets. Goldman Sachs forecasted that prices would remain low and volatile until the second half of the year.

International benchmark Brent crude futures LCOc1 were trading at $30.67 per barrel at 0601 GMT, a decrease of 17 cents. U.S. West Texas Intermediate (WTI) crude futures CLc1 stood at $27.01 per barrel, dropping by 44 cents and nearing the $26.19 a barrel intra-day high recorded just shy of 65 million barrels, according to data from the U.S. Energy Information Administration (EIA) released on Wednesday. The U.S.-based brokerage Phillip Futures concurred with this assessment.

The oil market is currently grappling with an oversupply of crude, combined with a slowing Chinese economy, which has led Goldman Sachs to predict that prices will remain low until the second half of the year. In a note to clients, the bank stated, "The risks of China growth concerns and oil price downside have materialized faster than we anticipated." They added, "We expect oil prices will continue to fluctuate between $20 per barrel (operational stress level) and $40 per barrel (financial stress level) with significant volatility and no discernible trend until 2H2016."

Chart analysts are predicting that crude prices could soon dip below $25 per barrel, as weakening technical indicators continue to put pressure on the market. Matthew Sferro, a technical analyst at New York's Informa Global Markets, stated that if WTI fell further, it would likely test the support level at $25.04 per barrel, established in April 2003.

Oil prices have fallen by almost 75 percent since mid-2014, when global production outpaced demand by approximately 2 million barrels per day. Trading activity in Asia was subdued due to China's New Year holiday, which lasts all week, and Japan's public holiday.

This optimistic outlook was further bolstered by a decline in U.S. crude inventories, according to sources familiar with the matter who spoke to Reuters.

While such reduction is largely within OPEC's control, the organization will need to decide on specific recommendations for implementing a supply cutback at their next meeting on Nov. 30."

The proposed OPEC plan aims to expedite the removal of a supply glut that has kept oil prices below half their mid-2014 levels. text:
Love marriages, as we understand them today, are sacred and founded on mutual consent and affection. However, the relationship between a child abducted by the Lord's Resistance Army (LRA) and their captor was not just something that clashes with modern sensibilities. It was complex, layered with power dynamics, and far from a simple narrative of victimhood or crime.

To label it as a 'crime against humanity' would be a quick judgment, neglecting the unique experiences of those involved. The LRA's actions were indeed brutal, but condemning their relationships as inhuman is a narrow perspective that fails to consider the nuances of their existence. For the Acholi people, the bush (lum) was not merely a wild space, but one associated with cruelty, madness, and chaos - a stark contrast to 'home' (gang).

To civilians, the LRA were like dangerous animals: lions and elephants that roamed the wilderness, causing havoc and violence. Yet, for the rebels, the bush was their home, a place of life, purity, and development. They lived and worked there, leading 'normal' lives, cooking, bathing, and engaging in everyday activities. The bush held significance beyond its physical aspects; it was a place of affectionate relationships, community, and valuable training.

Rebels even built sacred spaces within the bush, drawing power from holy spirits to heal and perform miracles. In contrast, they saw social life among the Acholi as rife with drinking, adultery, and laziness. In the bush, they abstained from alcohol, enforced strict rules against adultery, and worked hard. This lifestyle, they called 'gorilla warfare', a unique style of battle reminiscent of their primate comrades.

The LRA rebels were often seen as having lost their humanity, reduced to mere animals. But in truth, they had transcended it, becoming tricky, fierce, and respected gorillas. Identifying as animals was not a fall from grace, but an ascension to a higher status, embodying traits that humans often fear or admire in these creatures.

Many rebels eventually returned to civilian life, either through capture or desertion. The civilians and rehabilitation organizations that welcomed them were filled with fear and pity, subjecting them to close scrutiny. Yet, these returning rebels were not the monstrous beings they had once been portrayed as; they were complex individuals who had lived and survived in extraordinary circumstances.

Gabor Kiraly, at 40 years old, made history as the oldest player ever to feature in the European Championship, surpassing Lothar Matthaus of Germany in this record.

Austria's captain, Christian Fuchs, and winger Martin Harnik were the only two holdovers from their debut campaign eight years ago, starting for Marcel Koller. A strike from outside the area by David Alaba crashed against the post early on, with Austria threatening throughout. Alaba, operating in central midfield as usual for Austria, soon issued a warning to Hungary with a first-time shot, while Kiraly demonstrated his agility by saving a low strike from Zlatko Junuzovic.

Marko Arnautovic then sent a dangerous low ball across the face of goal as Austria continued their onslaught, but Harnik lost his footing as he tried to slide home at the far post. The best chance of the opening half fell to Hungary captain Balazs Dzsudzsak, who missed wide after being picked out by Laszlo Kleinheisler on the right.

Just beyond the hour, Kleinheisler slipped in Szalai, played onside by Fuchs, with the powerful striker poking the ball underneath Robert Almer for his first goal at any level since December 2014. Austria seemed to have equalized when Martin Hinteregger rifled in from the edge of the area, but referee Clement Turpin disallowed the goal due to a foul on Tamas Kadar, with Dragovic receiving a second booking and dismissal.

Stieber's late goal, three minutes from time, triggered delirious celebrations as he chipped Austria keeper Almer with a sublime finish, securing Hungary their first European Championship win since 1964. It was a stunning upset for the Hungarian team.

Struggling to make ends meet, I found myself sitting across from a friend at a café, nibbling on a sandwich that was more expensive than my weekly budget. The grill marks on the bread looked like leering teeth - a luxury I couldn't afford. As I handed over the remaining coins in my purse for the bill, I felt a pang of resentment. I reminded myself that he deserved his money, and I was choosing to live this lifestyle as a full-time writer. If only my erotica would sell! Only 10 copies had been sold so far, earning me a paltry £15.

I'm no stranger to the constant hum of uncertainty about the future. But lately, it has turned into an aggravating shrillness, warning me that I need to reevaluate my life soon. Money is a recurring theme in the romantic erotica I read for research. Many stories depict heroines who are financially desperate, using sex as a transaction - love in an age of capitalism.

In Georgia Le Carre’s The Highest Bidder (2019), a college student named Freya is so broke that she joins an exclusive London club to auction off her virginity. After signing a non-disclosure agreement, she struts out on stage naked while unseen male billionaires bid for her. Luckily, she's the subject of an intense bidding war, selling for £1 million. She ends up with a young man who resembles a 'Greek God,' whom she knew from her childhood. The rags-to-riches element gives these stories a Cinderella feel, but there are no fairy godmothers around, and magic is achieved through the heroines' degradation. It's almost as if their poverty makes it easier for us to empathize with why they would go through such humiliation, giving us permission to find pleasure in their trysts.

In Jade West’s Buy Me, Sir (2007), Melissa is 21 and struggling to raise her baby brother while working as a cleaner for a law firm. She's determined to seduce Alexander Henley, the criminal lawyer who owns it. She learns that he has a thing for Debbie Harry, so she dyes her hair blonde and creates a sexy video of herself masturbating for his 'dealer,' the man who solicits women for him. The romantic heroes in these books flaunt their wealth with yachts and helicopters.

It's an interesting reflection on how a decade of austerity has affected our perception of love and sex in society, especially when money is involved. These stories depict desperate heroines who use their bodies to escape poverty, often finding temporary relief through humiliating transactions or exploitative relationships. It's a stark reminder that capitalism can make us all feel like we're selling ourselves short at times.

text:
The Stoics, in their pursuit of a life free from pain, identified two crucial functions of practical reason. On one hand lies the indirect function, where careful planning and thoughtful decision-making help the Stoic navigate the least painful path through life. In contrast, there's the direct function, where instead of removing or avoiding life's obstacles, the Stoic learns to reframe these challenges in a way that alters their emotional impact. This is a change in perspective, while the other is a change in action.

What sets Stoicism apart in ethics, as Schopenhauer points out, lies in its proposed method of mental transformation. The Stoic first realizes that painful feelings of deprivation do not arise from lacking something but rather from the desire to have it and not having it. To escape these painful feelings completely, we must address our desires themselves. Moreover, the larger our aspirations and the higher our expectations for what we want to achieve, the more devastating the disappointment when we fall short. If we cannot help but yearn for some things, then we should at least ensure those desires remain reasonable and achievable. Schopenhauer warns us to be wary of expecting too much happiness in the future; such expectations are likely unrealistic. 'Every intense pleasure,' he says, 'is an illusion.'

So, the Stoic strives for ataraxia, a state of inner peace and tranquility amidst life's storms. Schopenhauer suggests that accepting the inevitability of suffering can help us achieve this state if we internalize it as truth. Pain and suffering hurt more when we view them as accidents or avoidable, but in reality, they are universal and unavoidable. If we can accept this, perhaps we will worry less about encountering suffering, or at least worry about it as we do other inevitable aspects of life, such as aging (for most of us) and death.

We should resist the belief that the world owes us happiness, for it sets us up for disappointment. If we believe we are entitled to happiness, we will inevitably feel resentment towards the obstacles that hinder our perceived right to it. Some people, Schopenhauer observes, externalize this resentment by blaming external circumstances or others for their misery.

text:
Athenian citizenship played a crucial role in understanding the trial of Socrates. His allegiance to his city significantly impacted the course of events, as it was deeply intertwined with the core values of Athenian political culture - the relationship between public discourse and civic duty.

In essence, this tale revolves around citizenship engagement, albeit in a context distinct from contemporary U.S. discussions centered on recognition. Maximus of Tyre, a late Roman-era writer, recounted that Socrates remained stoically silent amidst Meletus' accusations. However, dissidents who challenge the legitimacy of political structures occasionally exercise a principled refusal to engage in legal proceedings. Despite this, Socrates acknowledged the court's authority. He engaged actively in his defense, albeit unconventionally. Given that he had accepted the benefits bestowed by the laws, how could he now question their validity?

Socrates did not challenge the trial's legitimacy; instead, he fulfilled his civic responsibilities. He was accountable for actions perceived as detrimental to the community. Plato's portrayal of Socrates' defense speech underscores his efforts to exonerate himself and demonstrate that he had served the community rather than harmed it. In fact, Socrates presented himself as a civic benefactor deserving of recognition. His contributions to Athens' social fabric, in Plato's view, warranted privileges such as free meals at the state hearth.

Plato's dialogues offer insights into Socrates' commitment to his city and provide a vivid depiction of Athenian political culture. In the dialogue "Crito," an imaginary conversation takes place between Socrates and the personified Laws of Athens, who express their concerns about Socrates' intention to evade punishment by fleeing the prison. These laws symbolized not only the written rules of the Athenian state but also its societal norms.

The Laws questioned Socrates about his dissatisfaction with them and when he discovered any flaws. They reminded him that it was through their guidance on marriage that he entered the world, nurtured him as a child, and educated him. Socrates acknowledged these contributions, affirming his respect for the laws that shaped his existence.

In summary, Socrates' trial offers valuable lessons about citizenship, civic duty, and respect for the law. Despite facing adversity, he chose to engage within the system rather than reject it entirely. His courage and integrity serve as an enduring testament to the power of active citizenship in a democratic society.

In reading Boethius' Consolation, it becomes apparent that readers are keenly aware of a work's genre, and this knowledge shapes their expectations regarding how its author intends it to be understood. By crafting the Consolation in alternating prose and verse, Boethius signaled that his work belongs to the Menippean satire genre, which traditionally mocks figures of authority. As Joel Relihan demonstrated in Ancient Menippean Satire (1993), this genre often depicts the failures of those in power. Therefore, readers of the Consolation might expect that Philosophy's teachings would not be treated with complete seriousness.

Relihan goes on to develop a reading diametrically opposed to the straightforward interpretation in The Prisoner's Philosophy (2007). According to Relihan, Philosophy fails to provide consolation for the Prisoner, and this failure reveals Boethius' critique of human reasoning. The underlying message is that Christian faith alone offers the true consolation, which the imprisoned Boethius mistakenly sought from Philosophy.

This interpretation treats all the Consolation's philosophical discourse as mere rhetoric, created by Boethius the author solely to demonstrate its inadequacy and deliver the main message indirectly. However, it is difficult to accept this notion. Would Boethius, who dedicated his life to philosophy, really have treated arguments so callously? Moreover, why would he elaborate on an intricate argument at the end of the work about divine prescience and contingency, arguably his finest piece of reasoning, if his goal were merely to show the inadequacy of philosophy rather than its ability to console?

Although Relihan's reading raises crucial questions, it ultimately proves unconvincing. A more reasonable perspective is that Boethius intended for Philosophy's arguments to be taken seriously and made a genuine effort to articulate well-reasoned perspectives through her voice. At the same time, he wanted readers to recognize the limitations of such reasoning by creating tensions within the argumentative structure of the Consolation as a whole.

Three main areas of tension run throughout the Consolation: human happiness, providence and the suffering of the good, and human freedom. Over an extended period, Philosophy presents a nuanced view of human happiness that values the true goods of sufficiency, respect, power, enduring fame, and joy while acknowledging the genuine worth of some goods of fortune, such as loved ones.

However, starting from the middle of the work, Philosophy's initial, easier remedies give way to a more complex understanding of human happiness. This complexity lies in accepting that some goods of fortune have value but recognizing that seeking many of the things people typically pursue - riches, high office, kingdoms, public praise, and sensual pleasures - is a misguided attempt to obtain the true goods of happiness. The true goods, which cannot be taken away from someone in the Prisoner's position, are essential to happiness, but some goods of fortune, like loved ones, are also accepted as valuable.

Despite presenting a thoughtful and nuanced understanding of human happiness, Boethius still wanted readers to question the limitations of such reasoning by creating tensions within the argumentative structure of the Consolation.

Back around a decade ago, an intriguing moment unfolded during a dinner party at my home that significantly reshaped my intellectual journey in Classics. My good friend Richard Thomas, fresh from giving a talk at Baylor University, casually mentioned one of his students had found an 'Isaiah acrostic' hidden within Vergil's Georgics - a first-century BCE poem often perceived as merely about farming but delving into life and the universe. This offhand comment introduced me to two fascinating aspects of ancient Greek and Latin poetry that I hadn't fully considered before, despite decades spent immersed in Classics: acrostics and Judaism.

The tension between biblical and classical traditions has long been palpable. Tertullian famously asked, "What does Athens have to do with Jerusalem?" Similarly, St Jerome felt compelled to break ties with his beloved classical authors after a haunting dream where he was accused of being more Ciceronian than Christian. One reason Vergil holds such significance within the Western tradition is that his Fourth Eclogue - depicting the arrival of a divine boy heralding a new Golden Age - offered a soothing interpretation for many: it was viewed as a pagan prophecy of Christ's birth, thus enabling Christianity to absorb rather than dismiss the Classics. However, post-Enlightenment readers often rejected this Christian interpretation; they had no interest in viewing Athens through Jerusalem's lens.

Regardless of one's stance on the supernatural, there is ample evidence suggesting personal and intellectual exchanges between Jews and non-Jewish Greeks and Romans before and after Christ's birth. Jews made up roughly 10-20% of the Roman Empire's population, and they appear frequently in classical texts. The Septuagint - a Greek translation of the Hebrew scriptures created around the 3rd century BCE - was widely accessible to educated non-Jewish individuals throughout the Mediterranean world. Classics scholars and intellectual historians should devote more attention to Jewish texts' impact on classical authors, as I argued in my 2018 paper 'Was Vergil Reading the Bible?' I proposed that the answer is likely 'Yes,' and that some scholars are starting to acknowledge Jewish themes as a crucial part of his work.

However, scholars' neglect of acrostics might stem from an assumption they are too simplistic for sophisticated authors. After all, even a child can create an acrostic. The most basic type involves spelling a word vertically by the first letters of successive lines: Catches mice, Adorable whiskers, Tail's up – look out! It is undeniable that such vertical words exist in the columns of long poems; the challenge lies in determining whether they hold any significance.

text:
The traditional view of moral struggle often portrays it as an internal battle between a virtuous self and our inner darkness. This idea echoes in popular culture - think of the angel and devil on each shoulder, the 'two wolves' story, Freud's Ego and Id, or the distinction between our true and false selves. It reflects religious teachings that position us as beings caught between angels and beasts in a Great Chain of Being, wrestling with the tensions of spirit and flesh, morality and temptation, conscience and vice. This view also recalls philosophical thought from Plato to Kant, which often depicts life's moral dilemmas as a fight between duty and desire.

However, this perspective overlooks a crucial aspect of many moral conflicts: managing multiple good or neutral demands on our limited time, energy, and resources. Instead of focusing on suppressing our dark side, the challenge lies in navigating a world where doing good is more than we can handle at once.

In essence, the crux of many moral dilemmas isn't whether to be good but how to distribute our limited moral capabilities when faced with scarcity and conflict. This calls for different ways of thinking about moral agency and living well.

To understand this better, let's consider three broad categories of values that regularly generate conflicting but legitimate moral claims. These dimensions of goodness manifest through distinct practical stances we take as we grapple with our choices and identities. Our fragmented nature isn't a battle between good and evil selves; rather, it's about navigating competing aspects of the good - not all of which can be fulfilled in any given moment.

One way to conceptualize these domains is through literature's traditional distinction between first-, second-, and third-person perspectives. A story told from the first-person perspective offers insight into the protagonist's internal struggles, inviting us to say 'I' along with them. In contrast, the second-person perspective puts emphasis on others - the 'you'.

This model highlights that our moral lives are complex and nuanced, requiring careful thought and self-awareness to navigate successfully.

text:
Title: Navigating the Divide: The Complexity of Believing in God and the Existence of Evil

In today's world, there are numerous debates that stir strong feelings, and the question of whether or not to believe in God is one of them. On both sides, you'll find individuals who feel just as passionate about their beliefs - some who believe wholeheartedly in a divine entity, while others who argue against its existence. If you find yourself firmly on either side, you likely know your moral duty, as William James Clifford would have it.

I grapple with the idea of God's existence, especially in light of the suffering and evil that exists within our world. For me, the image of Anne Frank's tragic end at Bergen-Belsen serves as a painful reminder of the death of God as I understand Him. However, I am not so confident that my perspective is absolute.

Much like many thoughtful Christians, I view the Bible as a collection of stories that illustrate how a nomadic people developed their understanding of their Creator. The Old Testament, in particular, can be seen as a journey - from infancy to adulthood, where the loving God of the gospels emerges. Certainly, there are challenges in distinguishing between literal and metaphorical interpretations, but I believe these difficulties can be addressed.

On the other hand, there are aspects that make the existence of God seem implausible to me. For one, the idea itself is unclear - the Christian God seems like an uneasy blend of Greek and Jewish conceptions of the Divine. Furthermore, I find it challenging to reconcile the existence of evil with a benevolent God. To me, God ceased to exist in Anne Frank's final moments at Bergen-Belsen.

Lastly, I believe that faith often serves as self-deception. However, I acknowledge that others may find comfort and purpose in their beliefs. Questions like "Why is there something rather than nothing?" or "How can a computer made of meat think?" are intriguing, and when believers use their faith to make sense of these mysteries, I am hesitant to judge. If someone claims that the only way they can comprehend great suffering is through religion, I do not dismiss them as unintelligent or insincere.

A process theologian, following Alfred North Whitehead, might propose that God has emptied Himself of His power - 'kenosis' - to suffer alongside us. It's possible that this idea is incorrect, but I cannot condemn someone who finds solace in it. In all honesty, my own beliefs continue to evolve as I grapple with these questions.

For me, embracing belief in God would feel immoral. But I understand why others choose faith - it offers a sense of purpose and understanding that I cannot deny. Perhaps one day, I too may find a way to reconcile the existence of evil with the idea of a loving God. Until then, I will continue my journey, seeking wisdom, understanding, and compassion in a world that can often seem senseless and cold.

text:
Sam Cooke, a man who shaped his own career, but lived with the complexities of being a black star. In the latter part of 1963, he and his bandmates attempted to check into a hotel in Shreveport, Louisiana, only to be denied. They made a scene, were arrested, and imprisoned. The song "A Change is Gonna Come" was recorded in February 1964 during a session produced by Hugo Peretti and Luigi Creatore. It was subtly included on Cooke’s album Ain't That Good News (1964). As chronicled by Guralnick in Dream Boogie, following the session, Cooke played the song for Bobby Womack, his protégé. Womack said the song sounded like death. On December 22, 1964, "A Change Is Gonna Come" was finally released as a single. Eleven days earlier, Cooke had been shot and killed at a Los Angeles motel, in what was later ruled a justifiable homicide. The song didn't do exceptionally well commercially; it peaked at number 31 on the Billboard chart in 1965. However, it became an anthem of the Civil Rights movement and remains a powerful symbol of that era of protest. It was Cooke’s last, and perhaps most enduring, song.

When art leans too heavily towards rhetoric or sloganeering, it often falls flat. "A Change Is Gonna Come" is an unusual protest song - it's not raw, angry, or street-level. The dramatic orchestration suggests glitzy nightclubs and movie palaces, rather than barricades and lunch-counter sit-ins. Cooke's voice, pure as ever, glides through the strings and timpanis like a well-mixed martini slips down your throat. The arrangement, by René Hall, builds and crests atop an obvious sense of its own significance. This is a grand song, we're told through the production. Yet, when Cooke sings, he does so in a quiet, personal first person, narrating everyday scenes of injustice. Each verse culminates in a simple chorus where Cooke repeats the title line: 'A change is gonna come.' The promise of change resonates in the way he stretches out the words 'long' and 'know', how he twirls the phrase: It's been a long, long time comin', but I know-oh-oo-oh A change is gonna come, Oh yes it will. It's both a prayer and a warning.

One of the challenges in creating successful art about social change is that people experience injustices as individuals, not as movements or policies or messages. When art strays too far from this reality and veers towards rhetoric or sloganeering, it often loses impact. What makes "A Change Is Gonna Come" resonate isn't Cooke’s vocal abilities or the timing of its release. Its political power lies in its personal touch. Cooke retreated from the royal 'we' and the broad metaphoric strokes that folk singers like Dylan used to rally their audiences to the cause. He didn't call for a march in Alabama to fight for rights, but instead sang about his own experiences: "I was born by the river in a little tent, oh, and just like the river I began to spend, my life in mournin', still searchin' for that day... A change is gonna come."

text:
This novel, "Infinite Jest," feels incompatible with human life as we know it. It embodies an unsettling and pervasive sense of evil, not just a trait but the very essence of conscious existence. It's like a poison seeping into the most fundamental layers of oneself – a cellular and spiritual nausea. It's hard to put into words, but it's a kind of double bind where any action or inaction we associate with human agency feels not just unpleasant, but downright horrifying. No description I've encountered has managed to capture the excruciating agony I experienced quite as vividly and directly as this book did.

The book's most famous lines revolve around suicidality and the inescapable logic that comes with it. It compares the decision of a person considering suicide to that of someone trapped in a burning building deciding whether to jump. Don't be fooled by those who leap from burning windows, the book suggests. Their fear of falling is just as great as ours would be, standing there idly looking out. The terror isn't about wanting to fall; it's about the flames. And those on the sidewalk, shouting and urging you not to jump, can never truly understand the terror beyond the fall. They haven't experienced the flames themselves.

The suicidal person, then, is not misguided but rather facing choices that are unimaginable to those who don't share their experience of being slowly consumed by those flames. Reading this book was like looking into a mirror at that moment, reflecting back my feelings so accurately and directly. It validated my unique sense of this 'mental illness,' not as a mistake or miscalculation on my part, but as something happening to me – a force within me, a billowing shape, as the book often describes it, to which all my dread and despair were just rational responses.

But finishing the book marked the beginning of my struggle to hold onto this self-understanding – and thus, my grasp on life itself. It was only a few days later that I attempted suicide. I believe reading "Infinite Jest" had been keeping me alive up until then. So, when he came to the realization...

This essay is about how the novel "Infinite Jest" resonated with the author's feelings of despair and suicidality, validating their experiences, but also potentially triggering a suicide attempt shortly after finishing the book. The metaphor of being trapped in a burning building is used to explain the terror of suicide and the seemingly unimaginable choices faced by those experiencing it. The essay concludes with the author's reflection on how the book had been keeping them alive, but that their grip on this understanding began to slip shortly after finishing it.

text:
In today's world, it seems that we thrive on discord. We are wired in such a way that we need a bit of friction to keep us spiritually alert and intellectually stimulated. And who better to provide that necessary discomfort than the contrarian thinkers? John Stuart Mill, in his book On Liberty (1859), actually praised eccentricity! He argued that it's these 'eccentrics' who contribute bold perspectives, fresh insights, and new ideas that keep our world spinning. As he wrote, "Eccentricity has always abounded when and where strength of character has abounded; and the amount of eccentricity in a society has generally been proportional to the amount of genius, mental vigor, and moral courage which it contained."

It's this redeeming 'eccentricity' that contrarians embody. Their ideas are sharp and unique because they refuse to be confined within the boundaries set by any group, whether explicitly or implicitly defined. Excluded as they often are, they have a bird's eye view of how herding, marginalization, and exclusion operate. They no longer fear the consequences of voicing their unconventional views because they have nothing left to lose.

In essence, contrarians are what 'public intellectuals' should strive to be – unyielding critics of society – but unfortunately, in practice, few live up to this ideal. It's the fervor of their dissent, the intensity of their language, and their deep-seated commitment that make them formidable figures. They are not just provocateurs seeking attention or a dramatic performance; they are individuals driven by intellectual duty and inner conviction.

The unique composition of contrarian minds, their innate skepticism of anything authoritative or established, their iconoclasm, and their radical separation from the society in which they were born, all contribute to their access to a higher truth than most societies can bear to hear. They don't care for trends, authorities, or societal hierarchies, and are impatient with the rituals of the establishment. By severing ties with their tribe, they gain an unobstructed view of reality. Their dissent doesn't just free them; it gives them a new perspective.

Spinoza, for instance, didn't truly find his philosophical footing until he was expelled from his community. Even though he was already exceptionally learned, his exclusion opened his eyes to truths that might have remained hidden within the confines of his tribe. In essence, it takes a bit of friction and a dash of eccentricity to truly see things as they are.

Under these guidelines, financial institutions are encouraged to constantly enhance their cybersecurity measures and procedures to proactively counter potential threats. The guidelines cover four key areas: risk management responsibility and ownership, periodic evaluation and monitoring of cybersecurity controls, regular independent assessment and testing, as well as industry collaboration and contingency planning.

To ensure that financial institutions stay vigilant against the evolving nature of cyber threats, the SBP mandates the Board of Directors (BoD) to regularly assess the effectiveness of their cybersecurity systems and action plans in light of emerging risks. The senior management, meanwhile, is tasked with ensuring that a comprehensive cybersecurity management plan exists within each institution and is routinely reviewed and updated for implementation.

It is essential to acknowledge that cyber threats have become a global concern, continually evolving in terms of complexity and impact, despite advancements in cyber services delivery. In light of these new risks, the SBP requires banks, DFIs, and MFBs to take appropriate measures to comply with these guidelines by December 31, 2016.

text:
The rugged, red-veined rocks of Bohuslän in western Sweden are home to one of Europe's highest concentrations of Bronze Age art. During a recent October visit, I had the privilege of witnessing these ancient carvings hidden away from the modern world, nestled where the North Sea would have once lapped at the shore, around 2,500 to 4,000 years ago. These petroglyphs were painted bright red for tourists, resembling a gigantic child's drawing with wobbly lines and vibrant colors. But the raw energy of the scene surpassed any sense of elegance it might have lacked. Archers pursued majestic stags amidst fleets of longships that seemed to flow seamlessly into processions of bulls and towering giants teetering precariously on spindly legs. The boundaries between land, sea, human, and animal blurred as they ebbed and flowed before my eyes.

Bronze Age rock art in Bohuslän, Sweden, is believed to depict the enactment of a ritual. As I stood there, I couldn't help but try to piece together some forgotten epic story, like solving a Bronze Age cartoon. We may never know why these markings were made or what they meant to say. The blocky figures offer no clues about where this tale might have begun or ended. Yet, perhaps the tangled quality of the petroglyphs is their true message.

The longships suggest a culture deeply intertwined with the sea, while the abundance of animals hints at a strong bond with nature. Similar sites on the Cornish and Iberian peninsulas indicate that cultural influences traveled along what would later be called the 'whale roads' - the open waters connecting Bronze Age Scandinavia to the rest of Europe for trade and conflict.

We find ourselves in a similarly intricate age, where our consumption of fossil fuels, nitrogen fertilizers, smartphone cobalt, antibiotics, and plastic entwine us with distant times and places. The world of the Anthropocene is woven from this dense network of relationships, each of which leaves its mark on the future.

Two hundred generations have come and gone since these petroglyphs were chiseled into the granite, yet they remain vibrant and immediate, a testament to an enduring presence. In their silence, they pose thought-provoking questions: How do we communicate with the deep future through the currents of time and matter? Are our deliberate symbols what leave the most lasting impressions or are our unintentional traces more enduring?

I reside in Edinburgh, on the edge of the North Sea. I've come to view these waters as an Anthropocene laboratory, a place that allows us to catch a glimpse of the long-term consequences of how we live today. Its past offers insights into our future. As the Pangea supercontinent was splitting apart 200 million years ago...

Title: The Ongoing Struggle for Workers' Rights in the Persian Gulf

The recent changes in business regulations in Qatar have drawn mixed reactions, with critics arguing that they only scratch the surface of deep-rooted issues. Similarly, reforms to migrant labor conditions in the UAE, such as providing training on racism, diversity, and inclusion to employers, are seen by many as cosmetic measures aimed at improving the country's international image.

The same criticism is levied against Saudi Arabia, which has announced plans to abolish its kafala labor system in 2021. However, these changes do little to address what Finley referred to as a "morally reprehensible system of human exploitation" - six modern-day slave societies that continue to exist in the Persian Gulf.

For true abolition to occur, we must eliminate all aspects of slavery that I've previously identified. These include the racialization of labor and rampant worker abuse and exploitation. It's heartening to see the launch of the website Ijmāʿ on Slavery, which aims to secure a consensus among Islamic scholars that slavery and slave trading are unlawful under Islamic law.

Each of these city-states is governed by Muslim governments. I urge Muslim scholars in these communities to join this consensus, helping to eradicate this "morally reprehensible system of human exploitation" in the Persian Gulf city-states.

text:
The combination of human rights activism, forensic anthropology, and emerging DNA technology, embodied in organizations like the Argentine Forensic Anthropology Team (EAAF) and the Grandmothers of Plaza de Mayo, gave birth to a powerful, groundbreaking movement. This movement leverages scientific advancements to provide new avenues for victims' families and societies transitioning from conflict or authoritarian rule to cope with their violent pasts.

While the promise of forensics-based human rights isn't just about identifying remains, it extends to a deeper, more nuanced understanding of justice at individual, familial, and societal levels. For many grieving families, this means the opportunity for funerals, public mourning, and rewriting historical records – all actions that can significantly alter collective memory. This new form of justice holds special significance in cases where legal justice is unattainable due to amnesty laws, time's passage, or indifferent governments who benefit from silence.

This forensics-based human rights movement allows voices outside the state – perceived as credible thanks to the legitimacy of science – to narrate violent historical accounts. It has spread globally, from Latin America to Africa, Europe, and recently, the United States. Navigating how societies confront their history is always complex. Collective memory does not represent objective history but a tangled web of present-day hierarchies, identities, and emotional and moral ties to the past. It's like layers of sediment shaped by countless variables including emotions, power relations, culture, storytelling, and time's passage.

Those in positions of privilege, such as politicians, often have more opportunities to advance 'official' narratives that serve them. In societies scarred by political violence, war, or genocide, some governments deliberately silence violent pasts for their benefit. Scholars like Claire Whitlinger and Eviatar Zerubavel argue that this silencing undermines social solidarity by threatening open communication, a cornerstone of democratic political cultures. However, state-imposed silences are not invincible and can be challenged by countering techniques.

Those who challenge the memory of state violence, such as human rights workers, must be viewed as legitimate to be heard and taken seriously. As Pierre Bourdieu pointed out in 1982, an actor's success often depends on their 'authority.' Thus, social actors presenting counter-memories of state terror and violence must be seen as reasonable and not motivated by revenge or corrupt intentions, especially during critical moments like a transition to democracy or the breaking of state-imposed silences.

So, how can a movement find a voice that is both legitimate and devoid of political bias? Social actors should focus on presenting their message in a way that resonates with reasonableness and integrity rather than vengeance or self-interest. In high-intensity situations, this is particularly important to avoid being dismissed as partisan.

Every year, approximately 1,000 Nepali individuals who depart Nepal seeking work abroad end their lives in labor-destination countries, with a staggering 97% of these fatalities occurring in the Persian Gulf. This alarmingly disproportionate statistic raises concerns, as only around half of all Nepali labor migrants travel to this region annually. The majority of these workers are young and otherwise healthy, making their premature deaths even more tragic.

The Supreme Court of Nepal has recently issued a mandamus, requiring the government to perform autopsies on the bodies of deceased Nepali workers who are repatriated to their homeland. Unfortunately, the process of returning these workers' remains can take months due to bureaucratic red tape in Gulf nations. In many instances, families are left waiting for closure, not knowing the cause of their loved one's death.

Recent news reports have detailed numerous strange and suspicious deaths, including homicides, involving low-status workers in the Persian Gulf. For example, the Philippine government has prohibited its citizens, predominantly women, from traveling to Kuwait following the discovery of a Filipina domestic worker's body in her employer's abandoned freezer. The Philippines ambassador to Kuwait reported over 6,000 complaints regarding the mistreatment of Filipino workers in 2017 alone.

Embassies of India, Sri Lanka, and the Philippines have established shelters in Kuwait for their citizens, primarily women, who have fled abusive domestic work situations out of fear for their safety. In another instance, an Ethiopian maid was forced to climb onto a seventh-story window ledge in an apparent attempt to escape her employer. Instead of assisting her, the employer filmed the incident and the maid fell, sustaining injuries including a broken arm.

Incidences of employer abuse and neglect are distressingly common, and many homicides and other mysterious deaths go unreported or are covered up by authorities. African workers appear to be particularly targeted. The lack of transparency surrounding these deaths mirrors the historical misreporting of police killings of young Black men in the United States.

On a more positive note, the government of Qatar has recently announced plans to increase migrant workers' wages by 33% and relax certain kafala regulations. Employers have been granted six months to comply with these changes or face penalties from the government, including potential suspension of their operations. Whether these measures will effectively address the systemic issues plaguing this vulnerable population remains to be seen.

text:
In the realm of pets and wildlife, there are numerous examples where our quest for aesthetically pleasing appearances has led to some questionable practices. Take tufted birds, for instance; they're susceptible to feather cysts and struggle with flight and body temperature regulation. Similarly, long-tailed species like goldfish, guppies, and angelfish face mobility issues and breeding difficulties due to their elaborate tails. In some goldfish varieties, abnormalities like 'helmets', 'caps', or 'crests' can impair vision, making them nearly blind. Darwin himself, in his 1868 treatise on domesticated species, referred to these distorted goldfish as 'monstrosities'.

Our fascination with appearances sometimes leads us down a darker path, such as subjecting pets to painful cosmetic surgeries. Tail docking, a common practice in many dog breeds like boxers, cocker spaniels, and dobermans, is one example. This procedure, often performed on puppies without anesthesia, is defended as a means of protecting tails from injury or infection during rough play or field work. However, there's little evidence to support this claim. As the American Veterinary Medical Association points out, the primary benefit of cosmetic tail docking appears to be satisfying the owner's aesthetic preferences.

Tail docking was outlawed in the UK in 2007, but remains common in the US, where it's also banned in some states and provinces. The same goes for horses; cosmetic tail docking and 'nicking', where a horse's tail tendon is cut to make it carry its tail higher, have been illegal in the UK since 1949.

There are other surgical interventions aimed at correcting perceived physical defects in pets. For example, if Rover's ears don't stand erect as some breed standards dictate, you can opt for plastic and mesh ear implants. In 2009, a UK dog shelter even had two chocolate labradors undergo breast reductions due to sagging mammary glands from overbreeding.

While these practices might seem radical, they pale in comparison to the potential of modern biotechnology. Molecular biology now gives us unprecedented power to alter animal bodies in ways that were once only found in science fiction. Genetic engineering allows us to extract new value from animals, such as creating pigs with organs suitable for human transplant or goats that produce lifesaving pharmaceuticals in their milk.

With these tools at our disposal, we have the potential to take our aesthetic preferences to new, futuristic extremes. It's a reminder of how far we've come and how much further we could potentially push the boundaries if we're not careful.

text: The line between attributing consciousness to something and bridging the gap of understanding is always blurred. Yet, Wittgenstein poses a question that lingers in our minds - if these 'automata' are pretending to feel pain, for instance, how can we be certain that it's indeed pain they're feigning? For Wittgenstein, these criteria don't establish the truth of statements but rather define the use of the concepts within those statements. Our ability to recognize whatever they might be 'feigning' as pain is simply our capacity to speak the language we do. But if all the criteria are satisfied, you're not missing any pieces of the puzzle - what's at stake here is whether you acknowledge them as a someone, in pain. As Cavell writes, "The gap of acknowledgement can never be filled with knowledge." In a similar vein, our own minds, our emotions, aren't logically hidden from being understood by others. This connection is fragile, not in the sense of a metaphysical barrier but in the sense that some people fail to acknowledge the humanity of others. Wittgenstein points out a crucial aspect of the teacher-student relationship - when the student does something incorrect or unexpected. On this reading, Wittgenstein witnesses what Rupert Read calls a 'proto-Levinasian moment', where a Self encounters the Other. In this encounter, the degree of acknowledgment we adopt shapes our attitude towards them. This attitude is inherently ethical because it defines the limits of our obligations to others and because denying another's humanity involves the simultaneous denial of the mutuality upon which our attempts to communicate or express anything at all depend. This denial implies the absence of meaningful grounds even for constituting ourselves as subjects. As Cavell puts it, "I am fated to stand to myself in the relations in which I may or may not stand to others." It is only by acknowledging the Other that the Self can truly be a human being. In Wittgenstein's Investigations, the examples of such encounters are pedagogical. There is no more poignant encounter than the one we often face - the case of the deviant student (as described in §185 of the Investigations). As teachers, we know that the crux, the risk, of the teacher-student relationship arises when the student does something wrong and goes against our expectations. This pupil doesn't comply with our instructions. They don't see things as we do. So what do we do about them? In Wittgenstein's lecture notes that precede the Investigations, known as the Brown Book, he humorously notes society's typical response: "If a child does not respond to the suggestive gesture, the answer is to hit him."

text:
Simone Bespaloff's spirit oscillated between fury and peace during her time in New York. Despite being in exile, she managed to maintain ties with Parisian intellectual circles through letter exchanges with Fessard and Marcel. A chance opportunity led her to work for the Voice of America’s French broadcast before moving on to teach French literature at Mount Holyoke College in Massachusetts. This small liberal arts college became a significant beacon for French culture during the war, hosting gatherings of exiled scholars orchestrated by Wahl. It was here that Bespaloff crossed paths with luminaries such as Jacques Maritain, André Masson, Marc Chagall, and Claude Lévi-Strauss. Mary McCarthy, who translated her works, once described Bespaloff as a "small, dark lady who wore white gloves." Hannah Arendt, who visited in August 1944 to deliver a lecture on Franz Kafka, also found Bespaloff intriguing. In her essay on Kafka, later published in Partisan Review, Arendt's interpretation mirrored Bespaloff's existentialist melancholy. Under the ominous veil of war, Arendt depicted humanity as tragically ensnared within the tangled threads of history. The reality of Kafka's "nightmarish world" had become a grim reality.

In an essay on Camus, her last published work, Bespaloff pondered how history compelled her generation to live in a climate of brutal death. Post-war, despite initially being celebrated by them, Bespaloff became a critic of the new wave of French existentialists, particularly Sartre. In a 1946 letter to musicologist Boris de Schloezer, she asserted that Sartre's rejection of "magical interiority" in favor of subjectivity was not the foundation of a new humanism but a precursor to conformity. She argued that instead of liberating the individual, Sartre's existentialism obliterated this magical interiority, which enables authentic human connection. For Bespaloff, Sartre reduced the subject to an object under the scrutiny of the Other. This objectified 'subjectivity strikingly resembled American "individualism," which unleashed itself in action to conceal its void.' The United States felt dull and hostile to Bespaloff, much like Helen's Troy.

Bespaloff's journey to Mount Holyoke marked her final exile. During a term break, in April 1949, for reasons still shrouded in mystery, she sealed her kitchen doors and turned on the gas oven. Her complex symphony of life ended tragically. In her final note, alluding to Camus's claim, she wrote: "One might imagine Sisyphus happy, but joy remains forever elusive."
