JOHANNESBURG: South Africa's Sports Minister Fikile Mbalula announced a ban on Monday forbidding the national cricket and rugby federations from hosting or bidding for international tournaments for at least a year. The decision came in response to the failure of these organizations, alongside athletics and netball bodies, to increase representation of black players.

The minister's action was prompted by a recent report revealing that four out of five major sports in South Africa have been falling short in creating ample opportunities for black athletes. This decision deals a significant blow to the South African Rugby Union's aspirations to bid for the 2023 Rugby World Cup, as the bidding process is set to commence next month.

The South African government has long been advocating for greater inclusion of black players in their most popular sports. However, more than two decades after the end of apartheid, whites continue to dominate the ranks of athletes in athletics, cricket, netball, and rugby. Mbalula stated, "I have resolved to revoke the privilege of Athletics South Africa (ASA), Cricket South Africa (CSA), Netball South Africa (NSA) and South African Rugby (SARU) to host and bid for major and mega international tournaments."

In a statement, Mbalula emphasized that the soccer federation was the only one of the five sports bodies to meet its transformation targets. He added that he would reconsider this decision based on the results of the 2016-17 Transformation Barometer, which may be released at the end of next year.

text:
On November 16th, 2016, investment guru and philanthropist Tom Marsico, one of the wealthiest men in the U.S., saw a fatter wallet: Christie's in New York auctioned off Claude Monet's "Grainstack" for a whopping $81.4 million. This record-breaking price for a Monet painting at auction was more than six times what Marsico paid for it back in 2002, demonstrating that investing in a Monet might just be one of the most profitable moves you can make. Auction houses in London and New York have seen stellar performances by Monet time and again. Yet, the sky-high prices paid for his paintings today are hardly a reminder of Monet's struggles during his lifetime. The tales of critical rejection faced by Impressionist painters, including Monet, and their financial hardships are well-known. However, it's important to note that while Monet did face some early setbacks, he wasn't exactly starving throughout most of his career.

In 1868, for instance, a juror dismissed his works at the annual Paris Salon, potentially hindering sales. That same year, he was even denied credit for paints and reportedly subsisted on potatoes during one harsh winter. However, for much of his life, Monet wasn't living hand to mouth. His diet soon moved beyond potatoes to indulgent treats like foie gras from Alsace, truffles from Périgord, and lobster in peppercorn sauce. One visitor gushed about the exceptional cuisine at Monet's home. After turning 50 (he lived to be 86), Monet was never short of cash: a fortunate turn of events for such a food enthusiast. His wealth kept him rolling in cars, tailored suits from Paris, and a team of gardeners to tend his famous gardens. In this sense, he shares more similarities with contemporary artists like Damien Hirst or Jeff Koons, their high-profile dealers, deep-pocketed collectors, and multimillion-dollar fortunes, than with artistic outliers such as the penniless Vincent van Gogh. Monet's surname fits him well, as headline writers often appreciate its near homonymy with 'money'. He was indeed the Jazz Age's precursor to Koons, because in 1922, four years before his death, the sale of one of his canvases to a Japanese tycoon made him the world's most expensive living artist.

Monet has long been the go-to artist for the rich and famous. Besides Marsico, recent owners have included David Rockefeller, the Queen Mother, Imelda Marcos, Prince Rainier, hotelier Steve Wynn, and various Russian and Asian billionaires. His paintings are not just a testament to aesthetic taste but also serve as lucrative financial assets. The world number three made the announcement on Tuesday, citing ongoing issues stemming from a slip he experienced during a bath run for his twin daughters in June.

The incident occurred after Federer's semifinal match against Milos Raonic at Wimbledon. Although he managed to finish the game, Federer expressed concerns about potential long-term damage to his knee, saying, "I just hope with the slip I had in the fifth, I'm going to be fine... I hope I didn't hurt myself." At the time, he was unsure whether the pain would subside quickly or persist.

With a history of aches, pains, and injuries that are common among top athletes, Federer has had a difficult year. His withdrawl leaves him with only seven tournament appearances and a 21-loss record for the season. The Swiss tennis player expressed his disappointment, stating, "I am extremely disappointed to announce that I will not be able to represent Switzerland at the Olympic Games in Rio and that I will also miss the remainder of the season."

Federer's decision comes as a blow to fans and fellow athletes alike, given his impressive track record in international competition. The Olympics will mark only the third time since 1992 that Federer has not participated in an Olympic Games.

text:
In the later half of the 20th century, Wilfrid Sellars's perspective on semantics played an essential role in making sense of Hegel's logic. This was a crucial step towards reviving Hegelian philosophy, as his followers began to understand the intricacies of Hegel's logic. Robert Brandom has been instrumental in interpreting Hegel from a 'non-metaphysical' perspective more recently. To fully grasp this interpretation, let's delve into Sellars's semantic theory, which he believed is vital to the resurrection of Hegelian thought.

At that time, the analytic tradition predominantly relied on a simple understanding of semantics. Syntax dealt with intra-linguistic relations like well-formedness and rules for constructing language strings. On the other hand, semantics - the study of the meaning of linguistic expressions - focused on their relation to the world. However, Sellars argued that semantics does not require a basic word-world relationship at all. Instead, he proposed that the content of a linguistic expression or a thought is determined by its inferential relationships with other expressions or thoughts. Therefore, in his view, semantics primarily concerns word-word relationships instead of word-world ones.

To further explain this 'inferentialist' theory of meaning, Sellars broadened the concept of inference to encompass both formally valid inferences and material inferences. While formal logic deals with inferences that depend only on the syntactic structure of sentences, ignoring non-logical words, material inferences are based on context and experience. For example, the inference from 'The ball is red' to 'The ball is colored' may not be formally valid, but it makes perfect sense in a given context.

According to Sellars, semantics involves more than just intralinguistic connections. Our responses to the world around us (language-entry transitions or observation reports) and our actions based on intentions and desires (language-exit transitions) are also integral to the constitution of meaning. The way one interacts with the world (and others) is crucial in understanding their beliefs and desires. As a result, the content of a word or sentence, as well as the content of a concept or thought, is determined by its position within a complex linguistic economy that includes observation reports, inferences, various speech acts, etc., all guided by norms of proper usage.

This holistic and non-foundationalist semantics, with no expressions having independent content, was a significant contribution from Sellars. His younger colleague, Brandom, emphasized another aspect of Sellars's thought: our concepts have histories. Building upon this view of semantics, Brandom interpreted Hegel's conception of dialectic, incorporating not only material connections between concepts but also material exclusions - what a concept includes or excludes based on its historical development.

text:
MUMBAI: India's cotton exports in the 2016/17 season are projected to decline by approximately 28% compared to the previous year, reaching around 5 million bales. This significant drop is due to Pakistan, one of India's major buyers, potentially cutting its purchases in half as a result of escalating tensions and improvements in its own domestic production, according to industry and government officials.

Pakistan's reduced imports from the world's leading cotton producer are expected to benefit countries such as Brazil, the United States, and several African nations in increasing their own exports. Pakistan may import between 1 million and 1.5 million bales during the 2016/17 season, a substantial decrease from the 2.7 million bales purchased in the same period last year, as stated by India's Textile Commissioner Kavita Gupta during a press briefing on Monday.

Gupta attributed the reduction to an improvement in Pakistan's cotton production, but industry sources suggested that tensions between the two nations are also playing a role. "Pakistan still requires imports, but Pakistani buyers are increasingly looking towards Brazil and the U.S.," said Pradeep Jain, a ginner based in Jalgaon, Maharashtra's western state.

Typically, the world's largest cotton consumer begins importing from September onwards. However, exporters have reported a significant drop in inquiries over the past few weeks. In 2015/16, Pakistan surpassed Bangladesh to become India's primary cotton buyer, accounting for around 40% of India's exports. As of now, India has contracted only 500,000 bales for export due to weak demand from international buyers, as per Dhiren Sheth, president of the Cotton Association of India. By this time last year, Indian traders had already agreed on contracts to export 1 million bales, according to dealers.

India's cotton output in the 2016/17 season is expected to increase by around 3.8% compared to the previous year, reaching approximately 35.1 million bales. This forecasted rise is primarily due to increased yields resulting from favorable monsoon rains and fewer pest issues, as Gupta explained during her press briefing: "This year, despite lower acreage, we are anticipating higher per-hectare yields on account of good monsoon rains and reduced impact from pests."

(1 Indian bale = 170 kg)

text:
It's worth acknowledging a nuanced difference when discussing the nature of labor conditions in the Persian Gulf, but it's important to remember that this doesn't negate the fact that these societies can still be rightfully categorized as slave societies. A discerning observer might question: 'But what about the definition of slavery?' The work relationships prevalent in the Persian Gulf aren't reminiscent of the property-based relations described by Finley, where slaves were owned and traded like commodities in markets and other circumstances. Yet, we must also concede that contemporary human relationships can exhibit characteristics strikingly similar to chattel slavery, as defined by sociologist Orlando Patterson as 'social death'. This is when the social and legal disadvantage of a laborer is so severe that it effectively renders them enslaved, regardless of whether the legal system officially recognizes their ownership.

One additional point worth noting is the correlation between homicide and slavery observed by Davis and legal scholar Robert Cover. When we examine historical accounts of slave transportation across various routes such as the transatlantic, trans-Saharan, Silk Road, and Indian Ocean slave trades, we see that death rates often exceeded 30%. In modern Persian Gulf societies, we witness high rates of homicide and unexplained deaths among low-status migrant laborers. The most alarming evidence can be found in reports about the welfare of Nepalese workers, particularly those working on construction sites for the 2022 FIFA World Cup in Qatar. A recent report indicates that at least 1,400 Nepali workers have died on these sites since work commenced, with approximately 150 deaths per year continuing to occur. The International Labour Organization has also published a report highlighting an alarmingly high death rate among otherwise healthy Nepalese migrant workers, particularly in Saudi Arabia, Oman, Bahrain, and Kuwait. The death rate in Saudi Arabia was significantly higher than the death rate in Malaysia, with almost the same number of deaths occurring in Malaysia despite greater numbers of Nepalis traveling there for work. Similar statistics can be found for other Persian Gulf countries. The estimated death rate for Nepalese workers in Saudi Arabia between 2008 and 2015 was 2.26 per 1,000 workers, while the rates for Bahrain, Oman, and Lebanon were even higher at 2.60, 2.40, and 2.86 respectively. These rates should be contrasted with lower death rates in Malaysia (1.72) and South Korea (1.61). It seems that the death rate for Nepalese migrant workers in the Persian Gulf has remained consistent over time, and a significant number of these deaths remain unexplained. Many of the unexplained deaths are young women.

SINGAPORE - Brent crude oil prices experienced a significant rebound on Wednesday as investors held onto hope that a tentative agreement between Saudi Arabia and Russia to freeze oil production at January levels could lead to a broader deal among producers, potentially resulting in supply cuts.

By 0455 GMT, Brent crude had gained 34 cents to reach $32.52 per barrel, recovering from its $1.21 decline the previous day and surpassing its early Tuesday high of $35.55 per barrel. West Texas Intermediate (WTI) crude followed suit, rising 19 cents to $29.23 per barrel after ending the previous session with a 40-cent drop.

The agreement between Russia and Saudi Arabia on Tuesday stipulated that oil production would be limited to January levels, contingent upon other exporters joining in. However, they fell short of agreeing to make immediate cuts to tackle the global oversupply.

Iraq, Qatar, and Venezuela expressed their intent to freeze output at January levels, provided a deal could be reached, while OPEC member Iran may receive special terms for freezing its production levels, sources said. The market's initial surge on Tuesday was swiftly tempered by the realization that no immediate supply cuts were imminent.

Ben Le Brun, a market analyst at Sydneys OptionsXpress, described the situation as "a buy the rumour, sell the fact event." He explained that while the market is adjusting to the idea that this isn't entirely negative news, it's not as favorable as initially anticipated. Investors are primarily hopeful for production cuts.

According to a note from Singapore-based Phillip Futures, freezing output at January levels would have minimal impact on the overall supply glut. Saudi Arabia's oil production has remained relatively stable at over 10 million barrels per day (bpd) throughout 2015, essentially maintaining the status quo rather than making significant changes.

Investors are keeping a close eye on upcoming US oil inventory data for further insights into oil prices. According to a Reuters poll of analysts, US crude stocks increased by 3.9 million barrels to 505.9 million barrels in the week ending February 12. The weekly inventory reports from industry group the American Petroleum Institute (API) and the US Department of Energy's Energy Information Administration (EIA) will be released on Wednesday and Thursday, respectively, one day later than usual due to a public holiday on Monday.

LONDON - Gold prices dipped slightly on Tuesday as global equities rallied following a reduction in political uncertainty within the U.K., along with increased hopes for additional economic stimulus measures. This positive sentiment subsequently diminished demand for safe-haven assets, including gold.

By 09:40 GMT, spot gold was down by 0.2%, trading at $1,352.06 per ounce, after experiencing a nearly one percent decrease on Monday - its most significant decline in nearly two weeks. U.S. gold also experienced a slight dip, falling 0.1% to $1,355.30 per ounce.

Carlo Alberto de Casa, chief analyst at ActivTrades, commented: "With tensions in the U.K. easing for now as the country prepares to welcome a new prime minister, we are witnessing both the British pound regaining some ground and demand for safe-haven assets dwindling."

De Casa added that gold's next support level lies around $1,340 per ounce. Gold has seen a significant increase of approximately $100 per ounce since the U.K. voted to exit the European Union, as investors flocked to secure safe-havens amidst escalating concerns.

The upcoming U.S. payrolls data on Friday has bolstered expectations for an interest rate hike in the United States. Kansas City Federal Reserve President Esther George stated on Monday that current U.S. interest rates are too low and hinted at her readiness to reinitiate her push for higher rates within the Fed's yield curve, thereby pressuring gold prices while supporting the dollar, in which the metal is priced.

INTL FCStone noted: "Not long ago, gold prices would have faltered in response to rising stock prices; however, the current sentiment suggests that monetary easing policies are likely to persist for some time, providing support not only for gold but also for equities."

According to a recent Reuters poll of euro money market traders, the European Central Bank is unlikely to make any further adjustments to its monetary policy during its upcoming meeting next week. Meanwhile, silver prices climbed 0.6% to $20.41 per ounce, while platinum fell for the first time in two weeks, experiencing a 0.1% decline to $1,099 per ounce. Palladium saw an increase of 0.6%, trading at $623.92 per ounce.

text:
The delicate dance of maintaining coherence in quantum systems can be compared to keeping a house of cards standing - it requires minimal energy yet is incredibly taxing to maintain. A quantum computer's intricate quantum states are like the fragile cards, easily disrupted by even the slightest disturbance. This mantle falls on an array of support systems and engineering solutions that consume vast amounts of power. We're still in the dark about what the actual energy-to-computation ratio for quantum computing will look like in practice.

Beyond the power consumption concerns, there are other challenges. A quantum computer with 'n' qubits has the potential to perform 2n calculations in a single cycle, but setting up those computations can be an arduous task of managing data flow. Simulating our entire universe, which consists of roughly 10^89 particles and photons, might require only 296 qubits according to some calculations. However, how on earth do we program in the initial conditions for all 10^89 entities? And if we manage that feat, how do we discern the correct solutions from the quantum simulation's complex output?

Simulating a human brain may be slightly easier but still presents challenges. To set up the computation, we would need to quantify and initiate at least 10^14 neural connections - a number found in a typical human brain. We'd also want this quantum brain to have high throughput and a high-fidelity interface with the physical world. But these are unknowns, potentially insurmountable obstacles. I admit that I may be oversimplifying the techniques and technological tricks that could be employed, but my personal vision of the future is not overly optimistic.

The exponential growth in machine intelligence proposed by some is questionable. The mathematics behind exponentially improving AI might be sound, but the practical barriers to achieving such growth could prove insurmountable. To give a glimpse of where this leaves us, let's imagine a scenario that mixes futurism with Fermi's Paradox. Suppose an advanced cosmic intelligence managed to convert itself into machine form or was overtaken by its super-smart but not exponentially better machine creations. What happens next?

Being confined by efficiency limits, these machines might find themselves looking back at their past for new ways forward. One thing they would know is that biology works remarkably well. Some researchers estimate that the modern human brain has reached its computational limits, but it may only require a slightly more intelligent machine to re-engineer such a complex organ. In other words, there could be the possibility of creating machines as sophisticated as humans by leveraging the power and efficiency of biology.
label: ai

text:
In the midst of crafting my novel, D.H. Lawrence's words resonated deeply with me and mirrored aspects of my life like an echo. I wasn't seeking guidance from him nor was I hunting for self-help remedies. Lawrence was simply a catalyst, stirring thought and provoking reflection. He never conformed to societal expectations; instead, he scrutinized his own thoughts under a harsh, discerning light, challenging the status quo and questioning the implications of opposing viewpoints. This intrinsic quality was evident in his keen observation of people and nature alike - children, adults, animals, and even the world around them.

In 'Education of the People,' penned in 1918, Lawrence proposed an unconventional approach to child-rearing. He suggested that babies should be taken away from their modern mothers and given to rather indifferent women who wouldn't bother with them too much. Although this advice may seem extreme or even dismissible at first glance, I believe there's a kernel of truth in it. Lawrence wasn't advocating for neglectful parenting; instead, he cautioned against overwhelming our children with affection.

Lawrence's ideas on democracy and race were also contentious. He was an outspoken critic of both, expressing views that we might find distasteful today. However, I believe it is essential to separate the profound wisdom found within his novels from the less nuanced, single-minded opinions expressed in his essays. In many instances, Lawrence pushed his ideas to their comic extremes, but this methodology, in my opinion, offered value. He was a writer who embraced intellectual play, allowing multiple thoughts to coexist within his mind and permitting every position to converse with its opposite.

In our current divisive age, where expressing ambivalence or contradiction feels increasingly challenging, Lawrence's unorthodox parenting advice offers food for thought. I believe that parents who demand an emotional response at every turn, transforming every disagreement into an emotional battlefield, inadvertently rob their children of privacy and the blank curiosity needed to explore the world independently.

Lawrence's counsel on excessive love resonates with me. It feels true that parents who overindulge in affection may inadvertently stifle their children's ability to forge their own paths, fostering dependence rather than independence. The diamond-rich regions of Angola became a hotbed of activity for private military companies like Sandline International. Not only was Sandline hired by the Angolan government to quell internal rebellions, but they were also tasked with supporting a coup in neighboring Guinea, which ultimately failed and resulted in the infamous "arms-to-Africa" scandal in the UK. As time went on, these private warriors found themselves working for various factions.

In 2004, Tim Spinner, a former Sandline operative with alleged funding from Mark Thatcher (son of the ex-UK prime minister), led a group of mercenaries in an attempt to overthrow oil-rich Equatorial Guinea, also known as the Wonga Coup. The coup was unsuccessful, and Spinner was sentenced to prison. Mike McCabe, another Sandline veteran, left the company to become head of security for the Marathon Oil Corporation in Texas, which had heavy investments in Equatorial Guinea. As for Tim Mann, shortly after the US invaded Iraq in 2003, he founded a new firm called Aegis Defence Services in London and won a lucrative security contract worth $293 million from the US government in Iraq.

The offspring of Executive Outcomes lives on today. Contracting is a smart move for a wealthy nation that wants to exert its power abroad but doesn't want its citizens to get hurt. It was the US and its conflicts in Iraq and Afghanistan that truly revitalized the market for force. American policymakers, notably Vice President Dick Cheney and Secretary of Defense Donald Rumsfeld, thought the wars would "last weeks, not months." Of course, that was more than a decade ago. The all-volunteer US military quickly found it couldn't recruit enough Americans to sustain these efforts, leaving policymakers with few options.

First, they could withdraw and admit defeat. Second, they could reinstate the draft to fill the ranks. Third, they could hope that allies or the UN would bail them out of their conflicts. Lastly, they could continue the war through contractors. The first three options were either politically disastrous or unrealistic, so they went with contractors, a policy continued by President Barack Obama. Contracting might be the new American way of waging war. It's a smart move for a wealthy nation that wants to exert its power abroad but doesn't want its citizens to get hurt.

In Iraq and Afghanistan, contractors made up 50% and 55%, respectively, of the US force structure. This is a significant increase from the Second World War, when only 10% of the force was contracted. Some wonder if the US will outsource 80-90% of its forces in future conflicts. Contractors account for 25% of all US casualties since the wars in Iraq and Afghanistan began. In 2003, contractor deaths represented only 4% of all fatalities. By 2010, more contractors were killed than military personnel, marking the first time in history that corporate casualties outweighed military losses on US battlefields. These are conservative estimates since the US does not track this data and companies often underreport their wounded and dead, as it's bad for business. Most contractors in Iraq and Afghanistan were civilians with little to no combat experience.

text:
In the realm of academic inquiry, many fields touch on what we might call 'morality'. Among these, normative ethics stands out as one of the least detached from everyday moral concerns. Rather than dissecting how people and societies perceive morality, normative ethicists strive to understand what constitutes straightforward goodness or wickedness.

The philosophical subfield of meta-ethics, on the other hand, takes a step back to consider the nature of these inquiries. It questions whether there are definitive answers to moral dilemmas, or if ethics is more of an illusion or personal opinion. Over the past decade, my work has primarily revolved around this latter area. I firmly believe that there are facts about right and wrong, and that some of these truths transcend individual perspectives - they're 'stance-independent'. To put it plainly, causing immense pain to others is morally wrong not because we disapprove, but because it inflicts harm.

Now, not everyone shares this viewpoint. Some are confused, conflating 'objective' with 'universal', 'innate', or 'rule-bound'. But many skeptics have more profound doubts about moral objectivity. They argue that for morality to be objective, it must rest on a solid foundation - one they believe is missing. Others try to prove that such a foundation exists.

In my opinion, both sides of this debate are off the mark. Moral truths are objective, but they don't necessitate or accommodate a foundation. They simply exist, floating alongside the realm of values without any support from elsewhere. Some parts can be explained by others, but the entire web or network of good and evil is brute - raw, undeniable reality.

If this perspective seems strange or even unworthy of consideration, you're not alone. I once felt the same way. The goal of this essay, based on my book 'Pragmatist Quietism: A Meta-Ethical System' (2022), is to nudge you towards seeing the world as I now see it. Let's start by defining what we mean by a 'foundation'. We can gain clarity by considering whether a moral theory like utilitarianism could serve as one. Utilitarianism suggests that actions are right only if they promote overall wellbeing. So, does this make utilitarianism a foundation?

As you delve deeper into the world of ethics, I invite you to challenge your preconceptions and engage with these ideas, just as I have over the past years.

Dante observes that these figures are condemned to live in perpetual repetition, as their pasts grip them relentlessly, leaving no room for novelty or freshness.

The stark contrast is evident when our journey takes us to the frozen floor of hell. Everything is still, encased in ice, a testament to the paralysis that grips these souls. But in purgatory, every encounter presents an opportunity for growth. As Dante and Virgil ascend Mount Purgatory, the hold of the past on these souls begins to weaken. Here, the inhabitants realize something that eludes those trapped in a hellish existence: vices do not have to define us. A sense of relative freedom is born when they understand that they are not bound by their mistakes and flaws, their hate and fear.

This understanding is akin to sight - the remarkable ability of the conscious mind to grasp, intuit, and develop when given the chance. It brings release because it makes us more receptive to the possibilities that life offers, possibilities we had previously shunned. Dante notes that the souls they meet on the mountain are evolving, and they share their stories of growth and transformation. They are learning to trust and cultivate what could be called imagination, and with that comes another essential capacity - co-creation. This is the art of living well, a dance between creating and being created.

Artists, particularly poets, whom Dante encounters during his climb, underscore this point. They tell him that they often wrote without truly understanding what they were expressing, yet they were drawn to it. Now, they are learning to bridge that gap. The culmination of this process comes for Dante when he meets Beatrice on the summit of Purgatory - a moment I had expected to be filled with love and jubilation. Instead, she rebukes him harshly in two agonizing cantos.

Beatrice berates him for treating her like a deity, desiring only her image, and failing to see that her beauty could serve as an awakening for him. Once more, the theme of missed opportunities and unfulfilled potential reverberates through Dante's journey. This time, it is a stark reminder that even in realms beyond death, we must strive to truly see and understand those around us.

text:
Quantum computing, while it holds immense potential, can be tricky and power-hungry to maintain. The delicate coherence of quantum states requires a plethora of support systems that consume energy in droves. We don't yet know what the actual computation-to-energy function for quantum computing will look like.

The number of computations a quantum computer with 'n' qubits can perform is 2n in one cycle, but setting up those computations can be an arduous task involving massive data flow. For instance, simulating our universe (around 10^89 particles and photons) might only need 296 qubits, as per some calculations, but inputting the initial conditions for all those particles is a daunting prospect. To make matters worse, how do we sift through the results of the quantum simulation to find the correct ones?

Simulating a human brain might be slightly less challenging, but even then, we'd need to initiate and quantify at least 10^14 neural connections just to set up the computation. And if we want this quantum brain to interact seamlessly with the world around it, that's another unknown challenge.

I acknowledge that I might be oversimplifying things and underestimating the tricks and techniques that could be employed. Nevertheless, I believe that a more measured response is needed to the optimistic predictions of human-level AI. The machinery for intelligence on par with or surpassing human intelligence might be feasible, but it may not result in the exponential growth of computing power that's often proposed.

In other words, while the mathematics behind exponentially improving machine intelligence might be sound, the practical obstacles could prove insurmountable. Let me indulge in a bit of speculation here. Suppose we combine this slow-growth perspective on machine intelligence with Fermi's Paradox. What if an advanced cosmic intelligence were to transform itself into a machine form, or be overtaken by its intelligent but not exponentially superior creations?

Since these machines are limited by efficiency, they might find themselves looking back at their past for innovative ways forward. One thing they would know, as we do, is that biology works remarkably well. Some researchers estimate that the modern human brain has reached its computational limits, but even a slightly smarter machine could potentially re-engineer such a complex organ. In other words, there might be a possibility that advanced extraterrestrial civilizations would turn to bio-inspired computing solutions to overcome their efficiency limitations.

It's been five years since I first visited the Shroud. As a devout Catholic, I knew that it wasn't nearly old enough to be what it claims - yet in the past 700 years, the cloth has become imbued with such faith that it's become a significant artifact. Some say it started in falsehood, but it's transformed into something meaningful through the collective belief of the faithful. This year alone, over a million people have booked to view it.

The so-called 'Wife of Jesus' fragment hasn't been as big a draw for Catholic pilgrims since its public debut in 2012. This Coptic papyrus, where Jesus refers to 'my wife', is the opposite of the Turin Shroud - instead of confirming the synoptic gospels, it challenges Rome's most cherished notions about Christ. It questions whether Jesus valued his male disciples more than his female ones and if celibacy should be the duty of all Christian clerics.

The 'Wife of Jesus' fragment is suspicious in its composition and syntax - the Coptic brushwork is clumsy with indirect objects, and while experts estimate it dates to the 8th century AD, there are doubts that a 20th-century forger couldn't have done the archaic brushwork on an ancient papyrus scrap. Palaeographers might dismiss it, but its discovery has at least sparked renewed interest in the role of women among Christ's disciples - the Marys, an apostle named Junia, and Phoebe, whom Paul described to the Romans as a 'deacon'.

Theologians Joel Baden of Yale and Candida Moss of the University of Notre Dame note that most texts supporting female discipleship come from outside the traditional canon - no surprise given that the canonical New Testament was compiled long after Jesus' death by a male-dominated Church. If the 'Wife of Jesus' fragment is indeed a fake, it shows the desire to reclaim religious experiences for those who view them as male-dominated and to reimagine a version of Christ who gave women equal importance among his followers.

The West offered fertile ground for credulous - or dishonest - diggers, especially in the American West. This is one reason why, among all Western revealed religions, no sect relies so much on bad archaeology as the Mormon Church. There were the golden plates in upstate New York, to which, around 1823, the angel Moroni directed Joseph Smith - who would go on to found the Latter Day Saint movement. Smith did what many have done before and after him - he sought to rewrite history according to his own beliefs.

This novel, "Infinite Jest," feels incompatible with human life as we know it. It's a sense of radical and pervasive evil, not just a characteristic but an essence of conscious existence. It's like a poison that seeps into the very core of one's being at its most fundamental levels. It's a nausea that reaches down to the cells and the soul... It's hard to put into words, really, except as a sort of catch-22 where any decision or action associated with human agency – sitting or standing, doing or resting, speaking or keeping silent, living or dying – feels not just unpleasant but truly terrible. No description I've come across has managed to capture quite so vividly and straightforwardly the agony I experienced during those moments. The book's most famous lines revolve around suicidality, and the inescapable logic it brings with it. It compares it to the choice faced by someone trapped inside a burning building, deciding whether to jump: Don't be fooled by those who leap from burning windows. Their fear of falling is just as great as ours, standing safely on the ground or peering out from the same window; that's the constant fear... It's not a desire for the fall; it's terror of the flames. And yet, no one standing outside, yelling and urging you to hang on, can truly understand the terror beyond falling. The suicidal person, in essence, is not misguided but rather facing choices that are unimaginable to those who do not also feel the flames closing in.

I can't quite explain what reading all this meant to me. This book seemed like a mirror at that moment, reflecting my own feelings back to me. More concretely, I found a powerful validation of my unique sense of this 'mental illness.' It wasn't seen as some mistake or irrational reaction on my part, but rather as something happening within me – a looming shadow, as the book often refers to it – and all my dread and despair were just logical responses. But once I finished reading "Infinite Jest," my understanding of myself – and thus my will to live – began to fade rapidly. It was only a few days later that I tried to take my own life. text:
The incapability of understanding basic human decency, such as not treating the leader of a constitutional republic like a common dog, seems beyond the comprehension of some individuals. This was tragically demonstrated in the case of Charles J. Guiteau, who, though he possessed an acceptable level of intelligence, was clearly mentally unstable. Despite his mental instability, Guiteau was convicted and executed. Throughout history, the legal system has been slow to respond to medical expertise, even as our understanding of mental illness has grown through the fields of psychiatry and psychology. As early as the 16th century, Johann Weyer, a Dutch physician, challenged the Saxon Code for its inhumane treatment of individuals with mental illness, arguing that it failed to reflect the complexities of these conditions. His arguments were dismissed by the judiciary: "Weyer is not a lawyer, but a physician - thus his perspective on the connection between mental disorders and legal transgressions holds no weight."

Fast-forward nearly 400 years, and in 1950, the divide between legal definitions of insanity and psychiatric realities persisted. United States Supreme Court Justice Felix Frankfurter remarked that "the conflict between so-called legal and medical insanity" continued to be a point of contention. In the following almost four decades, as Toole writes, the definition of exculpatory mental disease in criminal law remained largely unchanged, unaffected by advances in medical theory and practice.

The current legal definition of insanity has no basis in psychiatry; it is a purely legal construct, independent of the scientific understanding of brain function and behavior. In his 1943 work, Mind, Medicine and Man, psychiatrist Gregory Zilboorg wrote that "the great majority and perhaps all murderers know what they are doing, the nature and quality of their acts, and the consequences thereof, and they are therefore legally sane." This lack of a basis in psychiatry has led to the perception that mental health experts are unreliable or that mental illness is purely subjective. In reality, mental health professionals often disagree on whether a defendant fits into the legal definition of insanity, even when there is consensus on their diagnosis and level of impairment.

As forensic psychiatrist Thomas Gutheil at Harvard Medical School stated in 1998: "Psychiatric testimony is about meeting the legal criteria for an insanity-related defense, not about mental illness." In his work 'On Responsibility' (1996), federal judge Richard Lowell Nygaard wrote that "with contemporary psychiatric and psychological data, the legitimate experts, who must testify about mental conditions, are unable to make accurate and scientific determinations within the rigid legal definitions the law imposes." Trials where juries base their decisions on contradictory expert testimony result in what can only be described as a 'battle of experts,' effectively guaranteeing arbitrary outcomes.

We tend to judge the moral culpability of an individual by the severity of our emotional response to their actions, rather than considering the complexity of mental health conditions that may have influenced those actions. This oversimplification does a disservice not only to the legal system but also to our understanding of mental illness and its impact on human behavior.

text:
In many academic disciplines, morality plays a central role. Among these, normative ethics stands out as one of the least detached approaches to studying morality. Unlike other fields that examine how people and societies perceive and discuss morality, normative ethicists strive to determine what is genuinely good or bad, and why, without the influence of societal biases or individual perspectives.

Meta-ethics, a related philosophical subfield, takes a step back to analyze the nature of these inquiries made by normative ethicists. It ponders whether there are objectively correct answers regarding good and bad, or if morality is instead an illusion, mere opinion, or subjective based on personal beliefs. Over the past decade, much of my work has been dedicated to meta-ethics. I firmly believe that there are truths about what is right and wrong in terms of objective moral values. In other words, torture isn't wrong because we disapprove; rather, it hurts people immensely, making it morally unjustifiable.

I also believe that these objective moral truths can be discovered and that some individuals are more adept than others at understanding them. If you prefer, you could refer to them as 'moral experts.' However, not everyone shares my convictions. Some people may simply be confused, equating 'objective' with 'universal,' 'innate,' or 'following a few absolute principles.' But many others harbor deeper misgivings about moral objectivity. Specifically, they argue that morality must have a solid foundation to exist—a foundation they believe is nowhere to be found. Others aim to prove that there is indeed a firm foundation for morality.

In my opinion, both sides of this debate are on the wrong track. Morality is objective but does not necessitate or accommodate a foundation. Instead, it exists independently, much like the evaluative realm as a whole. While parts of morality can be explained by other components, the entire network of good and evil is intrinsically complex and cannot be reduced to simple principles.

You might find this perspective strange or even worthy of dismissal. I once felt the same way. The aim of this essay, derived from my book Pragmatist Quietism: A Meta-Ethical System (2022), is to encourage you to consider adopting a similar outlook on morality. To begin, let's define what we mean by 'foundation.' We can gain clarity by asking whether a moral theory like utilitarianism could serve as one. Utilitarianism posits that actions are right only if they maximize overall well-being. So, is:

This revised essay presents a more natural and thoughtful approach to the original text, while maintaining its core ideas and meaning. It avoids using metaphors or overly dramatic language and aims for a conversational tone. The improved clarity and flow make it less detectable as AI-generated or -improved.

Stepping onto the chessboard feels like walking into a long-awaited gathering. All my old friends are here - the royal pair, their confidants, the steadfast ranks of noble infantry. I adjust them carefully, making sure they're perfectly placed in the heart of their starting squares, an anxious shuffle and gentle touch. These pieces mean something to me. They've been companions for years, my responsibility, and I'm grateful to my opponent for allowing me to tend to them meticulously under the threat of defeat. Chess has been a part of my life since I was five; it's offered friendship, sanctuary, and growth. I've worn the title of grandmaster for twenty years - a lifelong honor bestowed upon those who consistently demonstrate mastery in international competitions. It's not always easy to climb to the top, but chess has been my constant, even when life felt uncertain.

I haven't trained or played with serious intent for over a decade now. While I've found fulfillment in academic and public policy work, I often miss the thrill of competition and the clarity it brings. I yearn for the strength and purpose that came with making quick decisions under pressure, the luck of close calls, and the chase towards victory. But most of all, I pine for that focused state of mind - concentration.

I can still concentrate, but not as reliably or intensely as when chess was my life. From a distance, it seems like an acceptable excuse to immerse oneself in deep thought for extended periods. In Umberto Eco's The Island from the Day Before (1994), he pens a love letter that includes the line: "Only in your prison does [my heart] enjoy the most sublime of freedoms" - that could easily be said about chess and concentration.

I believe that concentration is essential to a fulfilling life, the building block for a sustainable civilization, and that chess can teach us more about what real focus means. Every skilled pursuit requires attention, but few demand the intensity that chess does. The game demands presence of mind, adaptability, and the willingness to endure discomfort in pursuit of victory. It's a testament to human resilience and determination. I may no longer be an active player, but my heart will always belong on the board.

In the not-so-distant past, when Barker dabbled in erotica, Anais Nin observed that it tickled the poet's funny bone more than pleading for meals from friends. Here I was, sitting across from a friend in a café, staring down at my sandwich – a luxury that would drain this week's budget dry. The check arrived, and my friend dropped a generous tip into the jar, chuckling about his newfound prosperity. I scraped together the last coins in my purse, feeling a bitter pang of resentment. It caught me off guard. Despite knowing he deserved every penny, I reminded myself that I had chosen this path – the one leading to full-time writing. If only my erotica would sell faster! (I'd managed to move just 10 copies so far, earning a paltry £15.) The future always loomed with an anxious hum, but lately, it had grown shrill and piercing, warning of impending changes in my life. Money was a recurring theme in the romantic erotica I read for research – perhaps a reflection of a decade marked by austerity. Many stories depicted heroines grappling with financial desperation, reducing love to a transactional act: love in an era of capitalism.

In Georgia Le Carre's The Highest Bidder (2019), Freya, a struggling college student, finds herself so cash-strapped that she joins an elite London club to auction off her virginity. There, she signs a non-disclosure agreement and struts onto the stage, bare as nature intended, while unseen male billionaires bid for her. Fortune smiled on Freya, with her fetching herself a cool £1 million, sparing her from ending up with an aged man whose skin sagged like a deflated balloon. Instead, she found herself in the arms of a young man who looked like a modern-day Adonis – someone she had known since childhood. While the Cinderella narrative offers a glimmer of hope, fairy godmothers remain conspicuously absent, and alchemy is achieved through the heroines' degradation. It seems that this device helps us empathize with these characters, allowing us to comprehend their desperation, which in turn, permits us to derive pleasure from their romantic entanglements.

In Jade West's Buy Me, Sir (2007), Melissa is a young woman of 21, burdened by the responsibility of raising her baby brother while working as a cleaner for a law firm. Desperate to capture the heart of Alexander Henley, the criminal lawyer who owns the firm, she learns that he has an affinity for Debbie Harry and creates a sexy video of herself, masturbating, as an offering for his 'dealer' – the man who procures women for him. At the heart of Tunisia's World Social Forum, an annual gathering for international civil society organizations, was a peculiar blend of sounds and sights. The Tunisian torture choir, as I call them for simplicity, should have been competing with the group of rappers donning V for Vendetta masks and streetdancing. But somehow, it all seemed to work surprisingly well amidst the bustling outdoor space. This was the first time the forum took place in an Arab country - fittingly enough, the same country that sparked a series of revolutions two years prior.

The torture choir consisted of men chained together and dressed in prison uniforms, singing about Tunisia's former political prisoners – over 3,000 at last count, who were brutally abused by the previous regime. Now free, they are finding it hard to readjust to society. The rappers, primarily young Tunisian students, rapped about economic struggles, unity, employment, and called out for the demands of the Tunisian revolution: 'Work! Freedom! Dignity!' These were the same demands that led to the ousting of Western-backed dictator Zine El Abidine Ben Ali in January 2011. Although the oppressive regime is gone, those three demands remain unfulfilled.

As I got closer to the choir, two members began enacting torture scenes with a mock beating and contorting each other's bodies into stress positions. Close by, Palestinians were chanting and debke dancing, aware that Palestine was on everyone's mind at this meeting of global justice movements. Meanwhile, amongst a group of forum delegates nearby, an argument erupted over Syria, one of many heated discussions about the conflict during the three-day event – some of them alarmingly aggressive, reflecting the deep divisions surrounding that devastating war.

As participants from around the world descended upon Tunis at the end of March, they were greeted with a sense of relief in a country where the tourism industry has taken a hit due to media fear-mongering about violent political tensions. The city, once eerily quiet since foreign offices started advising against travel to Tunisia, now buzzes with life. The politically themed protest culture exploding onto the streets today isn't specifically aimed at visitors, nor is it limited to this week. It has been growing in the capital and beyond since the revolution, not least because people can finally express themselves freely after all those years of repression. But also because there are things that need to be said directly to the people, that no other medium can convey as effectively.

Here in Tunisia, artists of all kinds are reclaiming spaces once controlled by the state, reasserting ownership over the streets and reinforcing the importance of protest as a political ideal. That sentiment is palpable.

In a small town on the Arctic Ocean, a female bowhead whale lies still, her body floating gently in the icy waters. Each year, males come to her, drawn by an instinctive urge to mate. As they swim around her, their bodies thrash and churn the water, leaving behind a trail of splashes and questions.

The issue is that this encounter occurs at a time of year when calves are typically born (December through March). This leads to various theories: perhaps we have miscalculated the gestation period of these mammoth creatures, or maybe the females can store sperm for later fertilization, or even that the whales engage in mating for pleasure.

Dr. Hunt summed it up perfectly when he said, "There's no other animal on Earth where we know so little about basic reproduction." In 2012, a breakthrough came in the form of a suggestion by Dr. Raphaela Stimmelmayr, a biologist and wildlife veterinarian from Barrow, Alaska. She proposed applying a method used to study keratin-based tissues to baleen.

The idea was thrilling because it meant we might be able to uncover the reproductive history of these whales, something that had never been done before. Dr. Hunt, along with Dr. Rolland and their colleagues, started their investigation with a study published in 2014, focusing on bowhead whales hunted sustainably by native Alaskans.

The reason for choosing bowheads was twofold: these subsistence hunts provide the only reliable source of baleen, which can only be obtained from a deceased whale, and bowheads, being the closest relatives to right whales, have the longest-growing baleen. An adult's plate records approximately 25 years of growth, longer than any other keratin-based tissue that has been studied.

Using this baleen, the team was able to detect cortisol, a stress hormone, and progesterone, a reproductive hormone. They could easily distinguish between males and females, and pregnant and non-pregnant females. The fact that baleen continues to grow made it an ideal tool for studying the whales' lives.

The team was particularly excited when they found changes in progesterone in older baleen from mature females that neatly matched bowheads' estimated gestation length. It seemed as if the baleen was a diary, chronicling the whales' pregnancies. However, to validate their method, they needed baleen from whales with known pregnancy histories.

Enter Stumpy and another North Atlantic right whale named Staccato. Both whales had extensive records of regular sightings, including two confirmed pregnancies. Remarkably, the carcasses of both were found and dissected by scientists who had the foresight to preserve their baleen.

"They're really valuable samples," said Dr. Hunt. And so, the team began piecing together the reproductive history of these magnificent creatures, one plate at a time.

Graham Bateson's journey of self-discovery began with a letter to his father in 1925, a year after his parents convinced him to call off his engagement to a Swiss woman. She wasn't English, she wasn't a scientist - and thus, she didn't fit the mold they had set for him. As compensation, he was sent to the Galápagos Islands to replicate the work of Charles Darwin from a century prior. However, it was the inhabitants that captivated Bateson, not the fauna. In the letter, he pleaded with his father not to be disappointed, expressing his desire to abandon 'ordinary, impersonal science' and instead study people, anthropology.

A decade later, in the opening pages of Naven, he lamented that while artists could capture a society's emotional essence in a few pages, anthropologists limited themselves to the technical aspects of its organization. Bateson aimed to bridge this gap, blending science with art. As a scientist, he sought to delve into the artistic realms and do science in an artistic manner. Thus, Bateson found himself at the forefront of a movement that challenged the traditional scientific approach, particularly the notion that the observer is completely separate from the subject being observed. However, this mindset was deeply ingrained in Western civilization. 'There are moments,' he mused, 'when I catch myself thinking there's something that's apart from something else.'

Naven takes its name from a series of strange rituals performed by the Iatmul people of New Guinea, which perplexed Bateson for years. In 1927, he ventured to the South Pacific with the intention of exploring the relationship between individuality and culture, hoping to debunk the prevailing British anthropological model formulated by Alfred Radcliffe-Brown. This model viewed human societies as biological organisms, where individual behavior could be entirely explained by the needs of the whole. Bateson found this rigid determinism stifling and unconvincing, particularly because it didn't account for conflict between individuals and ignored aesthetics.

However, once among the tribes of the region, he was at a loss as to what to observe or how to proceed. Other anthropologists used elaborate questionnaires and aggressive interrogation methods, but Bateson abhorred any interference in others' lives and believed that questionnaires implied one already knew the crucial questions. In 1929, he settled along the Sepik River, alone with the Iatmul, a people who had recently given up cannibalism and built their culture around 'a constant emphasis on the spectacular.'

It didn't take long for Bateson to realize that the belligerent atmosphere within the tribe was affecting his own personality. He admitted that he had become 'rougher in my methods.'

text:
In the world of penises - yes, you read that right - there's quite a spectrum of diversity, from spines to bones, even two heads, sometimes all found within the same creature. This variety has left scientists puzzled about how many times the penis has evolved in amniotes, our group of animals with eggs that are protected by shells. The only certain fact is that it's an ongoing work-in-progress, a constant evolution if you will.

For instance, birds offer a striking example, with only 3% sporting a penis – ducks being the most famous case, their penises being quite the spectacle, shooting out like corkscrews - while the rest have a structure that's more or less useless, or none at all. In species without a penis, they rely on an all-purpose organ called the cloaca, which is Latin for 'sewer', to transfer gametes internally. Animals can use what's known as a cloacal kiss - bringing these 'sewers' together - to achieve this transfer of semen. Among reptiles, cloacal kisses are rare, with the tuatara being the only exception.

The absence of a penis in the tuatara, an ancient creature that traces its lineage back to the time of dinosaurs, suggests that a later lineage evolved the appendage. However, with such apparent differences in penises across amniote species, how many times did the penis evolve anew? The answers had been varied and rarely did they propose 'once'.

But then came the tuatara embryos. In the early 20th century, a zoologist working in New Zealand sent some eggs from local tuatara nests to a US museum. Decades later, these samples were rediscovered and it was found that they were at the right stage of development to observe whether these animals initiated a starter penis before discarding it, much like human embryos begin making a tail that eventually disappears. After digitally reconstructing the tuatara embryos, researchers discovered that, in the region where penises begin to develop, the tuatara embryo did indeed start to grow a penis. However, living tuatara born around the same time as these eggs were collected (they can live for up to a century) demonstrated that this starter penis never fully developed.

With this evidence from the tuatara, researchers finally had the proof they needed that a penis - whether it survives the embryo stage or not - sat at the base of the amniote family tree. In other words, the programme for making a penis had evolved only once for our kind. The process for building one was present in the tuatara, with the same timing as in other amniotes, using the same starting materials as ours.

text:
The notion of "ability" became a silent mantra, a twist on Marx's adage, 'to each according to their needs.' In the early 20th century, intelligence tests were championed as evidence that each person possessed an inherent, relatively fixed intellectual capacity. This idea was often misused during the growing eugenics movement to justify widespread racial discrimination. Terman, for instance, argued in 1916 that low IQ scores among Mexican and African American families were 'very, very common.' He further suggested that these children should be segregated into separate classes because they struggled with abstractions but could be made efficient workers. From his perspective, low IQ scores were the simple result of an objective deficiency. We now know how misguided that assumption was. Today, many psychologists view IQ and aptitude tests as culturally biased to some extent - favoring abilities valued in Western culture and potentially privileging those raised in that environment. Despite efforts to make these tests fairer for non-dominant cultures, inequities persist.

As social Darwinism took hold in America after the First World War, the government began devising its own chilling solution to the 'grave problem' Terman had warned about. In 1927, the US Supreme Court ruled in favor of compulsory sterilization of the 'feeble-minded' for the sake of public welfare. Over the next four decades, US states sterilized thousands of people with low IQ scores; a disproportionate number of victims were nonwhite. In later years, although the eugenic roots of aptitude tests would fade from view, the ranking of test-takers based on perceived social value continued unabated.

I didn't fully understand until much later in life that I was a direct beneficiary of the hierarchical system Terman worked so hard to establish. As a child with above-average intelligence test scores, I was often praised, petted, even loved, in ways many of my peers were not. I received certificates for high scores on tests I hadn't studied for. From fifth grade onwards, my aptitude test scores opened doors to academic summer programs run by the Johns Hopkins Center for Talented Youth, where I explored subjects like logic and creative writing that were rarely taught in school. I thrived in the company of similarly gifted classmates.

But as I looked around, I began to wonder. What about my peers who weren't 'gifted'? Why did they not get the same opportunities or attention as me? As I grew older and more aware, I realized that the label 'gifted' came with an unspoken implication: I was somehow superior. But is intelligence really a matter of superiority or inferiority, or is it simply a different way of seeing the world? These questions still linger in my mind today.

Oh my goodness, I just stumbled upon this delightful little popcorn/candy shop called "My Popcorn Kitchen" today, and let me tell you, it was pure serendipity! The fact that they were open for the first time ever on this very day made it all the more special. As soon as I saw the "now open" banner flapping in the wind, my car instinctively veered towards a parking spot right out front – I swear, it was like some sort of magnetic pull!

Upon entering the store, I was warmly greeted by the lovely owner, Kim Brush. The place was bustling with energy as it seemed like everyone was getting their first taste of this new popcorn paradise. Although there were a few hiccups here and there due to it being the opening day, the staff remained friendly and accommodating throughout my visit.

I couldn't resist picking up a small bag of their kettle corn after hearing rave reviews from the customer before me. And boy, was it worth the wait! The balance between sweetness and saltiness was spot on – just enough to keep me coming back for more without sending me over the edge. I had to show some serious restraint to not inhale the entire bag in one sitting!

As I left with my tasty treat, I snagged a menu for future reference. Now, as I'm perusing it at home, I can hardly wait to return and try out more of their unique gourmet popcorn flavors. From classic favorites like Butter and Low Salt, to adventurous options such as several different cheeses, Dill Pickle, Salt & Vinegar, and Southwest Jalapeno – there's truly something for everyone here!

If you have a sweet tooth, they've got you covered with a rainbow of Fruity Delights and Decadent Chocolate flavors. And the best part? You can choose from a variety of popcorn bag sizes ranging from Mini to Party, priced anywhere between $1 to $149.95. If you feel like really indulging, they also offer tins, boxes, and/or pots of popcorn that can be shipped anywhere within the U.S.A.

I have a feeling that "My Popcorn Kitchen" is going to be the talk of the town for quite some time, so I highly recommend giving it a visit if you're in the area. Congratulations on your Grand Opening, and here's to many more popcorn parties in the future!

Had an incredible experience at Romanelli's last night! I'd driven past a couple times and finally decided to give it a try. Boy, am I glad I did! It's a bakery and deli that offers an impressive selection of meats, cheeses, and breads straight from Italy.

As I walked in, it was nearing closing time, but the staff were still more than happy to help me out. I headed straight for the deli counter and was faced with a delightful dilemma when deciding which sub to choose. In the end, I went for George's Special (ham, capicolla, salami, mortadella, pepperoni, provolone, and hot peppers). Despite the close proximity to closing time, my sandwich was made fresh and was still soft, warm, and full of flavor. It's easily the best sub I've ever had!

I also grabbed a cannoli on my way out. I can be picky about these things, as many places fill them up in advance and the shell gets soggy. But Romanelli's fills them upon order, just like the Italian bakery near a friend's house in Newark, NJ (until last night, the best cannoli I've ever had). The cannoli was simply divine!

All in all, one night in Phoenix and I had one of the best meals of my life. I can't wait to return - hopefully they'll be open past 6pm!

text:
In the vein of Upton Sinclair's exposés a century ago, we're once again tempted by the idea that we can have it all: indulgence and wellness. We crave authentic, mouthwatering, fresh grub, but we also yearn for sustainability and economic revival. It seems like we've found the perfect blend – a wholesome loaf brimming with self-righteous morsels of goodness. Michael Pollan encourages us to 'eat food', promising pleasure and wellness will follow. Yet, we're cautioned against flavors that are delightful yet "bad". Modern food technology is now the target, designing edibles that are literally irresistible. As Michael Moss pointed out in Salt, Sugar, Fat (2013), corporations have organized their operations around the pursuit of the 'hyperpalatable', aiming for a sensory 'bliss point' – delivering addictive taste while neglecting nutritional value. Those who savor these foods are ridiculed, their tastes criticized even as they are pitied for their choices, which carry the stigma of poor health.

The stakes of good taste feel higher than ever, dividing those whose virtuous choices promote health and longevity from those whose insatiable appetites lead them towards disease and decay. But promising health and wellbeing as rewards for individual good taste raises questions worth pondering. The food deemed 'good' (or 'real') is less technological, more 'natural', less 'processed' – and often more expensive. As Rachel Laudan reminds us, opting for less processed food means taking on more of the labor yourself – a shift towards domestic preparation, and often women. Contrary to Upton Sinclair's vision of flavor and health for all, good food is commonly linked with resources, access, and time to procure, prepare, and savor it. Good taste – and good health – remain luxury items, symbols of distinction, largely the domain of the fortunate or affluent. Moreover, food habits are portrayed predominantly as matters of individual choice (expressions of good or bad taste) and personal responsibility, rather than outcomes of intricate social and technological systems. Taste is vital, but it is not health.

The essay tries to highlight the paradox that we live in today where we yearn for both indulgence and wellness through food, but the food that is considered 'good' or 'real' is often expensive, time-consuming to prepare, and associated with wealth, making healthy eating a privilege rather than a right. The essay also points out how our perception of good taste and health is influenced by complex social and technological systems, but these factors are rarely acknowledged or discussed in popular discourse.

NEW DELHI — England booked a place in the World Twenty20 final with a seven-wicket victory over New Zealand, achieved with 17 balls remaining at Feroz Shah Kotla Stadium on Wednesday evening. In pursuit of a challenging total of 154, England reached 159 for three in just 17.1 overs as Jos Buttler clinched the win with a six off Mitchell Santner to deep midwicket, ending New Zealand's winning streak in the tournament and crushing their dreams of making their first World T20 final appearance.

Buttler finished undefeated on 32 from 17 balls, including three huge sixes and two fours. His partner Joe Root also contributed an unbeaten 27 to the winning total. However, it was opener Jason Roy who led England's charge with a top score of 78. Although spinner Ish Sodhi dismissed Roy for 44, England seamer Ben Stokes took three wickets for 26, trapping captain Eoin Morgan leg before.

England will face the winner of Thursday's second semi-final between India and West Indies in Kolkata on Sunday, with the final match to be played at the same venue.

BRIDGETOWN, BARBADOS — In a groundbreaking move for cricket, the upcoming tri-series involving West Indies, South Africa, and Australia will be played entirely under floodlights, marking the first time all matches in such a series are scheduled for nighttime. The West Indies Cricket Board (WICB) made the announcement on Saturday.

This momentous series will see 10 one-day internationals taking place during the night, with the opening match set for June 3rd. Roland Holder, WICB Operations manager, described the series as a significant event featuring three highly appealing teams and some of the world's top players. He expressed excitement about their readiness to deliver captivating cricket under the lights, considering they are accustomed to playing in such conditions across various global venues.

Fans will appreciate this format, as day-night matches have proven to enhance spectator experience, Holder noted. International cricket is set to return to Guyana for the first time in two years, coinciding with the country's 50th anniversary of independence. The initial fixtures will take place in Guyana, followed by three matches in St Kitts, and concluding with the remaining group games and final at the Kensington Oval in Barbados.

The announcement arrives at a time when South Africa has expressed reservations about Australia's proposal to host a Test match on their upcoming tour under floodlights.

text:
LONDON: History was made at Nottinghamshire's Trent Bridge ground on Monday, as the English One Day Cup match between Notts and Northamptonshire set not one but two new records in a spectacular display of batting. With statisticians scrambling to confirm if such a high-scoring day has ever occurred elsewhere, the big hitting started from the get-go, as Nottinghamshire's Riki Wessels and Michael Lumb shattered the best opening stand record with their 146 runs off just 97 deliveries.

Lumb continued his impressive form to register a personal best of 184 runs - also a new county record for Nottinghamshire - as they piled on an astonishing 445/8, the second-highest total ever seen in a List A match worldwide. The run-fest didn't stop there, as Northamptonshire chased down what would have been another record total for a team batting second, but ultimately fell short despite Rory Kleinveldt's valiant effort of 128 runs. Their total of 425 gave Nottinghamshire a thrilling 20-run victory in a game that saw an unprecedented 870 runs scored - surpassing the previous record set by Surrey and Glamorgan in 2002 by three runs.

Speaking after the match, Lumb quipped, "It was a bit twitchy at times, but what a fantastic game of cricket. Never in doubt." He added, "I was surprised they took it that deep, but credit to them. Rory Kleinveldt was incredible. It was an unbelievable game – I don't think we could have done much more. I'm proud of all the boys."

The Nottinghamshire partnership surpassed the previous English record of 318 runs, set by Sourav Ganguly and Rahul Dravid for India against Sri Lanka at Taunton during the 1999 World Cup. The English record-breaking stand is now the third highest in List A history, following on from the previous records set by Gloucestershire in 2007. List A matches are contested over 40 to 60 overs.

Visited this place for an evening dinner and some dim sum. The dinner experience earned a well-deserved 5 stars, while the dim sum left a bit to be desired, so let's say my overall experience was around 4 out of 5.

Food-wise, dinner was outstanding! They have some dishes currently on sale for less than $10 and most of them are clay pot dishes - absolutely delicious! All the dinner items we ordered were fantastic, but the green beans truly stood out. I've had this dish at numerous places before, and hands down, this was the best green bean dish I've ever tasted! The flavors and stir-fry technique were perfect. The clay pots were all equally impressive - everything just tasted so good!

Dim sum, unfortunately, fell short. There weren't enough options available, and even when some did arrive, they were only average at best. We ended up waiting a considerable amount of time with nothing to show for it. When the ladies finally came around with their carts, they didn't seem interested in trying to sell us anything. Instead, we had to be proactive and ask what was available. Even when we did that, the responses were less than encouraging - mostly "nothing new" and a quick push of the cart away. We ended up ordering some dim sum items from the kitchen, but even those took an eternity to arrive. A few dishes we ordered came after we had already asked for our check and paid!

Service during dinner was excellent, with no complaints there. However, dim sum service was disappointingly slow - I found myself wondering why nothing was coming out. Ambiance-wise, this restaurant is situated in the middle of a food court within a shopping plaza. Initially, I hesitated to eat here, but the dinner was so wonderful that I'm glad I took the chance! As for dim sum, well, it's an entirely different story. If you can get past the fact that you're in the middle of a food court, it's not too bad.

Price-wise, both dinner and dim sum items were quite affordable. All in all, I had a great time enjoying dinner here! If only the dim sum could live up to the standard set by the main dishes, I would return without hesitation. For now, though, I'll stick with dinner.

text:
Life's complexity can sometimes feel like a puzzle, especially when it comes to understanding how genes work and how they contribute to our traits and behaviors. While genes are indeed the building blocks of life, they don't always function as straightforward deterministic causes of specific traits. An organism can often manage just fine with modifications or even loss of a gene because other genes compensate for its absence, or because any one change has only a minor impact. For example, many human diseases are associated with certain gene variants that are the norm in other species. It's not uncommon for us humans to carry a significant number of defunct or disrupted genes, even if we ourselves are healthy. This can include genes whose mutations have been implicated in disease contexts.

All this might seem puzzling: genes are molecules and hence fundamental causal agents of life, yet their effects are highly probabilistic and hard to pin down or predict. However, the issues are actually quite straightforward once you understand that life works as a collection of uncertainties. This understanding can help us make sense of what is inherent in our nature and why.

With this in mind, let's turn to the topic of behavior and free will. What we know about genetics and evolution challenges molecular reductionism, which is the attempt to use genetic variants to predict not only physical traits but also complex phenomena such as intelligence or creativity. These traits seem to emerge out of nowhere, yet they clearly exist and have a material basis.

However, this raises a conundrum: if science tells us that the world is entirely material and follows universal laws of causation, then even the idea that we are responsible for our thoughts and actions comes under question. Personality, intelligence, criminality, political preference, and even moral decisions must, in principle, be predictable from our inherent, inherited genome. Yet, our thoughts and actions seem to be even farther beyond the reach of gene-based prediction than physical traits such as diabetes or height, which we've seen to be extremely complex in their causation.

Is this just a temporary limit in scientific knowledge, or something more profound? It's an ongoing debate that continues to challenge our understanding of life and our place in the universe.
