Memory isn't always black and white, it's more like a blurred snapshot from a distance. We might clearly remember the event itself and who was involved, but the peripheral details can sometimes be hazy. This is because our mind is often juggling multiple tasks under high-stress situations, which can affect how we perceive and recall events.

The identity of the person responsible for such an incident becomes engraved in our memory due to our survival instincts. We need to remember them to avoid them in the future. The environments where we share our story and the people we discuss it with can influence the way we recount the event. In certain situations, we might even intentionally mislead others for self-protection reasons - for instance, if we fear that sharing the truth could put us or someone else in danger.

In my personal experience, as an adult, it took me some time to pinpoint the exact timeline of the incidents I experienced. I had to go through old photos, recall connected events at school, and gather the courage to ask questions from my mother.

The need for consistency also pushes victims to create a straightforward narrative that's easy to remember. Susan Brison, a professor of philosophy at Dartmouth College in New Hampshire, writes about her harrowing experience of being raped and left for dead while out on a walk in France. When she finally testified in court, she felt a sense of relief because she no longer had to keep the story straight. Any minor discrepancy could have been used against her. She lived in constant fear of forgetting or misremembering details, especially during the trial when the perpetrator was present. After her testimony, Brison could finally think freely about the attack - what she remembered, how it affected her, and how she made sense of it all. For months before that, her brain had been stuck on repeat, struggling to move forward.

Brison argues that this pressure to maintain a perfectly consistent account can hinder the healing process. Of course, when someone's future and freedom are at stake, there needs to be a way to verify credibility and ensure a fair trial. However, in cases like the Kavanaugh hearing, accusers aren't just expected to testify with a consistent narrative and maintain it during cross-examination. They're also held accountable for statements made on text or email, social media, or to colleagues, therapists, friends, and family. Any differences across these domains will likely be used to question their credibility, stall the investigation, and (hopefully for the defense) exonerate the accused.

Resources are interconnected through competition; what one individual consumes can mean less for another. A pesticide or antibiotic attack wipes out all but those most resistant, favoring resistance over susceptibility. The survivors then flourish in numbers. New strategies are being developed to maintain both susceptible and resistant individuals within networks of cancer cells, pathogens, or agricultural pests. Integrated pest management and adaptive chemotherapy, for instance, aim to kill only some sensitive pests or cancer cells, leaving the rest to keep the resistant ones in check. These new control methods take into account the local interactions that govern these networks of pests or cells.

In social circles, the most popular girl is the most connected – all friendships seem to pass through her. Networks vary in shape based on the arrangement of links. The traditional hierarchical network, for example, follows a chain of command from the factory owner down to the workers. Evolution has produced phylogenetic tree-shaped networks, tracing ancestors and descendants from unicellular organisms to the vast bacterial tree or the branch of vertebrates that includes us mammals.

The tree and the fully connected network represent extremes in the spectrum of possible network shapes. Human social life can generate complex network shapes, as any girl who's been through middle school knows. Two girls are linked by public recognition as friends, with popular girls serving as hubs for many connections, some of whom have links to others. Loners or outcasts may not be connected at all. In between the hubs and the outsiders, there are those who are linked to someone within the inner circle but also those who are overlooked by the chosen ones.

A girl's position in this network can be described by how well-connected she is – her 'betweenness centrality.' It represents the number of connections that pass through her. The most popular girl is the most connected, with all bonds of friendship eventually passing through her. In my middle school, understanding betweenness centrality was so intricate and crucial that one girl kept a notebook to track daily changes in the links. By mapping out who was friends with whom, it became clear how friendships shifted over time.

In the chilling spring of 1764, the citizens of Milan bore witness to an appalling act - the execution of Bartolomeo Luisetti. Accused of sodomy, he was subjected to asphyxiation and then burned at the stake, all in full public view. Across Europe, those in power believed that justice must not only be served, but also seen to be served; that punishment must be harsh, instilling fear in the masses. Cesare Beccaria, a young man of just 26 years, was present, his heart heavy with disgust and disbelief. It seemed unthinkable that such barbarity could pass as reason.

Born in 1738 to a prominent Milanese aristocrat, Beccaria's early life was marked by the strict teachings of the Jesuits at a school in Parma. His studies in law at Pavia only furthered his disillusionment with society. But it was through Montesquieu’s Lettres persanes, an epistolary novel offering a critical perspective on Parisian customs, that Beccaria's eyes were truly opened. He embraced the ideas of the Enlightenment without hesitation, finding inspiration in French and British intellectuals such as Claude Adrien Helvétius, Denis Diderot, David Hume, and John Locke.

In 1759, Beccaria joined a group of young Milanese aristocrats who rebelled against the oppressive local elite, including their own families. They saw the interests of the few as consistently outweighing those of the many, and the laws as tools for strengthening the privileged class. Their collaborative efforts led to the creation of a reform-focused magazine called Il Caffé. In 1762, Rousseau's The Social Contract was published, providing Beccaria with an ideological foundation for his beliefs. Two years later, in 1764, Beccaria penned his treatise On Crimes and Punishments, which would be published 25 years before the French Revolution.

Beccaria's manifesto against cruel punishment quickly spread throughout Europe, sparking reforms of oppressive institutions across the continent. At that time, Europe was a deeply hierarchical society, where a select few held unaccountable power over the masses. Enlightenment thinkers saw inequality as the root of social injustice, and Beccaria sought to radically transform his society through reforms of its institutions and laws. This ambition was shared by all Enlightenment thinkers, though the methods for achieving this transformation varied. France chose revolution, while Milan pursued a more gradual approach, with Beccaria and his peers playing significant roles in shaping the city-state's administration.

European radical thinkers recognized the Church as one of the strongest enforcers of inequality and subjugation. The ideas of Niccolò Machiavelli continued to inspire Italian political minds, emphasizing the role of Christianity in perpetuating such issues.

Reintegration is a complex issue when it comes to former rebels in Uganda, particularly those associated with the Lord's Resistance Army (LRA). They were often seen as both perpetrators of terrible violence and victims themselves, many having been abducted as children. For civilians, reintegrating these individuals meant transforming 'animal-like' former rebels into 'human beings.' The process was described as 'taming,' where they were taught to abandon their old ways in the wilderness (Lum) and learn hygiene, social norms, and other skills. This process was referred to as 'repairing their minds.'

The brutalized, traumatized individuals who were once part of the LRA had crossed the line of human behavior and needed to be re-educated, disciplined or counseled to regain humanity. However, these former rebels didn't always see themselves in the same light as civilians did. Yes, they killed with machetes and beat people to death with logs, but they didn't always have access to guns or drones like state armies. Yes, they were kidnapped and forced into fighting against their will, but some found the spiritual and political aspects of the rebel cause appealing. They often regretted being returned home to towns and villages, where they encountered alcoholism, unemployment, and infidelity. In the Lum, they saw themselves as gorillas engaged in 'gorilla warfare.' Identifying with animals wasn't a fall from grace but rather a sense of elevation to a higher status of power and ferocity.

Many found the process of reintegration confusing. They viewed their time in the army as a period of living a meaningful and active life surrounded by close friendships and family. They considered each other as brothers and sisters, united in a spirit of togetherness and mutual support, as if they were blood relatives. Some even claimed that their relationships in the Lum were stronger and more supportive than those they had with civilians after leaving the army. Why were they treated like violent, dirty beasts who needed to learn humanity? Some former rebels retorted, "We too are human beings." They didn't want to be seen as brutal killers or traumatized victims. Yet, their claims to humanity seemed awkward. If they identified as animals, did that not put them in the realm of the divine and protected by holy spirits? Their claims to humanity aimed to avoid being treated as outcasts. But humanity could not recognize the truth of their experiences, beliefs, and ways of being, which often transcended human realms, entering into divine and animal domains.

The struggles of these former rebels were multifaceted, reflecting the complexities of identity, violence, and reintegration in post-conflict societies.

Title: Ireland Takes Encouragement from Stalemate Against Classy Sweden

Ireland can draw some optimism from their 1-1 draw against a strong Swedish side, who failed to register a single shot on target despite the efforts of talisman Zlatan Ibrahimovic. The Swede was kept relatively quiet by Ireland's defense in a performance that hinted at growing potential for O'Neill's team.

"We have two incredibly challenging matches ahead against two highly skilled teams," said O'Neill post-match. "However, if I can take anything from today's game, it was the impressive play of our players. They demonstrated their determination and are developing well within the international arena."

The atmosphere at the Stade de France was electric, with both teams backed by passionate fan bases. The vibrant yellow and green supporters brought a festive spirit to the stadium in stark contrast to the tournament's recent violent incidents. Ireland showed an impressive desire to win, making two attacking substitutions after Sweden equalized, while Sweden seemed sluggish and lacking bite until Ireland took a well-deserved lead three minutes into the second half.

Ireland's goal came from a brilliant half-chance by Wes Hoolahan, which caught out Ibrahimovic, the 36-year-old captain. Ibrahimovic, who left Paris St Germain at the end of last season after helping them win two straight domestic trebles, looked far from royal on Monday. In the first half, he struggled to find his footing on a pitch where he had previously won two French Cup and two League Cup trophies. Although he provided the cross that led to Ireland's own goal in the 71st minute, Ibrahimovic missed a chance to score an almost certain winner in the closing stages when Martin Olsson's cross narrowly eluded him.

Despite the late miss, Ireland can take heart from their performance. Manager O'Neill praised his team's resilience and determination, while Sweden manager Janne Andersson expressed disappointment with his side's first-half display. "Ireland were the better team for the first 50 minutes," he admitted. "Credit to Ireland. Our attack was poor in the first half. They managed to shut us down effectively."

Reflecting on his own performance, Ibrahimovic acknowledged that he could have done better, saying, "I tried, but there's always room for improvement."

LONDON: Pakistan's Mohammad Amir registered his first Test wicket in six years when he dismissed England captain Alastair Cook at Lord's on Friday. This was Amir's return to Test cricket, marking his comeback at the very venue where he served a spot-fixing ban and spent time in jail for deliberately bowling no balls during the 2010 Lord's Test against England.

Amir endured the frustration of seeing fellow left-arm wicketkeeper Sarfraz Ahmed drop two routine chances when Cook was on 22 and 55 respectively. However, with Cook nearing a century on the second day of the first Test of the four-match series, Amir's dismissal of Cook proved to be his first wicket in a Test match since he dismissed England's Graeme Swann during the 2010 fixing scandal.

Amir's initial deliveries lacked precision but as the sunny skies over Lord's gave way to swinging conditions, Amir became more effective. The 24-year-old pacer screamed in frustration before sharing a wry smile with Pakistan captain Misbah-ul-Haq, who offered Amir a consoling pat on the back. When Cook was out, there was no mistaking Amir's trademark arms-outstretched airplane celebration.

Earlier in the day, Amir was booed by a section of the capacity crowd as he walked out to bat. He inside-edged his first ball, from Stuart Broad, for four. The next delivery struck him on the helmet as he ducked into a Broad delivery. Yet, Amir also hit two well-struck boundaries before he was dismissed for 12, caught in the slips off Broad.

Upon arrival, I was greeted by a decent selection of around 5 burgers, ranging from the classic burger to more unique variations like green chile or bacon-focused ones. I opted for the basic burger with cheese, which cost $8 and came with a side of fries. The price jumped to around $10 for some of the other variations, and drinks were an additional charge.

The menu mentioned their burgers were char-grilled, but I couldn't really detect any char or smoky flavor in mine. I suspect they might be steamed instead. Despite this, I appreciate the focus on using local products for the regular menu, although I have my doubts about whether the burger patties themselves are sourced locally or from a national vendor.

The service was quick and friendly, which was nice. The fries were decent enough, though nothing out of the ordinary (I'm guessing they come from a frozen bag as well). The bun was average, and my burger came with pickles, onions, and lettuce. However, it wasn't clear to me if they make their own patties or if they're sourced elsewhere.

All in all, I paid around $12 after tip for the burger, fries, and a Coke. Unfortunately, there was no "wow moment" for me. If they could come up with something more unique, it might be worth the price. As it stands, I'd definitely recommend Harlow's for other dishes, but their burgers aren't quite their strong suit. text:
I often find myself feeling down or on edge, and it's hard not to acknowledge those feelings. After all, we have direct access to our own minds, don't we? This is a view that has been widely accepted in philosophy for much of the 20th century, known as the 'privileged access' perspective on self-knowledge. René Descartes even used introspection to arrive at his famous conclusion: "I think, therefore I am." He recognized the ease with which we can perceive our own minds, stating, "There is nothing more easily or clearly perceived by me than my own mind."

However, there are those who argue that self-knowledge isn't as immediate as it seems. We might infer our feelings based on certain signs – like a racing heart and heavier breathing potentially indicating anxiety about an upcoming presentation. But what if we're wrong? Perhaps I'm just excited instead. This kind of psychological reframing is something sports coaches often use to help athletes stay calm under pressure.

The philosopher who most famously proposed this inferential view is Gilbert Ryle, who suggested in The Concept of Mind (1949) that we gain self-knowledge by applying the same tools we use to understand others to ourselves: "The things I can discover about myself are the same as those I can discover about others, and the methods of discovery are much the same." Ryle's idea is encapsulated in a popular New Yorker cartoon where a husband says to his wife, "How should I know what I'm thinking? I'm not a mind reader."

Many philosophers have dismissed the strong inferential view as too extreme, writing it off before it could fully develop. However, if we look at history, the idea that we have some sort of special, direct access to our minds is actually the exception, rather than the rule. For ancient Greeks, self-knowledge was a journey, something to be pursued and worked towards, as captured by the saying "Know thyself," inscribed on the Temple of Delphi. This implies that most of us don't know ourselves very well.

This view persisted into medieval religious traditions. The Italian priest and philosopher Saint Thomas Aquinas suggested that self-knowledge is a process, not an all-encompassing truth. The ancient Greeks and medieval philosophers remind us that understanding ourselves requires effort and introspection – it's not something we automatically possess.

text:
In the late 18th century, a small town named Jena became a beacon for free thinkers and rebels. One professor mused, 'complete freedom to think, to teach and to write.' Compared to other places, censorship was more lenient here, and the range of topics that could be explored was vast. As Jena's most renowned resident, Friedrich Schiller, put it, 'The professors in Jena are almost entirely independent.' Intellectuals, writers, poets, and philosophers who had run afoul of authorities in their native lands were drawn to Jena by its openness and freedoms. Schiller himself had fled the Duchy of Württemberg after being arrested for his revolutionary play The Robbers (1781). On a good day, you might have seen more renowned writers, poets, and philosophers strolling through Jena's streets than in a larger city over an entire century.

Among the notable figures in Jena were the tall, gaunt Schiller, who wrote only with a drawer full of rotten apples on his desk; the obstinate philosopher Fichte, who centered himself in his work; and the young scientist Alexander von Humboldt, who predicted human-induced climate change for the first time. The brilliant Schlegel brothers, Friedrich and August Wilhelm, both writers and critics with pens as sharp as French guillotines, called Jena their home. So did the young philosopher Friedrich Schelling, who redefined the relationship between the individual and nature; G W F Hegel, who would become one of the most influential philosophers in Western history; and Caroline Michaelis-Böhmer-Schlegel-Schelling, a free-spirited woman who carried the names of her father and three husbands but lived life on her own terms.

The young poet Novalis, a former student from nearby Weißenfels, often visited his friends in Jena. During the winter months, you might have spotted Germany's most celebrated poet, Johann Wolfgang von Goethe, skating on the river, his belly held together by a flowery waistcoat. Though older and more famous, Goethe took on a protective role towards the younger generation. He was inspired, even rejuvenated, by their new ideas, while they revered him. These great minds attracted students from all over Germany and Europe to Jena. Starstruck at the sight of so many famous poets and philosophers seated together at concerts in Zur Rose tavern, they couldn't believe their eyes when it seemed like all of Germany's greatest minds gathered in one room at a party. Each of these intellects lived an extraordinary life, but the fact that they all converged in Jena during the same time is even more remarkable. That's why I've called them the 'Jena Set' in my book Magnificent Rebels (2022). In an age when most of Europe was still shackled by absolutism, the Jena Set was united by their passion for the free self.

Title: Terrible Experience at Pure Fitness - Avoid Adam at All Costs!

I've always had a good experience with Pure Fitness as a whole. No contracts, and it seems like everyone there is genuinely focused on working out instead of just being seen, which is refreshing compared to places like LA Fitness. Plus, you even get complimentary training sessions! However, my experience at the specific location I visited was absolutely horrendous, all because of their new General Manager, Adam.

I had a free pass and was assigned to him for my fitness test and initial training session. Keep in mind that I had already expressed my intentions to join but Adam just wouldn't stop trying to sell me on the gym, which I found extremely off-putting. I wanted to use my weeks free pass first before deciding to join, but he kept pushing for money right then and there without even giving me a tour!

I eventually told him I wanted to wait, and that's when things got weird. He started asking me questions about my financial situation and if it was an issue of money, which made me incredibly uncomfortable. I went ahead with the training session because I enjoyed the workout, but looking back, he pushed me way too hard for a first-timer. My arms weren't even sore - I know what sore feels like, and I'm okay with that kind of discomfort. But they were weak for five straight days, which made it difficult to sleep, gave me an irritable feeling, and even hurt when I needed to use a date stamp at work!

The final straw was when I decided to sign up online because it seemed so easy. When I called Adam to let him know, he exploded on me - short of yelling! He went on for about 5 minutes about how I was ruining his commission and the gym was losing points because of me. I was stunned by his unprofessional behavior, especially considering he's supposed to be in charge.

To add insult to injury, this Pure Fitness location is the smallest, least visually appealing, and has the worst customer service I've ever encountered at any gym. I ended up signing up with another location and love it, but I swear I will never set foot in that place again. Just be warned - avoid Adam at all costs!

PHILADELPHIA - Uruguay's Copa America Centenario journey came to an abrupt end on Thursday, as they were eliminated in the group phase for the first time since 1997 following a surprising 1-0 defeat against Jamaica. The loss, which occurred at the Pasadena Rose Bowl in front of an audience of 83,263 spectators, left the 2011 champions grappling with the sting of an early exit.

Venezuela and Mexico, meanwhile, advanced to the quarterfinals as Copa America champions, capitalizing on Uruguay's misfortune. The elimination was particularly bitter for Barcelona superstar Luis Suarez, who has yet to see any game time due to a hamstring injury sustained prior to joining the squad. Visibly frustrated and unable to take the field, Suarez could be seen punching the dugout wall in apparent frustration.

The decisive goal came on the 36th minute, when midfielder Alejandro Guerra capitalized on an opportunity created by Uruguay's goalkeeper Fernando Muslera stepping off his line. Unable to fully anticipate and parry Guerra's long-range effort, Muslera could only deflect the ball onto the crossbar before Salomon Rondon bundled it in for the opening score.

Despite their poor start in South America's World Cup qualifiers, where they currently sit at the bottom of the table having lost all three matches to date, Venezuela proved too strong for Caribbean minnows Jamaica. The contest saw a contentious penalty call against Jamaica in the second half, after striker Clayton Donaldson appeared to have been fouled inside the area. The drama unfolded in the 60th minute when Donaldson rounded his marker before being brought down by Yasser Corona. Replays suggested that the defender did not make contact with the ball, but the referee awarded the penalty.

Jamaica's misery was compounded in the 81st minute, as Mexico's Hirving Lozano set up Hector Herrera within the area. The midfielder deftly passed to substitute Oribe Peralta, who calmly finished first-time past the right-hand post for the final nail in Jamaica's coffin.

text:
Title: Herodotus' Comic Lament on Ancient Geography: The Circumambient Ocean

The iconography of the ancient Ocean was more than just vases and tile floors; it also included maps, where the ocean appeared in its physical form. Although the original drawings have long vanished, we have the historian Herodotus to thank for a detailed account of how these maps were crafted. Interestingly, his account elicited a rare moment of humor from this usually stoic scholar. "I chuckle," he wrote, disparagingly, "at the many who draw maps of the world in such an illogical manner; they make the Earth rounder than one could draw with a compass, and place Ocean all around the edges; and they make Europe and Asia equal in size."

The map design Herodotus describes can be visualized as follows: Why was this amusing to him? After months of research and analysis for what would eventually become my first published scholarly article, I too pondered over his laughter. The map holds good Homeric authority, as Ocean was depicted on the edge of Achilles' shield in Book 18 of the Iliad - a shield that, in essence, embodies an early image of our world. Was Herodotus being so dismissive of such a venerable mythical tradition, so deeply rooted in Greek literature?

One possible explanation for his amusement, it seems to me, lies in the satirical phrase "rounder than one could draw with a compass." There's an air of perfection in this geometry that Herodotus finds incongruous. Real-world geography, he suggests, does not form such flawless circles. While Achilles' shield may have been circular, Ocean's placement around the edge holds no cartographic significance.

A second source of humor for Herodotus stems from his concern with the veracity of information. No one, he asserted in a separate discussion, has ever observed the waters of Ocean lapping at the shores of northern Europe or eastern Asia. Thus, these regions might very well be connected to other landmasses, making the Atlantic and Pacific—while known by the former name, the latter was yet unnamed —disconnected bodies of water. In another passage, when discussing the river Nile, Herodotus goes so far as to call Ocean a downright fabrication: "I doubt that any River Ocean truly exists, and I believe that Homer or some other early poet invented the name and introduced it into his poetry."

This bold assertion could well be the first ever attempt in Western literature to question established geographical beliefs.

text:
It's important to consider the approach we should take when addressing the issue of lengthy sentences for Black individuals in our criminal justice system. Whether we lean towards 'levelling down' by reducing the sentences or 'levelling up' is a complex question, one that involves understanding if life sentences are ever truly just and appropriate. Those advocating for 'levelling up' can't assume their stance is the correct one, given that the very issue at hand is under debate. Here's one reason why 'levelling down' might be more fitting.

The US criminal justice system tends to treat Black and brown individuals less favorably than whites. It seems reasonable to suggest that the treatment of white individuals aligns more closely with our society's ideals of justice, as they are often perceived as the 'norm'. People of color, on the other hand, seem to be subjected to an unfair 'surplus penalty', while whites may not be getting off too easily. If this is true, then reducing penalties for Black individuals rather than increasing those for whites would move us closer to justice.

It's difficult to believe that someone should spend their entire life in prison without a solid reason. The only valid reasons would be if they pose an ongoing threat to public safety or if their actions deserve such a severe punishment. I've already argued that the majority of the over 200,000 individuals currently serving life sentences in the US do not pose a lifelong threat to public safety. Whether they should spend the rest of their lives behind bars depends solely on whether they deserve it. Deserving such a punishment would require them to be fully responsible for their criminal actions.

Responsibility is best understood as what it excludes - an individual who is not responsible has a partial or complete excuse. A complete excuse makes one wholly non-culpable, and this has required, since at least the 19th century, that one did not know what they were doing, did not know it was wrong, or could not control their actions due to an irresistible impulse. Under these definitions, most individuals serving life sentences probably are responsible for their actions. Growing up in environments of deprivation increases the likelihood that a person will commit crimes, but a broader understanding of responsibility is necessary.

For one thing, the insanity defense is a complete excuse that makes a person legally innocent (although they may still be institutionalized in a psychiatric facility). However, there are many more individuals whose responsibility for their actions might be reduced, even though it's not entirely absent. The Death Penalty Information Center reported in its 2021 report that all but one of the eleven people executed in 2021 had significant impairments, including evidence of mental illness, brain injury or damage, or an IQ within the intellectually disabled range, as well as chronic childhood trauma, neglect, and abuse. It's safe to assume that those sentenced to life also have extremely high rates of such impairments. Many have experienced abuse, neglect, and trauma.

text:
Growing up with a mixed lineage, my sisters and I were blessed with the privilege of multiple identities. A diverse genetic pool coursed through our veins, and we felt entitled to claim ownership over any piece of that family history that resonated with us. Boz, however, had no known family tree. His father worked at Trusham Quarry, his grandfathers were both laborers on the farm, and delving deeper into the past only brought us to the foggy tales of our ancestors: a great-grandmother who bred hens that folks from distant counties came to buy; a great-grandfather who knocked out a gypsy boxer at the Okehampton fair. Boz took for granted his lineage, believing they were all laborers who worked the land for meager wages, stretching back through time to peasants and serfs, seemingly unchanging. To him, this was an inevitable and honorable way of life.

I found myself drawn to Boz, despite our stark differences. He was unlike my own father, and I accepted his appreciation for manual labor on the land and around animals at face value. That is until one day, while driving through that region, I spotted a large Union Jack flag waving by the house of the pig farm where Boz worked. As I passed, I could see the ominous row of low buildings – these weren't fields for the pigs to roam but rather, a factory.

Boz's father was a bully, known to dish out physical abuse not just to his sons (Boz being the eldest of three) but also to his wife when he drank excessively, which happened more frequently as time went by. I remember Boz visiting my house occasionally, seething with anger that he rarely spoke of. I grew to recognize that particular mood – it always stemmed from his father and Boz didn't wish to discuss it.

In those days, women seldom left abusive marriages – the only exception being my own mother, who had done so less for escape than for excitement. Children were powerless in the face of a domineering husband. I learned bits and pieces about Boz's father from others. He had reportedly gotten into a dispute with a neighbor. Instead of involving the council or police, he opted to settle matters through fisticuffs. However, the neighbor did call the authorities, leading to Boz's father's arrest and eventual suspended sentence.

When we first moved to this village, my father visited house after house to introduce himself. Boz's father refused to see him – his mother had to explain that a previous rector had 'upset' him as a boy, leaving a lasting resentment.

text:
At the sprawling outdoor venue of Tunisia's World Social Forum, an international gathering of civil society organizations, an unusual mix of sounds filled the air. The Tunisian torture choir, as I'll call them for convenience, were there alongside a group of rap artists and street dancers, masked in Guy Fawkes attire. Despite the contrast, it all somehow seemed to fit harmoniously within the vibrant atmosphere of this historic event - the forum's first time being held in an Arab country, no less, and two years after Tunisia sparked a wave of revolutions across the region.

The choir consisted of men chained together, dressed in prison uniforms, singing about the thousands of political prisoners from Tunisia's previous regime who endured horrific abuse behind bars. Now free, they find themselves lost and without support as they struggle to reintegrate into society. The rappers, mostly Tunisian students, were voicing their opinions on economic issues, unity, employment, and the revolution's demands: work, freedom, dignity - the same demands that led to the downfall of dictator Zine El Abidine Ben Ali in January 2011. Though the oppressive regime may be gone, those three demands are still unfulfilled.

As I approached the choir, I could see two members enacting scenes of torture, one using a stick to simulate beating and contorting the other's body into stress positions. Close by, a group of Palestinians were chanting and dancing traditional Debke dances, aware that Palestine was on many minds at this meeting of global justice movements. Amidst them, an argument over Syria had broken out among a cluster of forum delegates - one of many heated debates about the conflict that took place during the forum's three days. Some were reportedly aggressive, a stark reminder of the deep divisions surrounding the devastating war.

As participants from around the world converged on Tunis at the end of March, they were met with a renewed sense of optimism in a country where tourism has taken a hit due to media-fueled fear over political turmoil. The capital's streets, once eerily quiet following travel advisories, are buzzing again with life. The politically charged protest culture now dominating the streets is not only for the benefit of visitors but has been growing steadily since the revolution. It's a testament to the newfound freedom of expression in Tunisia and a reassertion of the power and importance of protest as a political tool.

Artists across various mediums are reclaiming public spaces once controlled by the government, reinvigorating the streets with their voices. It's not just about speaking out; it's about asserting that change starts here, in the heart of the people.

text:
Human interactions can be quite intricate, not just social but also political. From playgrounds to the halls of power, people are constantly forming, adjusting, breaking, and complicating relationships. Consistent signals, like speech patterns or clothing styles, provide a sense of orientation in the chaotic social landscape. However, race might not always hold the weight its significance as a societal issue suggests, according to Cosmides, Tooby, and Kurzban's experiments from 2001. People do tend to categorize each other based on race automatically. Yet, it's possible to diminish race as a significant category in a matter of minutes by using neutral colors as markers. When people are told that individuals wearing grey or yellow shirts belong to two groups, they rely more heavily on the shirts than the ethnic appearance of the wearers to determine their group affiliation. Other researchers have reported that accent can overshadow race, with young children preferring friends who share the same accent, regardless of race.

This phenomenon might resonate with people living in Britain, where the Union flag obscured visible ethnic differences among Team GB athletes during last summer's Olympics, and young Londoners, regardless of their heritage, share a unique accent characteristic of their remarkably diverse city. Although Britons may be hazy about what it means to be British, the majority no longer view it as racially defined. Simultaneously, there is a widespread distrust towards Muslims and an inclination to perceive immigrants as unwelcome. The British in-group has undergone a transformation, redefining both itself and its 'others.' Britain has become a fairer nation, more inclusive than ever before – a development unthinkable just a generation or two ago. However, this reinvention of alliances inevitably opens the door for potential new biases and injustices. Perhaps we must accept that our relationship with 'them' is an ongoing process, always evolving and never truly complete.

text:
On November 16th, 2016, investment guru and philanthropist Tom Marsico, one of the wealthiest men in the U.S., saw his wealth grow a bit more as Christie's in New York auctioned off Claude Monet's Grainstack for $81.4 million. This record-breaking price for a Monet painting was over six times what Marsico had paid in 2002, demonstrating that few investments can match the returns of a good old Monet. Performance after performance by Monet at auction houses in London and New York has become commonplace. Yet, it's hard to imagine a starker contrast to Monet's financial struggles during his lifetime than what we see today. The tales of critical rejection faced by the Impressionist movement, and the resulting financial woes of its painters, are part of art history lore. While Monet did experience some early hardships – being rejected for the annual Paris Salon in 1868 and having to survive on potatoes that winter – he wasn't always a starving artist.

'We don't need any more of that kind of painting,' a juror sniffed in 1868, rejecting Monet's works for the Paris Salon. This rejection likely affected his sales. Yet, for much of his career, things were looking up for Monet. His diet, far from potatoes, often included delicacies such as foie gras from Alsace, truffles from Périgord, and lobster in peppercorn sauce. One visitor raved about the exquisite cuisine at Monet's house. After turning 50 (he lived to be 86), Monet was always flush with cash: a fortunate outcome for such a bon vivant. His wealth afforded him a fleet of cars, tailored tweed suits from Paris, and a team of gardeners to maintain his famous gardens. In this regard, he bears more resemblance to contemporary artists like Damien Hirst or Jeff Koons, with their high-profile dealers, deep-pocketed collectors, and multi-million dollar fortunes, than he does to artistic outliers such as Vincent van Gogh, who struggled financially. Monet's surname is almost a play on words with 'money,' a fact that headline writers and punsters appreciate. In truth, Monet was the Jazz Age's precursor to Koons because, in 1922, four years before his death, a sale of one of his canvases to a Japanese tycoon made him the world's most expensive living artist.

Monet has long been the preferred artist for the rich and famous. Besides Marsico, recent owners have included David Rockefeller, the Queen Mother, Imelda Marcos, Prince Rainier, Steve Wynn, and various Russian and Asian billionaires. His paintings serve as statements of both aesthetic taste and financial prowess, dazzling consumer goods that also offer lucrative returns. Much like they functioned during Monet's lifetime, his canvases today are more than just artwork – they are investments.

text:
Isaiah Berlin, a renowned thinker and writer, held an almost unparalleled status as an intellectual and moralist in the Western world during the latter half of the 20th century. He passed away on November 5, 1997. Soon after his demise, Henry Hardy, his editor and friend, described him as "one of the most remarkable and delightful individuals I've ever known, not just wise, but also insightful in judgment, and genuinely good."

Berlin's seminal essay on liberty - much like many of his essays which started as lectures - begins by distinguishing two concepts of political freedom. The first, referred to as 'negative liberty', is defined by the degree to which I am free from the intrusion of others or authorities. To be negative free, according to Berlin, doesn't mean being completely liberated from physical or psychological constraints; it merely indicates the extent of freedom from human interference or coercion, such as the freedom to refrain from participating in politics. In stark contrast is 'positive liberty', which is the freedom to act, rather than the freedom from something: for instance, the freedom to run for parliament.

At first glance, one might think that we're presented with an oversimplified distinction between two sides of the same coin. Berlin doesn't disagree, acknowledging that these concepts seem to be logically close. But what sets his account apart is his realistic and historical treatment of each concept, demonstrating not only a substantial and practical difference between negative and positive liberty but also showing how failing to recognize its significance can lead to far more damage than mere intellectual confusion.

The essence of Berlin's argument lies in flipping the traditional understanding: he makes theory respond to reality instead of the other way around. This bold move allows history and real-world experiences to take center stage in making sense of things. And what history and our current experience reveal is that negative and positive liberty cater to genuine and legitimate human needs and ideals.

Negative freedom has been the cornerstone of political liberalism, emphasizing the importance of individuals being left alone to their own devices, so long as their actions do not excessively harm others. In a similar vein, positive liberty has been at the heart of emancipatory theories of politics, from democratic and republican doctrines to those of nationalism and communism.

Berlin underscores that one of the major lessons of modern history and contemporary reality is how dangerously vulnerable the concept of positive liberty can be.

text:
The rugged, veined rocks of Bohuslän in western Sweden hold one of Europe's highest concentrations of Bronze Age art. During a recent October visit, I stumbled upon these intriguing carvings hidden off the side of a road, which once marked the shoreline of the North Sea - a distant memory from 2,500 to 4,000 years ago when these figures were first etched. The petroglyphs have since been painted pillar-box red for tourists, giving them an appearance reminiscent of a giant child's doodle. While they lacked refinement, they were brimming with energy. Hunters and warriors pursued stags amidst longships, blending seamlessly into a procession of bulls and towering giants precariously balanced on slender legs. Land, sea, human, and animal seemed to merge and intertwine. The Bronze Age rock art in Bohuslän, Sweden, is believed to depict the performance of a ritual. I found myself trying to piece together a story, imagining it as an ancient cartoon strip unfolding before my eyes. We may never know why these inscriptions were made or what they intended to convey. The blunt-limbed figures offer no insight into where such a tale might begin or end. Yet, perhaps the tangled quality of these petroglyphs is their true message - they speak of a culture deeply rooted in the sea and nature.

Similar sites on the Cornish and Iberian peninsulas suggest that cultural influences traversed what the Beowulf poet later called 'the whale roads' - the open waters connecting Bronze Age Scandinavia to the rest of Europe for trade and conflict. Today, we find ourselves in a similarly interconnected age, where our reliance on fossil fuels, nitrogen fertilisers, cobalt in smartphones, antibiotics, and plastic binds us to distant times and places. The world of the Anthropocene is woven from this intricate web of relationships, each one leaving an imprint on the future. For two centuries, humans have walked upon the same earth as the Bohuslän petroglyphs, yet they remain vibrant and powerful, echoes of a living presence. In their silence, they pose questions that resonate today: How do we communicate across the currents of time and matter with the deep future? Are our intentional signs and symbols the ones that leave the most lasting impact, or are our unintentional traces the ones that endure?

I reside in Edinburgh, perched on the edge of the North Sea. I've come to see these waters as a kind of Anthropocene laboratory, offering a glimpse into the long-term consequences of how we live today. Its past can help us understand our future. As Pangea was breaking apart 200 million years ago,...

text:
Hey there! So, yes, I'm a professor at Yale University, but let me tell you - I'm no genius. When I first mentioned to my four grown kids that I was going to teach a new course on genius, they thought it was the funniest thing ever. 'You? You're no genius! You're a plodder.' And they were right. So here I am, some dozen years later, still teaching a successful course on genius at Yale, and I even wrote an Amazon Book of the Year selection, The Hidden Habits of Genius (2020). How did that happen? Well, I guess I had the 'boldness of ignorance' that Nikola Tesla talked about.

I started my professional life trying to be a concert pianist, back in the days of the Cold War. The United States was then trying to beat the Soviet Union at every game. In 1958, Van Cliburn, a 23-year-old pianist from Texas, won the inaugural International Tchaikovsky Competition, which was like the Olympics of classical music. And then in 1972, Brooklyn's Bobby Fischer defeated Boris Spassky in chess. Because I had shown an interest in music and was also tall with huge hands, they thought I would be the next Cliburn. Although our family wasn't wealthy, my parents managed to provide me with a Baldwin grand piano and find the best teachers in our hometown of Washington, DC. Soon enough, I was sent off to the prestigious Eastman School of Music, where every opportunity was presented to me. And I had a strong work ethic - by the age of 21, I had clocked in around 15,000 hours of focused practice (Mozart only needed 6,000 to get to master-composer and performer level).

But within two years, I could see that I would never earn a dime as a concert pianist. I had everything going for me except one thing: I lacked musical talent. No special memory for music, no exceptional hand-eye coordination, no absolute pitch - all things very necessary to a professional performer. 'If you can't compose, you perform; and if you can't perform, you teach' - that's the mantra of conservatoires like the Eastman School of Music. But who wants to spend each day in the same studio teaching other likely soon-to-fail pianists? My intuition was to find a larger arena in a university. So off I went to Harvard to learn to become a college professor and researcher of music history - a musicologist, as it's called. Eventually, I found employment at Yale as a classroom instructor teaching the 'three Bs:' Bach, Beethoven, and Brahms.

But the most captivating composer I ran into there was an M: Mozart. My interest in him grew with the release of the Academy Award-winning film Amadeus (1984). For a while, it seemed like the entire world was obsessed with this funny, passionate guy.

MANILA — Gold prices edged higher today, reaching a three-month high, as increased volatility in other assets benefitted the precious metal and could lead to further gains this year, with global central banks potentially forced to ease monetary policy further to spur economic growth.

With interest rates already near zero, analysts anticipate that central banks may move either towards zero or negative rates, as Japan and certain European countries have done in an attempt to force banks to lend more. At the time of writing, spot gold stood at $1,126.55 per ounce, a level last seen on November 3. Global manufacturing expansion remained weak at the start of 2016, with a slight acceleration offset by a contraction in emerging economies. In China, a gauge of factory activity dropped to its lowest level since mid-2012.

Federal Reserve Vice Chairman Stanley Fischer warned that if recent volatility in financial markets persists and signals a slowdown in the global economy, it could negatively impact the U.S. economy. Gold is often viewed as an asset of choice during uncertain times, and posted its best monthly jump in a year in January, with gains of over 6 percent so far this year, after falling 10.4 percent last year.

Meir expects only one U.S. interest rate hike this year, given weaker macroeconomic numbers out of the U.S., which could suggest that the Fed's December move was an outlier, forced onto a central bank that had promised to do so for much of the past year.

text:
The leadership of Jawaharlal Nehru embodied a dedication to some Pan-Asian principles that resonated strongly at the Bandung Conference of 1955. This gathering of 29 Asian and African nations, representing more than half the world's population, was the last significant manifestation of Asian unity, eventually overshadowed by Cold War tensions and nation-building projects. Pan-Islamism has ebbed and flowed throughout the past century, from Turkey and Egypt to Indonesia and Algeria. The concept of Muslim intellectualism and global solidarity among Muslims empowered many 20th-century nationalist leaders and movements. By the mid-1960s, most of the world's Muslims had shaken off European colonial rule. The Ottoman Caliphate was abolished by the Turkish Parliament in 1924, and by the 1950s, it was a largely forgotten relic. However, as we approach nearly two decades into the 21st century, Pan-Africanism and Pan-Asianism seem to have faded away while Pan-Islamism and the ideal of Muslim world unity persist. Why is this?

The answer lies in the latter stages of the Cold War. It was during the 1980s that a new Muslim internationalism emerged, as part of a growing political Islam. This wasn't a conflict between the ancient civilizational traditions of Islam and the West, nor a reassertion of original religious values. Instead, it was a new formation born from the Cold War. A Saudi-US alliance began promoting the idea of Muslim solidarity in the 1970s as an alternative to the secular Pan-Arabism of Egypt's Gamal Abdel Nasser, who allied with the Soviet Union. Any ideas of an 'Islamic' utopia would have faltered without the failures of many post-colonial nation-states and the subsequent disillusionment of many Muslims.

The notion that Pan-Islamism represents authentic, ancient, suppressed Muslim political values in rebellion against global Westernization and secularization was initially a paranoid fear of Western colonial officers, but it has more recently come mainly from Islamists. Western pundits and journalists have made mistakes by accepting Islamist claims about Islam's essential political values at face value. The kind of Islamism associated with Egypt's Muslim Brotherhood or Ruhollah Khomeini's Iran did not exist before the 1970s. None of the Indian Muslims who met with Wilson, nor the late Ottoman-era caliphs, were interested in enforcing Sharia law in their societies. None of them aimed to veil women. On the contrary, the first generation of Pan-Islamists was highly modernist: they advocated for women's liberation, racial equality, and cosmopolitanism. Indian Muslims, for example, were proud that the Ottoman Caliph had Greek and Armenian ministers and ambassadors. They also wanted to see Hindu and Muslim ministers and high-level officials appointed in their governments. None would have wished for or anticipated the separation of Turks and Greeks in Ottoman lands, Arabs and Jews in Palestine, or Muslims and Hindus in India. Only the rudimentary form of early 20th-century Pan-Islamism remains.

"Grandpa, tell me 'bout the good old days,
Sometimes it feels like this world's gone crazy.
Grandpa, take me back to yesterday,
Where the line between right and wrong didn't seem so hazy."

In this song, nostalgia serves as a coping mechanism for dealing with the moral ambiguity of the present - but 'yesterday', instead of referring to her own past memories, now refers to the distant past of her grandpa's generation. She relies on him to provide clarity and assurance.

Research on the effects of golden age thinking is still in its early stages, with most studies focusing on nationalist nostalgia. A 2014 study found that participants who were primed with collective personal nostalgia - 'recall a past event experienced with fellow countrymen' - were more likely to sacrifice their own money to punish transgressions against in-group members. Another study led by Dutch psychologist Anouk Smeekes, published as 'Longing for the country’s good old days', discovered a significant correlation between collective historical nostalgia for 'the way it was' in the Netherlands and opposition to the rights of Muslim immigrants. A follow-up study suggested that national nostalgia fueled out-group prejudice by reinforcing a sense of ethnic national identity.

"You see this nostalgic discourse in Western Europe, or with the rise of Donald Trump," says Smeekes, explaining her inspiration for the research. "They say: 'There were glory days in our national past, when everyone was safe, and then all the changes came, one of which is immigration, and if you vote for me I will return us to the glorious past and give back the country to its original people.'"

This new research reveals how collective historical nostalgia can transform selflessness into in-group selfishness - a phenomenon described by theologian Reinhold Niebuhr in his 1932 classic Moral Man and Immoral Society. Individuals are naturally self-interested, argues Niebuhr, but they also possess the ability to 'consider interests other than their own'. In contrast, groups consolidate the egoism of their members at the expense of out-group empathy.

"The frustrations of the average man, who can never realize the power and glory which his imagination sets as the ideal, makes him the more willing tool and victim of the imperial ambitions of his group," writes Niebuhr. "His frustrated individual ambitions gain a measure of satisfaction in the power and aggrandisement of his nation."

In essence, collective nostalgia can lead to a dangerous distortion of reality, where individuals sacrifice their empathy for others to fuel their own sense of identity and belonging within their group.

It's crucial to maintain a balanced perspective when discussing the plight of Latinx children detained at borders. While they share some basic humanity with their white, middle-class American counterparts, we must not oversimplify or equate their experiences. The children in cages are extraordinary beings who have braved treacherous journeys to flee from the harsh realities of capitalism and empire that have shaped them. They've endured unimaginable hardships, and many of their peers didn't survive the journey.

It's important to acknowledge these differences rather than trying to force a false sense of sameness upon them. Material comforts like a bar of soap or a flu shot don't undo the injustices they've faced. Similarly, asserting their essential similarity to privileged youth growing up within the heart of global empire is misleading.

The abstract concept of humanity is meant to help us empathize with people in vastly different circumstances, but it can also lead us astray by making us believe we are more alike than we really are. This misunderstanding can do more harm than good. After all, the idea of universal humanity has often been used to justify oppression rather than liberation for non-Western communities.

Perhaps it's time to reconsider the term 'humanity.' Instead, let's call for specific justice and recognition for those who have been marginalized by historical and contemporary structures of imperialism, racism, colonialism, and capitalism. We should strive for a more precise language that reflects our genuine desire for emancipation and liberation, rather than relying on a term that has been historically tainted with oppression.

Exhibiting artworks requires specific guidelines and considerations to preserve their integrity, aesthetics, and interactive elements. Let's discuss three main categories of rules that shape how artists' visions are presented in museums.

First, the display rules dictate what exactly should be exhibited - whether it's a unique piece created by the artist, a type of object designated by them, or a new arrangement specifically designed for the exhibition. For instance, Félix González-Torres' 'Untitled' (Portrait of Ross in LA) (1991) is displayed by providing a pile of appropriate hard candies on the gallery floor, allowing visitors to take some. On the other hand, El Anatsui's Drifting Continents (2009), a large wall-hung sculpture made of liquor bottle tops and copper wire, requires the museum to acquire the fabricated panels and give installers flexibility in how they fold, drape, and orient them for optimal presentation.

Second, conservation rules focus on preserving essential elements of a work to maintain its material integrity. These rules are crucial in determining how the components will appear to future viewers and identifying which parts contribute to the artwork's ongoing existence. Takashi Murakami's paintings, whose paint is designed to flake away over time, embrace change within the work as an integral part of its development rather than treating it as damage.

Lastly, participation rules outline audience engagement with the artworks. For example, Jill Sigman's Hut Project (2009-) invites visitors not only to appreciate the visual aesthetics but also to engage in various activities such as contributing small notes or drawings. Similarly, Paul Ramírez Jonas's The Commons (2011) features a riderless horse sculpture with a cork surface pierced with thumbtacks, encouraging viewers to contribute items or messages, transforming the artwork into a collective communication platform.

Ultimately, these rules guide both the museum staff and visitors in experiencing and appreciating artworks as intended by their creators, fostering a deeper understanding and connection with the works on display.

'Untitled' (Portrait of Ross in LA) (1991) by Félix González-Torres. Courtesy the Art Institute of Chicago/Wikipedia
The Hut Project (2009-) by Jill Sigman
The Commons (2011) by Paul Ramírez Jonas. Materials: cork, pushpins, notes

I'd like to discuss a peculiar perspective I refer to as 'pro-extinctionist views', which has surprisingly garnered acceptance among several philosophers. Before we delve deeper, it's essential to clarify this stance. Proponents of this view would agree that the prospect of humanity's catastrophic end is appalling and unfortunate, and they believe efforts should be made to prevent such an outcome. No rational person would wish for billions to perish. However, pro-extinctionists propose that the state of Extinct – ceasing to exist – would be preferable to the alternative, Extant – continuing to exist. Why? There are numerous reasons put forth. One argument is that by no longer existing, we could prevent future human suffering from occurring, which might be less detrimental or more beneficial than our current state.

The idea can be approached in various ways. The first angle revolves around the possibility of an abundance of future human suffering. According to cosmologist Carl Sagan, who supported a further-loss view in his writings, if humanity persists on Earth for another 10 million years, there could potentially be over 500 trillion people. This number skyrockets when considering space colonization – philosopher Nick Bostrom estimated that as many as 1 followed by 23 zeros biological humans per century within the Virgo Supercluster in 2003, although the figure rises to 1038 if we factor in digital people living in virtual-reality computer simulations. Further calculations in Bostrom's book Superintelligence (2014) suggested that over 1058 digital people could exist across the universe as a whole. These estimates were driven by the further-loss view, which values the nonexistence of all these future people and the value they might have created as a significant opportunity cost of going extinct.

However, one could argue the opposite: even if these future people lead lives that are ultimately worthwhile, the suffering they will inevitably endure during their lives could still amount to an immense, absolute toll. Yet, if we were to go extinct, none of this suffering would occur. The second consideration is based on the premise – which I find persuasive – that there are certain forms of suffering that no amount of happiness can offset. Consider a hypothetical scenario where you must undergo a painful surgical procedure that leaves you bedridden for a month. You opt for the surgery, recover, and go on to live another 50 joyful years. The surgery and recovery process might have been excruciating – something you wouldn't wish to relive – but you might still contend that it was 'worth it'. In other words, the decades of happiness you enjoyed post-surgery counterbalanced the pain you experienced. But now ponder some of the most horrific events in our world: child abuse, genocides, ethnic cleansings, massacres; pain that no amount of happiness could ever compensate for. If we were to go extinct, none of this suffering would take place.

In essence, pro-extinctionists argue that the potential for alleviating future human suffering and the possibility of eliminating certain uncompensatable forms of suffering are reasons enough to embrace the prospect of going extinct. This is a complex and controversial argument, but one worth considering as we reflect on our role in this world and the future of humanity.

Gaining a clearer perspective on labor inequalities, specifically the unpaid work often performed by women, can provide an unique epistemic vantage point that's usually unavailable to many men. This idea is central to feminist epistemology – the understanding that our personal experiences significantly shape how we perceive and understand the world, as well as how our perspectives are received. Miranda Fricker's work has highlighted how stereotypes, social hierarchies, and traditional gender roles impact the acquisition, dissemination, and credibility of knowledge, coining the term 'epistemic injustice' for forms of injustice often overlooked before.

Fricker also drew attention to two specific types of epistemic injustice: 'testimonial injustice,' where one's testimony is not considered credible because they belong to a group subject to prejudice (such as women, LGBTQ+ individuals, or people of color); and 'hermeneutical injustice,' where lack of interpretive resources make it difficult to understand one's own experiences due to dominant assumptions and meanings. For instance, the concept of sexual harassment has helped women make sense of uncomfortable experiences that were previously dismissed as mere flirting by others.

Similarly, Kate Manne in 'Down Girl' (2019) shed light on a systemic bias in public life and politics, misogyny – defined not as individual hostility towards women but as a social mechanism for controlling and punishing women who challenge patriarchal norms. This helps explain many experiences that women encounter daily. Both philosophers have drawn our attention to sources of harm that were previously unseen or overlooked. They also introduced concepts that enable us to see things differently, because there are aspects of reality we might not perceive without the appropriate conceptual tools.

These works build upon the feminist tradition, such as Simone de Beauvoir's 'The Second Sex' (1949) and philosophers like Nussbaum, who have argued that freedom and rights mean little if our circumstances prevent us from acting upon them. The idea that legal barriers may fall but actual equality might still not be achieved has been a recurring theme in feminist thought since at least the 17th century – for example, in the work of writers like Mary Astell, Mary Wollstonecraft, and Harriet Taylor.

We must also remember that non-white thinkers, such as Sojourner Truth, Anna Julia Cooper, Audre Lorde, and W E B Du Bois, who belong to groups more marginalized than most white women, have long been arguing that their knowledge is not recognized or given sufficient credit. In essence, they've challenged the myth of a 'view from nowhere.' These thinkers remind us that epistemic injustice is not a new issue – it has been a concern for centuries. However, understanding and addressing it can help bring about much-needed change in our society.

In the heart of Athenian democracy lies the tale of Socrates' trial, a story deeply rooted in the citizen's role and responsibility within their state. The actions taken by Socrates, Meletus, and the jury must be viewed through the lens of the Athenians' emphasis on civic duty, their belief in the responsible citizen in a democratic society. It is more about active participation than contemporary debates over recognition. Maximus of Tyre, writing in the late Roman era, described Socrates as maintaining a dignified silence in response to Meletus' charges. Yet, dissidents who question political systems have often chosen silent protest or refusal to participate in court proceedings. However, Socrates accepted the authority of the court. Despite this acceptance, he defended himself in an unconventional manner, participating as a citizen should – to answer for any actions that may harm the community.

Socrates' defense was not about challenging the trial's legitimacy; instead, it was about proving his innocence and demonstrating his contributions to Athenian society. He saw himself as a civic benefactor deserving of honor, such as free meals at the state hearth. Socrates' commitments as a citizen are clearly depicted in Plato's dialogues, providing a rich portrait of Athenian political culture.

In Plato's dialogue, "Crito," we encounter a unique scenario – a 'dialogue within a dialogue.' Here, the personified Laws of Athens speak to an alternate Socrates who wishes to evade punishment by fleeing the city. The Laws represent not just the written rules but also the civic norms of Athens. They ask the disobedient Socrates about his grievances with them and when he discovered these flaws.

The Laws remind Socrates that they brought him into this world (through laws concerning marriage) and nurtured him (through norms regarding parental responsibility). Socrates acknowledges their role in his life, a testament to the profound impact of Athenian civic culture on its citizens.

In essence, Socrates' trial is not merely a historical event but a reflection of the citizen's duty towards their state and society. It serves as a timeless reminder that even in the face of adversity, one must uphold their civic obligations.

The interplay between components and wholes, along with the concept of shape or gestalt, has been a significant topic in thought. Ernst Mach, an influential scientist-philosopher, once described a gestalt as something greater than just sensory perception itself. A melody, for instance, is made up of individual notes but it's the way these notes are arranged with order and rhythm that defines its essence. Building on Mach's ideas, Ehrenfels made the study of these 'gestalts' central to his research, a move that would prove groundbreaking in psychology.

In everyday language, we often say that the whole is more than just the sum of its parts. However, the challenge was to pinpoint what exactly sets the whole apart from a simple collection of its constituent pieces. Using a melody as an example, Ehrenfels demonstrated how it remains recognizable despite changes in pitch or tone. So, if every note has been altered, what is it that makes a melody distinct? It appears to be something relational that binds the parts together.

Ehrenfels' work was deeply rooted in Mach's 'Analysis of Sensation' published in 1886. In a letter to a friend, Ehrenfels acknowledged sending Mach his paper "Gestalt Qualities" and receiving a friendly response from Mach, who noted that he had previously discussed these ideas in 1865. This seems like an unusually humble acknowledgement of intellectual continuity on Ehrenfels' part, but it seemed to be brushed off by Mach with a casual wave.

Ehrenfels, interestingly, was Max Wertheimer's first teacher. Wertheimer went on to study in Berlin under Stumpf and then in Würzburg under Oswald Külpe, where he completed his degree. Later, as a graduate student, Wertheimer developed gestalt psychology, which proved more successful than any preceding school of thought in highlighting the dynamic principles that organize our perceived world.

Ehrenfels played a crucial role in emphasizing the logical gap between perceiving individual parts and perceiving the whole, a difference that can subjectively be far more significant than the parts themselves. Years after Ehrenfels was his student, Ehrenfels sent Brentano a letter containing language that seems to come from something he wrote during his student years. The prose is direct:

"Regarding our relationship..."

Title: A Critique of Wittgenstein's Philosophy: Beyond the Limits of Silence

Wittgenstein's claim that philosophy should be dismissed as meaningless is a contentious one. I firmly believe that philosophy ought to serve a purpose and demand our serious attention; it should aid in clarifying our thoughts, thereby guiding our actions, or else it is a habit we must question. Wittgenstein argues that the central proposition of philosophy is self-contradictory, stating that philosophy is nonsense. However, I contend that we should not only acknowledge but truly embrace this supposed nonsense, rather than attempting to justify it as 'important nonsense,' as Wittgenstein himself does.

The absurd position mirrors a child's dialogue: "I can't say breakfast." "Why can't you?" "Because I can't say it." As famously noted by Ramsey, "We cannot say what we cannot say, and we cannot whistle it either." Ramsey voiced two criticisms of Wittgenstein's view that philosophy is a waste of time. Firstly, Wittgenstein cannot argue for a specific understanding of meaning, which, in turn, renders the argument itself void of meaning. Contrary to Wittgenstein's advice in the Tractatus, his philosophical argument is not a ladder meant to be discarded once we've climbed it. Secondly, this kind of philosophy is devoid of substance. If, as Wittgenstein suggests, philosophy's role is to organize the propositions of science and everyday life into a logical system with basic terms and definitions, it offers little value in practice.

In a note from 1929, Wittgenstein himself admitted that standardizing beer colors is not philosophy, but it may improve notation and thought clarity. This criticism holds true for the Vienna Circle as well, although they shared one thing with Ramsey: an incredulity toward Wittgenstein's idea that there exists something inexpressible yet valuable – something we can only glimpse through a fence and stand in awe of. Ramsey's perspective, his rebellion against Wittgenstein and the Vienna Circle, was beginning to take shape. As he succinctly put it in another note: "We cannot truly imagine the world as disconnected selves; the selves we know are part of the world." In other words, what we cannot do, we simply cannot do, and there's no use in trying.

Philosophy arises from misunderstanding our language's logic. However, Wittgenstein's understanding of the logic of our language is flawed. The images we create in our minds are not depictions of facts. If a proposition represents the world, disconnected from any reference to the self whose picture it is, then we become utterly vulnerable to skepticism or solipsism. How can we bridge the gap between ourselves and that world? How can we make claims about that world? Wittgenstein's notion of a world 'without thought' fails to capture the essence of our understanding of the world. To comprehend the world, we must not disregard the 'subjective side.'

Carnap also made the same mistake, as Ramsey pointed out in the context of solipsism: "Solipsism in the ordinary sense, for example, as in 'I am the only ego that exists' is nonsensical." In essence, Ramsey's approach and his rebellion against Wittgenstein and the Vienna Circle were starting to coalesce. He summed it up aptly: "We cannot really imagine the world as disconnected selves; the selves we know are in the world." What we cannot do, we simply cannot do, and it's no good trying to pretend otherwise.

text:
A friend once left her baby with me, and he started crying nonstop. In an attempt to soothe him, I found myself in one of life's most primal roles - that of a surrogate nurse. Without much thought, I offered my breast to the little guy, who eagerly latched on. It was a surprising moment, as I had never considered sharing what I thought of as my own daughter's milk with an unrelated baby. But it felt surprisingly natural, in the way that some things just do, even when they seem outside of our cultural norms. Since then, I've often thought of this little guy as a sort of son.

This experience got me thinking about the ancient Roman tale of Caritas Romana, where a mother nurses another woman's child. This story suggests that kinship systems may have been defined not just by bloodlines but also by milk lines. It reminds me of older goddess-centered cults and religions, where breastfeeding was seen as a sacred act.

The story of Romulus and Remus, the twin founders of Rome, who were nursed by a she-wolf, is another famous example. There are also tales that imbue the recipient with divine powers, showing ancient beliefs in the magical power of breastmilk. One such tale is even credited with giving the Milky Way its name - Tintoretto's The Origin of the Milky Way (c1575) shows the infant Heracles suckling from the goddess Hera while she sleeps. When the goddess woke up, Heracles was so strong that he accidentally sprayed her milk across the heavens and formed the Milky Way.

The tradition of adult breastfeeding as a cure is evidence of these ancient beliefs in the magical power of breastmilk. Images of fantastical plenitude and eroticism abound, with depictions of nursing nymphs and multibreasted mermaids. These images seem to defy reality, giving us a glimpse of a world beyond sexual reproduction and familial constraints.

The pagan breasts of powerful goddesses were eventually assimilated into Christian beliefs and imagery. The scene of filial piety described in the original version of Roman Charity became linked to the Christian virtue of charity, which was often represented as a breastfeeding woman. The Madonna nursing the infant Jesus, the ultimate image of maternal-divine care, became an iconic symbol of this tradition.

In conclusion, the act of breastfeeding has been seen as a sacred, magical, and divine act throughout history. From ancient Rome to Christian art, breastfeeding has been used to represent not just biological motherhood but also spiritual kinship and healing power. It's a powerful reminder of our shared humanity and the deep bonds that connect us all.

Title: A Romantic Evening at Cheuvront's - A Hidden Gem!

I recently took my date to this stunning restaurant that I simply cannot recommend enough - Cheuvront's. It's the perfect spot for a romantic night out, and trust me, it definitely sets the mood. The interior is modern yet intimate, with an urban vibe that's just dark enough to create a mysterious allure.

We started off our evening by ordering a bottle of wine from their impressive selection. The sommelier was more than happy to help us choose a wine to pair with our meal. To accompany the wine, we also opted for a cheese plate and a pate plate, allowing the staff to select cheeses and pates that complemented our chosen wine.

The cheese plate consisted of three exquisite cheeses, each more delicious than the last. It was served with fresh bread, some crunchy nuts, and tart dried cranberries - a perfect balance of flavors and textures. The pate plate was equally impressive, with two rich and creamy pates that seemed designed to be enjoyed together.

The food and drink were exceptional, and the service was friendly, if a bit slow on this particular evening. I believe our waitress must have been giving us some space to savor our meal and conversation. After finishing off the wine and indulging in the cheeses and pates, we both left feeling completely satisfied - no room for dessert!

Overall, it was an outstanding dining experience. My date raved about Cheuvront's all evening long, praising everything from the food to the atmosphere. It goes without saying that I'll be paying more attention to Kristi's recommendations in the future. If you're looking for a memorable night out, I highly recommend giving Cheuvront's a try!
