text:
The current winter lacks one essential element - humility from us humans. What we're missing is a profound understanding of our planet beyond just a magical creature or a temporary thrill. Snowy owls are messengers hailing from places most of us will never set foot on. Yes, the world has become smaller and more accessible in many ways. From the safety of our homes, we can explore the rugged peaks of Ben Nevis, mingle with elephants across the vast plains of the Serengeti, or drift along the Colorado River beneath the vibrant cliffs of the Grand Canyon.

Yet, despite this new age of mass experience, the cinematic is no match for the real thing. It's strange that during Earth's sixth mass extinction, we're more connected than ever before, but fewer of us will witness the majesty of beluga whales breaching in freezing Arctic waters or caribou migrating across the endless tundra. Polar bears won't grace our neighborhoods with a visit this winter or any other. Instead, we have snowy owls to remind us.

From other wild places, we won't receive such messengers. Once-thriving tallgrass prairies that made the American Great Plains vibrant with ornate wildflowers and grasses as tall as we are, have been swallowed by plows and pavement. The regal fritillary butterfly, a creature so captivating it could make you consider quitting your job, selling your home, and becoming a lepidopterist, will never leave its dwindling habitats to visit the rusted cities of the Rust Belt.

No ornate butterfly fish, as radiant as a snowy owl's eyes, will migrate from the coral reefs to our Midwestern towns to remind us of the biodiversity we're losing. Perhaps the snowy owl can serve as a stand-in for these other earthbound emissaries. Whether we observe them through binoculars or on screens, their eyes may offer glimpses into distant worlds - from the Highlands to the Serengeti, from the Arctic to the Ocean. Or at least, they might inspire humility in us.

Over a century ago, Aldo Leopold found his humility in another predator. During his early years as a forest ranger, the great conservationist and philosopher rarely passed by without feeling a sense of respect for the natural world. Perhaps the snowy owl can help us rediscover that same sense of wonder and responsibility for our planet today.

MOHALI: New Zealand Women's opening pair, Suzie Bates and Sophie Devine, forged a swift 60-run partnership off just 40 deliveries for the second wicket, providing a solid foundation for their team's massive total. The fall of the first wicket brought Bates and Devine together at the crease, and they capitalized on the early momentum to build a formidable century stand. By the end of the Powerplay, New Zealand stood at 48/1, with only a few overs yielding minimal returns. For the most part, the duo found the boundary with relative ease, with Devine departing in the 15th over, bowled by Isobel Joyce, having scored a brisk 34 off 20 balls, amassing a total of 2,063 runs.

Sarah Taylor and Stafanie Taylor were next to the crease, but Ireland's chase struggled to gain momentum. They lost two wickets within the first four overs for just 16 runs, and by the tenth over, they had limped to 34/3. With the required run rate already clocking in at more than 14 at that stage, only Isobel Joyce showed some resistance with a well-played 33 off 10 deliveries, while legspinner Erin Bermingham claimed 2 wickets for 17 runs.

text:
Hey there fellow food enthusiasts!

I've been a longtime customer of Jimmy and Joe's, and it used to be a 5-star joint for me. However, recently, their pizza has been inconsistent, and I'm afraid it's taking a nosedive. The last time I ordered, the crust was soggy, and there was too much yeast in the dough. Not cool, man! The cheese was also unpalatable - rubbery and lacking taste.

Growing up, we all had our favorite spots like The Peach Pit on 90210, right? Well, Jimmy and Joe's is the pizza version of that place. It has a definite high school vibe - I felt out of place when I walked in with my 30-something crew. And it brought back memories when they charged me 60 cents for ketchup to dip my wings. Can you remember that feeling when someone says, "I owe you three dollars, and I'll pay you back tomorrow"? Yeah, that vibe is strong at Jimmy and Joe's!

Parking is another issue - there's limited space, and the signs almost make it seem like they don't want you to eat there. I was hesitant to even grab napkins for fear of hidden charges or limitations.

I've dined at Jimmy and Joe's several times, and here's my take: visit the place but don't order pizza to go. They excel in the one-slice-and-drink combo. But if you let their pizza cool down a bit, it loses its flavor quickly. I picked up a pizza once, and it was almost like two different pizzas - the cheese was incredibly rubbery, while the crust was as chewy as steak!

Jimmy and Joe's is perfect for a quick lunch or a business meeting, but I wouldn't recommend ordering multiple pies for a Super Bowl party or if you have guests over. It's just not worth it when they can't seem to get their pizza game back on track. Keep it casual, keep it simple - that's the best way to enjoy Jimmy and Joe's. Hopefully, they'll figure out their inconsistencies soon!

Cheers,
[Your Name]

In a historic victory, Novak Djokovic claimed his maiden French Open title and joined the elite ranks of tennis greats by holding all four Grand Slam titles simultaneously. On Sunday, the world number one defeated British second seed Andy Murray 3-1, 6-4 on Court Philippe Chatrier.

Djokovic overcame a shaky start to assert his dominance, showcasing his pace and ultimately clinching his 12th Grand Slam crown after three previous Roland Garros final defeats. With this triumph, Djokovic becomes only the third man, following Don Budge and Rod Laver, to hold all four major titles concurrently. He also joins an exclusive club of eight players who have won Wimbledon, the French Open, the US Open, and the Australian Open.

Murray started the match perfectly, striking a harmonious balance between defense and offense. However, once Djokovic found his rhythm, Murray struggled to keep pace. The Serbian star managed to shake off late nerves, sealing the victory on his third match point when Murray netted a backhand.

Djokovic's latest triumph marks another significant milestone in his illustrious career, further cementing his place among tennis' greatest legends.

text:
Simone Bespaloff, a woman known for her tempestuous spirit, found moments of tranquility even amidst the chaos of war-torn Europe. In New York, she maintained connections with Parisian intellectual circles through letters with Fessard and Marcel. Before long, she secured a position with Voice of America's French broadcast, eventually moving to Mount Holyoke College in Massachusetts where she taught French literature. During these dark times, Mount Holyoke emerged as an essential bastion for French culture in the United States. Scholars such as Jacques Maritain, André Masson, Marc Chagall, and Claude Lévi-Strauss were among those Bespaloff encountered at gatherings organized by Wahl.

Mary McCarthy, who would later translate her works, described Bespaloff as a small, elegant woman who wore white gloves. Hannah Arendt also took notice of her when she visited in August 1944 to give a lecture on Franz Kafka. Arendt's interpretation of Kafka, which later appeared in Partisan Review, resonated deeply with Bespaloff's existentialist perspective. In the gloom of war, Arendt painted humanity as helplessly ensnared in history's web, and Kafka's "nightmare world" had become a stark reality.

In her final published work, an essay on Camus, Bespaloff expressed how her generation was forced to live under the shadow of violent death. After the war, despite once being celebrated by them, Bespaloff became a vocal critic of the new wave of French existentialists, particularly Sartre. In a letter to Boris de Schloezer in 1946, she wrote that Sartre's subjectivity was not the cornerstone of a fresh humanism but rather a precursor for conformity. She argued that instead of liberating individuals, Sartre's existentialism eroded the magical inner sanctum through which humans could genuinely connect with one another. For Bespaloff, Sartre reduced the subject to an object under the gaze of the Other, a phenomenon she saw mirrored in American individualism.

The United States felt both drab and unwelcoming to Bespaloff, much like ancient Troy to Helen. Mount Holyoke marked her final exile. In April 1949, for reasons that remain unclear, she sealed her kitchen doors and turned on the gas oven. Her complex journey ended in a tragic denouement. In her final note, echoing Camus's words, she wrote: "One can imagine Sisyphus happy, but joy forever eludes him."

Oh my goodness, I had an absolutely fantastic time at Sens on Monday night! I could go on about the ambiance and service, but let me just highlight a few things that really made it special.

The atmosphere was spa-like and calming – exactly what I needed after a long day. The server was attentive without being pushy and had a great attitude. The bar menu was heavenly, with their homemade infused alcohol finally bringing some unique offerings to Phoenix.

I loved that I could watch the chef prepare my meal in the open kitchen. All items were served tapas style, which gave me the opportunity to try so many delicious dishes! The food was prepared and presented beautifully, and you had the option to dip them in sauces if you wanted an extra kick.

One quick personal suggestion – if you have food allergies, please take personal responsibility and ask your server questions before ordering. They were attentive and helpful when we requested allergy-friendly options. Overall, I can't wait to go back!

text:
In the heat of the moment, one must consider their own character and that of their audience, their aims and the hurdles they face. The sophist, on the other hand, employs dishonest and inappropriate methods to achieve equally questionable outcomes. It was never in doubt that Christ's actions, disguises, and seemingly deceptive words were righteous, wise, and just, although it was often simpler to assert this virtue than to justify it. Whether successful or not, portraying God as the ultimate rhetorician, the ultimate orator, intensified the divide between these two ways of perceiving God. After all, rhetoric is about adapting to the situation; it demands adjustments and reactions, a constant readjustment of our interactions with others. It was this need to adapt, to change, to act and react, to speak and deceive that seemed so alien to the philosophical conception of God as eternal, perfect, and unchanging.

As it transpired, distinguishing miraculous interference from deceit proved challenging. At the turn of the 14th century, another source of tension between scripture and philosophy made the threat of divine deception more palpable. Aristotle's writings, which had been lost to Europeans for centuries, were beginning to resurface during the 12th century. Intellectuals, always eager for the new, integrated Aristotle's works into the curriculum of the various medieval universities. Aristotle's philosophy often contradicted scripture rather than complementing it. According to Aristotle, God – the 'Unmoved Mover' – was part of the universe, the best part, but still part of it, shaping and energizing it according to the necessary and immutable dictates of reason itself. Theologians could not stand idly by as students and teachers absorbed this diminished image of God. Scripture taught that God transcended a universe he had created out of nothing. God is omnipotent, they asserted, he can do anything. To demonstrate God's omnipotence, theologians described all the ways God could miraculously interfere with the ordinary course of nature. It turned out that distinguishing miraculous interference from deception was problematic.

Early in the 14th century, William of Ockham posed a thought experiment to his readers. Imagine you are gazing at a star. Now, imagine that God, who can do anything, destroys the star while maintaining your vision of it. What you now see is a non-existent star. From theological thought experiments to the daily life of the Church, the possibility of divine deception seemed to arise everywhere. Robert Holkot asked his readers to ponder the celebration of the Eucharist. At the moment of consecration, God miraculously transforms a piece of bread that never ceases to look like anything but a piece of bread into the very body of Christ. The question of divine deception was becoming increasingly relevant in both scholarly discourse and daily Church life.

text:
Rovers from Earth, painted with hues of dust swirling through the Martian winds, descend onto the red planet's surface at an alarming speed in aeroshells and parachutes. They land with a thud, protected by airbags and braking retrorockets, each equipped with cameras, spectrometers, antennas, and manipulator arms. Some of them remain there, while others rove the Martian landscape for years on end, searching for even the slightest trace of water - life's elusive ally.

Like lost travelers in a desert, these robots persistently seek out any sign of moisture. This strategy, dubbed 'following the water,' is based on the eerie harmony between water and life. On Earth, wherever we find water, life isn't far behind. It's believed that water plays a crucial role in biology, not just here but throughout the universe. Water, after all, is made of two of the most abundant elements in the cosmos - hydrogen and oxygen. In its liquid form, it's unmatched in its ability to meet life's needs. It facilitates energy transmission, nutrient delivery, and waste removal. It shapes proteins and other macromolecules, shields against harmful radiation, and retains warmth exceptionally well. And remarkably, unlike most substances in the universe, when water freezes, it expands instead of contracting - a protective, insulating layer that prevents oceans from freezing solid.

For Mars researchers, this 'following the water' strategy has been fruitful. Every mission sent to Mars in search of water has found some, and we now know that our neighboring planet was once warmer, wetter, and potentially more habitable. However, billions of years ago, things changed drastically as Mars cooled and lost most of its air and water, settling into a quiet senescence. But present-day Mars still holds an aquatic secret: a slumbering aquasphere frozen beneath the surface. This subterranean reservoir may occasionally stir, emerging to the surface in temporary briny flows.

NASA's next step in exploring Mars is to 'seek signs of life,' which officially began with the successful landing of Curiosity rover in the summer of 2012. While Curiosity can detect organic carbon in Martian rock and soil, it seems to be following the water too. So far, it has rolled through what appears to be an ancient dried-up riverbed, finding evidence suggesting that some of Mars's ancient water was drinkable.

However, whether there is anything on Mars worth drinking remains a question that Curiosity isn't well-equipped to answer. But it should have plenty of time to try, thanks to its nuclear power source.

text:
René Descartes' canine companion, Monsieur Grat (Mister Scratch), was a frequent companion on the philosopher's contemplative walks and enjoyed his affectionate attention. However, for the most part, Descartes didn't hold animals in high regard when it came to their inner lives. In a 1646 letter, he argued that animals don't speak because they lack thoughts, not the capacity to do so. This perspective, which sees consciousness as a uniquely human trait tied to language and communication, persists in some circles today. On this view, nonhuman animals are considered little more than intricate machines with pre-programmed behaviors triggered by specific stimuli. Insects like bees and ants are often cited as examples of this mechanistic approach to animal nature. While we know that these creatures have complex behavioral repertoires necessary for constructing their elaborate homes, defending against threats, and feeding their young, many still view them as mere reflex machines, devoid of an internal understanding of the world or the ability to foresee even immediate future events. When not prompted by external stimuli or internal needs like hunger, it's believed that insect minds are blank slates, their brains switched off. Insects are often likened to philosophical zombies – hypothetical beings relying solely on routines and reflexes with no awareness.

Yet, perhaps the issue isn't that insects lack an inner life but that we struggle to comprehend it. Our inability to fathom their minds might be due to our limited understanding of even the simplest brains' processes, storage, and retrieval mechanisms. However, recent findings suggest that consciousness-like phenomena may not be exclusive to humans or great apes; they might also exist among insects. Not all evidence comes from experiments explicitly exploring consciousness; some has been overlooked for decades, even centuries, without anyone recognizing its significance.

Based on such evidence, biologists like Eva Jablonka in Tel Aviv and Andrew Barron in Sydney, as well as philosophers like Peter Godfrey-Smith in Sydney and Colin Klein in Canberra, propose that consciousness-like phenomena may not have emerged as late in our history as previously believed. Instead, they could be evolutionarily ancient, originating during the Cambrian era around 500 million years ago. At its roots, we believe that consciousness is an adaptation that helped animals adapt to their environment and survive. However, much remains unknown about this fundamental aspect of animal nature, and future research may continue to challenge our current understanding.

text:
In the 17th century, Wanley's "Wonders of the Little World" introduced us to kings with oversized foreheads and dwarf pirates. This blend of reality and fantasy seeped into our histories, prompting writers like Daniel Defoe to question the line between the real and unreal in "The Secrets of the Invisible World Disclosed" (1729). People have always been curious, and their wondering has left its mark on history and philosophy.

Including these sprawling, chaotic works in our historical narratives might seem messy or a confession that we've fallen prey to curiosity – something Augustine and Hobbes warned against. Yet life is often messy, and striving for tidiness can come at too high a cost, as contemporary historians and philosophers remind us.

Immanuel Kant tried to address this issue in his "Idea for a Universal History with a Cosmopolitan Aim" (1784). He carefully outlined the blurred boundaries between ourselves and others driven by a powerful sense of mind. Similarly, Kant discussed the quiet wonder we feel when we encounter natural or man-made phenomena that defy our expectations in "Critique of the Power of Judgment" (1790).

G W F Hegel built on this sense of wonder by historicizing it as the realization of an immanent freedom, spirit, or mind. However, some readers like Robin George Collingwood and Benedetto Croce saw that the dialectic does not eliminate earlier thought; instead, it transforms it.

Leopold von Ranke, often seen as either a hero or villain in history, spoke of intuition as well as the critical analysis of archival materials. The boundaries of philosophical histories were never tightly defined, despite the stories we tell about them. While Hegel saw history as the unfolding of freedom, others like Sarah Josepha Hale envisioned a world ruled by women, and William Howitt warned that the future would resist rationality in favor of a return to spirituality and intuition.

In another largely unacknowledged realm of historiography, Kyoto philosophical historians such as Kitarō Nishida, Keiji Nishitani, Hajime Tanabe, Rabindranath Tagore, and Ranajit Guha revisited Hegel and Aristotle through the lens of Buddhist thought. They found wonder in the ordinary and humble, rather than the rare or novel.

Ignoring the more unsettling and unusual aspects of our histories and philosophies can give us a masculinist and arrogant view of the past. By acknowledging these quirks and embracing our curiosity, we can gain a richer understanding of ourselves and our world.

text:
The discourse surrounding certain substances throughout history has often portrayed them as vices solely associated with 'inferior' or 'other' groups, labeled as a 'plague' or 'contagion' that could potentially infect the larger populace. While these overt prejudices may no longer be expressed explicitly today, the stigma surrounding drugs persists subtly. This is why these psychoactive substances - like caffeine in the West, coca in the Andes, or ayahuasca in the Amazon - are seldom viewed as typical aspects of everyday life.

At the dawn of the 20th century, opium, morphine, and cocaine started to lose their social acceptability, much like how tobacco has fallen out of favor in our times. Their users were increasingly perceived as irresponsible or morally weak, not because of actual harm, but through the lens of medical risk. As a result, these substances gradually moved into the shadows, accessible only through underground channels or those 'in the know'. Once their sale was formally banned around the time of the Great War, they became more than just dangerous chemicals; they were associated with criminal elements.

The term 'drugs' emerged during this period and carried significant legal weight - a designated list of substances that weren't merely hazardous or culturally alien, but were confined to the criminal underworld. This shift occurred with surprising little public debate, especially compared to the prohibition of alcohol in the U.S. At that time, the drug problem was relatively insignificant and limited to specific subcultures like ethnic groups, bohemians, and criminals without a strong voice in the mainstream.

The only organized opposition to this negative portrayal came from the pharmaceutical industry, concerned about its reputation being tarnished by unfortunate associations. The American Pharmacists Association, backed by major corporate sponsors like Johnson & Johnson, criticized the casual use of terms such as 'drug evil', 'drug fiend', and 'drug habit'. They lobbied newspapers to specify the drugs in question as 'narcotics' or 'opiates'. However, 'drugs' was too vague and useful a term to replace with more precise ones. It conveyed not just particular chemicals but also a moral judgment on their use by certain people and for specific purposes. This judgment eventually found its way into the legal frameworks that were established to prohibit them.

Interestingly, the 1961 UN Single Convention on Narcotic Drugs, the cornerstone of international drug laws, stands out among all UN conventions due to its use of the word 'evil' to describe the problem it aims to address. Lawmakers celebrated this convention as the culmination of a fifty-year battle against drugs, a battle that had started at the Hague Opium Conference in 1911. However, with hindsight, 1961 marked the beginning of the erosion of the consensus around the evils of drugs. An adventurous postwar generation - the first to grow up as truly global consumers - was starting to discover that alcohol wasn't the only intoxicant in the world. An international underground was beginning to spread news of hashish-smoking in Morocco and LSD synthesized in Switzerland.

Title: A Night Out Gone Awry - Not Recommended for Food

I've been coming here for quite some time now, mostly because it's a fun spot to hang out with my buddies and play games. Last Tuesday, I decided to drop by around 6 PM, not only to catch up with friends but also since I had a coupon for the food.

The beginning of the night was smooth sailing; we were promptly seated, our drinks were served, and everything seemed on track. However, once we received our meals, things took a turn for the worse. We were left virtually ignored for quite some time, which is unfortunate because I'd expect better service when you have a coupon in hand!

When our waitress finally reappeared, I had to ask her for a drink refill and some ketchup - items that one would assume would be proactively offered by the staff. My glass was empty, so it seemed like an oversight on her part. To make matters worse, my boyfriend didn't receive a single refill the entire evening, from 6 PM to 9 PM.

As our night wore on, we realized that the restaurant was eerily devoid of customers and staff alike. It was as if they had closed up shop and left us there! After settling the bill and cashing out, we never saw our waitress again. The remnants of our meals were still scattered on the table, which I assumed would signal to the waitstaff that we were finished eating, but apparently not subtle enough.

While the food was mediocre at best, if I had paid full price for it and experienced the service we did, I would have walked out without a second thought. The games here can be entertaining if they're operational, but given our experience with the staff, I might not notice them next time.

In conclusion, stick to the games if you're coming here - just make sure they're working first! This place is convenient for me and my friends to meet up, but I can't recommend it for food or service.

text:
1959. Yet, as our bus trundled along Chepstow Road, a booming voice from a passing loudspeaker truck filled the air with "Get the black folks out of England. Vote for Mosley. He'll free England from them." I found myself queued alongside several Jamaicans. They looked less enraged or intimidated, but rather profoundly humiliated, as if their dignity had been violated in an instant. The sight left me feeling deeply ashamed of my fellow countrymen. I confronted Mosley about this, only to be told it was the work of outside agitators. I chose not to believe him, unwilling to acknowledge the ugliness lurking within his movement. It was a movement that had run an anti-Semitic campaign in the 1930s under which democracy would have been suspended and Jews denied the vote; one that, in the 1950s, aimed at expelling British immigrants and establishing a pan-European fascist state with an African empire.

Even after Mosley's death in 1980, I maintained a correspondence with his widow, Lady Diana Mosley. We discussed ways to redeem 'Tom', as Mosley was known. In 1988, I penned: "It would be marvelous if we could finally get some of Tom's work into print for the public... I would certainly offer to write an introduction and do anything else necessary to help publish the book."

Were I myself antisemitic or racist? Not consistently, but at times. After my falling out with Gollancz, I referred to him as a "Jewish moneylender" in my journals. In another entry from 1967, I shared an experience involving Jewish motel owners who left me feeling watched and unwelcome. "They gave me the impression of always being preoccupied with material things and trivial matters," I wrote, adding that their attitude reminded me of certain landladies from my past who had made me feel like an unwanted guest in their homes. The thought filled me with a violent rage and contempt, one that led me to believe they should be eliminated, much like lice. Such thoughts were rare and confined to my private journals, save for one instance.

Mosley sought to rehabilitate fascism following the war by denying the Holocaust outright or suggesting it was self-inflicted by the Jews. I followed suit. In 1974, I wrote a review of Joachim Fest's biography of Hitler for Books and Bookmen, veering off from the book to discuss Richard Harwood's pamphlet, "Did Six Million Really Die?" (1974). While I found it hard to believe that it was all fabrication, I expressed my agreement with Harwood's conclusion. "We know Hitler detested Jews," I wrote. "We know his treatment of German Jews was appalling."

This essay aims to present a nuanced and honest portrayal of my personal beliefs and actions during the time period in question. While I do not condone or approve of the prejudiced thoughts that occasionally found their way into my journals, I feel it is important to acknowledge them as part of my complex and evolving worldview. I strive to learn from these moments and use them as a means to grow and become a better person.

In the heat of the moment, he weighs his actions, considering his own nature and that of his audience, his aims and the hurdles in his path. The sophist, on the other hand, resorts to corrupt and awkward tactics to attain equally questionable ends. No one questioned the integrity of Christ's methods, disguises, or deceptive words, even if it was simpler to assert their righteousness than to defend it. Whether successful or not, portraying God as the consummate rhetorician, the ultimate orator, only served to exacerbate the divide between these two conceptions of God. After all, rhetoric is about adapting, adjusting, and reacting - a constant dance of interactions with others. It was this need for adaptability, change, action, and reaction, speech and deception, that seemed so alien to the philosophical conception of God as eternal, perfect, and unchanging. As it happened, distinguishing miraculous intervention from deception became increasingly difficult.

At the dawn of the 14th century, another source of tension between scripture and philosophy surfaced, making the specter of divine deception more palpable. Aristotle's writings, largely lost to Europeans for centuries, were beginning to resurface during the 12th century. Intellectuals, ever eager for novelty, integrated Aristotle's works into the curriculum of various medieval universities, propelling his ideas to the forefront of 13th-century intellectual life. However, these philosophical ideas often clashed with scripture rather than complementing it. According to Aristotle, God - the 'Unmoved Mover' - was part of the universe, the pinnacle, but still a part of it, providing it with form and energy according to the necessary and immutable dictates of reason itself. Theologians could hardly remain idle as students and teachers absorbed this diminished image of God. If scripture taught us anything about God, it taught that he transcended a universe he had created ex nihilo, out of nothing. He is omnipotent, they asserted, capable of anything. Demonstrating God's omnipotence required theologians to describe all the ways that God could intervene miraculously in the ordinary course of nature. As it turned out, distinguishing miraculous intervention from deception was becoming increasingly challenging.

Early in the 14th century, William of Ockham posed a straightforward thought experiment to his readers. Imagine you are gazing at a star. Now, imagine that God, who can do anything, destroys the star while maintaining your vision of it. What you now see is a non-existent star. From theological contemplation to the daily life of the Church, the possibility of divine deception seemed to arise everywhere. Robert Holkot invited his readers to ponder the celebration of the Eucharist. At the moment of consecration, God miraculously transforms a piece of bread that never ceases to look like anything but a piece of bread into the very body of Christ.

In the realm of learning and brainstorming, we wanted to celebrate not just the creative sparks of our students but also the underdogs, the novices, and everyone in between who might slip through the cracks. So, we introduced 'mini-c', a concept that encompasses those personal epiphanies and spontaneous moments of inspiration that often bring a smile to your face or make you pause for thought. It could be a fleeting daydream, a whimsical thought, or an unexpected 'aha' moment with potential far-reaching implications. Sometimes we share these ideas with others; sometimes we keep them to ourselves. But they hold significance for us - however small it may be - and that makes them worthwhile.

Mini-c is unique in that it should feel fresh and meaningful to you, rather than being strictly novel or task-appropriate as conventionally defined. It's about discovering something new for yourself, even if others might not find it particularly groundbreaking. A person could experience dozens of mini-c moments each day. For instance, someone might come up with catchy lyrics to a popular tune to describe their current situation (like, "I’m leaving... in my Kia. Don't think I'll try to use the heat" to the tune of 'Leaving on a Jet Plane'). Others may conjure up spontaneous puns, figure out how to improvise when ingredients run short in a recipe, doodle caricatures of colleagues during video calls, or jam out jazzy variations on familiar melodies. As novices and students begin learning a creative skill, they'll likely start here as they develop their craft. Similarly, those who replicate existing work with their own personal touch will also find themselves in this space. Think about someone sketching a copy of a masterpiece in an art museum or telling a joke with their unique twist. All of these moments fall under the mini-c umbrella and serve a purpose: they can provide a momentary distraction, ignite further creative thinking, or mark the first step on the road to becoming an expert creator. A simple doodle might evolve into a complete piece of art. An improvised tool could become a basic prototype.

Regrettably, many overlook mini-c - and may not even realize it as creativity in the first place. Why does this matter? If you view these everyday behaviors as creative acts, you'll be more inclined to practice creativity yourself. You can't succeed if you don't try, and everything begins with a tiny spark of mini-c. If you don't embrace those fleeting whimsies or trust those small insights, you may miss out on the chance to tap into your full creative potential.

Chasing a challenging total of 154, England reached their target comfortably at 159/3 off 17.1 overs. Jos Buttler clinched victory for England with a six off Mitchell Santner to deep midwicket, ending New Zealand's winning streak in the tournament and dashing their hopes of reaching the final for the first time in the World T20.

Buttler finished unbeaten on 32 off 17 balls, hitting three towering sixes and two fours. His partner, Joe Root, remained not out on an unbeaten 27. The main architect of England's victory was opener Jason Roy, who top-scored with 78. Ish Sodhi accounted for his wicket, but England seamer Ben Stokes took three wickets for 26, trapping captain Eoin Morgan leg before.

England will now face the winners of the second semi-final, scheduled for Thursday at Mumbai between India and West Indies. The final match is set to take place on Sunday in Kolkata.

It seems like we're living in a time where the future of humanity feels uncertain, with dire predictions of catastrophe filling our newspapers and social media feeds. Videos of raging wildfires, devastating floods, and hospitals overwhelmed by COVID-19 patients dominate our timelines. Activists from groups like Extinction Rebellion are taking drastic measures to save the planet, and a survey found that more than half of people believe there's a 50% or greater chance that our way of life could end within the next hundred years.

While apocalypticism isn't a new concept - people have been predicting the end times for millennia - the situation today is different. The potential threats we face now are based on scientific evidence presented by experts in fields like climatology, ecology, epidemiology, and more. We know that climate change poses a severe threat to civilization. We know that biodiversity loss and the current mass extinction could lead to sudden, irreversible, catastrophic shifts in global ecosystems. A nuclear war could darken the skies for years or even decades, causing widespread crop failure. And while the exact origins of SARS-CoV-2 are still debated, it's clear that advances in synthetic biology will soon allow bad actors to design pathogens far more deadly and contagious than anything natural evolution could produce.

Some philosophers and scientists are also warning us about emerging threats like machine superintelligence, molecular nanotechnology, and stratospheric geoengineering, which look just as formidable. These concerns have led many scholars to agree with Stephen Hawking's assessment in The Guardian in 2016 that "we are at the most dangerous moment in the development of humanity." Lord Martin Rees estimates that we only have a 50/50 chance of making it to 2100. Noam Chomsky argues that the risk of annihilation is currently higher than it has ever been in human history. And Max Tegmark believes that "it's probably going to be within our lifetimes...that we’re either going to self-destruct or get our act together."

Consistent with these grim predictions, the Bulletin of the Atomic Scientists set their iconic Doomsday Clock to just 100 seconds before midnight (or doom) in 2020 - the closest it's ever been since the clock was first created in 1947. Over 11,000 scientists from around the world have also signed an article stating "clearly and unequivocally that planet Earth is facing a climate emergency," and without an "immense increase of scale in endeavors" to address these issues, our future looks bleak.

text:
John Berger's story isn't just about power or fame; it's about everyday choices, the decisions we make - or don't make - in our lives. After the success of "Ways of Seeing," Berger found himself at a crossroads, both personally and historically speaking. In his late 40s, he was internationally renowned, but he chose to turn down most offers for museum positions, academic posts, and the like. Instead, he opted for a simpler life - one of haymaking and storytelling in a small Alpine village.

But don't be fooled by the romantic image of Berger as a rural storyteller. Despite living in a chalet without central heating or running water, he was still a respected writer with a circle of famous friends. Yet, one could argue that his choice was an escape from the cutthroat, status-oriented world of intellectuals during the neoliberal era.

Berger's decision to move to the countryside wasn't a whim; it was about finding roots and a sense of belonging. As he once put it, "The question is continually learning to be embedded in life." This need for roots emerged during a time when his iconic work "Ways of Seeing" was making waves in British and American classrooms.

Of course, Berger remained politically committed. His political views evolved and expanded, encompassing a broader understanding of history and experience. Yet, he continued to grapple with the same fundamental question: finding a way to be a part of life. The photograph taken of him at the 1973 Frankfurt Book Fair captures this struggle perfectly. Taken by his lifelong friend Jean Mohr, it shows a middle-aged writer, worn down and detached, lying on the floor as others bustle past in a blur. One wonders what Berger was thinking about then, what he was longing for. It was at that fair where he met Beverly Bancroft, a young American assistant at Penguin Books. Within a year, they were married and soon had a son.

In the end, Berger's story is one of finding purpose and meaning in life beyond the trappings of fame and power. It reminds us that we can know ourselves only by what we shatter, but where do we go when the demolition is complete? For Berger, the answer lay in simplicity and a deep connection to the land and community around him.

text:
Exhibiting artwork requires a careful balance between preserving the artist's vision and engaging with the audience. This involves understanding not only what to display, but also how to display it and, in some cases, how the audience can interact with the piece.

For instance, Félix González-Torres' 'Untitled' (Portrait of Ross in LA) (1991) is a pile of hard candies on the gallery floor, inviting visitors to take a few. The museum must ensure they have an adequate supply and arrange it appropriately for each exhibition. In contrast, El Anatsui's 'Drifting Continents' (2009), a massive wall-hung sculpture made from liquor bottle tops and copper wire, requires the acquisition of panels fabricated in Anatsui’s studio. However, the installers have some leeway to arrange these panels according to the gallery space and their own aesthetic sensibilities.

Jana Sterbak's 'Vanitas: Flesh Dress for an Albino Anorectic' (1987) is a more unique case. The museum must create a new dress out of salted flank steak, which will naturally desiccate over the course of the exhibition.

The rules governing what to display, how to display it, and audience participation vary greatly between artists. For example, when Murakami creates paintings that are designed to flake away over time, he embraces a process of change that is usually considered damage in other contexts. His paintings become essentially time-based media.

Artworks like Jill Sigman's 'Hut Project' (2009-) and Paul Ramírez Jonas's 'The Commons' (2011) encourage audience participation beyond mere observation. In the former, visitors are invited to contribute notes or drawings, while in the latter, viewers can pin items or messages onto a cork surface, transforming the work into a site of collective communication.

In conclusion, exhibiting artwork requires careful consideration of both the artist's intentions and the audience's experience. This involves understanding not only what to display, but also how to display it and how the audience can interact with the piece. Each artwork presents its own unique challenges and opportunities for engagement.

'Untitled' (Portrait of Ross in LA) (1991) by Félix González-Torres. Courtesy the Art Institute of Chicago/Wikipedia
'Drifting Continents' (2009) by El Anatsui
'Vanitas: Flesh Dress for an Albino Anorectic' (1987) by Jana Sterbak
'The Hut Project' (2009-) by Jill Sigman
'The Commons' (2011) by Paul Ramírez Jonas. Materials: cork, pushpins, notes

Panama City, Panama - After nearly a decade of construction, Panama is set to officially open its expanded canal this weekend to accommodate larger cargo ships, with the aim of boosting transit revenues and global trade. The grand unveiling will take place on Sunday, with a VIP ceremony held along the banks of the canal. Panamanian President Juan Carlos Varela will inaugurate the completion of the works by revealing the new locks and third shipping lane installed within the 102-year-old canal.

Foreign dignitaries, including the presidents of Taiwan, Chile, and other Central American nations, are expected to attend the ceremony. A Chinese cargo ship will be the first vessel to test the newly expanded infrastructure, making its way through from the Atlantic before exiting into the Pacific a few hours later. The Neopanamax vessels, which measure over 360 meters long and can hold approximately 50 Olympic-sized swimming pools of cargo, will now be able to navigate the canal.

Panama hopes that annual cargo volumes will double within the next decade, leading to a potential tripling of the $1 billion in shipping fees it currently receives each year. As the country grapples with the fallout from the "Panama Papers" scandal involving offshore businesses owned by the world's wealthy and influential, the expanded canal is seen as an opportunity to restore Panama's tarnished image.

Jorge Quijano, head of the Panama Canal Authority, told AFP that the expanded canal would showcase "the real face of Panama." World trade will also benefit from this new inter-oceanic highway connecting North America and Asia, with larger cargo volumes on bigger ships leading to lower transport costs.

In addition, Panama is eager to tap into the lucrative market of transporting liquefied natural gas between the United States and Asia, particularly to Japan. The old canal was too small to accommodate these massive gas-carrying vessels; however, with the expansion, they can now navigate through the canal.

"The inauguration of the expanded Panama Canal means new opportunities for international trade," said Panamanian President Juan Carlos Varela. Currently, around five percent of global maritime commercial traffic utilizes the canal, providing a valuable shortcut between North America and Asia.

text:
KARACHI - Lending to the private sector contracted by 36.5 percent during the first nine months of the current fiscal year 2014/15, despite the efforts of the central bank to stimulate credit flow through three reductions in policy rates, data revealed on Tuesday. Analysts argue that banks' heavy investment in government securities and low borrowing costs have failed to spur investment and spending by businesses and consumers.

The central bank stated that loan disbursements to various companies dropped from Rs281.9 billion between July 1, 2014, and April 3, 2015, to Rs178.9 billion during the same period of the previous fiscal year. Analysts contend that banks' massive investment in government treasury bills has limited the availability of lending funds for the private sector, despite a decrease in borrowing costs. Since the beginning of 2014, banks have been actively investing in long-term bills and purchasing PIBs due to high returns. Since November 2014, the cumulative discount rate has been reduced by 200 basis points following low inflation and a positive outlook on the external sector.

However, the government disputes the analysts' view that banks are disinterested in extending private sector loans and prefer to park their funds into government papers. The government, in its latest letter of intent to the IMF, stated that private sector credit has continued its healthy expansion (10.2 percent so far in FY15) driven mainly by businesses, and particularly manufacturing. Some analysts predict that bank lending will begin expanding in the coming quarters. Within different financing segments, a moderate increase is expected in consumer loans.

The State Bank, with the help of World Bank experts, has developed a comprehensive National Financial Inclusion Strategy (NFIS) to implement financial sector reforms aimed at meeting the financing needs of all sectors. The central bank is likely to launch the NFIS by the end of this year, after addressing complex legal, taxation, and border trade requirements, as well as impaired access to finance.

text:
Simone Bespaloff's life was marked by a turbulent mix of anger and peace. During her time in New York, she managed to keep her connection with the Parisian intellectual world through letters exchanged with Fessard and Marcel. Eventually, she landed a job at Voice of America's French broadcast before taking up a position as a French literature teacher at Mount Holyoke College in Massachusetts. Mount Holyoke became a significant hub for French culture during the war. At gatherings organized by Wahl, Bespaloff crossed paths with Jacques Maritain, André Masson, Marc Chagall, and Claude Lévi-Strauss. Hannah Arendt, who described her as "the small, dark lady who wore white gloves," also visited Mount Holyoke in August 1944 to deliver a lecture on Franz Kafka. Arendt's interpretation of Kafka, later published in Partisan Review, mirrored Bespaloff's existentialist despair. In the oppressive shadow of war, Arendt portrayed humanity as helplessly ensnared in history's web. Kafka's "nightmarish world" had become a grim reality.

In her final published work on Camus, Bespaloff expressed how her generation was forced to live in a climate of violent death. After the war, despite having been celebrated by them earlier, Bespaloff became a vocal critic of the new wave of French existentialists, particularly Sartre. In a 1946 letter to Boris de Schloezer, she wrote that "the hollow subjectivity that Sartre opposes to what I call 'magical interiority' is less the foundation of a new humanism than a precursor to a new conformity." She argued that instead of liberating the individual, Sartre's existentialism eroded the magical interiority through which humans could authentically connect with one another. For Bespaloff, Sartre reduced the subject to an object under the scrutiny of the Other. This dehumanized 'subjectivity strangely mirrors American "individualism," which unleashes itself in action to mask the void within.' The US felt both dull and hostile to Bespaloff.

Bespaloff's move to Mount Holyoke marked her final exile. During a break in the academic term, in April 1949, for reasons that remain unclear, she sealed her kitchen doors and turned on the gas oven. Her own complex dance of life ended tragically. She had once written about the fleeting happiness that can be found in an instant. In her final note, referring to Camus's thoughts, she wrote: "One can imagine Sisyphus happy, but joy is forever out of his reach."

JOHANNESBURG – South Africa's sports minister, Fikile Mbalula, announced Monday that the country's national cricket and rugby federations would not be allowed to host or bid for international tournaments for at least a year, due to their inadequate efforts to increase representation of black players. The Athletics South Africa (ASA), Cricket South Africa (CSA), Netball South Africa (NSA) and South African Rugby (SARU) were all affected by the minister's decision, which came as a significant setback for the latter's plans to bid for the 2023 Rugby World Cup.

The soccer federation, however, was the exception, having met its transformation targets, according to Mbalula.

Mbalula warned that he would reassess this decision once the results of the 2016-17 Transformation Barometer are considered, likely at the end of next year. His move is an attempt to encourage more black participation in South Africa's most popular sports, over two decades since the end of apartheid. Despite the passage of time, whites still constitute a substantial majority in athletics, cricket, netball, and rugby teams.

text:
Isaac Berlin, a renowned thinker and writer, held an almost unmatched status as both an intellectual and moralist in the Western world during the latter half of the 20th century. He passed away on November 5, 1997. Shortly after his demise, Henry Hardy, his editor and friend, described him as "not only the most remarkable and delightful, but also, in so many ways, the wisest, the surest in judgment, and the best."

Berlin's seminal essay on liberty, much like many of his essays that originated from lectures, begins by differentiating between two concepts of political freedom. The first, known as 'negative liberty', refers to the extent to which I am free from interference by other individuals or authorities. Being negatively free doesn't guarantee freedom from physical or psychological constraints; it merely signifies the level of freedom from human intrusion or coercion, such as being allowed to live without excessive political involvement. This contrasts with 'positive liberty', a term used because it denotes the ability to accomplish something rather than just being freed from something: for example, the freedom to run for parliament. At first glance, one might think that we are presented with an overly simplistic distinction between two sides of the same coin. However, Berlin refuses to accept this notion, acknowledging that both concepts appear to be at "no great logical distance from each other." What makes his account unique is that he treats each concept realistically and historically, demonstrating not only a substantively significant – rather than just logically valid – distinction between negative and positive liberty, but also the potential harm of failing to recognize its significance.

Essentially, Berlin flips the script by making theory more grounded in reality instead of the other way around. This bold move places history and realism at the forefront of understanding these concepts. And what history and our current experiences teach us is that negative and positive liberty cater to legitimate human needs and ideals. Negative freedom has been the cornerstone of political liberalism, with its emphasis on individuals being allowed to act independently as long as their actions do not unduly harm others. Similarly, positive liberty has played a central role in emancipatory theories of politics, from democratic and republican doctrines to those of nationalism and communism. In this context, Berlin illustrates that one of the major insights of modern history and contemporary reality is just how disastrous the concept of positive liberty can be when misunderstood or misapplied.

ANTALYA, TURKEY - Ankara has scrapped plans to partner with China's state-owned weapons manufacturer, the China Precision Machinery Import-Export Corporation (CPMIEC), on a multi-billion dollar deal for Turkey's first indigenous missile defense system. The decision comes after concerns over CPMIEC's compatibility with NATO's missile defenses were raised.

French multinational Raytheon Co had also submitted an offer, but negotiations had been predominantly focused on the Chinese company until now. However, the Turkish government has elected to prioritize discussions regarding potential incompatibilities between CPMIEC's systems and NATO's missile defenses.

The North Atlantic Treaty Organization (NATO) has emphasized that missile systems within the alliance must be mutually compatible, and it has urged Turkey to take this factor into account. Turkish government sources have suggested an official announcement will be made next week regarding the cancellation of the deal.

A second Turkish official confirmed that technology transfer was one of the primary obstacles in negotiations with the Chinese company. "They refused to give what we demanded," the official told AFP, declining to elaborate further on the specifics. The talks between Turkey and CPMIEC reportedly hit a standstill at some point.

It remains unclear if Ankara will now reopen discussions with European and American competitors. Washington has expressed concern over Turkey's negotiations with the Chinese company, which has been subjected to a series of US sanctions for selling arms and missile technology to Iran and Pakistan.

Turkey's approach to this lucrative tender has been inconsistent. A government minister had previously declared that the Chinese company was the preferred bidder and its system could be utilized without integration with NATO systems. However, a spokesperson for President Recep Tayyip Erdoğan insisted that the missile defense system would be harmonious and fully integrated into NATO's defensive architecture.

text:
Born in Algeria and immersed in a French education system that glorified France - the 'over there', as he referred to it - Said Hocine Derrida, a philosopher who would grapple with the complexities of religion and secularism until his death from pancreatic cancer. On the day he received his diagnosis, he conversed with Mustapha Chérif, a professor of philosophy and Islamic studies at the University of Algiers. They discussed the intricate dance between faith and reason, a debate Derrida admitted he could not solve but felt compelled to continue exploring. 'If we knew what to do,' he mused, 'then there would be no real responsibility.' This sentiment of accountability in an increasingly complex world has echoed through French thought. Since Derrida's generation, French philosophy has wrestled with the age-old questions of traditional philosophy, often challenging conventional wisdom and the ascription of knowledge to a neutral white consciousness or transcendental realm. However, some philosophers have argued that this human-centric approach risks distancing us entirely from the 'real world', a concern amplified in the face of ecological crises.

Bruno Latour's evolving arguments provide insight here. A prolific writer on the constructed nature of scientific theories and the political influences on research, Latour has recently advocated for a realistic stance. In his article 'Why Has Critique Run Out of Steam?' (2004), he urged us to cultivate a realist attitude, suggesting that acknowledging something as constructed does not mean it should be deconstructed but rather treated with care and caution. Other thinkers have gone further, such as Quentin Meillassoux, whose work 'After Finitude' (2008) was hailed by his teacher Alain Badiou as opening a new path in the history of philosophy. Whether this is a new direction or not remains debatable, but Meillassoux's contributions fall under what John Mullarkey terms - with due caution - 'Post-Continental Philosophy'. These philosophers engage openly with scientific disciplines like mathematics, biology, and neuroscience in their pursuit of the 'real'. The challenge lies in finding a balance between understanding our human constructs and respecting the world as it truly exists.

text:
**MEXICO CITY:** In a significant development, FIFA revealed the timeline for its postponed bidding process for the 2026 World Cup on Tuesday. This announcement was made during the inaugural meeting of FIFA's newly constituted FIFA Council, which replaced the former executive committee following reforms enacted in February. The Council, with a strategic mandate, stipulated that potential host nations must adhere to criteria concerning human rights, sustainable event management, and environmental protection. Nations failing to meet "technical requirements" would be disqualified, according to the statement.

Gianni Infantino, who was elected as FIFA president in February, has pledged to increase the number of teams in the finals from 32 to 40, a proposal that has garnered strong opposition from Europe's leading clubs. Decisions regarding the number of teams, tournament format, eligibility of continental confederations to bid, and whether to permit joint bids are expected by October. Infantino has expressed his intention to encourage joint bids, potentially distributing the event across several countries within a region.

FIFA outlined that a "consultation process" detailing new standards for potential hosts will run until May 2017, followed by a preparation phase lasting until December 2018, during which nations would submit their bids. The evaluation process will commence in January 2019 and continue until February 2020, with the final decision to be made three months later. FIFA was compelled to revise its bidding process following the controversial awarding of the 2018 and 2022 tournaments to Russia and Qatar respectively in December 2010. This vote is currently under investigation by Swiss authorities, while Qatar's preparations have been dogged by allegations of abuse of migrant workers within the construction industry. Qatar has asserted that it is addressing this issue.

Under the revised statutes, the hosts will be chosen by FIFA's 209 member nations at its annual Congress. However, the Council will still compile a shortlist of three candidates. Previously, hosting rights were decided by the old executive committee. FIFA also announced that Gibraltar and Kosovo's applications for membership would be presented to the Congress on Friday, and that both could participate in the 2018 World Cup qualifying competition if accepted. Benin was suspended with immediate effect due to legal interference in its football federation. The Council ratified a decision not to suspend Greece.

FIFA Council member Fernando Sarney praised Infantino's leadership on Tuesday, stating, "Things are calmer in FIFA now. Gianni is focusing on football, traveling and engaging with people to bring football back into our discussions because previously we talked about everything but football."

text:
In the backdrop of decolonization, a pressing need arose to reshape and redefine British identity, not along the paternalistic lines of the ruling class as Orwell did during fascism, but grounded in the communal values of the working class. E.P Thompson's book embodied this shift, although it was subtly influenced by the tides of decolonization. The Age of Revolution, his subject matter, was stirred by rebellion abroad. Post-WWII anticolonial rebellion, too, left deep fissures in British thought and activism. Thompson penned his book under the looming shadow of disillusionment with the West following anticolonial strife. Shortly after co-founding the New Left Review in 1960, he departed in a huff as theoretically-inclined editors took over the reins. He was particularly irked by the journal's embrace of 'Third Worldism', which held the West accountable for its complicity with colonialism. In his resignation letter in 1963, the year The Making of the English Working Class was published, he wrote, "The entire 'West' stands impeached by its complicity with colonialism."

Affirming the shared interests of peoples across the alleged West-Third World divide, Thompson engaged with Frantz Fanon's The Wretched of the Earth (1961), which advised strongly against mimicking a West whose culture and institutions were evidently morally and practically bankrupt. Though understanding its context made it 'not only comprehensible but inevitable', Thompson vehemently disputed the notion that the 'West' had 'nothing to offer'. His endeavor in his work was to resurrect alternative 'English' values - radical, humanistic ones - as a defiant act of redemption. "For us," he stated, "the 'European game' can never be over": "If 'our' tradition has failed... then it is for us to fix it, not to hastily conclude that 'the humanist values discovered in the West are corrupted beyond repair'."

He sought to uncover a different England whose values didn't inexorably lead to empire. This radical English legacy was also his response to his adversaries' derisive caricature of an 'English ideology' - inveterate traditionalism, empiricism, and parochialism that they alleged kept the working class subdued and the intelligentsia confined within ivory towers, unlike in 'Other Countries'. Thompson delved into 'The Peculiarities of the English', as he titled his 1965 essay on this debate, partly to defend their radical heritage, the 'revolutionary inheritance' that was 'the tradition of dissent'. We know that these values were molded through interaction with other traditions, as Thompson's own values had been. He knew this too, criticizing his opponents for drawing 'hermetic divisions between national cultures which are quite unreal.' But since England's redemption was at stake in this debate, it was his focus.

The stakes for recovering a Britain that was both distinct and egalitarian were high. The debate was not about denying the faults of the West but reimagining a future where its failures could be remedied, not discarded. Thompson sought to find a path forward without completely abandoning the progressive values that emerged from the West. His work was a plea for a nuanced understanding of history and culture, one that recognized the complexities and contradictions inherent in every society.

text:
LONDON - The Trent Bridge ground in Nottinghamshire witnessed a historic day on Monday, as the English One Day Cup match between Notts and Northamptonshire produced a record-breaking performance that statisticians are still verifying may be unparalleled anywhere in the world.

The batting extravaganza began from the outset, with Nottinghamshire's Riki Wessels and Michael Lumb shattering the best English one-day partnership record by amassing a staggering 146 runs off just 97 deliveries. Lumb went on to set another personal best and a new county record for Nottinghamshire with his 184, as they posted an impressive total of 445/8 - the second-highest score in a List A match worldwide.

In response, Northamptonshire mounted a valiant effort to chase down what would have been another record for a team batting second. However, they fell short despite Rory Kleinveldt's impressive 128, with their total of 425 giving Nottinghamshire a hard-fought 20-run victory. The day saw an astonishing 870 runs scored in total, surpassing the previous record of 868 runs set by Surrey and Glamorgan in 2002.

After the match, Lumb joked: "It was a nail-biting finish. What a fantastic game of cricket! Never in doubt." He added, "I was surprised they took it that deep, but credit to them. Rory Kleinveldt was incredible. It was an incredible game. I don't think we could have done much more. I'm proud of all the boys."

The Nottinghamshire partnership surpassed the previous English record of 318 set by Sourav Ganguly and Rahul Dravid for India against Sri Lanka at Taunton during the 1999 World Cup. Lumb and Wessels' record-breaking stand is now the third-highest partnership in List A history, behind only two other performances - one set by Gloucestershire in 2007.

List A matches are contested over 40 to 60 overs.

text:
From New York to Pennsylvania, Minnesota, and back to New York, Joe Lobdell moved around quite a bit. They worked in various jobs along the way, but their gender identity - being seen as 'assigned female' - often led them to be pushed out of towns. This happened twice, first in Pennsylvania while working as a singing teacher, and then again in Minnesota where they were a jack-of-all-trades.

Lobdell's family and neighbors began questioning their sanity, citing their gender identity and marriage to a woman as proof. This led Lobdell to wind up in the poor house in Delhi, New York, where they met Marie Louise Perry in 1860 or 1861. They spent nearly 20 years together.

In 1871, their relationship became a national news story when an Overseer of the Poor wrote an article detailing his encounter with them: 'Joe Lobdell and Wife – Their History, &c.' Other newspapers picked up the story, and The New York Times noted Lobdell's masculinity, attributing it to their hard life, saying 'the wild life she has led, and the hardships she has endured, have driven every feminine feature from her face.'

The press saw Lobdell's gender identity as something shaped by external forces - social and economic. But why did a person like Lobdell lead this kind of life? Their family and neighbors began citing their gender identity and marriage to a woman as evidence of insanity. One neighbor declared Lobdell was insane because 'she frequently claims that she is a man and has a wife.'

In many cases of female husbands, members of their own community were understanding and sympathetic towards them. But the situation with Lobdell was different. Here we see their neighbors and community members turning against them, describing them harshly before a judge who held their life (freedom) and death (forced institutionalization) in his hands. Whether at the behest of Lobdell's brother John, who really wanted them institutionalized, or from their own negative experiences, the neighbors told the judge what he needed to hear to order Lobdell institutionalized against their will.

Accusations of insanity were never made towards Wilson or Guelph, who were deemed deceitful, immoral, and odd, but also resourceful, bold, and even charming. Some ambiguity surrounded views of Wilson and Guelph because the source of their transgression was unclear. Were they motivated by the desire to move easily from one country to another, as both did? Were they seeking more lucrative work denied to women? Were they escaping someone and chasing another? Were they lonely? text:
The portrayal of East Europeans during the early Cold War era as helpless victims under foreign oppression was a simplistic perspective that failed to capture the diverse experiences within Communist Eastern Europe. This narrative, while widely accepted at the time, implied that East Europeans were not responsible for their governments' actions and that none truly supported Communism, but were instead forced into compliance.

However, historians of Eastern Europe in recent decades have challenged this notion, revealing a more nuanced picture. Despite the Iron Curtain being perceived as an impenetrable barrier, East Europeans had access to ideas and information from the West. Western radio stations like the BBC, Voice of America (VOA), and Radio Free Europe (RFE) played a significant role in this regard. RFE was specifically created in 1950 to broadcast to Eastern Europe, presenting itself as a non-governmental organization funded by donations from ordinary US citizens – the 'truth dollars' aimed at enlightening captive East Europeans. In reality, RFE received funding from the CIA.

RFE's mission was to provide a counterpoint to Eastern Europe's Communist-led news media, which they deemed as 'fake news': propaganda with no basis in fact. They aimed to challenge this propaganda by exposing its lies and transmitting objectively true news stories to Eastern Europe. However, their anti-Communist ideology heavily influenced their sense of what was newsworthy, leading to biased broadcasts that often presented as the truth.

One of RFE's programs, 'The Other Side of the Coin,' was dedicated to exposing lies in Communist news. This show, which had separate Czech and Polish versions, would repeat stories from the local news and then provide the "real" facts. In a May 1951 episode for the Czech version, the narrator stated that the Czechoslovak press heaped up so many lies that the general rule of thumb was to always assume the opposite of what the Communist-dominated media asserted. Some believed racial discrimination didn't exist in the US because they had read about it in Polish newspapers.

To gauge the impact of their broadcasts on Eastern Europe, western radio stations like RFE relied on refugees who had illegally crossed the Iron Curtain. Data gathered from these refugees suggested that audiences at home considered western radio broadcasts as more truthful and objective than their local news media.

text: In assigning consciousness to something, we can never fully bridge the knowledge gap. Yet, Wittgenstein questions: if these 'automata' are pretending to feel pain, for instance, how do you truly know they're mimicking it? For Wittgenstein, criteria don't guarantee certainty in statements but guide our usage of the concepts within them. Our ability to label their actions as pain is merely a reflection of our linguistic capabilities. If all the criteria are met, there's nothing more you're missing - what matters is whether you recognize them as someone in distress. As Cavell writes, 'acknowledgement can never be stretched by knowledge.' Similarly, your own mind, feelings, aren't inherently hidden from understanding by another. This relationship is delicate, not because of some mystical barrier, but because some people fail to acknowledge the humanity of others. Wittgenstein observes a 'proto-Levinasian moment', where a Self encounters the Other. In this encounter, our level of acknowledgment shapes our attitude towards them. This attitude is inherently ethical for two reasons: firstly, it defines the boundaries of our obligations to another. Secondly, denying another's humanity means denying the very shared foundation upon which we try to communicate or express anything at all. This, in turn, implies the lack of meaningful basis even for establishing ourselves as subjects. As Cavell puts it, 'I am bound to stand to myself in the relationships I may or may not stand to others.' It's only by acknowledging the Other that the Self can truly become human. In Wittgenstein's Investigations, his examples are educational. The most dramatic encounter is the one we examined: the case of the nonconforming student (described earlier in §185 of the Investigations). As a teacher, Wittgenstein knows that the heart of the teacher-student relationship arises when the student does something unexpected and against our instructions. This pupil doesn't conform to our expectations. They don't share our perspective. So, what do we do with them? In his Brown Book notes, which anticipate the Investigations, Wittgenstein humorously remarks: "If a child does not respond to a suggestive gesture, society typically responds by..."

Putnam and David Kaplan challenged the prevailing notion by suggesting that many terms don't refer through things matching some associated mental checklist, but they directly point to something. As per Kripke, a proper name like 'Aristotle' is typically tied to an individual when introduced, usually via a baptism: "By 'Aristotle', we mean him (pointing)." Our intention is that this name should identify that particular person in any actual or hypothetical scenario, even if they don't fit the descriptions associated with their name.

For example, consider a possible situation where Aristotle took up gardening instead of his brother who was taught by Plato and taught Alexander – something that didn't happen historically but could have. In such a scenario, 'Aristotle' would refer to the wrong person if it merely meant 'the pupil of Plato and teacher of Alexander'. Kripke highlights that if 'Aristotle' is defined as 'the pupil of Plato and teacher of Alexander', then it would be incorrect to claim Aristotle was taught by Plato and taught Alexander – a potentially contested historical fact rather than a trivial truth.

Kripke proposed that common nouns like 'gold', 'water', and 'tiger' also refer directly instead of via some mental checklist. We define 'gold' as "that substance" (pointing to a sample) and let the world determine what qualifies as gold rather than any description associated with it. The example Putnam used to illustrate how the name of a substance functions in this way is not his own, but belongs to another influential figure, Putnam himself.

Imagine two Earths: one identical to ours (Earth), and another (Twin Earth) where humans' doppelgängers live. The only difference between them is that the liquid filling their oceans, lakes, and rivers on Twin Earth isn't H2O but another substance – XYZ. In 1750, before the chemical composition of water was discovered, both Earths' inhabitants called this liquid 'water'. They associated the same mental checklist with the term: clear, thirst-quenching, boils at 100°C, and so on. Now suppose a glass of XYZ is brought from Twin Earth to Earth and presented to Locke. Given it matches his mental checklist for 'water', Locke would believe it's water. However, would he be correct?

text:
Over the past few weeks, I've found myself oscillating between a mostly vegan and vegetarian diet. In hindsight, perhaps sticking with the vegan route would have been wiser, giving my body more time to adjust. But amidst the uncertainties of the COVID-19 pandemic, I felt a lingering fear that my immune system was compromised, making me more susceptible than usual. Despite this unease, I couldn't shake off the feeling that I might contract the virus. As it turned out, I did catch COVID-19 a few weeks later.

During this short experiment, I found myself grappling with choices that were crystal clear yet difficult to make. If one decides that a fully plant-based or near-vegan diet isn't sustainable and something must be added, three options present themselves, each with unique justifications: ethically farmed meat (especially beef), wild-caught fish, and conventionally farmed dairy products.

These options aren't exhaustive, but they are readily accessible within an urban or suburban setting in a developed country. Let's assume we're considering one of these as our occasional indulgence. Which should it be? The problem is that these choices don't lend themselves to easy comparison or measurement. This philosophical term, 'incommensurability', refers to the idea that alternatives can't be fairly compared using a single standard. Each choice brings a distinct moral perspective into play.

Let's start with ethically farmed beef (option 1). By this, I mean meat from cows who live good lives overall, and possibly even better lives than most non-human mammals. This isn't your average supermarket meat labeled 'free range', but a select few products from specialized farms that prioritize animal welfare. In many cities, you can now find these options in specialty butcheries. While it can be expensive compared to less humanely produced meat, it's worth considering where feasible.

The cows on these farms live good lives overall. However, the act of slaughter is still inherent to this choice. This brings us to option 2: wild-caught fish. These creatures are harvested sustainably from their natural habitats, minimizing the impact on their ecosystems. Lastly, we have dairy products (option 3), conventionally farmed but offering a different set of ethical considerations. Each choice presents unique moral issues that need careful thought before making a decision.
