text:
The philosophical movement known as absolute idealism, championed by thinkers like Bertrand Russell, G E Moore, and Samuel Alexander, also defended the tangible nature of time. As realism gained traction, philosophers began to challenge McTaggart's argument against the existence of time. Early critics included C D Broad, R B Braithewaite, Susan Stebbing, John Alexander Gunn, Hilda Oakeley, J N Findlay, and Victoria, Lady Welby. As the 20th century progressed, these critiques persisted. McTaggart's paper was only 17 pages long, yet it has since sparked countless debates among philosophers, with tens of thousands of pages written about it. In the 21st century alone, it has been cited over 1,600 times - an impressive feat for a piece published in a journal decades ago. It might seem that these criticisms would have silenced McTaggart's argument, but instead, they breathed new life into it. As the saying goes, "All publicity is good publicity." The criticism kept the argument alive in philosophical discourse, ensuring its survival over the years.

If every moment of time truly exists, then 1066 and 2055 are equally real. This is a concept that may seem counterintuitive, but it has become a central theme in discussions about the nature of time. In response to these debates, philosophers have adopted McTaggart's framework, aligning their perspectives on realism with either his A-series or B-series. Over time, this process gave birth to two distinct families of time theories.

B-theories argue that time simply represents the ordering of events based on before and after. Past, present, and future are not inherent qualities of the world; they are mere human constructs. To say that a moment is "now" is akin to saying a location is "here": "here" and "now" depend on one's perspective. In contrast, A-theories assert that past, present, and future are genuine aspects of the world. The moment I wrote these words is truly in the past, and you are reading them now. These labels have developed a life of their own, independent of McTaggart.

In the 1950s, Donald Williams championed B-theory, vehemently denouncing the concept of a "moving present" as repulsive and confounding. In the 1960s, Richard Gale offered a passionate defense of A-theory. I believe that the debate between A- versus B-theories was at least partially fueled by advancements in physics. After Hermann Minkowski and Albert Einstein, a view known as "manifold theory" gained popularity among physicists: they combined time and space into a single, unified entity called spacetime. On this theory, every moment of time is as real as any part of space. This lends itself to B-theory, which posits that "now" and "here" are subjective based on one's point of view.

However, some philosophers disliked manifold theory for this very reason: if every moment of time exists, then 1066 is just as real as 2055. Some felt that this depiction of time is too static, failing to capture the essence of time's flow, its "jerk and whoosh," as Williams put it. Philosophers who support A-theory often reject manifold theory, denying that every moment of time is real. For example, in 1923, Broad argued that the past and present are real, while the future is not. Today, the philosophy of time remains a vibrant and ongoing debate.

Hey there, folks! I'm just a regular guy who happens to be a professor at Yale University, but let me tell you, I'm not one of those genius types. When I told my four grown kids that I was going to teach a new course on genius, they thought it was the funniest thing they'd ever heard! "You? You're no genius! You're more of a plodder." And they were right.

So how did I end up teaching a successful course on genius at Yale and even writing an Amazon Book of the Year selection, The Hidden Habits of Genius (2020)? Well, I guess I must have taken Nikola Tesla's advice to heart: "The boldness of ignorance."

I started my professional life trying to be a concert pianist during the Cold War era. Back then, the U.S. was in a race with the Soviet Union to excel at everything. In 1958, Van Cliburn, a young pianist from Texas, won the inaugural International Tchaikovsky Competition, which was like the Olympics of classical music. And then in 1972, Bobby Fischer from Brooklyn defeated Boris Spassky in chess.

Seeing my interest in music and being tall with big hands, my mom thought I'd be the next Van Cliburn. Although we weren't rich, my parents managed to get me a Baldwin grand piano and find the best teachers in our hometown of Washington, D.C. Soon, I was off to the prestigious Eastman School of Music where every opportunity was thrown at me. And I worked hard: by the time I was 21, I had put in around 15,000 hours of focused practice (Mozart only needed 6,000 to become a master composer and performer).

But within two years, I realized that I wouldn't make a dime as a concert pianist. I had everything going for me except one thing: I lacked musical talent. No special memory for music, no exceptional hand-eye coordination, no perfect pitch – all things crucial to a professional performer. "If you can't compose, you perform; and if you can't perform, you teach" – that's the mantra of conservatoires like the Eastman School of Music. But who wants to spend each day teaching other likely soon-to-fail pianists?

My intuition was to find a larger arena in a university. So off I went to Harvard to learn how to become a college professor and a researcher of music history – a musicologist, as it's called. Eventually, I landed a job at Yale as a classroom instructor teaching the "three Bs": Bach, Beethoven, and Brahms. But it was Mozart who truly captivated me, especially after the release of the Academy Award-winning film Amadeus (1984). For a while, the entire world seemed obsessed with this funny, passionate composer.

The role of harmful mutations and stringent selection by natural processes has long been debated. It's not uncommon for an organism to function just fine even with modifications or loss of certain genes, as other genetic factors step in to compensate, or because a single modification exerts only minor effects. For instance, many known gene variants strongly linked to severe human diseases are the norm in other species. Even whole-genome sequencing studies have revealed that we all carry a considerable number of nonfunctional or significantly disrupted genes, including those implicated in various disease contexts, despite our own good health.

This information may seem puzzling: genes, being molecular entities, are fundamental agents of life, yet their effects are probabilistic and difficult to predict. Don't be alarmed; although genetics and evolutionary research can get technical, the concepts are essentially straightforward. A comprehension of how life and evolution function as a system of uncertainties can lead us to a deeper understanding of our inherent nature and why.

Let's delve into the intriguing subject of behavior, specifically free will. The insights from genetics and evolution undercut the explanatory power of molecular reductionism—the idea that genetic variants can be used to predict not only physical traits but also higher-level phenomena like calculus skills or poetic creativity. For scientists endeavoring to decipher life's complexity, this might be a challenging phase, much like winter. However, it's essential to remember that complex organization arises from the intricate web of interactions among various causal factors.

Even though individual factors cannot be solely blamed for specific developments, complex phenomena such as people, skills, personalities, languages, and even sports teams manifest tangibly, and they have a material rather than mystical basis. In fact, emergent complexity poses the same challenge in diverse contexts like biology, ecology, anthropology, sport, and free will.

However, here lies the predicament we mentioned earlier: if science asserts that the world is purely material and governed by universal causal laws, then the notion of personal responsibility for our thoughts and actions becomes vulnerable. Traits like personality, intelligence, criminality, political preference—even moral decisions—in principle should be predictable from our inherent genetic makeup. Yet, our thoughts and actions appear to be even more elusive than physical traits like diabetes or height, which we've seen to be highly complex in their causation. Is this merely a temporary limit in our scientific knowledge, or...?

Putnam and David Kaplan challenged the prevailing notion, suggesting that numerous terms don't refer by matching objects with a mental checklist, but rather directly. According to Kripke, when we introduce a proper name, it's often tied directly to an individual. For instance, during a baptism, we might say, "By 'Aristotle', we mean this man [pointing]." Our intention is that the name should then identify that person in any real or imaginary situation, even those where they don't fit the descriptions associated with the name.

Let's consider a hypothetical scenario where Aristotle takes up gardening, while his brother is the one taught by Plato and the one who teaches Alexander. That didn't happen, but it could have. Kripke points out that if 'Aristotle' merely means 'the student of Plato and teacher of Alexander', then 'Aristotle' would refer to the wrong person in this hypothetical scenario.

Indeed, it appears that the actual situation might turn out to be one where Aristotle has few, if any, of the characteristics we associate with him. Historians could potentially uncover evidence suggesting that Aristotle was a fraud who claimed credit for others' achievements and managed to deceive everyone. If this is true, then the name 'Aristotle' can't merely mean 'the student of Plato and teacher of Alexander'. Because in such a scenario, it would be a trivial truth, not a debatable historical fact, that Aristotle was taught by Plato and taught Alexander. Kripke posits that many common nouns, such as 'gold', 'water', and 'tiger', also refer directly, rather than based on some mental checklist. We define 'gold' by stating, "Gold is this substance" [pointing to a sample], allowing the world itself to determine the conditions under which something qualifies as gold.

Putnam, another advocate of the 'theory of direct reference', provides the most famous example of how a substance's name functions in this way. He invites us to imagine Twin Earth, similar to our own but with doppelgängers instead of humans. The only difference is that on Twin Earth, the liquid filling the oceans, lakes, and rivers isn't H2O, but another substance - XYZ. In 1750, before the chemical composition of water was discovered, both worlds would call their liquid 'water'. Since it's 1750, they associate the same mental checklist with that term: clear, thirst-quenching, boils at 100°C, and so on. Now suppose a glass of XYZ is brought from Twin Earth to our world and presented to Locke. Locke would believe it's water because it would fit his mental checklist. But would he be correct?

Johann Gottlieb Fichte, an underestimated post-Kantian philosopher of his time, was once hailed as a 'new hero' in the realm of truth by Friedrich Wilhelm Joseph Schelling in 1795. Fast forward to today, Allen Wood, an American philosopher, proposes that Fichte has been the most significant figure in the continental tradition since 1800. This impact can be attributed to his direct and indirect influence on key philosophers like Schelling, Hegel, Marx, Kierkegaard, Lukács, Heidegger, Levinas, as well as neo-Kantians Rickert and Lask who shaped the understanding of Kantian philosophy in early 20th century Germany. However, outside of the confines of Hegel scholarship and the German Idealism tradition—which includes Fichte, Schelling, and Hegel among others—Fichte's philosophical ideas remain largely unknown today. His theories on self-consciousness and social recognition have been eclipsed by Hegel's influence, often reduced to a mere stepping stone leading to Hegel's 'improved' understanding of intersubjective self-consciousness. Regrettably, Fichte's insights on freedom and intersubjectivity have received scant attention outside the narrow confines of his philosophy scholarship, despite being developed prior to Hegel's, and shaping the perspectives of Schelling and Hegel themselves. A reevaluation is overdue.

Born in 1762, Fichte hailed from a small German town named Rammenau, around 30 miles east of Dresden. His beginnings were modest, with his father being a ribbon weaver. If not for two serendipitous incidents that altered his fate, Fichte might have followed in his father's footsteps as an artisan instead of becoming a scholar or philosopher. The first incident occurred on a Sunday in 1770 when Baron Ernst Haubold von Miltitz missed the sermon in Rammenau. Intrigued and desiring an account, Miltitz was introduced to the nine-year-old Johann Gottlieb, whose prodigious intellect allowed him to recite the sermon nearly word for word. Impressed, Miltitz, with the pastor's endorsement, agreed to support Fichte's education. Under Miltitz's patronage, Fichte attended the Schulpforta, a prestigious boarding school where other notable figures such as poet Friedrich Gottlieb Klopstock, historian Leopold von Ranke, and philosopher Friedrich Wilhelm Nietzsche also studied. Without the support of the Miltitz family, it's doubtful Fichte would have ever emerged from his artisan class status to eventually fulfill his calling as a scholar or philosopher.

text:
In various academic realms, one could argue that the pursuit of morality is a central focus. Among these, normative ethics within philosophy stands out as it approaches the study of morality in a manner less detached from its essence. Rather than examining how people perceive and discuss morality, normative ethicists strive to uncover what constitutes moral goodness or badness fundamentally.

Meta-ethics, another philosophical branch, takes a more introspective view on the nature of the inquiries carried out by normative ethicists. It grapples with questions such as whether there exist absolute answers concerning good and evil, or if morality is, instead, an illusion or subjective matter. Over the past decade, much of my work has been dedicated to meta-ethics. I am convinced that moral right and wrong are not mere constructs of our perspectives but have inherent truths. These truths can be considered as 'stance-independent', implying that their moral wrongness is not contingent upon our disapproval; rather, actions like torture are immoral because they inflict immense suffering on people.

I believe that these objective moral truths can be grasped, and some individuals possess a greater aptitude for understanding them than others. If you'd prefer to label these individuals as 'moral experts', I have no objection. Of course, not everyone shares my viewpoint, and there are various reasons for this. Some misunderstand the concept of objectivity, equating it with cultural universality or innateness or adherence to a few unwavering principles. However, many people's doubts about moral objectivity stem from more profound concerns.

One common misgiving revolves around the idea that for morality to have any basis, it must rest upon a foundation that, in their opinion, remains elusive. Others are eager to demonstrate that such a foundation or ultimate ground for morality exists. In my view, both sides of this debate miss the mark. Morality is objective, but it does not necessitate nor accommodate a foundation. It simply exists, along with the evaluative realm more broadly, unsupported by anything else. Portions of it can be rationalized through other components, but the entirety of the moral spectrum is largely brute – raw and unfiltered.

You might find this perspective strange or even deserving of rejection outright. I once held similar reservations. The goal of this essay, inspired by my book "Pragmatist Quietism: A Meta-Ethical System" (2022), is to prompt you to consider this facet of the world as I now do. To clarify, what exactly is a 'foundation'? We can better understand what a foundation entails by questioning whether a moral theory like utilitarianism could serve as one. Utilitarianism proposes that actions are right only if they promote overall wellbeing. So, does this make it a foundation for morality? Not quite – at least not in the traditional sense of a foundation providing a solid ground upon which other ideas can be built.

Today, philosophy finds itself thriving, yet it's important to acknowledge certain challenges that have surfaced within the realm of Cold War philosophy. Historically, philosophers have delved into areas such as logic, metaphysics, and epistemology, shying away from political discourse. However, Cold War philosophy has some evident flaws on a philosophical level. Take its conception of ethics for instance – it's quite distinct from traditional ethical theories that emphasize the integration of the individual into a larger moral framework. Instead, Cold War philosophy champions individualism and moral neutrality, favoring Gordon Gekko's famous mantra of "Greed is good."

Another point of contention is what I refer to as 'disidentification.' In essence, every choice we make implies the existence of an alternative; otherwise, there would be no choice. However, if we identify ourselves too closely with any given option at the outset, we become incapable of choosing anything but that option, as making a different choice would essentially mean losing our identity and possibly even compromising our integrity. This is not problematic when it comes to making everyday decisions such as selecting a toothpaste brand or a political candidate. But when this rational decision-making process becomes the guiding principle for all aspects of life – as is the case with Cold War philosophy – it can lead us to abandon our own identities.

For instance, if I'm grappling with choosing my religion between Catholicism and Hinduism, I must distance myself from both faiths to make an objective decision. This 'disidentification' becomes a necessity when adhering to Cold War philosophy's insistence on taking this approach in all things. At its extreme, the moral agent is expected to be disconnected from everything, leaving them with no fundamental identity beyond that of a rational chooser – someone who prioritizes transitivity and completeness in their preferences before making a decision based on utility. That's a rather shallow identity, you might agree.

We are all defined by certain characteristics that we refuse to let go under any circumstances. What else is there to live for? The prevalence of rational choice theory, coupled with the flaws inherent in Cold War philosophy, seems to indicate that the root of the problem lies in its claim to universal validity and singular relevance – a claim inherited from Allen.

text:
Over the past few years, the Kindle hardware design team has been focusing on creating the perfect digital reading device - a universal container for books if you will. However, I've recently found myself drifting away from purchasing digital books, despite my daily consumption of digital content on my phone.

The pile of unread books on my bedside table has always felt like a graveyard of good intentions, but the list of unread books on my Kindle feels more like a black hole of fleeting intentions. I realized this only recently when taking stock of both my physical and digital libraries. While I continue to acquire physical books at an alarming rate, particularly works of literary fiction, my interest in digital books has waned.

I split my time between New York and Tokyo, and each trip to the city means a visit to bookstores like McNally Jackson or Three Lives and Company. The allure of their curated front tables is too much to resist. I still love giving and receiving physical books as gifts. Yet, ironically, I spend hours every day reading articles and essays on my phone, sharing them effortlessly with others.

The romance between our smartphones and short-to-mid-length content feels almost natural at this point, given the convenience and ease of use they offer. This is part of the reason why venture capitalists have poured hundreds of millions of dollars into New York publishing startups like Vox, Vice, and Buzzfeed. The smartphone coupled with the open web provides a near-perfect container for distributing journalism at scale.

However, when it comes to digital books, I've found myself drawn back to print. Once bought by a reader, a book moves through a routine - it is read and underlined, dog-eared and scuffed, and most importantly, reread. Reading a book once feels like knowing it in passing; reading it over and over again deepens the relationship between the reader and the book. The bond between a reader and a book is measured not in hours or minutes but, ideally, in months and years.

As a consumer of digital books, I find the Kindle experience pitch-perfect - with a boundless catalogue, instant distribution, reasonable prices, and seamless integration into Amazon's ecosystem. But as a reader, I feel a sense of emptiness. All the consumption parts are great, but the tactile experience of holding a physical book, turning its pages, and marking it up is missing.

Amazon's dominance in the publishing industry is well-known, powering not just publishers but also hosting files and providing servers for many large companies around the world. The revenue generated from this business alone accounts for nearly $2 billion of Amazon's bottom line. So it's no surprise that they have built a seamless, efficient system for digital book distribution. But for me, there's something missing - a sense of connection, of intimacy, that I find in a physical book. Perhaps this is why I continue to buy and read physical books voraciously, despite my daily consumption of digital content on my phone.

Living in harmony through collective actions: Bridging the gap between traditional Darwinian beliefs and proponents of Gaia songs isn't always straightforward, but it's worth the effort. Just as a song requires singers, life on Earth seems to require these individual steps working together.

Songs, themselves, don't reproduce, but they are re-created and do evolve. The current nitrogen cycle may not be identical to the one on Archaean Earth, but it can be seen as a continuation of that cycle - a legacy passed down through time, much like a family surname in my earlier analogy. If we think of songs as enduring entities (rather than replicators/reproducers), and the various species that occasionally come together to perform them as their interpreters, Hull's replicator/interactor framework provides a powerful explanation.

However, there is a potential problem here - it seems strange to suggest that patterns of interaction, which are not physical entities, could cause the evolution of species, which are. It's uncertain whether the Jones family name directly impacts the reproductive behavior of individual Joneses, although stories passed down through generations about family lineage might have some influence. But this conundrum isn't unique to this perspective - it's a challenge that many theories face.

In fact, Dawkins himself pondered over the idea that genes are immortal information while their physical manifestations and vehicles they produce are temporary. He referred to these material genes as 'lumbering robots', recruited to serve their selfish ends. The evolutionary biologist David Haig wrote: "Material genes were physical objects, but informational genes were the abstract sequences of which material genes were temporary vehicles." He further explained that continuity resides in the recursive representation of immortal patterns by ephemeral avatars. So, this issue isn't a new one - it's been debated for some time.

Adopting a replicator/interactor framework seems to lessen this debate, as 'perpetuation' can mean persistent presence rather than continuous presence. Replication is taken to include reproduction, recurrence, and re-creation. Processes such as the nitrogen cycle can be considered entities that are perpetuated (the replicators, if you will).

Beyond scientific benefits, embracing Gaia as part of a Darwinian framework could encourage us to view nature holistically - considering its evolutionary trajectory and how we might influence it. After all, we're already doing that, whether we realize it or not. It would be comforting to bridge the divide between traditional Darwinism and the concept of Gaia, even though there's still much work needed to strengthen this theory. I can only hope that Darwin, if he had lived another 138 years, would appreciate our efforts to reconcile Gaia within a selectionist framework, without pushing his grand theory too far beyond its limits.

NEW DELHI - England narrowly avoided total collapse after slumping to 7 wickets for 85 against underdogs Afghanistan in the World Twenty20 Super Ten Group One match at Feroz Shah Kotla on Wednesday. After winning the toss and electing to bat, England's batsmen struggled against Afghanistan's spinners, but it was Moeen Ali who saved his team from a humiliating defeat.

Ali remained unbeaten on 41 off 33 balls, striking four boundaries and a six to lead England to 142 for 7 in their 20 overs. He was well supported by David Willey, who contributed an unbeaten 20 with two sixes. Together, they added 57 runs from just 33 deliveries for an eighth-wicket partnership that proved crucial. Only three other batsmen managed double figures: James Vince (22), Chris Jordan (15), and Joe Root (12).

For Afghanistan, off-spin bowler Rashid Khan took two wickets for 17 each, while leg spinner Amir Hamza claimed one. England made two changes to their lineup, bringing in James Vince in place of Alex Hales who is suffering from a stiff back, and opting for Liam Plunkett over Reece Topley. Afghanistan also made one change, with Shafiqullah replacing Dawlat Zadran.

England will hope to bounce back when they take on Sri Lanka in their next match, while Afghanistan will look to build on this promising start against the same opponents later this week.

After seven years living in Phoenix, I stumbled upon this hidden gem of a hotel while returning for a friend's baby shower. With Old Town just steps away, I was initially skeptical about its quality. The exterior from the back entrance resembles converted apartments, but don't let that fool you - inside, it's contemporary and charming, if a bit older.

The landscaping is what truly captivated me; it's immaculate and adds an air of sophistication to the property. As a woman expecting, I appreciate the comfort of the beds - they're heavenly, inviting you to sink in as soon as you lay down.

To my delight, there was no dated tube TV. Instead, we had an LG LCD with a reasonable selection of channels, though they were all analog. I was concerned about noise given the proximity to Old Town, but it turned out to be a peaceful retreat - except for the occasional midnight bark from our neighbor's dog (they are pet-friendly and offer amenities for pets like beds, bowls, and walks).

Each room comes equipped with a patio that, if the Arizona heat permits, offers a delightful spot to relax in the morning and enjoy the melodies of birds and observe passersby. Our room even came with a bucket'o'beer deal, although we didn't partake as I don't drink. The selection was lackluster, offering only domestics chosen by the bartender.

The only drawback for me was the pool area - it was crowded most of the day. We returned to the pool around 10:30 PM in search of relaxation and were disappointed to find several children still frolicking about. But considering the room price of $92 a night, including all the included amenities, it's more than worth it. I highly recommend this hidden gem for anyone visiting Phoenix.

NEW YORK — Oil prices experienced a decline on Monday as traders grappled with developments stemming from negotiations over Iran's nuclear program, Greece's bailout agreement with its European creditors, and a somewhat conflicting OPEC report.

By the close of trading in New York, US benchmark West Texas Intermediate (WTI) for August delivery stood at $52.20 per barrel, representing a decrease of 54 cents from Friday's settlement. Brent Crude for August delivery in London dropped by 88 cents to $57.86.

Tim Evans, an analyst with Citi Futures, commented that "the crude oil market is playing defense in Monday's trade as investors sift through the headlines since Friday." The ongoing dialogue between six major powers and Iran regarding Tehran's nuclear program was reportedly making "real progress," but obstacles persist, according to the United States, suggesting that the Vienna talks would carry on into another day. A potential deal could lead to the removal of sanctions that have significantly limited Iran's oil exports.

"The market is cautious about the possibility of an Iranian nuclear deal leading to increased supply, although it won't happen immediately," explained Evans. Meanwhile, Greece's bailout agreement has bolstered the dollar, which makes dollar-denominated crude oil less appealing to buyers using other currencies.

"Oil traders are more concerned that the euro is weaker and the dollar is stronger on this news," said Evans.

In its July monthly report, OPEC revised its forecast for growth in crude demand this year by 100,000 barrels to 1.28 million barrels per day. For 2016, demand is anticipated to surge by 1.34 million barrels per day, reaching 93.94 million barrels per day due to stronger economic growth, according to the OPEC report.

Brian Swan, a commodity analyst at Schneider Electric, stated that "OPEC raised its demand forecast... but with the robust supply picture, the market still maintains a bearish outlook on pricing."

Argentina faced a moment of concern as Lionel Messi was forced to exit the friendly match against Honduras on Friday after sustaining a back injury in the 64th minute. The incident occurred following a collision with substitute Oliver Morazan. However, the team later alleviated concerns of a severe injury, confirming via their Twitter account that Messi had only suffered bruising on his lower left back and ribs.

Gonzalo Higuain was the lone scorer for Argentina in this warm-up tournament held in the United States. In the 30th minute, Higuain found the back of the net after receiving a low cross from Marcos Rojo on the left flank. With his back to goal, he skillfully turned central defender Maynor Figueroa and delicately chipped the ball into the net. Later in the match, Higuain was awarded a penalty minute for a foul by Weslie John on midfielder Nicolas Lodeiro, which Messi converted before being substituted, giving Argentina a 1-0 lead.

Meanwhile, Edinson Cavani scored twice as Uruguay triumphed over Trinidad and Tobago 3-1 in another warm-up match. Higuain opened the scoring for Argentina, but Cavani took center stage with his brace. The first goal came from a right-sided shot at a corner in the 39th minute, while the second was a result of a foul by Weslie John on midfielder Nicolas Lodeiro, which earned Uruguay a penalty that Cavani converted. Matias Vecino, who made his international debut against Brazil in a World Cup qualifier in March, added to Uruguay's tally seven minutes into the second half with his first goal for the national team.

Uruguay now faces Mexico in Glendale, Arizona on June 5, and will also meet Jamaica and Venezuela in Group C. Jamaica, who will take on Venezuela in Chicago on June 5, stunned Chile's reserve side in Vina del Mar by scoring through Clayton Donaldson and Simon Dawkins before substitute Nicolas Castillo pulled one back late in the game.

When it comes to the morality of eating animals, we must weigh what's good for them against our need to consume them. But if their lives are overall good before we take them, it's reasonable that we do so, as it benefits us both. Some might question whether our rationality is what truly sets us apart and grants us protection from being eaten by others of our kind. While other traits like intelligence or creativity could also warrant rights, the appeal to rationality encompasses many aspects of human life that we value. Consider our extensive knowledge or boundless imagination – these qualities might have inherent worth, granting us unique rights, such as the right to not be eaten without consent. These characteristics seem distinctly human. However, much of this depends on our capacity for rational thought. Knowledge, acquired in the way humans typically learn, is only possible for reflective, rational beings. The scientific process, for example, relies heavily on self-reflection about methods and evidence – especially measurement. As for creative imagination, many believed that excessive rational thinking led to the horrors of World War I, prompting a focus on creativity over logic, as in André Breton's Surrealist Manifesto (1924). But what is human creative imagination? Do animals possess such capacity? Perhaps a pet dog can imagine going for a walk. However, this is not the same as the human ability to create art, literature, or revolutionize scientific theories, or envision new ways of living. Only a reflective, rational mind can wield this kind of creativity.

So, it appears that many aspects of humans that seem unique and valuable, potentially deserving of rights, are underpinned by rationality. With this understanding in mind, let's examine the minds of our closest relatives – apes and monkeys. Do they share our rational capabilities? The jury is still out on this one. Researchers disagree, with some suggesting that these creatures can reason or think in a manner akin to humans. In fact, the strongest evidence for primate reasoning might be indirect, showing that some apes and monkeys exhibit irrational behaviors similar to those observed in humans. Psychologists Laurie Santos and Alexandra Rosati made this argument in an article published in 2015. It's worth noting that...

In the Bible, we find instances where God appears to deceive not just demons and sinners, but even the faithful. Consider the story of Abraham, the patriarch of the Jewish people, who was ordered by God to sacrifice his son Isaac. As Abraham held a knife over his bound and trembling son, God intervened at the last moment, sparing Isaac's life.

Two centuries later, John Calvin found himself grappling with this same issue while reflecting on the passage in I Kings where God is said to 'will that the false king Ahab be deceived.' God commanded the Devil to lie to Ahab through all his prophets. For Calvin, this was a troubling passage, as it seemed to suggest that God not only allows evil, but actively commands it.

Since Augustine, theologians had attempted to absolve God of any blame for evil by distinguishing between what God wills and what He permits. But Calvin argued that scripture goes further than this distinction implies. God does more than just allow the Devil to lie to Ahab; He commands it.

Calvin struggled to understand how a God who hates all liars, and commands us never to lie, could seem to lie Himself. He concluded that while God may never lie, the reasons for this are beyond our comprehension. Much like the Devil, God has a way of making bad deals sound good as He tempted the Devil to crucify the innocent Jesus.

Even those who argued that God by definition could not deceive were forced to acknowledge that God does sometimes deceive. This theological backpedaling often takes place off the philosophical stage, in commentaries on the Bible, sermons, and letters, particularly when discussing the life of Christ.

For example, why did God need to send His only Son to redeem humanity? And why did Jesus conceal His divinity, appearing as a man instead of God? Gregory of Nyssa, Augustine's slightly older peer, suggested that we read Jesus' life as something like a 33-year ruse.

The Devil had deceived Adam and Eve into disobeying God's command not to eat from the Tree of Knowledge of Good and Evil. By making a bad deal seem good, he promised them they would not die if they ate the fruit, and that they would be like God themselves. Deceived, they ate, and in eating, they became Satan's slaves, subject to death and disease, pain and suffering.

To undo this tangle of despair, Gregory argued, God decided it was best to deal with the Devil much as the Devil had dealt with Adam and Eve - by making a bad deal seem good. In other words, Jesus came to Earth not as God, but as a man, to deceive the Devil into thinking He was just another human, vulnerable to temptation and death. Through this ruse, Jesus was able to overcome the power of sin and death, offering humanity a way out of despair and into salvation.

Title: Bringing Native Peoples Back into the Picture: A Call for an Inclusive U.S. History

In today's world, it's essential to question our understanding of history, particularly when it comes to 'The Invasion of America'. Comparing this narrative with a map of 'Territorial Acquisitions', produced by the US Geological Survey in 2014, raises some unsettling questions. The large, single-toned areas on the map signify exchanges between imperial powers, ignoring the native peoples who had long claimed these lands as their own.

It's crucial to reconsider our historical narrative, focusing more on the dispossession of indigenous populations. Such a revised history erases the artificial barriers that earlier generations drew to diminish the significance of native presence, avoids overemphasizing the rise of the nation-state, and better reflects the multicultural makeup of modern America, which is rapidly becoming majority non-white.

The themes of this inclusive history resonate with contemporary concerns, such as state-sanctioned social engineering, large-scale population displacement, environmental degradation, and global capitalism. However, the most compelling reason for this reevaluation is its faithfulness to the past. As a teacher in Georgia, I am mandated by law to ensure my students understand U.S. history as part of their public university education, borne out of the belief that an informed citizenry is vital to democracy. Good citizens need good history. A history that neglects the conquest of the continent fails to provide a complete picture, leading to misinformation and flawed debates about the present.

In planning for our future, let's retrace our steps and put the dispossession of native peoples back on our map.

Brea's collection of biota from the Late Pleistocene is truly astonishing, Harris shared with me. 'We have over 600 different species of animals and plants from the final stage of the last Ice Age, and there's more diversity preserved here than any other deposit of similar age,' he said. The sheer number of some species is astounding. 'If you want to study living wolves,' Harris explained, 'you'd be lucky to find 20 in a museum. Here, we have over 4,000 dire wolves.'

As the camel chews and starts to digest its meal of shrubby plants, carbon and nitrogen isotopes from those plants become integrated into its tissues and bones. Unlike charismatic megafauna like mammoths or saber-toothed cats, many of the 600 species extracted from La Brea are still around today. 'Most animals smaller than a coyote are still living,' Harris explained, 'but they're not found in this part of the world.' For instance, a specific type of snail found trapped in the asphalt is now only found in cooler, wetter habitats at elevations above 5,000 feet in California and Arizona.

Thanks to La Brea's natural asphalt, we have an incredibly detailed understanding of the plants and animals that existed during that time. 'Asphalt acts as a great preservative,' Harris says. In most cases, bones trapped in asphalt are not even mineralized, and remain the original bone of the animal. Although they still count as fossils, they're not like the hard stone dinosaur bones you might be used to seeing. When a museum volunteer uncovers a Smilodon skull, they're actually touching the stained bone of the big cat.

Early 20th century paleontologists would boil the bones in kerosene to remove the tar and paid little attention to the microfossils found in the sludge that came off the skeletal parts. Today, researchers at La Brea take great care to extract every fossil they can. Not only are the bones carefully collected and documented so that the entire bonebed can be reconstructed from notes, but the matrix surrounding those bones is also labeled to record its association with a specific grid square. This matrix is then treated with biodiesel to reduce pounds of sediment and asphalt into a siftable slurry that yields small fossils. These are some of the most crucial pieces of evidence in the collection. Sabercats and mammoths may grab visitors' attention, but the microfossils provide a much more detailed picture of the ancient environment.

Even when bones make it to the lab, volunteers spend a significant amount of time picking out the tiny fossils that have been preserved inside skull cavities or in the matrix clinging to bones. 'They keep you chained to your chair until you identify all the little bones,' Herb Schiff, a friend and Page Museum volunteer, told me while showing off a dire wolf skull. A hassle for volunteers perhaps, but a treasure trove for researchers. Despite its challenges, La Brea remains an invaluable resource for understanding our planet's past.

text:
MELBOURNE - Fernando Verdasco stunned the Australian Open on Tuesday, knocking out Rafael Nadal in straight sets (7-6(3), 6-4, 6-3) and ending the 2009 champion's run at Melbourne Park for the first time since his debut. This early exit is only the second time in a Grand Slam that Nadal has bowed out so early.

The match was a rematch of their epic five-set final from 2009, which remains one of the longest recorded matches at the Australian Open. Despite being down by two minutes in their training camp last season, Verdasco found his footing and fought back.

Verdasco's victory was part revenge for that loss a decade ago, as he battled back from the brink with an impressive display of 90 winners against 91 unforced errors. In the second set tiebreak, Verdasco showcased his serving prowess to keep the match alive, but early in the fifth set, he faltered and was broken.

The game took a turn when Verdasco swung wildly at a second serve, sending the ball far off court, only to redeem himself with an apology-worthy ace that gave him the advantage over Nadal. Verdasco continued to press on his more fancied opponent's serve, eventually breaking down Nadal, who made six double faults during last year's Grand Slam finals.

Speaking after the match, a disappointed Nadal acknowledged that this year was different. "I had been playing and practicing well, so it's tough when you put in all that work only to be eliminated so early from such an important event."

text:
KUWAIT CITY - The number of foreign workers in the energy sector within the Gulf Cooperation Council (GCC) states equates to the native population, according to a recent report. These overseas employees are estimated to contribute 6.2% to the combined GDP of the six GCC nations, which totals approximately $1.6 trillion, as per figures from the International Monetary Fund (IMF) and World Bank. In contrast, foreign workers in the United States and Britain send home only 0.7% and 0.8% of their respective GDPs, the report noted.

Saudi Arabia leads the list with an estimated 10 million expatriates remitting a staggering $44 billion, followed closely by the UAE with $29 billion in transfers. Remittances from Kuwait and Qatar amount to $12 billion and $9.5 billion, respectively, while smaller amounts are sent home by residents in Oman and Bahrain, according to the report.

The majority of Gulf expatriates hail from India, Egypt, the Philippines, Bangladesh, Pakistan, Indonesia, Sri Lanka, and Yemen. The high level of remittances can be attributed to the restrictive foreign ownership and investment policies implemented by GCC states, as well as the fact that citizenship is not offered to foreigners regardless of their length of residence.

The report encourages GCC states to stimulate investment from expatriates by providing specialized services and opening their markets to foreign residents, particularly in the real estate sector. This could help reduce the reliance on remittances and foster a more stable economic environment within these nations.

text:
BERLIN — In an exclusive interview with German broadcaster ZDF, set to air on Thursday evening, embattled former FIFA President Sepp Blatter acknowledged that the fallout from his ban has taken a significant toll on his mental health. The 80-year-old Swiss statesman, who is currently residing in the Swiss canton of Valais, expressed that the scandal surrounding FIFA still weighs heavily upon him: "I have been affected by it, because of what happened to me, this attack on FIFA left a lasting impression. I continue to suffer as a result of this ordeal."

Blatter, who recently underwent treatment for skin cancer, has since taken his battle to clear his name to the Court of Arbitration for Sport (CAS) in Lausanne, Switzerland. This challenge comes against the ban imposed on him and ex-UEFA President Michel Platini by FIFA's Ethics Committee in December 2015, for an alleged payment of 1.8 million euros ($2 million).

"I am not corrupt!" Blatter emphatically declared, expressing his confidence that the truth will eventually be vindicated when his case is heard at the CAS on August 25th. "Everything will become clear, and it will happen—I am one hundred percent certain of that: nothing will stick to Blatter."

In December 2015, FIFA judges initially handed down an eight-year ban to both Blatter and Platini over the controversial payment that Blatter made to Platini in 2011. However, an appeals body at FIFA reduced their bans to six years in February, taking into account mitigating circumstances. Blatter and Platini have maintained that the funds were part of a legitimate oral contract in connection with consulting work that Platini performed for FIFA between 1999 and 2002.

Blatter currently faces criminal investigation in Switzerland regarding this transaction, as well as other aspects of his management of FIFA.

text:
LONDON - Monday's clash between Nottinghamshire and Northamptonshire at Trent Bridge shattered records, leaving statisticians searching for a comparable day in cricket history. The contest, an English One-Day Cup fixture, saw Nottinghamshire amass 445/8, the second highest total in a List A match globally.

The scoring frenzy began from the get-go as Riki Wessels and Michael Lumb forged a record-breaking opening stand of 146 off just 97 deliveries. Lumb, who finished the day with an astounding 184 runs—a new personal best and Nottinghamshire record—and Wessels contributed significantly to this massive total.

However, Northamptonshire's Rory Kleinveldt played a heroic knock of 128, but it wasn't enough as they came up short in their pursuit of an unprecedented run chase. Their total of 425 meant Nottinghamshire emerged victorious by 20 runs, with a staggering 870 runs scored throughout the day—three more than the record set by Surrey and Glamorgan back in 2002.

In the post-match interview, Lumb quipped, "It was a bit twitchy at times, but what a fantastic game of cricket! Never in doubt." He added, "I was taken aback by how deep they took it, but credit to them. Rory Kleinveldt was incredible; it was an incredible match. I don't think we could have done any more. I'm proud of all the boys."

The Nottinghamshire partnership surpassed the previous English record set by Sourav Ganguly and Rahul Dravid for India against Sri Lanka at Taunton during the 1999 World Cup. The English pair's record is now the third-highest stand in List A cricket, following a similar feat achieved by Gloucestershire in 2007. List A matches consist of between 40 and 60 overs.

MUMBAI - Former New Zealand cricket great Richard Hadlee believes that one day-night test match per series could be a feasible compromise as discussions continue regarding the prospect of floodlit Tests between the two nations, with Australia proposing a pink-ball Test at Adelaide Oval last November and Pakistan agreeing to play a day-night Test in Brisbane in December.

Addressing reporters during his visit to Mumbai, Hadlee expressed his openness towards the concept, stating, "We're trying to move with the times; it seems to be the game of the future." However, he acknowledged that traditionalists might prefer the more familiar daytime format.

The durability of the pink Kookaburra ball and the dew factor in India have cast some doubts on the feasibility of a floodlit Test within the country. Hadlee emphasized the importance of giving players ample practice before such a contest, stating, "It's crucial for the players to have adequate preparation; asking them to step out there and play a day-night game with the pink ball wouldn't be realistic in today's professional era."

He continued by explaining that this necessity for practice games arises from the fact that conditions vary across the globe, making it uncertain how the pink ball will perform in India. "Given these uncertainties, it's essential for players to have practice matches so they can gain some confidence," Hadlee added.

text:
ISLAMABAD: After gaining approval from the National Assembly, the upper house, the Senate, passed the Gas Infrastructure Development Cess (GIDC) bill 2015 on Wednesday amid protests from opposition members. During the ensuing debate, opposition party members criticized the government for introducing the bill in the house without first presenting it to the Council of Common Interest (CCI) for consultation with the provinces. Senator Tahir Hussain Mashhadi of MQM argued that the bill should have been presented in the CCI, stating that any tax on natural resources cannot be imposed without provincial consultation. Senators Nauman Wazir and Saleem Mandviwala advocated for a mechanism to finance gas infrastructure development, while also acknowledging the need to address opposition concerns.

Opposition Leader Aitzaz Ahsan and several other senators, including Usman Saifullah, Mohsin Aziz, Shahi Syed, Saeed Ghani, Usman Kakar, Sassui Paleejo, Robina Adnan, Naseema Ehsan, and Mohsin Leghari voiced their opposition to the bill during the discussion. Members belonging to Muttahida Qaumi Movement (MQM), Pakistan Tehreek Insaf (PTI), Awami National Party (ANP) and Balochistan National Awami Party walked out in protest from the House.

Earlier, Minister for Petroleum and Natural Resources Shahid Khaqan Abbasi, in his concluding remarks, explained that the National Assembly had already passed the bill and that the government needed Rs1000 billion annually to maintain and improve gas infrastructure within the country. He noted that Rs100 billion would be collected annually through this levy, with the funds allocated for gas infrastructure development. Abbasi also highlighted several major projects, such as the IP gas pipeline project, Turkmenistan-Pakistan-India (TAPI) pipeline project, LNG import projects, and LPG supply enhancement projects in both public and private sectors, which necessitated the creation of this new cess. He warned that without the necessary infrastructure for these projects, the government would be compelled to import more expensive liquefied fuels, potentially leading to liquidated damages or payments.

Currently, neither the federal government nor any gas utility has sufficient funding to implement these crucial projects. To address this issue, Abbasi suggested forming a committee to identify and rectify any anomalies in the bill, which would then present recommendations to make the bill more beneficial. He also emphasized that it is the current government's intention to provide gas to every district of Balochistan. APP/Geo News

text:
COLOMBO: The Sri Lankan Cricket Board lodged a complaint with the Australian team on Monday, following an incident involving their bowling consultant and local cricketing legend, Muttiah Muralitharan. The controversy stems from Muralitharan's recent engagement as a freelance bowling consultant for Australia, which has sparked criticism on social media.

In response to the backlash, Muralitharan released a video on YouTube, explaining his professional decision to work with the Australians. "I am simply doing my job," said the 44-year-old former spinner. "I am not a traitor. Unfortunately, Sri Lanka has not sought my services, so I am assisting a team that values my contributions."

When approached for comment, Team Manager, Romesh Kaluwitharana, declined to discuss the matter. However, a source close to the team revealed that Muralitharan and Kaluwitharana had exchanged words at Pallekele stadium on Saturday, during the first Test match in Kandy.

Sumathipala, the president of Sri Lanka Cricket, expressed his disappointment, stating, "It's ironic that Muralitharan is coaching a team from Australia, given his history." He went on to say, "Professionally, it is acceptable for Murali to coach any foreign team. However, the irony lies in him supporting Australia, which once tried to oust him from cricket." Sumathipala further expressed concerns about potential long-term damage to Muralitharan's reputation among his fans.
label: ai

text:
KARACHI — In a surprising turn of events, private-sector lending contracted by 36.5 percent during the first nine months of the current fiscal year, contradicting the State Bank's efforts to boost credit flow via three cuts in policy rates. The latest data, released on Tuesday, revealed this stark contrast.

Analysts argue that banks have been favoring government papers over private-sector investments, as the low borrowing costs and heavy investment in long bills and PIBs have not translated into increased investment and spending by businesses and consumers. Consequently, the flow of loans to various companies dropped from Rs281.9 billion during July 1, 2014 to April 3, 2015 to only Rs178.9 billion during the same period this year, according to central bank figures.

The reduced supply of lendable funds for the private sector is attributed to banks' heavy investment in government treasury bills, despite falling borrowing costs. Since the beginning of 2014, banks have increasingly opted to place their funds into long-term bills and PIBs, attracted by high returns. The cumulative discount rate has been reduced by 200 basis points since November 2014, following low inflation and a positive outlook on the external sector.

However, the government disagrees with this analysis, stating that private-sector credit continues to grow healthily (at 10.2 percent in FY15) primarily driven by businesses, particularly manufacturing. Some analysts predict an expansion in bank lending in the coming quarters, with a moderate uptick expected in consumer loans.

In an effort to address these challenges and implement financial sector reforms, the State Bank has collaborated with World Bank experts to develop a comprehensive National Financial Inclusion Strategy (NFIS). The central bank aims to launch the NFIS by the end of this year, taking into account complex legal, taxation, border trade requirements, and impaired access to finance.
label: ai

LONDON - Pakistan triumphantly celebrated their win against England in the first Test at Lord's on Sunday, marking the occasion with a series of press ups followed by a military-style run. The victory was particularly sweet for Misbah-ul-Haq, who at 43 years old became the oldest man to score a Test century in 82 years.

Speaking about his performance, Misbah said, "I'm not trying to take offense, but at such an emotive time, it's not pleasant viewing." He added, "When you lose a game of cricket within the first 20 minutes or so, it's certainly not pleasant."

Alastair Cook, England's captain, acknowledged Pakistan's victory and praised their spirit. "They are entitled to celebrate as they please," he said. "It shows the challenge we face moving forward."

Looking ahead to next week's second Test in Manchester, Cook expressed hope that the "cricketing gods" might favor England at Old Trafford.

I've dined here several times, but last night was a complete disappointment. The service was exceptional and attentive, yet the food fell flat. I anticipated a top-tier steak given their reputation for seafood. A ribeye priced at $39 was my order of the evening. What I received was far from what I expected - a mere 3/4 inch thick slab of meat that was mostly fat and gristle, hardly resembling Pittsburgh-style preparation as requested. The salad was unremarkable, similar to something you'd find at Chick-fil-A. The bread basket was ample, but it tasted stale, not fresh.

To add to my disappointment, we were crammed into a tiny room with six other tables, leaving us shoulder-to-shoulder. Overhearing snippets of conversations from fellow diners wasn't ideal - whether it was about someone's recent bout with pinkeye or a couple speaking exclusively in French, only to switch to English as soon as the waiter appeared.

I've been a regular here for some time now, but last night pushed me over the edge. When I spend $150 on dinner, I expect nothing less than perfection - thick, juicy steaks free of fat and gristle. Places like Morton's or Maestro offer just that, with their 1-1/2 inch thick steaks cooked to perfection. I'll be dining there from now on.

text:
Title: Disappointing Burgers at Harlow's, But Still a Great Spot for Breakfast!

I recently stopped by Harlow's after seeing it pop up as one of the top burger spots in Tempe on Yelp. I've been here before for breakfast and sandwiches, but never considered it a burger destination. So I thought I'd give it a try.

Let me tell you what I discovered: Harlow's is not really a burger destination. They offer a few options, starting with a basic burger and then some variations based on toppings like green chile, Texas chili, bacon-focused, etc. I went for the basic one with cheese. For $8, you get the burger and fries, with the variations costing an additional dollar or two. Drinks are extra.

The menu says their burgers are chargrilled, but mine didn't have that smoky flavor at all - it tasted more like it came from a steam table. I also suspect they use frozen patties instead of fresh ones because it reminded me of those tailgate parties where we grill up frozen packs of burgers. The service was quick and friendly, but the burger itself wasn't anything special.

The fries were decent, though nothing to write home about (they might even be from a frozen bag). The bun was just okay, and it came with pickles, onions, and lettuce. It's unclear if they make their own patties or buy them pre-made.

In the end, I paid around $12 after tip for a burger, fries, and a soda. There was no "wow moment" - if they had something unique, it might be worth it. But as it stands, I'd recommend Harlow's for their breakfast menu or sandwiches, but maybe skip the burgers. They just don't seem to be their forte.
label: ai

text:
ISLAMABAD: England and Wales Cricket Board (EWCB) director Andrew Strauss announced on Wednesday that a points system will be implemented across all formats for the upcoming series against Sri Lanka and Pakistan this summer. Known as the Super Series, the competition begins with the first Test between England and Sri Lanka at Headingley on Thursday.

As is customary, individual series trophies will still be up for grabs in the Tests, ODIs, and one-off T20Is against both touring sides. However, a new system will also be introduced whereby each side can earn four points for a Test victory, two points for a draw, and two points for a win in a white-ball match. An overall win may not be secured until the final match of each series, which will be the T20Is this summer and an unspecified prize of £25,000 will be put up by the home board.

Strauss conceived the idea himself and explained the reasoning behind it at a press conference at Headingley on Wednesday. "Today I am introducing the Super Series," Strauss said. "The winner of this competition will be declared the overall tour winner for both the Sri Lanka and Pakistan series across all formats."

According to Strauss, the aim is to ensure that cricket continues to evolve and develop in a meaningful way. "We believe that the Super Series will provide the necessary context and relevance for every game of cricket played," he said. "By connecting the formats, we can ensure that every match counts for more than just itself."

Two separate white-ball teams and a red-ball team will be united by something greater than their immediate interests, without replacing any of the individual series awards this summer. "There will still be a Test series victory plus the one-day international series award, but there are some worrying signs, so we need to act now before it's too late," Strauss added.

text:
Let me clarify something - yes, I'm a professor at Yale University, but I'm no genius. When I first told my four adult children about teaching a new course on genius, they found it downright hilarious. 'You? You're no genius! You're more of a grinder.' And they weren't wrong. So how is it that now, some dozen years later, I'm still teaching a successful course on genius at Yale, and have even written an Amazon Book of the Year selection titled "The Hidden Habits of Genius" (2020)? The answer: I guess I just had the audacity to be ignorant, as Nikola Tesla would put it.

I started my career with dreams of being a concert pianist, during the Cold War era. The United States was then trying to outdo the Soviet Union in every way possible. In 1958, Van Cliburn, a 23-year-old pianist from Texas, won the inaugural International Tchaikovsky Competition - akin to the Olympics of classical music. And then in 1972, Brooklyn's Bobby Fischer defeated Boris Spassky in chess. With my interest in music and my towering height, I too would become the next Cliburn, or so my mother believed. Although we weren't financially well-off, my parents managed to provide me with a Baldwin grand piano and find the best teachers in our hometown of Washington, D.C. Before I knew it, I was off to the prestigious Eastman School of Music, where every opportunity seemed to present itself. And I had a strong work ethic: by the age of 21, I had logged an estimated 15,000 hours of focused practice. (Mozart supposedly needed only 6,000 to reach the level of master-composer and performer.) Yet within two years, I could see that I would never make a living as a concert pianist. The one thing I lacked was musical talent - no special memory for music, no exceptional hand-eye coordination, no absolute pitch - all things crucial to a professional performer. 'If you can't compose, you perform; and if you can't perform, you teach' - that's the mantra of conservatories such as the Eastman School of Music. But who wants to spend each day in the same studio teaching other likely soon-to-fail pianists? My instinct was to find a bigger stage in a university. So off I went to Harvard to learn to become a college professor and researcher of music history - a musicologist, as it's called. Eventually, I landed a job at Yale as a classroom instructor teaching the "three Bs": Bach, Beethoven, and Brahms. Yet the composer who truly captivated me was an M: Mozart. My interest in him deepened with the release of the Academy Award-winning film Amadeus (1984). For a time, the entire world seemed consumed by this funny, passionate man.

text:
In a historical moment for tennis, Novak Djokovic clinched his maiden French Open title and joined the esteemed ranks of Don Budge and Rod Laver as one of only three players to hold all four Grand Slam titles concurrently. The world number one achieved this feat on Sunday, defeating British second seed Andy Murray in straight sets (3-1, 6-4) on Court Philippe Chatrier.

Djokovic overcame a shaky start to dominate the match, showcasing his exceptional pace and claiming his 12th Grand Slam title after three previous Roland Garros final defeats. Murray began the match with an impressive display, striking the perfect balance between defense and offense, but he was unable to sustain this level of play as Djokovic found his rhythm.

As Djokovic gained momentum, Murray struggled to keep up, ultimately falling victim to the Serb's relentless pursuit of victory. The 34-year-old from Serbia fought off late nerves to seal the match on his third match point, as Murray netted a backhand on the decisive play.

With this win, Djokovic becomes only the eighth player in history to triumph at Wimbledon, the French Open, the U.S. Open, and the Australian Open. The tennis world eagerly awaits his next milestone as he continues to etch his name into the annals of tennis history.

text:
I stumbled upon this hidden gem, and I must say, it was worth the search! Parking is a breeze with both a lot right nearby and street parking available. As you walk in, the atmosphere transforms - dim lighting creates a cozy ambiance without being eerie. A DJ spins tunes that add to the overall setting, though they were a bit unconventional for my taste.

Our server was nothing short of wonderful - welcoming, attentive, and quick on her feet. She started us off with refreshing cucumber water, which was a delightful surprise! I'm picky about customer service, and she exceeded expectations, making our visit even more enjoyable.

Each table featured a lit pink candle, casting a soft glow around the restaurant that was soothing and romantic. Pink lights adorned the chef's area, adding to the relaxed vibe. The mirrors framing a splatter painting on the wall were a thoughtful touch that I appreciated.

Unfortunately, everything on the menu is served as appetizers, which can add up if you're not careful. Prices range from $6-$12 per dish, and the waitress suggested getting at least 2-3 per person. With drinks, it can get pricey, but we shared everything to cut costs.

The lemongrass chicken (I can't remember the exact name) was outstanding - simply delicious! The gyoza were alright, a tad too minty for my liking. We also tried a dish where you cook your own meat on a hot stone - it was okay, but the stone didn't stay hot enough to cook the meat thoroughly, which is a pet peeve of mine. I prefer my meat well-done, so it wasn't my favorite. The beef wrapped in grape leaves looked promising, but they were incredibly spicy - more than I had anticipated. There was no warning on the menu about the spiciness, and after a server noticed us struggling, he confirmed they were indeed spicy. A little heads-up would have been appreciated!

Despite some minor issues with the food, the atmosphere was outstanding, and the staff was exceptional. The menu could use some work - if you're going to serve spicy dishes, it should be clearly indicated! All in all, I'd consider returning, but there are other dishes on the menu that I'm eager to try. Perhaps a different location would offer a more diverse selection?

text:
LOS ANGELES — The U.S. sports community was left reeling on Thursday following the tragic shootings in Dallas that claimed the lives of four police officers and injured seven others, sparking a wave of shock and sorrow across the nation.

Members of the United States Women's National Soccer Team, led by Alex Morgan, took to social media to express their grief, with Morgan stating, "Hurting for Dallas right now." She went on to lament that it often feels like the country is constantly waking up to more tragedies than not. "I want to wake up from this nightmare," she added.

NBA superstar LeBron James shared a powerful message with his 31.5 million social media followers, stating, "We are all hurting tonight." He emphasized that violence is not the answer to the chaos unfolding in America. "#StoptheViolence" was the poignant hashtag he used to end his tweet.

The tragic events unfolded during what was intended as a peaceful protest against perceived police brutality and racial bias in downtown Dallas, Texas. Police confirmed that at least two snipers opened fire on officers who were guarding the march, turning an otherwise peaceful gathering into a scene of unimaginable horror.

As the nation grapples with this latest act of senseless violence, it's worth noting that over 12,000 people have lost their lives to gun-related crimes in America alone - making it one of the countries with the highest gun-related murder rates in the world.

Oklahoma City Thunder player Anthony Morrow shared his heartache on Twitter, stating, "I want to hope this is all a bad dream...it ain't." Another sports figure, Spencer Dinwiddie, who played 12 games with the Detroit Pistons last season, voiced similar sentiments, calling the killings senseless and bemoaning the rampant hatred that seems to permeate society today.

The Dallas Stars NHL club tweeted their deepest sympathies to all those affected by the tragedy, offering their thoughts and prayers to DART PD (Dallas Area Rapid Transit Police Department) and the Dallas Police Department.

Oh man, I was really looking forward to dining at Saguaro Grille for the first (and last) time! It's always refreshing to find a reasonably priced restaurant that isn't part of a corporate chain.

But let me tell you, our experience wasn't exactly what we hoped for. Our waiter had no idea about the soup or bread options because apparently, their actual inventory was nothing like what was printed on the menu. The waitress even scolded him for not knowing, and it became clear she didn't know much either. She tried to convince us to get croissants, but neither of us were interested in that.

My friend ordered a portabella burger, which is essentially a giant mushroom cap instead of meat for vegetarian or vegan friends. I went for a chicken sandwich and Italian wedding soup. We had to repeat our order multiple times before the waiter got it right, and when my friend's burger arrived, it was a regular beef burger - not the portabella they were out of.

The soup came in one of those skinny coffee mugs, and the waiter accidentally served it to the wrong person. It was decent soup, but there was no way to adjust the seasonings without making a mess. My sandwich was okay, nothing special. I was starving and didn't realize until I was almost finished that the promised buttermilk ranch dressing was just regular ranch with a bit of buttermilk. My friend's fruit was past its prime, and her tuna melt wasn't exactly mouthwatering. We must be masochists!

We decided to have dessert, but of course, the one thing on the menu we wanted was out of stock. So, we just opted for coffee instead. My friend asked for a small iced coffee, and I went for a double shot of espresso in my iced coffee. Simple enough, right? The waiter came back to make sure I really wanted a double shot of espresso in my iced coffee. Unfortunately, it wasn't worth the price or the strange trip through the Twilight Zone.

text: In the early 21st century, photography on smartphones like the iPhone took over portraiture, making smiling the go-to method for expressing personal identity through social media. This trend transcended cultural barriers and even brought smiles to cultures that had previously been less inclined towards Western practices. Today, one out of every five tweets includes an emoji, making it a universal language in our globalized digital age. The 'smile with tears of joy', an updated version of the original smiley, is the most widely used emoji among the 3,000+ available. However, the advent of COVID-19 in 2019 put a halt to this long-lasting Western smile trend. Suddenly, smiles were hidden behind surgical masks. Although some might have noticed that genuine and sincere smiles cause the muscles around the eyes to crinkle, not everyone possesses such perceptiveness. The emoji reflected this change. While the 'smile with tears of joy' remained popular, another emoji gained significant traction: the masked face. When Apple updated its mask emoji in November 2020 by adding color to the cheeks and wrinkles around the eyes, it seemed as though the smile was fighting back. It's hard to imagine that the iconic cultural significance and global appeal of the smile will fade away completely as the world recovers from the pandemic.

4 stars. Had my first visit at Valley Oregano's tonight, and it was worth the wait. I live just minutes away from the Camelback location, but I decided to give the Tempe spot a try since I was meeting friends there for dinner.

The wait was a bit longer than ideal (around an hour), which almost had us considering Thai Basil instead. But we stuck it out because none of us have ever tried Oregano's before, and we didn't want to miss out. When we were finally seated, we ordered dishes that I won't be able to eat anything similar to for the rest of the week: the spinach salad (which I might even consider eating again), the cheesy bread, a large thin-crust pesto pizza, and the pizokie for dessert.

As a deep-dish enthusiast (I know, I know, not everyone's cup of tea), I won't fully judge Oregano's until I've had their deep-dish offering. However, the thin-crust pesto pizza was quite decent - nothing mind-blowing, but good enough to satisfy my craving. The salad was flavorful, and the cheesy bread was okay. But the real standout was the dessert pizokie. Perhaps it's because I haven't had one in a while (since BJ's back in college), but it was surprisingly delicious.

The atmosphere was reminiscent of a college hangout, with a lively and noisy crowd. But hey, that's what you can expect from a place like this. Despite the not-so-healthy menu options, I can now say I've had my first Oregano's experience, and it was worth the wait. If anything, it just reminded me of how much I love local pizza chains!

text:
In this exploration, I delve into a synthesis that draws upon the wisdom of African ethnophilosophy while tackling the questions posed by non-African philosophies. The term 'ethnophilosophy' gained prominence through the African philosopher Paulin Hountondji, who used it in a critical light to describe the philosophical pursuit that aims to recognize traditional African viewpoints as philosophically significant and, for some African scholars, a distinctive African addition to humanity's philosophical legacy.

While Hountondji was correct in rejecting ethnophilosophy as representative of substantive African philosophy, he overlooked its potential as a rich source of African thought. Western philosophers like Thaddeus Metz at the University of Johannesburg, who have ventured into African philosophy, have greatly benefited from ethnophilosophical resources. Ethnophilosophy, comprising African traditional worldviews and scholarly writings on traditional African phenomena, has provided me with the foundational ideas for what I call 'consolation philosophy.'

From the monistic worldview of the Idoma people of Central Nigeria, I took the concept of mood as the essence of things. From Senghor, I borrowed the idea of the melancholic being, and built a comprehensive philosophy of human existence around it. From African philosophers such as Placide Tempels, Asouzu, Ramose, and Chimakonam, I took the idea of an interconnected universe filled with missing links – a cosmic picture echoed by Julius Nyerere and Senghor in their descriptions of the African extended-family system. Consolation philosophy is African philosophy, but I believe its principles are universally applicable, engaging the interests of Europeans, Americans, Indians, Chinese, and others alike.

My intellectual journey has continued to unfold, enriched by my interactions with fellow African philosophers, Western analytic and continental philosophers, and my growing curiosity about Oriental philosophies. As an African philosopher, my personal struggle is finding a philosophical balance – navigating the waters between the challenges of tradition and the allure of modernity.

Title: The Ongoing Struggle for Worker Rights in the Persian Gulf Region

The recent changes in business regulation within Qatar have drawn mixed reactions. Critics argue that these reforms merely skim the surface of a much deeper issue. Similarly, the UAE's efforts to educate employers on issues like racism, diversity, and inclusion regarding migrant labor are viewed skeptically as potential image-enhancement measures rather than genuine steps towards change.

Similar criticisms have been levied against the UAE's reforms in its treatment of migrant workers. Some argue that these changes are merely cosmetic, intended to improve the country's global standing rather than addressing the systemic issues at hand. The anticipated abolition of Saudi Arabia's kafala labor system in 2021 offers a glimmer of hope, but it doesn't erase the grim reality Finley described as a "morally reprehensible system of human exploitation" - six modern-day slave societies thriving in the Persian Gulf.

For genuine abolition to occur, we must address and eliminate the roots of this exploitative system. These include racialization of labor and rampant worker abuse and exploitation. It's encouraging to see the launch of the Ijmāʿ on Slavery website, which aims to gather a consensus among Islamic scholars that slavery and slave trading are illegal under Islamic law.

Each of these Persian Gulf city-states is governed by Muslim governments. I encourage Muslim scholars within these communities to join this consensus, helping to eradicate this morally reprehensible system of human exploitation in our region.

text:
Title: Waqar Younis Resigns as Pakistan Coach Amidst Disappointing Results

LAHORE - After a string of underwhelming performances, Waqar Younis has tendered his resignation as head coach of the Pakistani cricket team, three months ahead of the expiration of his contract. The announcement comes just a day after Shahid Afridi stepped down as T20 captain.

The resignations were prompted by Pakistan's poor showing in the Asia Cup in Bangladesh and the World T20 in India. Across both tournaments, the team managed to win only three out of eight games, failing to advance past the initial round. The last series that Pakistan won in any format was back in October 2015, when they defeated England in a 2-Test series in the UAE. Since then, they have lost both the ODI and T20 series against England, as well as the T20 and ODI series in New Zealand. Out of the 12 limited overs series during his tenure as coach, Pakistan won only two.

Waqar's resignation letter was submitted to PCB chairman Shahryar Khan and the board's selection committee, marking the end of a second stint as head coach that began in 2014. His previous tenure was cut short in 2011 due to personal reasons.
