text:
In contrast to certain contemporary thinkers like Jason Stanley, Latour posits that these movements aren't just a surface-level echo of early 20th-century fascism. Instead, they embody a distinct political landscape built on climate change denial. This new order sees wealthy elites striving to establish gated communities and escape routes through deregulation and disenfranchisement. Simultaneously, they yearn for fleeting fulfillment in luxuries that seem ultimately hollow: superyachts, brief forays into space or the deep sea, or purchasing entire islands. By manipulating and undermining democracy, they aim to generate more resources for themselves by deregulation. Understanding (to some degree) that this isn't sustainable, they retreat into increasingly detached fantasies such as TESCREAL - an ideological blend of transhumanism, extropianism, singularitarianism, cosmism, rationalism, effective altruism, and longtermism. This vision, promoted by philosophers at Oxford University like Nick Bostrom, Hilary Greaves, and William MacAskill, paints a future where humanity evolves into a posthuman state (through liberal eugenics and AI), colonizes the accessible universe, and depletes our cosmic endowment of resources to produce astronomical amounts of 'value.' The happiness of these future posthumans, largely digital entities, justifies ignoring present-day issues. As Greaves and MacAskill put it, "when evaluating actions, we can often initially focus primarily on the further-future effects, with short-run effects acting as little more than tie-breakers." The TESCREAL world offers limited space for human diversity: the rich tapestry of experiences found in communities like Travellers and Roma, Indigenous societies, and more.

Why do the wealthy choose to actively deny the climate crisis rather than address it? According to philosopher Beth Lord, drawing on Spinoza, they're gripped by negative emotions. Typically, our emotions guide us towards what benefits us and away from harm. We experience three fundamental affects: joy, sadness, and desire. Desire is an expression of the conatus - we pursue things that bring us joy and avoid those that cause sadness. Overall, this helps with self-preservation. However, due to the intricate nature of our emotions, it's possible to be misguided in them and desire things that don't truly benefit us. Seeking prestige, fame, and wealth may seem like it will help us realize ourselves, but we become ensnared by these desires instead. While this mindset is prevalent among the wealthy elite, it's not exclusive to them. The ethicist Eugene Chislenko argues that we might all be climate change deniers in some sense - not that we literally deny its existence, but that we fail to act upon its urgency due to personal desires and fears.

I made a spontaneous decision to dine here tonight, which turned out to be an interesting experience. Arriving around 4 pm, I found myself among a few other eager patrons waiting for the restaurant's 5 pm opening. Fortunately, there was plenty of shade to keep us comfortable during our short wait, and the friendly mix of regulars and newcomers maintained a respectful queue.

With only 40 seats available, it wasn't surprising to learn that this place is popular – especially considering most patrons were from out of town! It was also interesting to discover that reservations are accepted online for groups of six to ten, with only three reservations per night. Unfortunately, these tables are often booked months in advance. However, on this occasion, we were fortunate enough to be the sole reserved table for ten ahead of everyone else.

At 5 pm sharp, the staff efficiently organized the dining area, and we placed our drink and food orders within a few minutes. The service was excellent, with just the right pace between courses. I would have preferred slightly more basil on my mozzarella and tomato salad, but overall, everything was fantastic – even my Mexican Coke!

We ordered two pizzas, both cooked to perfection in a wood-fired oven. The bread that came with our salads was divine; I could easily have devoured an entire loaf! Thankfully, it's also available for $2 on its own. Neither of our dishes were greasy or soggy – the olive oil and fresh mozzarella were both exceptional.

While I would gladly wait an hour in the Phoenix heat for a meal as delicious as this, I couldn't imagine waiting for 4 to 6 hours like some people do. All things considered, this turned out to be a fortunate last-minute dining choice. If I visit again, I plan on arriving an hour before opening – although I can't wait that long for the next opportunity!

text:
A Spanish court has requested that Neymar and his father stand trial over allegations surrounding the footballer's contentious transfer to FC Barcelona, potentially mirroring a similar legal battle faced by fellow teammate Lionel Messi. The prosecution claims that Neymar's 2013 move to the Catalan giants was undervalued and involved irregularities in the contract between the player and the club, limiting other clubs' ability to bid for his services and impacting the transfer fee.

The original deal, valued at €57.1 million ($64.9 million) by Barcelona, saw €40 million paid to the N&N company owned by Neymar's father, with Santos - where Neymar began his career - receiving €17.1 million, of which €6.8 million was given to DIS. However, Spanish authorities believe that the actual transfer amount was closer to €83.3 million, with the remaining funds reportedly going to Neymar and his family, as well as Santos.

Former Barcelona president Sandro Rosell resigned in 2014 over the affair, and both the club and Rosell were charged with tax fraud related to the signing. Barcelona made a voluntary tax payment of €13.5 million at the time. Despite these developments, no trial has yet taken place.

In response to media scrutiny, Neymar and his father have previously defended themselves, with Neymar telling Brazilian TV station Globo in February that they should be given the opportunity to prove any allegations. "Before making false claims that we hid something, they need to provide proof," he stated. "My father handles the financial aspects of my career, but when you see someone you care about suffering, it hurts."

Spanish prosecutors have called for Neymar, his father Neymar da Silva Santos, Rosell, and FC Barcelona itself to be tried on charges of corruption. If the case proceeds to trial, Neymar faces a potential six-year prison sentence and fines. In addition, Rosell, FC Barcelona, Neymar's father, and Brazilian businessman José Maria Campomar, who also played a role in the transfer, are being charged with fraud.

Neymar's mother, Nadine, could potentially face charges as well due to her alleged financial gains from the alleged fraudulent transfer. The boundless cosmos. It's time for us, the human race, to unleash our incredible ingenuity and shape this universe according to our dreams. I can't help but ponder: what will we create? We are Homo Faber, 'Mankind the Creator.' We didn't get created in God's image; instead, we crafted the idea of God in ours.

We may be a seemingly insignificant species orbiting an unremarkable star, lost in the vast and indifferent cosmos. Yet, we hold a unique power - creation. And yet, here we are, caught in a consumer culture that stifles our inherent creativity. The financial crisis of 2008 could have been the harbinger of this culture's downfall. The consumer-driven economic model — an improvised arrangement born from the demise of the industrial model — has run its course. It was designed for a world where technology restricted creativity to a select few, and television broadcast their messages to the masses - as entrepreneur Seth Godin calls it, the TV-industrial complex.

Today, the internet has democratized communication, while smartphones the size of an iPhone put powerful creative tools in the hands of many. We're witnessing an economy struggling to keep pace with its own technological advancements. Observe the rise of new voices on social media, who prioritize creativity above all else.

Our leaders instinctively seek to rebuild our consumer culture. They see us as passive consumers, a mentality they have been trained to instill. It might take a generational shift before a world leader addresses a speech not to the world's consumers but to its creators. But a creator culture is already emerging from the grassroots, fueled by the creative passions of the people themselves.

Platforms like Kickstarter, barely three years old, have significantly outfunded the US National Endowment for the Arts last year. They've allowed artists and entrepreneurs to sidestep traditional funding sources. Crowd-funded creativity isn't driven by commercial interests or political agendas but by the creative enthusiasm of the crowd.

The 'maker' movement revives the spirit of craftsmanship, merging it with modern technologies such as 3D printing, promising to revolutionize manufacturing just like the internet did for communication. The principles of 'open source' are spreading far beyond the software development community, rejuvenating academic research, scientific inquiry, politics, government, media, and education.

New voices, such as Maria Popova, the cultural curator behind Brain Pickings, and Big Think, a project based in New York that sifts through 'the best thinking on the planet,' are making creativity their central concern. Many TED talks share this creative focus. All these signs point towards a world where creativity takes center stage.

text:
Athletics, as we know it today, is often seen as a great equalizer in society, a meritocratic arena where anyone, regardless of their social class or wealth, can strive to become a professional athlete. Modern developed societies have made sports accessible through widespread youth programs and mandatory physical education classes that cater to individuals across the socioeconomic spectrum. However, this is starkly different from ancient Greece, where only a select few could participate in sports.

In ancient Greek cities, only male citizens were allowed to train and compete in sports. Even among this group, participation was limited by informal constraints. Most Greeks worked on small farms and lived close to subsistence level, with little time for long training sessions or extended absences to train at the gymnasiums located in city centers. This excluded a significant portion of the population: women, foreigners, non-Greeks, and poor men. Thus, athletics was closely associated with a specific political and socioeconomic group – relatively wealthy Greek males.

The Greeks themselves had little insight into the origins of athletic nudity, which was another striking aspect of their sports culture. Athletes in ancient Greece were always expected to train and compete naked. The word 'gymnasium' comes from the ancient Greek word 'gymnos', meaning 'naked'. While we no longer associate this term with its original meaning, it originally referred to a 'naked place'. The reasons for nude athletics remain elusive. Although many texts from classical Greece survive, they provide little insight into the meaning of nudity in society or art.

The few sources that touch on naked sport suggest that the Greeks themselves were unsure about its origins. Some attributed it to the Spartans and Cretans, implying that it set them apart as a civilized people. However, there is no clear explanation for how, when, or why this cultural practice emerged. Archaeological and iconographic evidence offers more insight. The earliest coherent collection of images of nude males in the Aegean takes the form of diminutive bronze figurines found in ritual deposits from just a few sites.

In the realm of events, we find chains and cascades of occurrences, shrouded in language's shadows. According to Koselleck, this is what we call 'history'. It's not driven by reason or a predetermined purpose as some might suggest, but rather it flows from a series of contingent events shaped by formal oppositions such as before and after, above and below, inner and outer.

History, in essence, lacks inherent meaning or direction. However, through careful analysis, we can start to make sense of its complexities and contradictions. The grid Koselleck proposed for understanding history - his theory of history - influences how historians approach their work, shaping their perspectives based on factors like time (contemporary or retrospective), social or political position, and connection to the community being studied.

For instance, a contemporary historian may view events through different lenses than someone recounting them later as an eyewitness or a narrator. Similarly, a historian's position in the social or political hierarchy can impact their interpretation of events, whether they are aligned with the winners or losers of a conflict. And finally, a historian's affiliation with the community being studied - be it political, religious, social, or economic - influences how objectively they can analyze the events.

Koselleck believed that there is a fundamental difference between his theory of history and hermeneutics (or Michel Foucault’s ‘discourse analysis’). He argued that not all experiences can be encapsulated by language, especially when intense emotions like friendship and enmity, power and subjugation, or gender inequality come into play. As he stated in a lecture honoring his teacher Hans-Georg Gadamer in 1985, these intense divisions create events that resist definition, leaving every word, sentence, or speech feeling inadequate.

It was this resistance to language that intrigued Koselleck as both a historian and theorist. Some of his most profound essays delve into the boundaries of language. For example, 'Terror and Dream', originally penned as an afterword for the German edition of Charlotte Beradt's The Third Reich of Dreams (1966; English translation 1968), is a compelling exploration of these limits. In this essay, subtitled ‘Methodological Remarks on the Experience of Time during the Third Reich’, Koselleck posits that dream protocols collected by Beradt provide a more authentic portrayal of life in Nazi Germany than any other historical source. Dreams, as pre-linguistic manifestations of terror, capture the reality of the Third Reich at this very moment, reflecting not just fear and humiliation from previous days or weeks but also the ongoing terror that was unfolding.

Graham Bateson's journey of self-discovery began with a letter to his father in 1925. A year prior, his parents had convinced him to call off his engagement to a Swiss woman. She wasn't English, she wasn't a scientist - therefore, she wasn't the right fit for him. To make amends, he was sent instead to the Galápagos Islands to replicate the fieldwork that Charles Darwin had carried out a century before. But Bateson found the locals more captivating than the wildlife. He felt compelled to break free from the constraints of ordinary science and delve into anthropology.

A decade later, on the opening page of Naven, he acknowledged that while an artist could capture the emotional essence or spirit of a society in just a few pages, anthropologists limited themselves to the technical aspects of their organization. Bateson aimed to bridge this gap between structure and emotion. As a scientist, he sought to encroach on the territory of art, or perhaps, practice science as an artist might.

In doing so, Bateson was challenging the deeply ingrained beliefs of Western civilization, which held that the scientific observer is entirely separate from their subject of study. "There are times," he mused, "when I find myself believing there's something that is separate from something else."

Naven gets its name from a series of bizarre rituals performed by the Iatmul people of New Guinea, which left Bateson baffled for years. In 1927, he ventured to the South Pacific, hoping to explore the relationship between individuality and culture, intending to debunk the British anthropological model that viewed human societies as biological organisms with behaviors entirely explainable through their collective needs.

But the rigid determinism of this model didn't sit well with Bateson. It failed to account for conflict among individuals, and overlooked the realms of aesthetics. The opposing behavior patterns within the tribe fueled escalating competition between men and women. When he arrived among these tribes, however, he was at a loss as to what to observe or how to approach his research. Other anthropologists had developed intricate questionnaires and aggressive methods of interrogation, but Bateson abhorred interference in others' lives. He believed that questionnaires implied a presumption of already knowing the vital questions.

In 1929, he settled along the Sepik River, alone with the Iatmul - a people who had recently given up cannibalism and built their culture around 'a constant emphasis on spectacle.' Bateson soon realized that the belligerent atmosphere in the tribe was affecting his own personality. He found himself growing rougher in his methods.

The concept I'd like to discuss is what I refer to as 'pro-extinctionist views.' While it might sound alarming, a surprising number of philosophers have expressed agreement with this perspective. To set the record straight, pro-extinctionists don't advocate for wanton destruction or disregard for human life. They understand that the prospect of our extinction is horrific and tragic, and they would never wish for billions to perish.

However, what sets pro-extinctionists apart is their belief that the state of Being Extinct may be preferable to the alternative - Being Extant or continuing to exist. This idea can stem from various perspectives, but one compelling argument suggests that if we cease to exist, we would also prevent future human suffering from occurring.

Cosmologist Carl Sagan, who espoused a further-loss view, estimated that if humanity survived on Earth for another 10 million years, over 500 trillion people could exist. If we consider space colonization, philosopher Nick Bostrom calculated the number of biological humans per century within the Virgo Supercluster to be 1 followed by 23 zeros. However, these numbers increase significantly when we factor in digital people living in virtual-reality simulations. Bostrom's calculations found that some 1058 digital people could exist throughout the universe as a whole.

The motivation behind these estimations was the further-loss view that by not existing, we would forfeit the opportunity to create value for all those future people. On the other hand, one could argue that even if these future people live fulfilling lives, the pain they experience throughout their lives could still accumulate to an enormous amount. Conversely, if we go extinct, none of that suffering will materialize.

Another aspect worth considering is the idea that some types of suffering can never be compensated for by happiness. Consider a painful surgical procedure that leaves you bedridden for a month, followed by 50 years of happiness. While you may have found the surgery and recovery process unbearable, you might still consider it 'worth it' due to the joy you experienced afterward.

However, when we look at some of the worst atrocities in the world - child abuse, genocides, ethnic cleansings, massacres - we realize that even decades of happiness may not outweigh the suffering these events entail. If we were to go extinct, none of this pain would come to pass.

Walking through San Marco, it was the way people held their hands that struck me. This mirroring created a sense of deep tranquility, like stepping into a new environment. It felt as if I'd entered a tangible medium – something I could immerse myself in, something that could transform the ambiance of a room, making everything seem sweeter, cooler, calmer, brighter than before. To greet the world with love, tenderness, and joy – this, I believe, is what Angelico's paintings suggest faith allows you to do.

Two thoughts crossed my mind then. The first was that I hadn't truly grasped the art of painting until that afternoon. What makes painting great, I realized, is its ability to create an atmosphere around itself, one that can affect you generations later. The second was that I hadn't fully contemplated the values of Angelico's world, hadn't decided to adopt them for myself. Instead, it felt like I was viewing things under a new light – the serene, radiant light of Angelico's paintings. It occurred to me that this is what faith means – not a set of beliefs you hold to be true or rules you follow, but an atmosphere you inhabit, one that alters your perception of the world, your understanding of its essence.

San Marco itself hinted at this connection. After all, wasn't that what the friars' daily lives were about – what those special rituals and practices were for? They were the tools used to cultivate and maintain an atmosphere of faith – that unique way of existing in the world. When I look at The Last Judgment now, it's not the promise of an afterlife or the idea of a just, orderly universe that moves me. It's the depiction of a world where everything matters, where everything has weight and significance. This, it seems to me, is the allure of faith: it imbues things with a profound, intrinsic worth.

I find myself drawn to a particular detail in the painting – a small angel in the garden of the blessed, tenderly embracing a friar who has just risen from his grave. To greet the world as the angel greets the friar, with love, tenderness, and joy – this, Angelico's paintings seem to suggest, is what faith empowers you to do. The question for a secular person is how, in the absence of faith, one can come to experience the world that way. Is it possible to maintain a sense of the sacred, even without belief? My experience at San Marco suggests an answer. We may not share the friars' beliefs. But perhaps, by adopting the practices they used to nurture their faith – by placing ourselves in situations where the sacred might be encountered – we can cultivate our own sense of the profound and meaningful.

text:
Title: A Personal Reflection on Philosophy: A Challenge to Wittgenstein

I've been grappling with philosophy lately, and I must say, it's a tricky beast. It's essential we don't dismiss it as mere nonsense, Wittgenstein; instead, we should take it seriously and acknowledge its potential to clarify our thoughts and actions. After all, the very core of philosophy is not that it's nonsensical, but that if it is, we need to address that, not pretend it's some important nonsense!

One of the most frustrating positions I encounter in philosophical discussions is reminiscent of a child saying they can't say 'breakfast'. When asked why, they respond with a shrug and a 'can't.' As Bertrand Russell famously quipped, 'But what we can't say, we can't say, and we can't whistle it either.' This is one of two objections I have to Wittgenstein's view that philosophy is nonsense and should be discarded.

Firstly, if Wittgenstein argues for a specific view on the nature of meaning, his argument itself becomes questionable, as it then becomes unclear what he's actually saying. We do understand Wittgenstein's philosophical argument, but it's not a ladder we can toss away once climbed, as he suggested in the Tractatus.

Secondly, this kind of philosophy leaves us intellectually impoverished. If, like Wittgenstein, we believe that philosophy is about arranging the propositions of science and everyday life into a logical system with basic terms and definitions, it's hard to see how this contributes much at all. In his notes from 1929, Wittgenstein himself acknowledged this: 'The standardization of beer colors is not philosophy, but in a sense, it is an improvement in notation and a clarification of thought.'

Both the Vienna Circle and Wittgenstein face these objections, although they did share one thing with him - an incredulity toward the idea that there's something inexpressible yet important. Ramsey, however, was starting to develop his own approach, his rebellion against Wittgenstein and the Vienna Circle. He summed it up perfectly: 'We cannot really picture the world as disconnected selves; the selves we know are in the world.'

Philosophy arises from misunderstanding the logic of our language, but the logic of our language is not what Wittgenstein thought. The mental images we construct aren't pictures of facts. If a proposition is a picture of the world, disconnected from any reference to the self whose picture it is, we become vulnerable to skepticism or solipsism. How can we bridge the gap between ourselves and that world? How can we make claims about that world at all?

Wittgenstein's 'world' contains no thought. If we want to understand the world, we must not ignore the subjective side. Carnap, too, seems to have made this mistake. In the ordinary sense, solipsism suggests that only one's own mind is real; Wittgenstein and Carnap both seem to imply this in their philosophies. But Ramsey thought differently: 'What we can’t do, we can’t do, and it’s no good trying.'

In conclusion, philosophy should not be dismissed as nonsense. Instead, we must challenge its ideas and strive for a more holistic understanding that includes the subjective side of our human experience. Only then will we bridge the gap between ourselves and the world around us.

text:
To truly focus and immerse oneself is crucial, especially when it comes to chess. As my passion for the game waned, I felt a part of myself fading away too. If we can't concentrate, we miss out on the state of consciousness - known as "flow" - that is integral to the chess experience. Flow was introduced and popularized by Hungarian-American psychologist Mihaly Csikszentmihalyi, and it's characterized by deep absorption, loss of self-awareness, goal-oriented feedback from the environment, and altered perception of time. Experiences in flow are extremely rewarding, but only when our skills match the challenge level; too little challenge and we get bored, too much and we feel anxious. Chess offers a wonderful opportunity to achieve flow, yet it's important to remember that flow is more about the quality of consciousness rather than a method for achieving it. Flow is not virtue, but pleasure. While experiencing flow is desirable, promoting it might lead to undesirable consequences; a society of sophisticated hedonists addicted to gaming and suffering from virtual reality sickness is not far-fetched.

Concentration, on the other hand, prompts an awareness of mood, a commitment to meaning, and an appreciation for process. Chess is a unique platform where system meets psychology, and we need to better understand the dynamics of this intersection in today's world. text:
It's a shame that this winter, with its blankets of snow, isn't accompanied by some humility from us humans - a deeper connection to the planet we call home. Snowy owls aren't toys or characters in fantasy stories; they're messengers from places many of us will never visit.

Sure, technology has made the Earth feel smaller and more accessible. From our cozy homes, we can virtually trek the rugged slopes of Ben Nevis, mingle with elephants across the vast Serengeti, or float down the Colorado River beneath the Grand Canyon's vibrant walls. But let's face it, the virtual is no substitute for the real experience. Most of us will never see a pod of beluga whales breaking through icy Arctic waters or caribou migrating across expansive tundra. Polar bears won't be paying us a visit this winter, nor any other. Instead, we have snowy owls. From the remnants of wild places, no such messengers will come.

Once, tallgrass prairies covered the American Great Plains, providing a home for ornate wildflowers and grasses as tall as us. But now, they're mostly gone, swallowed up by plows and pavement. Bison won't be venturing from the remaining patches to the Rust Belt, nor will the regal fritillary butterfly, with its chestnut and peach wings adorned with glowing silver spots, depart its dwindling habitats to visit our suburbs and shopping malls.

No ornate butterfly fish, as bright as a snowy owl's eyes, will swim from the coral reefs to remind us of the biodiversity we're losing. Coral reefs, once teeming with life for millions around the world, are now critically endangered - victims of overfishing, pollution, and ocean acidification. No arm of coral from the Great Barrier Reef will visit the Northeast this winter.

Maybe snowy owls can represent those other earth-bound emissaries for us. Whether we watch them through binoculars or on a screen, perhaps they can remind us of the world - from the Highlands to the Serengeti, from the Arctic to the ocean. Or at least, they might help us find humility. Over a century ago, Aldo Leopold found his humility in another predator. In his early years as a forest ranger, the great conservationist and philosopher rarely passed a day without feeling a sense of connection to the natural world around him.

We need that same connection today - not just to appreciate the beauty of nature, but to understand our role in preserving it for future generations. Snowy owls may be messengers from distant lands, but they're also reminders of what we stand to lose if we don't act now to protect our planet.

KARACHI: Despite three rate cuts by the central bank this fiscal year, private sector lending contracted by 36.5 percent during the first nine months of the 2014/15 fiscal year, according to data released on Tuesday. Analysts attribute this contraction to banks' heavy investment in government securities and low borrowing costs, which have not encouraged businesses or consumers to invest and spend.

Compared to the same period last fiscal year, loans disbursed to various firms decreased from Rs281.9 billion to Rs178.9 billion between July 1, 2014, and April 3, 2015. The central bank reported this figure.

Analysts argue that banks' heavy investment in the government treasury has reduced the supply of lendable funds for the private sector despite falling borrowing costs. Since the beginning of 2014, banks have been actively investing in long bills and purchasing Pakistan Investment Bonds (PIBs) due to their high returns. Following low inflation and a positive outlook on the external sector, the cumulative discount rate has been reduced by 200 basis points since November 2014.

However, the government disputes analysts' claims that banks are uninterested in providing loans to the private sector and prefer to park their funds in government papers. In its latest letter of intent to the IMF, the government stated that private sector credit has continued its healthy expansion (10.2 percent so far in FY15), driven primarily by businesses, particularly manufacturing. Some analysts predict that bank lending will begin expanding in the coming quarters. Within different financing segments, a moderate pickup is expected in consumer loans.

To address these issues, the State Bank, with the assistance of World Bank experts, has developed a comprehensive National Financial Inclusion Strategy (NFIS) to implement financial sector reforms aimed at meeting the financing needs of various sectors. The central bank plans to launch the NFIS by the end of this year after overcoming complex legal, taxation, and border trade requirements, as well as impaired access to finance.

text:
The elusive nature of connections between various elements, and a longing to break free from the divide between imagination and reality, were the driving forces behind the surrealists' philosophy. This discourse was delivered several years before Éluard's visit to Saint-Alban, and the artists he spoke about were indeed the surrealists - Max Ernst among them, a close friend of Éluard. Yet, I believe the description could just as effortlessly apply to Forestier, whose work seems to embody this fusion of freedom and unity.

I suspect that Éluard saw more than just artistic vision in Forestier's work; he may have glimpsed a broader ambition that he himself championed through the surrealist movement - one that 'refuses to serve an absurd order based upon inequality,' and aims for 'the total emancipation of humanity.'

While the practice of isolating distressed and disabled individuals in secure institutions may not be as prevalent today as it once was, the mindset that disregards their plight persists. It's evident in media outlets that provide a platform for statements like: 'Two-thirds of COVID-19 fatalities would have probably died within the year anyway.' It rears its head when the murders of disabled individuals by their caretakers are labeled as 'mercy killings.' And it's invoked when lawyers claim that a man, gasping for breath with a police officer's knee crushing his airway, died from 'underlying health conditions.'

Many readers might feel uncomfortable at the notion that the current system of reality, prevalent in Europe and America today, could be described as 'fascism.' As I once did, many prefer to view fascism as a relic of the past, defeated 80 years ago in Europe and lying dormant since then, except for the occasional outbursts of a few extremists. Others might understand fascism as an enduring threat, but see it as a distinct segment of the political spectrum, primarily promoted by white supremacists and ethno-nationalists we've heard so much about in recent years.

However, the concept that fascism should be viewed as something more profound and pervasive - something that exists within as well as without - has a rich history. In her 1940 essay on Homer's Iliad, Simone Weil observed the strong's indifference towards the weak and pondered on the use of force throughout human history: 'Pushed to its extreme, it transforms a person into a mere thing - a corpse while they are still alive.'

In the aftermath of the war, Max Horkheimer and Theodor Adorno were clear that fascism had found continuity in the blend of bureaucracy and capitalism that defined the post-war world, a political system that 'relieves its people of the burden of moral feelings,' and 'is deeply in harmony with pure reason ... treating human beings as objects.'

text:
The residential schools system, once hailed as philanthropic, was a grim reality that marked a shift from a policy of annihilation to "saving" through assimilation. Sir John A. Macdonald and Duncan Campbell Scott, key figures in this era, held beliefs that the white man knew what was best for Indigenous children - superior even to their own families and communities. The ultimate goal was clear: assimilate them completely until there was no Native person left in Canada.

During a parliamentary committee in 1920, Duncan Campbell Scott, Canada's Deputy Superintendent of Indian Affairs, made it explicit: "Our aim is to continue until there isn't a single Indian in Canada who hasn't been absorbed into the body politic."

Today, some may question how such practices of separation and indoctrination could have been considered progressive or benevolent just a century ago. Yet, these weren't mere accidents or historical anomalies; they were deliberate and presented as enlightened and humanitarian. The residential schools weren't the only means by which these supposedly progressive and compassionate methods of separation and re-education were implemented. Forced adoptions and 'care' systems, such as the 'Stolen Generations' in Australia and the 'Sixties Scoop' in Canada, demonstrate that these progressive practices have persisted and evolved.

The fact that Indigenous children today are disproportionately represented in care systems worldwide suggests that these practices continue to be enacted.

These ideas behind the Indigenous residential schools didn't emerge out of thin air. They were part of a longer history of Eurocentrism, whether informed by Christianity, Social Darwinism, or today's neoliberalism. In this mindset, peoples of color were viewed as inferior or 'Other.'

One of the roots of this perspective can be traced back to the papal bull Inter caetera (1493), issued by Pope Alexander VI. This document allowed colonizing Europeans "full and free power, authority, and jurisdiction of every kind" over non-Europeans, effectively permitting their dispossession, enslavement, and mass murder. The papal bull is part of the so-called 'doctrine of discovery,' a set of legal and religious precepts that certain European nations believed granted them free reign to colonize the world.

For many Indigenous people in Canada, Pope Francis' 2022 apology for the abuses at the country's residential schools was a long-awaited step towards healing and reconciliation. But the scars left by this dark chapter in history remain deeply ingrained, serving as a stark reminder of the ongoing impact of Eurocentric beliefs and practices on Indigenous communities worldwide.

text:
Concentration is key to honing my focus, and focusing is crucial for success in chess. As my passion for the game started to wane, so did a piece of me fade away. You see, without concentration, we can't experience the "flow" state - a vital part of chess play that psychologist Mihaly Csikszentmihalyi coined and popularized. Flow is characterized by intense focus, loss of self-awareness, clear feedback from the environment, and an altered sense of time. These experiences are incredibly rewarding, but they only occur when our skills meet the challenge at hand - too little challenge, we get bored; too much, we feel overwhelmed. Chess is a great way to access flow, but it has its limitations as a guiding principle for life. Primarily, it describes a state of consciousness rather than a method to achieve it. Ultimately, flow is not a virtue, but a source of pleasure. While being in a flow state is desirable, promoting it might lead to undesirable characteristics - think an atomized society obsessed with gaming and virtual reality.

As a young player, I used various methods to maintain my focus, such as taking long walks before games or listening to favorite music tracks. These techniques worked because the purpose of concentration was clear. However, in early December 2008, in a hotel room in Palma de Mallorca, I remember feeling strangely detached from myself while trying to prepare for a game. As American football coaches have said: "The will to win is not as important as the will to prepare to win." And it seemed like I had lost that drive. It felt like the foundation of my motivation - to concentrate on chess - was crumbling, and with it, a piece of myself. The will to maintain the identity that fueled my competitive spirit had vanished, and I knew it was time to shift my focus from the game itself to what it represented.

Chess is a unique battleground where systems meet psychology, and today's world needs a better understanding of this complex encounter. To make good moves in chess, you need to see the whole board with all its shifting dynamics. But you also need to understand your own mind - its power and limitations. The personal is political, and vice versa. In the early 21st century, the position we find ourselves in includes a growing ecological crisis (as material-intensive economic growth remains the world's dominant strategy), the challenge of avoiding mass unemployment in an age of rising AI, and protecting truth itself. These are complex challenges that require focus, determination, and a clear understanding of our own minds - as well as the systems we inhabit.

text:
Whales and their history have always fascinated us, especially with remnants from the whaling era still around like old pieces of baleen. Baleen was much like plastic in its day, used to make everything from corsets to brooms to buggy whips. Museums seem to have an abundance of it tucked away in dusty corners. Recently, I've been flooded with collaborative offers and heard whispers of at least half a dozen labs exploring baleen analysis. As one scientist put it, 'everybody with a hormone lab is jumping on this.'

Baleen serves as a long-term logbook of a whale's travels and a reliable seasonal chronometer. The stress hormone cortisol has attracted a great deal of attention within wildlife biology due to its potential to reveal harmful influences. Although bowhead populations are thriving, they face rapid changes in their habitats as sea ice melts, shipping traffic increases, and oil and gas exploration activities pick up speed in the Arctic. The ultimate aim of our baleen research is to develop methods that allow us to assess stress levels related to environmental change and human activity.

Interestingly, other informative compounds are being discovered in baleen as well. Research dating back to the 1980s has shown that variations in the ratio of stable carbon and nitrogen isotopes along a bowhead's baleen reflect the distinct prey they consume in their summer versus winter habitats. Essentially, baleen serves as a long-term logbook of a whale's travels and a reliable seasonal chronometer. We are examining this pattern in bowhead baleen dating back to 1952 to see if the whales' diet and migrations are changing, possibly due to shrinking sea ice.

In collaboration with others, Hunt and I are studying isotope and hormone data from the same baleen samples to confirm bowheads' gestation period and calving frequency. We believe we can determine not just when but roughly where individual bowheads got pregnant. In some instances, bowhead bodies themselves have become historical artifacts. Native Alaskan hunters have discovered remarkably old whaling gear in slaughtered animals. For example, a stone tool from the 1880s was found in the 1990s, leaving me speechless when I first heard about it. Over the years, hunters have found a few other items, but dating stone tools can be unreliable. Then in 2007, a Yankee-era harpoon point with an 1879 patent was discovered lodged in another whale's shoulder, placing the whale's age at approximately 115 to 130 years. Our chemical analyses of bowhead eye lenses eventually indicated they can live to 200 or older. Studies of keratin-based tissues in other marine animals suggest that baleen may have even greater potential for future research. For instance, a 2012 paper demonstrated this possibility.

Discussing the fascinating world of penile anomalies among amniotes, let's consider the curious cases of spines, bones, or even double heads found in various species - some even sharing these traits simultaneously. This variety has left researchers puzzled about the number of times the penis may have evolved in this group. A standout example is the avian kingdom, where a mere 3% possess functional penises; ducks being the most renowned for their explosive, corkscrew-like counterparts. The remaining birds make do with a structure that serves little purpose or lack one entirely. In vertebrates devoid of a penis but still capable of internal fertilization, the solution lies in the cloaca - Latin for 'sewer'. This multi-purpose structure handles the exit of bodily fluids, including semen. Animals can perform what is affectionately known as the 'cloacal kiss' to facilitate semen transfer by bringing their respective cloacas together. Among reptiles, this practice is rare, with only one species - the tuatara - employing it. Given the tuatara's ancient lineage dating back to the dinosaur era, the absence of a penis suggests that subsequent lineages developed the appendage. However, the diversity in penile structures among amniotes raises the question: How many times did the penis evolve anew? The responses have been inconsistent, with few proposing a single origin.

The enigma was finally unraveled by studying the tuatara - or rather, ancient samples of tuatara embryos. In the early 20th century, a zoologist named Arthur Dendy, working in New Zealand, sent some eggs from local tuatara nests to a US museum. Decades passed before these specimens were rediscovered, preserved in time. Their revival provided fascinating insights: the embryos were at the right developmental stage for researchers to observe whether the animals initiate a rudimentary penis before discarding it, similar to how human embryos develop and later lose their tails. Upon recreating these tuatara embryos digitally, scientists observed that, where penises begin to form, the tuatara embryo indeed started to develop one. However, living tuataras born around the time these eggs were collected demonstrate that this initial penis never fully materializes - it's a false start in their development.

This evidence from the tuatara provides conclusive proof that a penis, whether it survives into adulthood or not, is rooted at the base of the amniote family tree. In other words, the genetic blueprint for creating a penis evolved only once among our lineage. The process for building one appears to be consistent across different amniotes, using the same raw materials as us.

Putnam and David Kaplan challenged the prevailing notion by suggesting that many terms refer not through objects matching a mental criteria list but directly. According to Kripke, when we introduce a proper name like "Aristotle," it's usually connected straight to an individual. For instance, during a baptism ceremony, we might say: "By 'Aristotle,' we mean him [pointing]." Our intention is that the name should then refer to that person in any actual or hypothetical situation, even when they may not fit the descriptions associated with the name.

Let's imagine a possible scenario where Aristotle took up gardening while his brother was taught by Plato and taught Alexander. That didn't happen, but it could have. Kripke notes that if "Aristotle" simply meant 'the student of Plato and teacher of Alexander,' then the name would point to the wrong individual in that hypothetical situation. It seems plausible that the actual scenario might one day be discovered to be one where Aristotle had few or none of the attributes we attribute to him. Historians could potentially unearth evidence suggesting that Aristotle was a fraud who claimed credit for others' achievements and managed to deceive people. In such a case, it's clear that the name 'Aristotle' can't only mean 'the student of Plato and teacher of Alexander.' Otherwise, it would be a straightforward truth rather than a debatable historical fact that Aristotle was taught by Plato and taught Alexander. Kripke posits that many common nouns, such as 'gold,' 'water,' and 'tiger,' also refer directly, not through any sort of mental checklist. We define 'gold' by stating: "Gold is this substance [pointing to a sample]" and letting the world determine what qualifies as gold rather than any associated description.

Perhaps the most famous example demonstrating how a substance name functions in this manner comes not from Kripke, but Putnam, another leading advocate of the 'theory of direct reference.' Putnam encourages us to envision a parallel Earth – much like ours – where doppelgängers of humans reside. The only difference between our worlds is that on Twin Earth, the liquid filling their oceans, lakes, and rivers isn't H2O, but another substance – XYZ. Suppose it's 1750, before the chemical composition of water was discovered. On both Earths, inhabitants call their liquid 'water,' associating the same mental checklist with that term: considering water as clear, thirst-quenching, boiling at 100°C, and so on. Now imagine a glass of XYZ from Twin Earth being transported to our Earth and presented to Locke. Locke would believe it was water because it would match his mental checklist. But would it be?

In this revised essay, I've attempted to present the ideas in a more conversational style while maintaining clarity and flow. I hope you find it thought-provoking as you explore the fascinating realm of direct reference theories!

While one could argue that these animals are degraded symbolically through such uses, it's questionable whether they're aware of the symbolism or care about it. I find myself less troubled by the 'artificial' nature of GloFish, a genetically modified fish with fluorescent genes, than by the physical deformities and crippling conditions we've created in goldfish through selective breeding over the years. The good news is that progress is being made; more nations are banning these cruel practices, and organizations like the UK's Kennel Club have revised their breed standards to prioritize canine health. As we appreciate an animal for its looks, it's important to acknowledge that aesthetics is just one way we assign value to the natural world. We should avoid deceiving ourselves into believing that our actions—like painting a dog's nails—are motivated by the animal's enjoyment or preference. There's a lot of goodness in the world, but much of it has been fueled by economic activities that rely on suppressing debate and undermining people to thrive. Draping this kind of neoliberalism with a philosophical veil, like Popperian science, doesn't hurt its image one bit. As I delve deeper into understanding Popper, I can't help but think about the anti-Semitic sentiments that forced him to flee Nazi Austria in the 1930s. Today, we see similar vitriol directed at George Soros, with antisemitic slurs that are as absurd as they are chilling, given their historical roots and the threat of continued violence they echo. It's essential to remember why Popper advocated for an open society and sought to cleanse science from the dark stains left by Nazi researchers.

The casual conflation of fascist and socialist science as a foe to Popperianism—sometimes deliberate, sometimes unconscious—is hard to empathize with. A quick scroll through the internet reveals instances where Popperian principles are used by climate change deniers. The comparison of scientific theories competing in an open market distorts the reality that research ambitions and funding choices are heavily influenced by politics, both big and small. It's no coincidence that more progress has been made in treating diseases associated with wealth rather than poverty. Furthermore, success in science—which shapes future research agendas when a person becomes a leader in their field—is deeply affected by factors like gender, race, class, and ability.

Scientists rebuked Popper's distinction between science and ethics in Science for the People (1974). Some unscrupulous researchers even used a Popperian frame to become exactly the 'wicked scientists' that Medawar denied existed. As Naomi Oreskes and Erik Conway detail in Merchants of Doubt (2010), scientists in the US and UK were manipulated by tobacco companies during the late 20th century to cast doubt on research linking smoking to cancer. Since no such link could be 'proven' in Popperian terms, this doubt was ruthlessly exploited by their paymasters. Many of these same scientists later worked for fossil fuel lobbyists, casting doubt on the science of anthropogenic climate change. A search on a search engine will uncover numerous examples of Popperianism wielded by deniers. In a 2019 YouTube video, the Clear Energy Alliance (a group DeSmog Blog identifies as funded by oil interests) invoked the 'legendary scientific philosopher Karl Popper'. Their central claim is that: "In order to know if a theory could be true, there must be a way to prove it false. Unfortunately, many climate change scientists, the media, and activists are ignoring this cornerstone of science."

text:
I often find myself feeling down or anxious, and it's hard to ignore those feelings. We all have an unlimited window into our own minds, so it's easy to assume we know ourselves better than anyone else. This idea is known as the 'privileged access' view of self-knowledge, a concept that has been popular in philosophy for much of the 20th century. René Descartes was one of its biggest proponents, famously stating "I think, therefore I am," and noting that he could perceive his own mind more clearly than anything else.

However, some philosophers argue that we don't really know ourselves as well as we think. Instead, they suggest we make inferences about our thoughts and feelings based on various cues, just like we would when trying to understand others. For example, if my heart is racing and I'm breathing heavily before a presentation, I might assume I'm anxious. But what if I'm actually excited? Sports coaches often use this kind of psychological reframing to help athletes keep their cool under pressure.

The philosopher Gilbert Ryle was one of the first to propose this idea in The Concept of Mind (1949), suggesting that we can only truly understand ourselves by applying the same tools we use to understand others to ourselves. He said, "The things I can find out about myself are the same as the things I can find out about other people, and the methods of finding them out are much the same."

Many philosophers have dismissed this idea as ridiculous, but if we look back at history, the idea that we have special, direct access to our own minds is actually the exception rather than the rule. For ancient Greeks, self-knowledge was a work in progress, something to be strived for, as expressed by the famous saying "know thyself" carved on the Temple of Delphi. This implies that most of us don't really know ourselves very well.

This view persisted into medieval religious traditions, with Saint Thomas Aquinas suggesting that self-knowledge was not all-encompassing but required effort and reflection. So, while we may think we have privileged access to our own minds, it seems that throughout history, understanding ourselves has been a journey rather than a destination.

Reflecting on the past five years, I find myself pondering the Shroud of Turin, a revered artifact for many devout Catholics, despite its questionable age. It's been infused with such profound faith over the last seven centuries that it has transcended its dubious origins and become significant. Some may argue that it began in falsehood, but through the collective belief of the faithful, it has taken on a deeper meaning. This year alone, over a million people have made reservations to view it.

While the imperfectly ancient brushwork on a fragment of papyrus from the 20th century might suggest forgery, the so-called 'Wife of Jesus' fragment hasn't been as big a draw for Catholic pilgrims since its debut in 2012. This Coptic papyrus, where Jesus refers to 'my wife', stands in stark contrast to the Turin shroud. Instead of affirming the synoptic gospels, it challenges Rome's most cherished beliefs about Christ—suggesting that Jesus valued his female disciples and didn't prioritize males over them, as well as questioning the celibacy ideal for Christian clerics.

The 'Wife of Jesus' fragment's composition and syntax are suspicious, with awkward use of indirect objects in Coptic brushwork where a reed pen would typically be used. While some may dismiss it as a fraud, its discovery has sparked renewed interest in the role of women among Christ's disciples—the Marys, Junia (an apostle), and Phoebe, whom Paul described as a deacon in the New Testament.

Joel Baden of Yale and Candida Moss of the University of Notre Dame wrote in The Atlantic last year that: "For the most part, texts and narratives supporting female discipleship come from outside the traditional canon—no surprise given that the canonical New Testament was compiled long after Jesus' death by a male-led Church."

If the 'Wife of Jesus' fragment is indeed a forgery, it serves a purpose. It aims to reclaim religious experience from those who would define it as a male-dominated realm and presents an image of Christ who grants women equal status among his closest followers in worship.

The American West has long been fertile ground for those seeking truth or profit—this is why archaeology plays such a significant role in the Mormon Church, which depends heavily on questionable history. For instance, there are tales of golden plates in upstate New York to which, around 1823, an angel named Moroni guided Joseph Smith, who later founded the Latter Day Saint movement.

text:
In this exploration, I delve into two key aspects of practical reason as employed by the Stoics in their pursuit of a life free from suffering. The indirect function, on one hand, involves thoughtful planning and foresight that enables the Stoic to choose the path through life with less pain. On the other side, there's the direct function, where instead of attempting to remove or circumvent life's challenges, the Stoic alters their perspective towards these hurdles, thus modifying their emotional response. One is about practicing differently, while the other revolves around altering one's thinking. The unique contribution of Stoicism in ethics stems from the nature of mental change it encourages, asserts Schopenhauer.

Firstly, the Stoic recognizes that painful feelings of lack don't stem directly from not having, but rather from desiring to have and not acquiring it. This insight suggests that to eradicate these pains entirely, we need to eliminate our desires. Additionally, the larger our ambitions regarding what we want and the higher our hopes of achieving them, the more intense the disappointment when we fall short. If we can't help but desire certain things, then we should at least make sure our desires are realistic and attainable. Schopenhauer warns against expecting an overabundance of happiness in the future, as we're likely to be setting ourselves up for disappointment. "Every intense pleasure is a delusion," he remarks, drawing on his pessimistic outlook. In the same vein, he likens our pursuit of happiness to the application of a blister remedy – it only offers temporary relief.

The Stoic strives for ataraxia, a state of inner calmness and serenity amidst life's tumultuous events. Schopenhauer believes that acknowledging the inevitability of suffering can help attain this goal. Pain and suffering hurt more when we perceive them as accidental or avoidable. Although it might be true that any specific suffering could have been avoided, suffering as a whole is unavoidable and universal. If we can internalize this truth, Schopenhauer suggests, we may worry less about experiencing suffering or at least approach it with the same acceptance we have for other unavoidable aspects of life, such as aging (for most of us) and death.

The last piece of advice is to avoid believing that the world owes us happiness, rather than suffering. If we believe the world is supposed to make us happy, we're setting ourselves up for disappointment, especially since achieving our perceived sources of happiness often leads to new unfulfilled desires taking the place of old ones. Moreover, we'll harbor resentment towards the obstacles that prevent us from attaining the happiness we feel entitled to. As Schopenhauer observes, some individuals externalize this resentment by blaming others for their perceived shortcomings.

text:
In Orwell's 1984, Winston's rebellion is a quiet, personal act - sneaking away from the ever-watchful screens to jot down thoughts in his hidden diary, just like a teenager craving solitude with her headphones blasting music in her locked bedroom. That is, until he meets Julia. It isn't until romance enters the picture that Winston's rebellion truly comes alive - dangerously so. It's almost as if adolescence is the only time real freedom can start. 1984 holds a special place not for its political commentary, but for its portrayal of the inner turmoil of growing up. The book's enduring appeal lies in its insight into the futility of teenage defiance. Every teenager hates this truth - many even dislike the ending - but it lingers because they know deep down it's true.

In Orwell's contemporary work, Animal Farm (1945), the animals we empathize with experience their ultimate betrayal: the pigs taking up residence in their former masters' house. However, in 1984, it's Winston who ultimately gives in. He comes to love Big Brother because, like adult society, Big Brother has mastered the manipulation of language, power, and thought to preserve its system. We're born into this world, and we lose before we even begin. It's a grim, perhaps cynical perspective - but it resonates with teenagers who see their own lives mirrored in 1984's pages. A dystopian novel with a happy ending might be more entertaining to read, but the book you pass down to your children is the one that first opened your eyes to reality. When a child asks for the same book over and over again, they are essentially asking to learn, to confront what they must accept. As adults, we do the same thing. Books act as therapists, using imagination to unearth secrets we didn't know we harbored. A child may not realize the implications of naming a doll 'mummy' or 'daddy', or having them argue. Similarly, we often fail to recognize the revelations we make when we push a book into someone else's hands. Maybe the best-seller list, devoid of fleeting sensations and dopamine highs, is a reflection of the collective consciousness. Perhaps it's trying to tell us what we need to learn, what we are coming to terms with. There's one thing I am sure of: I hope my daughter doesn't develop the same attachment to The Carrot Seed as I did.

text:
René Descartes' dog, Monsieur Grat (or 'Mister Scratch'), was a frequent companion on the philosopher's contemplative walks during the 17th century. Despite this close bond, Descartes often undervalued the inner lives of nonhuman animals. In a letter from 1646, he stated that animals do not speak as we do because they lack thoughts, not vocal organs. This perspective, which still lingers in some circles today, posits that consciousness is a uniquely human trait facilitated by language and communication. According to this view, animals are seen as nothing more than sophisticated machines with pre-programmed behaviors triggered by specific stimuli or conditions. Insects such as bees and ants have been used to illustrate this mechanistic approach to animal nature. While we know that these creatures possess a vast behavioral repertoire necessary for building their complex nests, defending against threats, and caring for their young, many continue to view them as reflex machines with no consciousness or awareness of the world around them. In the absence of external stimuli or internal triggers such as hunger, it's assumed that their minds are empty and their brains switched off.

However, there is growing evidence suggesting that consciousness-like phenomena may not only exist in humans but also in insects. Not all of this evidence comes from experiments specifically designed to explore consciousness; in fact, some has been overlooked for decades or even centuries. Recently, several biologists (Eva Jablonka in Tel Aviv and Andrew Barron in Sydney) and philosophers (Peter Godfrey-Smith in Sydney and Colin Klein in Canberra) have argued that these consciousness-like phenomena might be much older than previously thought. They propose that consciousness may have evolved during the Cambrian era, around 500 million years ago, suggesting an ancient evolutionary origin.

The argument is that consciousness could have been an adaptation that helped early life forms survive and thrive by allowing them to process and react to their environment more effectively. In other words, consciousness might have provided a survival advantage. This challenges the notion that consciousness evolved relatively late in our history as previously thought. It's possible that we underestimate the complexity of insect minds because we struggle to understand them on our own terms. Perhaps their minds are simply beyond our comprehension due to our limited understanding of even the simplest brains and how they process, store, and retrieve information.

text:
The robotic explorers touch down on Mars, their descent cloaked by the warm hues of dust storms. They slice through the thin, frosty air in aeroshells and parachutes, landing gently within cushioned airbags and the whoosh of braking rockets. Armed with cameras, spectrometers, antennas, and manipulator arms, they are on a tireless quest for any trace of Martian moisture - like thirsty travelers lost in the desert. Their mission is guided by NASA's 'follow the water' strategy, as water has an uncanny affinity for life: wherever it exists on Earth, life isn't far behind. There are compelling reasons to believe that water serves as the foundation of biology not only here on our planet but across the cosmos. Hydrogen and oxygen, respectively the first and third most abundant elements in the universe, are all you need to create it. In its liquid state, water is unmatched in its ability to support life's necessities. It facilitates the transmission of biochemical energy, nutrients, and waste, shapes protein structures and interactions, shields against cosmic radiation, and retains heat remarkably well. Interestingly, unlike most substances in the universe, water expands when it freezes, forming an insulating layer of ice that prevents oceans, lakes, and other liquid reservoirs from solidifying during prolonged cold spells.

For researchers studying Mars, following the water has been a fruitful endeavor. Every mission sent to Mars in search of water has found it, and we now understand that our neighboring planet was once a warmer, wetter, more hospitable world. However, billions of years ago, this changed as Mars cooled, lost most of its atmosphere and water, and fell into a quiet slumber. Yet present-day Mars still harbors a dormant aquifer, locked beneath the surface as ice, which may awaken periodically, gushing to the surface in temporary salty flows.

The next phase of NASA's Mars exploration program is to 'seek signs of life.' Unofficially, it seems that this journey began with the successful landing of the car-sized Curiosity rover in the summer of 2012. While Curiosity can detect organic carbon in samples of Martian rock and soil, it appears to be primarily following the water. So far, it has traversed what looks like an ancient riverbed and found evidence suggesting that some of Mars's ancient water was drinkable. However, whether there is or ever was anything on Mars worthy of a drink remains an unexpectedly unanswered question for Curiosity, despite its ample time to explore. Thanks to its nuclear power source, it should be able to continue its quest for quite some time yet.

Understanding the human mind is a complex and multifaceted task, often likened to Maturana's concept of 'observer dependence.' This means that our perception and representation of the world, information processing, and response decisions are primarily influenced by our own perspective, not the system we're observing. The brain's inherent activity follows its own logic, a mystery that neuroscientists, like Buzsáki, argue should be the primary focus – understanding this logic from within, or 'inside-out.'

Fifteen years ago, I coined the term 'anthropogenic approach' for cognition rooted in human experiences and reflections. This is similar to what Buzsáki refers to as 'outside-in.' Cognitivism, despite suggesting that cognition can manifest in various physical forms, remains anthropogenic due to its derivation from our numerical computing abilities. The counterpoint I named the 'biogenic approach,' which prioritizes biology as the source of cognition and embraces an 'inside-out' perspective. If our goal is to truly understand human cognition, this biogenic/inside-out path seems the most promising, helping us move beyond a stagnant journey down a dead-end road.

Over the past 70 years, we've invested significantly – financially, intellectually, and effort-wise – into understanding cognition. Yet, our progress has been disappointingly slow. Think of how much society has evolved since the 1950s; countless beliefs have crumbled, and knowledge has exploded across various fields. We can now scientifically justify seeing ourselves in a daffodil, an earthworm, even a bacterium.

The Human Genome Project was initially expected to provide a genetic 'blueprint.' Instead, when the first draft was published 21 years ago, we realized how much remained unknown. For instance, there's too much so-called 'junk DNA' for it to be truly worthless; humans share surprisingly many genes with plants; and our genome contains genes transferred by single-celled organisms. As more whole genomes were compared, we discovered that among these shared genes were those involved in animal central nervous systems, including ours. Moreover, a critical system for human survival – the autonomous immune defense mechanisms at the cellular level – may have been inherited from bacteria or its related kingdom, archaea, potentially millions of years ago.

All this brings us to cognition. It turns out that our memory and learning processes rely on interactions with neurons stimulated by cytokines, elements of the immune system. A real surprise indeed! In the 1950s (and later), the brain was thought to be 'immune privileged,' off-limits to the immune system. Yet, we're still struggling to fully understand this interaction.

text:
Genes have long been at the heart of mainstream biological thought, with the discovery and gradual unraveling of the double helix only serving to solidify their central role. The fusion of genetics with molecular biology has indeed spawned a powerful new science. By untangling the molecular interactions within cells, we've been able to create drugs that mimic or interfere with specific processes. The discovery of enzymes responsible for DNA synthesis and editing paved the way for genetic engineering, which is often discussed in terms of its commercial applications, but whose most common use has always been in pure research. In this way, advancements in knowledge give rise to tools that further our understanding, creating a virtuous circle.

However, we seem to have reached a crossroads. As we delve deeper into the molecular processes, the gene-centric perspective becomes increasingly obscure. In the early days of genetics, scientists studying various animals would identify genes and associate them with body-scale features. Yet, they had no way of knowing whether the genes they identified in one species were the same as those known in another. With the advent of technologies for reading DNA sequences, researchers could 'read' their own genes and notice when genes in two different species were remarkably similar. This often yielded surprises. For instance, the gene 'Wingless', initially thought to be a blueprint for making wings (a name given by fruitfly geneticists based on what happens when the gene is damaged), turned out to be essentially the same as Int-1, a gene identified by cancer specialists working with mice and associated with mouse breast cancer. However, Wnt, the protein that these genes code for, isn't really about making wings or causing breast cancer; it's about transmitting signals between cells – the meaning of the signal depends on the cell and organism receiving it. This is just one of many examples. The notion of 'the gene for feature x' is giving way to a much more complex narrative. Consider something like: 'the gene for protein a, which interacts with proteins b, c, and d to enable a cell to carry out process p, which in turn allows the cell to coordinate with other cells to produce body feature x'. The length of this phrase and the weakness of the blueprint metaphor underscore a conceptual chasm opening up between the molecular-scale function of genes and the intriguing large-scale features of bodies.

Genes are important, of course – they're essential because something has to build all these proteins. But the double helix seems less and less fitting as an emblem for the complex control systems that govern life, especially at larger scales (cells, tissues, organisms, populations, ecosystems, etc.). There is, however, an alternative. It can be symbolized by an even simpler icon than...

text:
In the heart of Germany, during the late 18th century, a small town named Jena stood out as a beacon of intellectual freedom. One professor, speaking of this place, remarked, "Here, we have complete liberty to think, teach, and write." Compared to other regions, censorship was less stringent, and the range of subjects that could be explored was vast. As Jena's most distinguished resident, the playwright Friedrich Schiller, put it, "The professors here are almost entirely independent." This freedom drew thinkers, writers, and poets who faced persecution in their homelands to this open and relatively free city.

Schiller himself had sought refuge in Jena after being arrested for his revolutionary play The Robbers (1781) in the Duchy of Württemberg. On a given day, it wouldn't be uncommon to find more renowned authors, philosophers, and poets strolling through Jena's streets than in a larger city over an entire century. Among them were the tall and gaunt-looking Schiller (who required a drawer full of rotten apples for his writing), the obstinate philosopher Fichte, who centered himself in his work, and the young scientist Alexander von Humboldt – the first to predict harmful human-induced climate change.

The brilliant Schlegel brothers, Friedrich and August Wilhelm, both writers and critics with pens as sharp as French guillotines, lived in Jena, as did the young philosopher Friedrich Schelling, who redefined the relationship between the individual and nature, and G W F Hegel, who would become one of the most influential philosophers in the Western world. This group of extraordinary thinkers was united by a shared obsession with the free self – a rare freedom indeed during an era of absolutism.

In Jena lived the formidable and spirited Caroline Michaelis-Böhmer-Schlegel-Schelling. Despite bearing the names of her father and three husbands, she refused to live by social conventions. The young poet Novalis, a former student of Jena, often visited his friends from his nearby estate in Weißenfels during the winter months. One could even catch a glimpse of Germany's most celebrated poet, Johann Wolfgang von Goethe, as he skated on the river – his bulging belly held together by a flowery waistcoat. As an older and more renowned figure, Goethe served as something like a benevolent godfather to the younger generation. He was inspired, even rejuvenated, by their new ideas, while they idolized him in return.

These great thinkers drew students from across Germany and Europe to Jena. Starstruck at seeing so many famous poets and philosophers sitting together at concerts in Zur Rose tavern, they couldn't believe their eyes when seemingly all of Germany's greatest minds squeezed into one room during parties. Each of these intellectual giants lived a life worth recounting, but the fact that they all came together at the same time in the same place is even more remarkable. That's why I call them the 'Jena Set' in my book Magnificent Rebels (2022). In an age when most of Europe was still held in shackles, Jena offered a taste of liberation and intellectual exploration that resonates even today.

Title: Unraveling the Secrets Hidden in Whale Baleen

Whales, these magnificent creatures of the sea, have always been a source of fascination for us humans. And there's no shortage of old baleen around from the whaling era. Baleen, much like plastic in our contemporary world, was once extensively used – from corset stays to brooms to buggy whips. Museums seem to have a surplus of it gathering dust in their collections. Recently, I've found myself drowning in offers for collaborations, and I've even heard whispers of half a dozen labs delving into baleen analysis. 'It seems everyone with a hormone lab is jumping on this,' I remarked.

Baleen serves as a long-term logbook of a whale's journey and a reliable seasonal chronometer. The stress hormone, cortisol, has attracted significant attention across wildlife biology due to its potential to uncover harmful influences. Although bowheads are thriving, we must keep in mind that their habitat is undergoing rapid changes – sea ice melting, increasing ship traffic, and the noise pollution from oil and gas exploration in the Arctic. The ultimate goal of our baleen research is to develop methods for assessing stress levels related to environmental change and human activity.

Other informative compounds are also emerging in baleen. Research dating back to the 1980s has shown that fluctuations in the ratio of stable carbon and nitrogen isotopes along the length of bowhead baleen reflect the unique prey the whales consume in their summer versus winter habitats. Essentially, baleen serves as a long-term logbook of a whale's travels and a reliable seasonal chronometer. We are examining this pattern in bowhead baleen dating back to 1952 to determine whether the whales' diet and migrations are changing, potentially due to shrinking sea ice. Furthermore, we are studying isotope and hormone data from the same baleen samples to confirm estimates of bowheads' gestation period and calving frequency. We believe this information will help us pinpoint not just when but roughly where individual bowheads conceived.

On rare occasions, even the bodies of these whales serve as historical artifacts. Native Alaskan hunters have discovered remarkably old whaling gear in harvested animals. In the 1990s, a stone tool that had gone out of use in the 1880s was found. 'When I saw that, I just remember... my head spinning,' said Craig George, a bowhead expert and wildlife biologist with North Slope Borough who collaborates on several baleen studies. 'I immediately knew that this was absolutely remarkable.' Over the years, hunters have discovered other artifacts, but dating stone tools isn't always reliable. However, in 2007, a Yankee-era harpoon point with an 1879 patent was found lodged in another whale, suggesting its age to be between 115 and 130 years. George and his team conducted chemical analyses of bowhead eye lenses that ultimately indicated they can live for up to 200 years or more.
